### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the "Design Process" as per Pahl and Beitz. List the four major phases of the design process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Design Process** is a systematic, iterative sequence of engineering activities used to convert a customer need into a fully defined, manufacturable product.

The four major phases are:
1. **Clarification of the Task** – Problem identification, requirements, and abstraction.
2. **Conceptual Design** – Generation and selection of solution concepts.
3. **Embodiment Design** – Conversion of concept into a defined layout.
4. **Detail Design** – Finalization of drawings, analysis, and production documents.

*(Each phase = 1 mark; definition = 1 mark; introduction = 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a "Requirements List" (Lastenheft)? Why is it essential in the design process?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Requirements List** is a formal, structured document that converts customer wishes into objective, measurable engineering specifications. It includes:
- **Geometric requirements** (size, clearance, weight).
- **Functional requirements** (load, speed, power, life).
- **Manufacturing and cost constraints**.
- **Safety, ergonomic, and environmental specifications**.

**Importance:** It eliminates ambiguity, provides a design baseline, enables objective comparison of concepts, and forms a legal/contractual reference between client and designer. Without it, designs become subjective and prone to costly revisions.

*(Definition = 1 mark; contents = 1 mark; importance = 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the sequential steps involved in the design process. Mention the iterative nature of the process with at least two feedback loops.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A design team has shortlisted 3 concepts (X, Y, Z) for a new gearbox housing. The weights and scores (on a 1–10 scale) are given below. Use the **Weighted Scoring Method** to select the best concept.

| Criterion | Weight | X | Y | Z |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | 0.35 | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| Strength | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Manufacturability | 0.20 | 6 | 8 | 9 |
| Weight Reduction | 0.20 | 9 | 6 | 7 |

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Sequential Steps of Design Process:**

The design process consists of 12 steps grouped into 4 phases:

1. **Problem Identification** – Understanding the customer need.
2. **Requirements List** – Writing objective specifications.
3. **Abstraction and Function Analysis** – Identifying core functions.
4. **Concept Generation** – Brainstorming multiple solutions.
5. **Concept Evaluation** – Using decision matrices.
6. **Concept Selection** – Selecting the most viable concept.
7. **Product Architecture** – Defining sub-functions and sub-systems.
8. **Layout Drawing** – Sketching spatial arrangements.
9. **Embodiment Validation** – Checking feasibility (DFM/DFA).
10. **Detailed Engineering Drawings** – Final 2D/3D CAD with tolerances.
11. **Engineering Analysis** – FEA, fatigue, vibration, and tolerance stack-up.
12. **Production Documentation** – BOM, process plans, assembly instructions.

**Iterative Loops:**
- *Loop 1:* Concept Evaluation → Concept Generation (if no concept is feasible).
- *Loop 2:* Engineering Analysis → Detailed Drawing (if analysis fails verification).
- *Loop 3:* Production Documentation → Concept Selection (if DFM constraints are violated).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe the steps as a **linear sequence** and forget to mention **feedback loops**. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction. Always mention that the design process is **iterative**, not linear.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Weighted Scoring Solution:**

The weighted score equation:

$$
S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ij}
$$

**Concept X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X &= (0.35 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 9) \\
S_X &= 2.80 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.80 \\
S_X &= 7.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Concept Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Y &= (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 6) \\
S_Y &= 2.45 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 1.20 \\
S_Y &= 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Concept Z:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Z &= (0.35 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) \\
S_Z &= 2.10 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.40 \\
S_Z &= 7.30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
S_X \, (7.55) > S_Y \, (7.50) > S_Z \, (7.30)
$$

**Conclusion:** **Concept X** is the best design choice, with a weighted score of **7.55**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to:
> - Convert the criterion name into a unit (e.g., "0.35" with no unit is fine, but weights must sum to 1.0).
> - Show intermediate calculation steps. The KTU valuation key requires **at least 2 intermediate steps** per concept. Skipping directly to the answer will cost 2–3 marks.

---

**Question B. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Modified] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between **Conceptual Design** and **Embodiment Design**. List at least 3 activities performed in each phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mechanical system has 5 components in series with individual reliabilities: $R_1 = 0.97$, $R_2 = 0.95$, $R_3 = 0.90$, $R_4 = 0.98$, $R_5 = 0.92$. Calculate the overall system reliability. If a parallel redundant unit of the weakest component is added, what is the new system reliability?

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Conceptual vs. Embodiment Design:**

| Aspect | Conceptual Design | Embodiment Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Generate and select solution principles. | Convert the chosen concept into a defined layout. |
| **Output** | Solution principles, sketches, decision matrix. | Layout drawings, sub-system definitions. |
| **Detail Level** | Abstract, qualitative, function-focused. | Quantitative, geometric, dimensionally defined. |
| **Creativity vs. Discipline** | High creativity, low constraint. | Lower creativity, high discipline. |
| **Cost Impact** | Highest (decisions here lock ~70% of cost). | Medium (refines cost and manufacturability). |

**Conceptual Design Activities (3 examples):**
1. Brainstorming and morphological analysis.
2. Function-structure analysis.
3. Concept evaluation via Pugh chart or weighted scoring.

**Embodiment Design Activities (3 examples):**
1. Defining product architecture (sub-functions, sub-systems).
2. Creating layout drawings with spatial arrangements.
3. Embodiment validation via DFM (Design for Manufacturing) and DFA (Design for Assembly).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Conceptual Design** with **Embodiment Design** by treating them as synonyms. The KTU valuation key **requires a clear distinction** with at least 3 differentiating points. Drawing a comparison table earns 5 out of 7 marks easily.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Series System Reliability:**

The equation for a series system:

$$
R_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i
$$

**Identify the weakest component (lowest reliability):**
From the given data, $R_3 = 0.90$ is the lowest.

**Calculate original system reliability (no redundancy):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sys} &= 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.98 \times 0.92 \\
R_{sys} &= 0.97 \times 0.95 = 0.9215 \\
R_{sys} &= 0.9215 \times 0.90 = 0.8294 \\
R_{sys} &= 0.8294 \times 0.98 = 0.8128 \\
R_{sys} &= 0.8128 \times 0.92 = 0.7478
\end{aligned}
$$

**Original system reliability: $R_{sys,1} \approx 0.7478$.**

**With parallel redundancy on the weakest component ($R_3$):**
The new equivalent reliability of the redundant pair:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{3,new} &= 1 - (1 - R_3)(1 - R_3) \\
R_{3,new} &= 1 - (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.90) \\
R_{3,new} &= 1 - (0.10)(0.10) \\
R_{3,new} &= 1 - 0.01 \\
R_{3,new} &= 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

**New system reliability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sys,2} &= 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.92 \\
R_{sys,2} &= 0.9215 \times 0.99 = 0.9123 \\
R_{sys,2} &= 0.9123 \times 0.98 = 0.8941 \\
R_{sys,2} &= 0.8941 \times 0.92 = 0.8225
\end{aligned}
$$

**New system reliability: $R_{sys,2} \approx 0.8225$.**

**Conclusion:** Adding parallel redundancy to the weakest component increased the system reliability from **74.78%** to **82.25%** — a substantial improvement of **~7.5 percentage points**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often apply the parallel formula to the **wrong** component. The KTU answer key explicitly states: *"Identify the weakest component first, then apply the parallel formula only to that component."* If the student applies redundancy to all 5 components, marks are deducted.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Design Process Steps" (KTU PEMRT522 Module 1):**

- ⭐ The design process is **iterative**, not linear. Always include feedback loops in flowcharts.
- ⭐ The four phases are: **Clarification → Conceptual → Embodiment → Detail**.
- ⭐ The **Requirements List (Lastenheft)** is the foundation — it converts subjective needs into objective specs.
- ⭐ **Function Analysis** is a key technique to separate essential functions from non-essentials.
- ⭐ **Concept Generation** uses brainstorming, synectics, and morphological matrices.
- ⭐ **Concept Selection** uses the **Weighted Scoring Method** and **Pugh Chart**.
- ⭐ **Embodiment Design** validates the concept for DFM, DFA, and DFE (Design for Environment).
- ⭐ **Detail Design** includes FEA, tolerance stack-up, fatigue, vibration, and final drawings.
- ⭐ **Series Reliability** is multiplicative: $R_{sys} = \prod R_i$. System is weaker than its weakest link.
- ⭐ **Parallel Reliability** formula: $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$. Always redundant the **weakest** component.
- ⭐ **Factor of Safety (FoS)** for static loads is typically **1.5 to 4.0** depending on material and load uncertainty.
- ⭐ **KTU Exam Pattern:** Part A = 3 marks (2 questions); Part B = 14 marks (with internal choice).
- ⭐ **Common marks-losing mistakes:** (1) Linear flowcharts, (2) Missing units in weighted scores, (3) Wrong parallel reliability application, (4) Skipping intermediate steps in calculation problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, the *Factors Influencing Design* constitute the complete set of **functional, manufacturing, economic, ergonomic, environmental, and regulatory constraints** that a design engineer must simultaneously satisfy while conceiving a product. These factors act as the **boundary conditions** of the design optimization problem, determining the feasible design space within which the optimal solution must lie.

Mathematically, the design objective can be expressed as a multi-constrained optimization:

$$
\min \; f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to:} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the vector of design variables, $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the objective (e.g., cost, weight, or manufacturing time), and $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ represents the **factor constraints** (functional, manufacturing, economic, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT522 Module 1):** A designer who ignores even a single dominant factor produces a product that is either *non-functional*, *non-manufacturable*, *un-economic*, or *unsafe*. The art of engineering design lies in the **simultaneous balancing** of all these factors.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Designing a House"**
> Imagine you are an architect designing a house. You cannot just focus on making it *beautiful* (aesthetics) or just *cheap* (cost). You must consider:
> - **Who will live in it?** (Functional requirements — number of rooms, accessibility)
> - **What materials are available locally?** (Manufacturing & material factors)
> - **What is the budget?** (Economic factor)
> - **Will it withstand earthquakes and rain?** (Quality, reliability, safety)
> - **Is it easy to maintain?** (Serviceability)
> - **Does it follow local building codes?** (Standards & regulations)
>
> A house designed with only aesthetics and budget in mind will collapse in the first monsoon. Similarly, a **product designed without considering all factors** will fail in the real world.

## 1.3 Classification of Factors Influencing Design

The factors are broadly classified into **eight primary categories** as per standard KTU Module 1 reference frameworks:

| # | Factor Category | Primary Concern |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Functional Requirements** | What the product must *do* |
| 2 | **Material Selection** | What the product is *made of* |
| 3 | **Manufacturing Process** | How the product is *made* |
| 4 | **Economic / Cost Factors** | What it *costs* to make, sell, and use |
| 5 | **Quality & Reliability** | How *well* and how *long* it works |
| 6 | **Safety & Ergonomics** | How *safely and comfortably* it is used |
| 7 | **Aesthetic & Environmental** | How it *looks* and its *ecological impact* |
| 8 | **Standards, Codes & Regulations** | What *legal and industry rules* must be obeyed |

## 1.4 The Design Triad — Central to KTU Module 1

A foundational concept in KTU PEMRT522 is the **Design Triad** (also called the *Three-legged stool* of design):

$$
\boxed{\text{Design} = f(\text{Function}, \; \text{Material}, \; \text{Process})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every successful design must achieve the **right balance** between **Function** (what it does), **Material** (what it's made of), and **Process** (how it's made). Changing any one leg destabilizes the other two.

## 1.5 Physical & Standard Design Constants (Used in Module 1 Calculations)

- **Factor of Safety (FoS):** $n = 1.5$ to $4.0$ for general mechanical components; higher for critical aerospace/pressure-vessel applications.
- **Standard cost basis:** $\text{Cost} = f(\text{Material}, \text{Process}, \text{Assembly}, \text{Inspection}, \text{Overhead})$
- **Standard tolerance grades:** **IT01** (finest) to **IT18** (coarsest) as per **ISO 286**.
- **Standard surface roughness range:** $R_a = 0.012 \; \mu m$ (mirror finish) to $R_a = 100 \; \mu m$ (rough cast).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Factor

### 2.1.1 Functional Requirements
The **primary driver** of any design. The product must satisfy the intended purpose under specified operating conditions.
- **Performance specifications** (load, speed, power, capacity).
- **Operating environment** (temperature, humidity, corrosive media).
- **Service life** and duty cycle.
- **Compatibility** with interfacing systems.

> **KTU Insight:** A design that does not meet the functional requirement is a **design failure**, irrespective of how elegant it looks or how cheap it is.

### 2.1.2 Material Selection
Material choice governs **strength, weight, cost, manufacturability, and recyclability**.
- **Mechanical properties:** Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{uts}$), Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$), Hardness (HRC/HB), Fatigue Limit ($\sigma_e$).
- **Physical properties:** Density ($\rho$), Thermal Conductivity ($k$), Coefficient of Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$).
- **Chemical properties:** Corrosion resistance, oxidation behavior.
- **Manufacturing properties:** Castability, machinability rating, weldability, formability.
- **Economic properties:** Raw cost, availability, recyclability.

### 2.1.3 Manufacturing Process & Design for Manufacture (DFM)
The chosen manufacturing process imposes **geometric, dimensional, and tolerance constraints** on the design.
- **Casting:** Uniform wall thickness, draft angles, no undercuts (or parting-line friendly).
- **Forging:** Draft angles, fillet radii, grain flow direction.
- **Machining:** Accessibility for tools, standard tool sizes, datum features.
- **Sheet Metal:** Minimum bend radius, hole-to-edge distance, bend relief.
- **Injection Molding (Plastics):** Uniform wall thickness ($1\text{–}3 \; mm$), draft $\geq 1°$, no sharp corners.

### 2.1.4 Economic / Cost Factors
Cost is often the **deciding factor** in commercial success.

$$
C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{process} + C_{assembly} + C_{inspection} + C_{overhead} + C_{tooling}
$$

- **Tooling cost** is dominant in injection molding and die casting.
- **Material cost** is dominant in machined and forged parts.
- **Assembly cost** can constitute **40–60%** of total product cost in complex products.

### 2.1.5 Quality & Reliability
- **Quality:** Conformance to specifications (**zero-defect** philosophy, Taguchi loss function).
- **Reliability:** Probability of performing intended function under stated conditions for a stated period.

$$
\text{Reliability } R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the failure rate and $t$ is the mission time.

### 2.1.6 Safety & Ergonomics
- **Safety Factor:** $n = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$ or $n = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$.
- **Ergonomics (Human Factors):** Ease of use, grip, weight, visibility, hearing/vibration limits.

### 2.1.7 Aesthetic & Environmental
- **Aesthetics:** Form, color, finish, perceived value.
- **Environmental:** Recyclability, energy consumption in manufacturing, end-of-life disposal, **RoHS** and **WEEE** compliance.

### 2.1.8 Standards, Codes & Regulations
- **BIS** (India), **ISO**, **ASTM**, **DIN**, **JIS**.
- **Dimensional standards:** Preferred numbers (Renard series), standard fits, standard tolerances.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU ESE — these formulas and definitions are repeatedly tested in Module 1 questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Use / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Optimization Objective | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | General design problem |
| 2 | Factor of Safety | $n = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\sigma_{d}}$ | $n$ is dimensionless, $\sigma$ in MPa |
| 3 | Total Product Cost | $C_T = C_m + C_p + C_a + C_i + C_o$ | All costs in INR/USD |
| 4 | Cost per Feature (DFMA) | $C_f = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{features}}$ | INR per feature |
| 5 | Reliability (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ in failures/hr |
| 6 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours |
| 7 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq A \leq 1$ |
| 8 | Bending Stress (rect. beam) | $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I} = \dfrac{6 M}{b t^2}$ | Pa or N/mm² |
| 9 | Deflection (cantilever, tip load) | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^3}{3 E I}$ | mm |
| 10 | Preferred Numbers (Renard) | $R_a$ series: $1, 1.25, 1.6, 2.0, 2.5, \ldots$ | Standard sizing |
| 11 | Minimum Wall Thickness Rule | $t_{min}$ based on process (e.g., casting: $5\text{–}8 \; mm$) | mm |
| 12 | Bend Allowance (sheet metal) | $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{2} (R + k t)$ | mm |
| 13 | Hole-to-Edge Distance | $\geq 1.5 \, d$ (for sheet metal) | mm |
| 14 | Draft Angle | $1°$ to $3°$ (plastics), $3°$ to $7°$ (castings) | Degrees |
| 15 | Taguchi Loss Function | $L(y) = k (y - m)^2$ | $k$ = loss coefficient |
| 16 | DFM Complexity Index | $C_i = N_{parts} \times T_{assembly}$ | Parts $\times$ seconds |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These factors are not academic abstractions — they are the **daily decision tools** of a practicing design engineer in:
> - **Automotive industry** (DFM for body panels, ergonomic dashboard design, crash safety, BS-VI emission standards).
> - **Consumer electronics** (thin-wall injection molding, miniaturization, RoHS compliance, antenna design).
> - **Medical devices** (biocompatibility, sterilization, ISO 13485, ultra-high reliability).
> - **Heavy machinery & aerospace** (high FoS, fatigue analysis, AS9100 certification, weight optimization).

A clear understanding of these factors enables the engineer to produce designs that are **simultaneously functional, manufacturable, economical, safe, reliable, and legally compliant** — the true hallmark of a KTU-trained engineering graduate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for Factor Selection

The **Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP)** style matrix below is the KTU-recommended approach for prioritizing factors when designing a product. Each factor is scored on a scale of **1 (lowest impact)** to **5 (highest impact)**.

| Design Factor | Function Score (1–5) | Manufacturing Score (1–5) | Cost Score (1–5) | Safety Score (1–5) | Total Weighted Score |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Functional Requirements | 5 | 3 | 3 | 4 | 15 |
| Material Selection | 4 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 17 |
| Manufacturing Process | 3 | 5 | 5 | 3 | 16 |
| Cost / Economics | 2 | 4 | 5 | 2 | 13 |
| Quality & Reliability | 4 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 14 |
| Safety & Ergonomics | 3 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 12 |
| Aesthetics & Environment | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 6 |
| Standards & Codes | 2 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 10 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In this example, **Material Selection** carries the highest weighted score (17), making it the **dominant factor**. The design engineer must therefore invest maximum effort in optimizing the material choice before finalizing the geometry.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Cost of a Simple Designed Component

**Problem Setup:** A simple cylindrical shaft is to be designed for a moderate-load application. Estimate the **total manufacturing cost** per piece, given the following:

- Material: Mild Steel (AISI 1020), density $\rho = 7850 \; kg/m^3$.
- Finished shaft dimensions: Diameter $D = 50 \; mm$, Length $L = 300 \; mm$.
- Raw stock is a bar of diameter $D_0 = 55 \; mm$ (oversize for machining).
- Material cost: $C_m^{rate} = \text{INR } 80 \; / kg$.
- Machining time: $T_m = 12$ minutes.
- Machine hourly rate: $C_{mhr} = \text{INR } 400 \; / hr$.
- Tooling (amortized): $C_t = \text{INR } 5$ per piece.
- Inspection: $C_i = \text{INR } 8$ per piece.
- Overhead: 20% of (material + machining) cost.

### Step 1: Compute the Volume of the Raw Stock (Bar)

The raw stock is a cylinder of diameter $D_0 = 55 \; mm$ and length $L = 300 \; mm$.

$$
V_{raw} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 \times L
$$

Substituting values (converting $mm$ to $m$ for SI consistency: $D_0 = 0.055 \; m$, $L = 0.300 \; m$):

$$
V_{raw} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.055)^2 \times 0.300
$$

$$
V_{raw} = 0.7854 \times 0.003025 \times 0.300
$$

$$
V_{raw} = 0.0007127 \; m^3
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Mass of the Raw Stock

$$
m_{raw} = \rho \times V_{raw}
$$

$$
m_{raw} = 7850 \; kg/m^3 \times 0.0007127 \; m^3
$$

$$
m_{raw} = 5.595 \; kg
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Material Cost

$$
C_{material} = m_{raw} \times C_m^{rate}
$$

$$
C_{material} = 5.595 \; kg \times 80 \; INR/kg
$$

$$
C_{material} = 447.60 \; INR
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Machining Cost

Convert machining time to hours:

$$
T_m = 12 \; \text{min} = 0.200 \; \text{hr}
$$

$$
C_{machining} = T_m \times C_{mhr}
$$

$$
C_{machining} = 0.200 \; hr \times 400 \; INR/hr
$$

$$
C_{machining} = 80.00 \; INR
$$

### Step 5: Add Tooling and Inspection Costs

$$
C_{tooling} = 5.00 \; INR
$$

$$
C_{inspection} = 8.00 \; INR
$$

### Step 6: Compute the Overhead

$$
C_{overhead} = 0.20 \times (C_{material} + C_{machining})
$$

$$
C_{overhead} = 0.20 \times (447.60 + 80.00)
$$

$$
C_{overhead} = 0.20 \times 527.60
$$

$$
C_{overhead} = 105.52 \; INR
$$

### Step 7: Sum All Costs to Get the Total Cost per Piece

$$
C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{machining} + C_{tooling} + C_{inspection} + C_{overhead}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 447.60 + 80.00 + 5.00 + 8.00 + 105.52
$$

$$
C_{total} = 646.12 \; INR \; \text{per piece}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** Notice that the **material cost** (447.60 INR) constitutes **~69%** of the total cost. This immediately tells the design engineer that to reduce cost, the most effective lever is to **reduce material usage** — e.g., by using a hollow shaft, switching to a cheaper material, or redesigning with near-net-shape processes like forging instead of machining from a bar. **This is the essence of DFM-driven cost engineering.**

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Choosing Factor of Safety

**Problem:** A pressure vessel is to be designed for a maximum internal pressure $P = 2.5 \; MPa$. The wall is made of mild steel with yield strength $\sigma_y = 250 \; MPa$ and ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{uts} = 410 \; MPa$.

**Step 1: Determine the type of loading and consequence of failure.**
- This is a **static pressure load** with **catastrophic failure consequence** (vessel explosion can be fatal).

**Step 2: Look up the recommended FoS from standard KTU tables.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{ductile, ductile} &= 1.5 \text{–} 2.0 \\
n_{ductile, brittle} &= 2.0 \text{–} 3.0 \\
n_{static, known loads} &= 1.25 \text{–} 1.5 \\
n_{static, uncertain loads} &= 2.0 \text{–} 3.0 \\
n_{fatigue, ductile} &= 3.0 \text{–} 4.0 \\
n_{pressure vessels (ASME)} &= 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Select $n = 4.0$ for pressure vessel (per ASME BPVC).**

**Step 4: Compute allowable working stress.**

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{n}
$$

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{410 \; MPa}{4.0}
$$

$$
\sigma_{allow} = 102.5 \; MPa
$$

**Step 5: Compute the required wall thickness** (for thin-walled cylinder, hoop stress dominant):

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t} \leq \sigma_{allow}
$$

Rearranging for $t$:

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

Assuming vessel radius $r = 250 \; mm = 0.250 \; m$:

$$
t = \frac{2.5 \; MPa \times 0.250 \; m}{102.5 \; MPa}
$$

$$
t = \frac{0.625}{102.5} \; m
$$

$$
t = 0.00610 \; m = 6.10 \; mm
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Takeaway:** A higher Factor of Safety **directly increases the material required** (wall thickness grows), which in turn **increases cost and weight**. This demonstrates the classic **trade-off triangle** of engineering design: **Safety vs. Cost vs. Weight**. The designer's job is to find the *minimum acceptable* FoS that satisfies regulations.

## 3.4 Analytical Trade-off Curve (Conceptual)

The trade-off between Factor of Safety, Cost, and Weight is captured by:

$$
C \propto n \quad \text{and} \quad W \propto n
$$

> A plot of $C$ vs. $n$ is a straight line through the origin with slope proportional to material cost per unit mass. The designer selects the **minimum $n$** satisfying all three constraints (regulatory, economic, operational) — the **design optimum**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Eight Factors Influencing Design

This diagram shows how the **eight primary design factors** interact in a typical product design workflow, and how they collectively constrain the final design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Product Concept] --> B[1. Functional Requirements]
    B --> C[2. Material Selection]
    C --> D[3. Manufacturing Process]
    D --> E[4. Economic Cost Analysis]
    E --> F[5. Quality and Reliability Check]
    F --> G[6. Safety and Ergonomics Audit]
    G --> H[7. Aesthetic and Environmental Review]
    H --> I[8. Standards Codes and Regulation Check]
    I --> J{All Factors Satisfied?}
    J -- NO --> K[Iterate and Redesign]
    K --> B
    J -- YES --> L[FINAL DESIGN APPROVED]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Design Triad and Trade-offs

This diagram visualizes the **Design Triad (Function, Material, Process)** and shows how an **economic constraint** and **safety constraint** act as outer guard-rails on the triad.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[FUNCTION\nWhat the product does] --> X[DESIGN SOLUTION]
    M[MATERIAL\nWhat it is made of] --> X
    P[PROCESS\nHow it is made] --> X
    
    EC[Economic Constraint\nCost Budget] -.->|Limits| X
    SC[Safety Constraint\nFactor of Safety] -.->|Limits| X
    QC[Quality Constraint\nReliability Spec] -.->|Limits| X
    RC[Regulatory Constraint\nISO ASTM BIS] -.->|Limits| X
    
    X --> OUT[OPTIMAL PRODUCT]
    
    style F fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style M fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    style RC fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

This diagram shows the typical **cost-driver hierarchy** for a manufactured product, illustrating which factor dominates in which manufacturing scenario.

```mermaid

    ROOT --> L1B[Process Cost 20 to 40 percent]
    ROOT --> L1C[Assembly Cost 5 to 30 percent]
    ROOT --> L1D[Inspection Overhead 5 to 10 percent]
    
    L1A --> L2A1[Material Selection Factor]
    L1A --> L2A2[Buy vs Make Decision]
    
    L1B --> L2B1[Process Selection Factor]
    L1B --> L2B2[Tooling Cost Factor]
    L1B --> L2B3[Batch Size Factor]
    
    L1C --> L2C1[DFA Design for Assembly]
    L1C --> L2C2[Part Count Reduction]
    
    L1D --> L2D1[Quality Standards]
    L1D --> L2D2[Regulatory Compliance]

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cost vs Factor of Safety Trade-off Curve

This conceptual diagram illustrates the **classic engineering trade-off** between safety (FoS) and cost/weight.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOW[Low FoS n=1.2] -->|Unsafe may fail| RISK[High Risk of Failure]
    LOW -->|Cheap lightweight| CHEAP[Low Cost Low Weight]
    
    MED[Medium FoS n=2.5] -->|Balanced| OPT[OPTIMAL DESIGN ZONE]
    MED -->|Acceptable cost| ACC[Acceptable Cost and Weight]
    
    HIGH[High FoS n=5.0] -->|Over engineered| WASTE[Material Waste]
    HIGH -->|Expensive heavy| EXP[High Cost High Weight]
    
    RISK -.->|Redesign upward| MED
    WASTE -.->|Optimize downward| MED
    CHEAP -.->|Add safety margin| MED
    EXP -.->|Reduce overdesign| MED
    
    style LOW fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style MED fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style HIGH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style OPT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style RISK fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style WASTE fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style CHEAP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style EXP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style ACC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The middle zone ($n = 2.0$ to $3.0$) represents the **engineering optimum** — a balance between safety, cost, and weight. Both extremes (under-design and over-design) are penalized in real-world engineering practice.
