## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q1.** Define *Material Index*. State the material index to be maximized for designing a **lightweight, stiff beam** with rectangular cross-section, fixed length and applied load.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Material Index** is a combination of material properties that characterizes the performance of a component in a specific application, with all geometric and loading terms factored out. It is derived by expressing the performance objective (mass, cost, stiffness, etc.) in terms of material properties, identifying the constraint, and isolating the material-dependent group.

**Derivation of the index for a lightweight, stiff beam:**

For a beam of length $L$, width $b$, depth $d$, carrying load $F$ with deflection limit $\delta$:

$$ \delta = \frac{C \cdot F \cdot L^3}{E \cdot I} \quad \text{where } I = \frac{b d^3}{12} $$

For a square section, this reduces to the index:

$$ M = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho} \quad \text{(maximize)} $$

> **(3 marks awarded for definition + derivation + final expression)**

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]`

**Q2.** List the **four stages of the Ashby material selection methodology**. Mention the role of *Material Property Charts* in the procedure.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The four stages are:
> 1. **Translation** — Convert design requirements into function, constraints, objectives, and free variables.
> 2. **Screening** — Apply property limits to eliminate unsuitable materials.
> 3. **Ranking** — Use Material Indices to rank the surviving candidates.
> 4. **Documentation & Process Selection** — Final selection considering manufacturing feasibility.

**Role of Material Property Charts:**
- These are **logarithmic plots** of two (or more) material properties (e.g., $E$ vs $\rho$, $\sigma_y$ vs $\rho$).
- They allow **visual screening** of material families.
- A **Material Index** appears as a **straight line** on the chart, enabling rapid identification of optimal families.
- The Cambridge Engineering Selector (CES) software is built on this concept.

> **(3 marks awarded for stages + role of charts)**

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Set A]`

**Q.A.** (a) *Explain the systematic procedure for the selection of engineering materials in design using the **Ashby Methodology**. Use a suitable case study (e.g., design of a lightweight tension tie in an aircraft wing) to illustrate the procedure.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *A cantilever beam of length 1.5 m carries an end load of 5 kN. The allowable tip deflection is 5 mm. The cross-section is square. The candidates are:* **(7 marks)**
- *Structural steel: E = 210 GPa, ρ = 7850 kg/m³*
- *Aluminum alloy: E = 70 GPa, ρ = 2700 kg/m³*
- *GFRP: E = 25 GPa, ρ = 1800 kg/m³*

*Determine the most suitable material and the cross-section dimension. Use the material index method.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A.(a):**

**Step 1 — Define the case study (function):**
- **Component:** Aircraft wing tension tie.
- **Function:** Carry tensile load $F$ from the wing structure to the fuselage.

**Step 2 — Constraints:**
- $\sigma_y \geq 350\,\text{MPa}$ (must not yield)
- $\rho \leq 5000\,\text{kg/m}^3$ (lightweight requirement)
- Operating temperature: $-50°\text{C}$ to $+80°\text{C}$
- Must be corrosion-resistant (humid cabin environment)

**Step 3 — Objective:** Minimize mass $m$.

**Step 4 — Derive the material index:**

$$ m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L, \quad A = F/\sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{F \cdot L \cdot \rho}{\sigma_y} $$

$$ M = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \text{(maximize — specific strength)} $$

**Step 5 — Plot on Ashby $\sigma_y$–$\rho$ chart** (line of slope 1).
- **Selected material family:** Titanium alloys and high-strength Al-alloys lie on or near the optimum line.
- **Final choice:** Ti-6Al-4V (specific strength $\approx 250\,\text{kNm/kg}$) — best balance of strength, weight, and corrosion resistance.

**Step 6 — Cross-check process:** Ti-alloys require forging or additive manufacturing (3D printing for complex shapes).

> **(7 marks — full mark for translation, constraint, index derivation, chart interpretation, and process compatibility)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A.(b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5\,\text{m}$, $F = 5\,\text{kN} = 5000\,\text{N}$
- $\delta_{\max} = 5\,\text{mm} = 0.005\,\text{m}$
- Cross-section: square, side $a$

**Step 1 — Derive the mass-deflection relationship for a cantilever:**

$$ \delta = \frac{4 F L^3}{E a^4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad a^4 = \frac{4 F L^3}{E \delta} $$

**Step 2 — Mass of beam:**

$$ m = \rho \cdot L \cdot a^2 = \rho \cdot L \cdot \left( \frac{4 F L^3}{E \delta} \right)^{1/2} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $m \cdot (E^{1/2}/\rho)^{-1}$ for each material (or compare index $E^{1/2}/\rho$):**

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $E^{1/2}/\rho$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel | 210 | 7850 | $\sqrt{210 \times 10^9}/7850 = 0.00185$ |
| Al-alloy | 70 | 2700 | $\sqrt{70 \times 10^9}/2700 = 0.00310$ |
| GFRP | 25 | 1800 | $\sqrt{25 \times 10^9}/1800 = 0.00278$ |

**Step 4 — Rank by index (maximize):**
- **Al-alloy > GFRP > Steel** → Al-alloy is the best.

**Step 5 — Compute $a$ for Al-alloy:**

$$ a^4 = \frac{4 \times 5000 \times (1.5)^3}{(70 \times 10^9) \times 0.005} = \frac{4 \times 5000 \times 3.375}{3.5 \times 10^8} $$

$$ a^4 = \frac{67500}{3.5 \times 10^8} = 1.9286 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^4 $$

$$ a = (1.9286 \times 10^{-4})^{1/4} = 0.1174\,\text{m} \approx 117.4\,\text{mm} $$

**Step 6 — Compute mass of Al-alloy beam:**

$$ m = 2700 \times 1.5 \times (0.1174)^2 = 2700 \times 1.5 \times 0.01378 = 55.8\,\text{kg} $$

**Step 7 — Final answer:**
- **Selected material:** Aluminum alloy
- **Cross-section side:** $a \approx 117.4\,\text{mm}$
- **Mass:** $\approx 55.8\,\text{kg}$

> **(7 marks — index computation, ranking, dimensional calculation, final selection)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Set B]`

**Q.B.** (a) *With the help of a labeled schematic, explain the **Ashby Material Property Chart** for Young's Modulus ($E$) versus Density ($\rho$). Explain how a **Material Index** appears as a straight line on this chart. **(7 marks)**

(b) *A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 300 mm and operates at an internal pressure of 4 MPa. The allowable stress is 250 MPa, and the material density must not exceed 4500 kg/m³. Compare three candidate materials based on the **strength-to-weight ratio** index:* **(7 marks)**
- *Carbon steel: σy = 350 MPa, ρ = 7850 kg/m³*
- *Al 6061-T6: σy = 240 MPa, ρ = 2700 kg/m³*
- *GFRP: σy = 200 MPa, ρ = 1800 kg/m³*

*Also determine the wall thickness required for each material and recommend the best choice for a portable pressure vessel.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B.(a):**

**Schematic Description:**
- **X-axis:** $\log_{10}(\rho)$ in kg/m³, ranging from 100 to 100,000.
- **Y-axis:** $\log_{10}(E)$ in GPa, ranging from 0.001 to 1000.
- **Bubble regions** for material families: Metals (top-right), Polymers (bottom-left), Ceramics (top-left), Composites (between).

**Material Index as a Straight Line:**

For an index $M = E^{1/n}/\rho$:

$$ \log E = n \log \rho + n \log M $$

This is the equation of a **straight line of slope $n$** on the $\log E$–$\log \rho$ chart. Materials lying on lines progressively further towards the top-left have higher $M$ values. The selection is made by drawing such a line through the most attractive region of the chart and choosing the material family closest to it.

> **(7 marks — schematic explanation + index-to-line mapping with derivation)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B.(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the strength-to-weight index for each material:**

| Material | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $\sigma_y/\rho$ ($\text{kNm/kg}$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon steel | 350 | 7850 | 44.6 |
| Al 6061-T6 | 240 | 2700 | 88.9 |
| GFRP | 200 | 1800 | 111.1 |

**Step 2 — Rank by $\sigma_y/\rho$ (maximize):**
**GFRP > Al 6061 > Carbon steel**

**Step 3 — Compute wall thickness for each material (thin-wall formula):**

$$ t = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{\text{allow}}} $$

- **Carbon steel:** $t = (4 \times 300) / (2 \times 250) = 2.4\,\text{mm}$
- **Al 6061-T6:** $t = (4 \times 300) / (2 \times 250) = 2.4\,\text{mm}$ (using $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 250$ MPa)
- **GFRP:** $t = (4 \times 300) / (2 \times 250) = 2.4\,\text{mm}$ (assuming same allowable; if $\sigma_{\text{allow, GFRP}} = 200$ MPa, $t = 3.0$ mm)

**Step 4 — Compute mass per unit length for each:**

$$ m/L = \rho \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot t $$

- **Carbon steel:** $m/L = 7850 \times \pi \times 0.3 \times 0.0024 = 17.75\,\text{kg/m}$
- **Al 6061-T6:** $m/L = 2700 \times \pi \times 0.3 \times 0.0024 = 6.11\,\text{kg/m}$
- **GFRP:** $m/L = 1800 \times \pi \times 0.3 \times 0.0024 = 4.07\,\text{kg/m}$ (or using 3 mm: 5.09 kg/m)

**Step 5 — Final recommendation:**
- For a **portable pressure vessel**, **GFRP** is the best choice due to highest $\sigma_y/\rho$ ratio and lowest mass.
- For a **cost-driven application**, Al 6061-T6 offers a good balance.

> **(7 marks — index computation, thickness calculation, mass comparison, justified recommendation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up the index:** Students often write $E/\rho$ for a beam, but the correct index is $E^{1/2}/\rho$ for stiffness and $E^{1/3}/\rho$ for a panel. **Always derive the index from scratch** using the deflection formula for the *specific* boundary condition.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint:** In screening, never skip the property limits. A polymer with $E^{1/2}/\rho$ better than steel is useless if $\sigma_y$ is below the requirement.
> 3. **Unit errors:** $E$ must be in Pa (not GPa) when computing deflection. The numerical answer for $a$ depends on consistent units. **Always convert to SI base units before substituting.**
> 4. **Ignoring process compatibility:** A textbook answer that selects Titanium for a mass-critical part without confirming the manufacturing route is incomplete. **Always cross-check with Section 9 of the procedure.**
> 5. **Skipping the "separation of variables" step:** The Material Index must be a pure function of material properties — no geometry or load terms should remain. Examiners specifically check this in the derivation.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Material Selection Process**

**1. Core Definitions**
- ✅ Material Selection is a **multi-criteria, coupled** decision involving Function, Material, Geometry, and Process.
- ✅ **Material Index** is a pure material-property grouping that ranks candidates.
- ✅ **Ashby Methodology** has 4 stages: Translation → Screening → Ranking → Documentation.

**2. The Seven Standard Material Indices (memorize)**
- ✅ Tie (stiffness): $E/\rho$
- ✅ Beam (stiffness, square): $E^{1/2}/\rho$
- ✅ Beam (strength, square): $\sigma_y^{2/3}/\rho$
- ✅ Beam (stiffness, free height): $E^{1/3}/\rho$
- ✅ Panel (stiffness): $E^{1/3}/\rho$
- ✅ Column (buckling): $E^{1/2}/\rho$
- ✅ Spring (energy storage): $\sigma_y^{2}/E$

**3. Standard Equations to Master**
- ✅ $\delta_{\text{axial}} = FL/(AE)$
- ✅ $\delta_{\text{bend}} = C F L^3/(EI)$
- ✅ $I_{\text{rectangle}} = bd^3/12$
- ✅ $I_{\text{circle}} = \pi d^4/64$
- ✅ $P_{\text{cr}} = \pi^2 E I / L^2$
- ✅ $U = \sigma^2 V / (2E)$

**4. The Ashby Chart — Key Families and Their Locations**
- ✅ Metals: **top-right** of $E$–$\rho$ chart
- ✅ Polymers: **bottom-left**
- ✅ Ceramics: **top-left** (high $E$, moderate $\rho$)
- ✅ Composites: **far top-left** (best specific properties)

**5. Critical Problem-Solving Steps**
- ✅ Step 1: Write the **objective function** (mass, cost, volume).
- ✅ Step 2: Write the **constraint equation** (deflection, strength, buckling).
- ✅ Step 3: Solve the constraint for the **free geometric variable**.
- ✅ Step 4: Substitute back and **separate material terms**.
- ✅ Step 5: **Maximize** (or minimize) the material index.
- ✅ Step 6: **Verify** with manufacturing process availability.

**6. Real-World Material Selection Heuristics**
- ✅ Aerospace: Ti-alloys, CFRP — high specific strength
- ✅ Automotive engine: Cast iron, forged steel — high temperature strength
- ✅ Biomedical implants: Ti-6Al-4V — biocompatibility + fatigue
- ✅ Heat sinks: Al, Cu — high thermal conductivity
- ✅ Cost-driven products: Mild steel, PP, PE — easy manufacture

**7. KTU-Specific Traps**
- ✅ Examiners expect **derivation**, not just formula memorization.
- ✅ Always include **units** in the final answer.
- ✅ Always mention **at least two candidates** when comparing indices.
- ✅ Always show the **Ashby chart logic** for full marks on graphical questions.

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — Material Selection Process**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation Methods for Material Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluation Methods for Material Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Material Selection** is the systematic engineering process of identifying the most suitable material from a set of candidates that satisfies a design's functional, geometric, manufacturing, economic, and service-life requirements. **Evaluation Methods** are the formal, mathematically-rigorous procedures (quantitative indices, charts, expert systems, and decision algorithms) used to rank, screen, and select the optimal material for a given engineering application.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: PEMRT522 – Design of Manufacture), Module 1 establishes that material selection is the *pivotal step* linking design intent to manufacturing feasibility, and the evaluation method chosen must bridge **engineering performance**, **economic viability**, and **manufacturability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Evaluation methods transform vague qualitative preferences (e.g., "light but strong") into **numerical merit indices** that can be plotted, compared, and optimized on charts such as the celebrated **Ashby Material Property Charts**.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Talent Show Judging Panel"

Imagine a talent show where contestants (the **candidate materials**) are evaluated not by gut feeling, but by a structured panel of judges (the **evaluation method**).

- Each judge represents a **design requirement** (stiffness, weight, cost, corrosion resistance, machinability).
- Each judge scores the contestant on a **scale (1–10)**.
- The judges then **weight** their scores — stiffness might matter 40%, cost 30%, weight 30% (weighted sum).
- The contestant with the **highest total weighted score wins** (optimal material).

In engineering terms, the panel's scoring sheet is the **Performance Index**, the judge weights are **design priorities**, and the contestants are the **engineering materials** plotted on **Ashby charts** or fed into **AHP matrices**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No single material is universally "best." The "best" material is always **context-dependent** — it is the one that maximizes a specific **performance metric (M)** under a set of **constraints (g, h)** for a given **function (F)**, **geometry (G)**, and **objective (O)**. This **F-M-A-G-O framework** is foundational to all modern material selection methods.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally used in material evaluation:

| Metric | Symbol | Units (SI) | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ | $kg/m^3$ | Mass per unit volume |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | $Pa$ or $GPa$ | Stiffness (stress/strain) |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ | $MPa$ | Onset of plastic deformation |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{uts}$ | $MPa$ | Maximum tensile stress |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | $GPa$ | Resistance to shear |
| Fracture Toughness | $K_{IC}$ | $MPa \cdot m^{1/2}$ | Resistance to crack propagation |
| Thermal Conductivity | $\lambda$ | $W/(m \cdot K)$ | Heat conduction rate |
| Thermal Expansion Coeff. | $\alpha$ | $1/K$ | Dimensional change with temperature |
| Specific Heat | $C_p$ | $J/(kg \cdot K)$ | Heat storage per unit mass |
| Cost per Unit Mass | $C_m$ | $\$/kg$ | Raw material cost |
| Cost per Unit Volume | $C_v$ | $\$/m^3$ | Bulk raw material cost |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | — (dimensionless) | Lateral to axial strain ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **$1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa}$** — always normalize units before computing any performance index.

---

### 1.4 The Fourfold F-M-A-G-O Design Framework

Every material selection problem, irrespective of evaluation method, can be described using this canonical framework:

- **F — Function:** What does the component *do*? (e.g., support a load, store energy, conduct heat, insulate electrically)
- **M — Material Index:** The performance metric to be maximized or minimized (e.g., $E/\rho$, $\sigma_y / \rho$, $E^{1/3}/\rho$).
- **A — Attribute Limits:** Mandatory constraints (e.g., $T_{service} \leq 250°C$, $K_{IC} \geq 20 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}$).
- **G — Geometry:** Free design variables (length $L$, cross-section, etc.) that the designer can adjust.
- **O — Objective:** The optimization goal — minimize mass, minimize cost, maximize life, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Evaluation Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups evaluation methods into **five families**, each with a distinct mathematical foundation:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five canonical evaluation methods covered in **PEMRT522 Module 1** are:
> 1. **Performance Index (Ashby) Method** — uses log-log property charts
> 2. **Weighted Property Index (WPI) Method** — linear additive utility
> 3. **Digital Logic Method** — boolean screening
> 4. **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)** — pairwise comparison
> 5. **Cost-per-Unit-Property Method** — economic optimization

---

### 2.2 The Performance Index Method (Ashby Method) — Core Theory

**Developed by Prof. Michael F. Ashby (Cambridge University)**, this is the most widely-taught and rigorously mathematical evaluation method in modern design curricula. It transforms the selection problem into an optimization of a single composite quantity called the **Material Index (M)**.

#### 2.2.1 The Working Principle

The Ashby method follows a **4-step procedure**:

1. **Translate Design Requirements → Function & Constraints.** Express the design as: maximize/minimize a performance metric $P$, subject to constraints $g_i \leq 0$, with $k$ free geometric variables.
2. **Identify Free Variables.** Free variables are those that the designer can change to meet the constraints (e.g., thickness, length, cross-sectional area).
3. **Eliminate Free Variables.** Use constraint equations to express the objective (mass, volume, cost) solely in terms of **material properties** and the **fixed** functional requirements.
4. **Read the Material Index off the resulting equation.** The grouping of material properties is the **Material Index $M$**. A material that maximizes (or minimizes) $M$ is the optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Index"?** Because $M$ is dimensionless (or has well-defined units), it can be plotted directly on a log-log "Ashby chart" against any other material property, enabling rapid visual comparison of thousands of materials.

#### 2.2.2 General Formulation

For a general design, the objective $\Omega$ (e.g., mass $m$, cost $C$, stiffness $S$, energy capacity $W$) can be written as:

$$\Omega = f(\text{Function}, \text{Geometry}, \text{Material Properties})$$

The material index $M$ is the **grouping of material properties** that remains after free variables are eliminated via constraints. Mathematically:

$$\boxed{\text{Optimize } M = f_1(P_1) \cdot f_2(P_2) \cdot \ldots \cdot f_n(P_n)}$$

where $P_i$ are material properties ($E$, $\rho$, $\sigma_y$, $\lambda$, $K_{IC}$, etc.) and $f_i$ are exponents determined by the underlying physics.

---

### 2.3 The Weighted Property Index (WPI) Method

The WPI method is a **linear additive multi-attribute utility model** that assigns each material a single numerical "merit score" by weighting its normalized properties.

**Working Principle:**

For each candidate material $j$ and each property $i$:

1. Normalize the property to a dimensionless score: $X_{ij} = \dfrac{P_{ij}}{P_{i,\text{reference}}}$ (larger-is-better) or $X_{ij} = \dfrac{P_{i,\text{reference}}}{P_{ij}}$ (smaller-is-better).
2. Apply the designer-assigned weight $W_i$ (with $\sum W_i = 1$).
3. Sum the weighted scores.

The material with the **maximum weighted sum** is selected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation of WPI:** It is *additive* — assumes properties contribute independently. In reality, material properties are **strongly correlated** (e.g., high $E$ often correlates with high $\rho$ in metals), causing WPI to "double-count" performance. Ashby charts handle this correlation implicitly.

---

### 2.4 The Digital Logic Method (Boolean Screening)

The **Digital Logic Method** is a **two-stage** screening tool — *not* a ranking method. It performs **sieve-like elimination** based on pass/fail binary criteria.

**Stage 1 — Sieve (Filtering):**
A list of property requirements is converted into a **pass/fail test** for each material. Materials that *fail* any single criterion are eliminated.

**Stage 2 — Ranking with Weighted Sum or Merit Index:**
The surviving materials are then ranked using a numerical index (often the WPI).

**Key Feature:** Uses logical operators (AND, OR, NOT) in a **decision matrix** (rows = materials, columns = tests, entries = 0/1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Digital Logic Method is the **first step in a complete selection workflow** — it narrows ~100,000 materials down to a handful, *then* ranking methods (Ashby or WPI) take over.

---

### 2.5 The Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)

**AHP** is a **structured multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM)** technique developed by **Thomas L. Saaty (1980)**. It decomposes a complex selection problem into a hierarchy and uses **pairwise comparison matrices** to derive consistent weightings.

**AHP Hierarchy (3 Levels):**
- **Level 1 — Goal:** Select the best material.
- **Level 2 — Criteria:** Properties / attributes (e.g., strength, cost, weight).
- **Level 3 — Alternatives:** Candidate materials.

**Pairwise Comparison Scale (Saaty's 1–9 Scale):**

| Scale | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 1 | Equal importance |
| 3 | Moderate importance |
| 5 | Strong importance |
| 7 | Very strong importance |
| 9 | Extreme importance |
| 2, 4, 6, 8 | Intermediate values |

The **Consistency Ratio (CR)** must be $\leq 0.10$ for the comparisons to be valid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AHP matters in KTU 2024:** It is the only method that *explicitly* handles **qualitative criteria** (e.g., "aesthetic appeal," "recyclability," "perceived quality") alongside quantitative ones — essential for consumer product design.

---

### 2.6 Cost-per-Unit-Property Method

This method ranks materials by the **economic efficiency** of a key performance property. It is especially useful when the material's contribution to **value** is the deciding factor.

**Working Principle:** For each material, compute:

$$\text{Cost per Unit Property} = \frac{C_m \cdot \rho}{P_{\text{key}}} \quad \text{[currency per unit of property]}$$

The material with the **lowest cost per unit property** is optimal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat:** This method assumes *all other properties* are equivalent — it must be combined with a screening step (Digital Logic) to ensure non-key properties are acceptable.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam-ready cheat sheet** for the five evaluation methods. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute-value notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Method | Core Formula | Goal | Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ashby (Min Mass, Stiff Tie) | $M = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Maximize $M$ | Load $F$ fixed |
| Ashby (Min Mass, Light Beam) | $M = \dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ | Maximize $M$ | Deflection $\delta$ fixed |
| Ashby (Min Mass, Light Column) | $M = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ | Maximize $M$ | Buckling load $P_{cr}$ fixed |
| Ashby (Min Mass, Torsion Bar) | $M = \dfrac{G^{2/3}}{\rho}$ | Maximize $M$ | Twist angle $\phi$ fixed |
| Ashby (Min Mass, Pressure Vessel) | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | Maximize $M$ | Burst pressure $P$ fixed |
| Ashby (Min Cost, Light Beam) | $M = \dfrac{E^{1/3}}{C_v \cdot \rho}$ | Maximize $M$ | Deflection $\delta$ fixed |
| Ashby (Min Mass, Spring) | $M = \dfrac{G}{C \cdot \rho}$ | Maximize $M$ | Energy stored $W$ fixed |
| WPI | $\gamma = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot X_i$, $\sum W_i = 1$ | Maximize $\gamma$ | All properties normalized |
| Digital Logic | $\text{Result} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \text{Test}_i$ | Pass/Fail | Boolean AND/OR |
| AHP | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{CI}}{\text{RI}} \leq 0.10$ | Maximize score | Pairwise consistency |
| Cost-per-Property | $\xi = \dfrac{C_m \cdot \rho}{P_{key}}$ | Minimize $\xi$ | $P_{key}$ at design limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** "Why is the beam index $E^{1/3}/\rho$?" — because **bending stiffness** scales as $I \propto h^4$ (or $b \cdot h^3$), giving the cube-root dependence. This kind of derivation is **frequently asked** in 14-mark problems.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Method | Industry Use Case |
|---|---|
| Ashby Charts | Aerospace airframe material selection (CFRP vs. Al alloy vs. Ti) |
| WPI | Automotive body panel comparison (steel vs. aluminum vs. composites) |
| Digital Logic | Initial filter for >50,000 materials in CES EduPack / Granta databases |
| AHP | Consumer electronics casings (balance of weight, cost, finish, recyclability) |
| Cost-per-Property | Civil construction (selecting rebar by cost-per-yield-strength) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Index for a Lightweight Stiff Tie (Tension Member)

**Design Problem:** A tie rod must support a tensile load $F$ without yielding, with a prescribed stiffness $S$ (load per unit elongation). **Minimize the mass $m$**.

#### Step 1 — Express the Functional Requirements

The load-carrying constraint (no yielding):

$$F \leq \sigma_y \cdot A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{F}{\sigma_y}$$

The stiffness constraint:

$$S = \frac{E \cdot A}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{S \cdot L}{E}$$

#### Step 2 — Express the Mass

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$

#### Step 3 — Eliminate the Free Variable $A$ Using the Stiffness Constraint

Substituting $A = S \cdot L / E$ into the mass equation:

$$m = \rho \cdot \frac{S \cdot L}{E} \cdot L = \frac{\rho \cdot S \cdot L^2}{E}$$

#### Step 4 — Isolate the Material-Dependent Grouping

Since $S$ and $L$ are **fixed by the design specification**, the only way to minimize $m$ is to **minimize the ratio $\rho / E$**, or equivalently **maximize the inverse**:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(Stiffness-to-Weight Index)}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Materials high on the $E/\rho$ axis of an Ashby chart (CFRP, ceramics, beryllium) are optimal for lightweight stiff ties.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Index for a Lightweight Stiff Beam (Bending)

**Design Problem:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ and rectangular cross-section $b \times h$ must support an end load $F$ with deflection $\delta \leq \delta_{max}$. **Minimize the mass $m$**.

#### Step 1 — Stiffness Constraint

The tip deflection of a cantilever under end load:

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}, \quad I = \frac{b \cdot h^3}{12}$$

The stiffness $S = F / \delta$ becomes:

$$S = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot I}{L^3} = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot h^3}{12 \cdot L^3} = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^3}{4 \cdot L^3}$$

#### Step 2 — Express the Mass

$$m = \rho \cdot b \cdot h \cdot L$$

#### Step 3 — Eliminate Free Variables ($b$ and $h$)

From the stiffness equation:

$$b = \frac{4 \cdot S \cdot L^3}{E \cdot h^3}$$

Substituting into the mass equation:

$$m = \rho \cdot L \cdot h \cdot \frac{4 \cdot S \cdot L^3}{E \cdot h^3} = \frac{4 \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot L^4}{E \cdot h^2}$$

#### Step 4 — Optimize with Respect to $h$

For minimum $m$, set $\partial m / \partial h = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial m}{\partial h} = -\frac{8 \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot L^4}{E \cdot h^3} = 0$$

This is minimum at $h \to \infty$, but $h$ is bounded by other constraints (e.g., space). For a **square section** ($b = h$ — a common design choice that minimizes mass for a given stiffness), substitute $b = h$:

$$b^4 = \frac{4 \cdot S \cdot L^3}{E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b^2 = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot S \cdot L^3}{E}}$$

The mass becomes:

$$m = \rho \cdot b^2 \cdot L = \rho \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot S \cdot L^3}{E}}$$

$$\boxed{M = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho} \cdot \text{(function of } S, L \text{)}}$$

For the more general case of a beam with width $b$ as the free variable (height $h$ fixed):

$$\boxed{M = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho} \quad \text{(Stiff Beam Index)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The cube root emerges** because the cross-section's second moment of area $I$ involves $h^3$, but the mass involves the *linear* dimensions — the geometric mean gives the $1/3$ exponent.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Material Index for a Pressure Vessel (Thin-Walled Cylinder)

**Design Problem:** A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of radius $R$ and length $L$ must contain an internal pressure $P$. **Minimize the mass $m$**.

#### Step 1 — Hoop Stress Constraint

For a thin-walled cylinder, the hoop (circumferential) stress is:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot R}{t}$$

For no yielding:

$$\sigma_h \leq \sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{P \cdot R}{\sigma_y}$$

#### Step 2 — Mass Equation

For a thin cylindrical shell (neglecting end caps):

$$m = \rho \cdot (2 \pi R L) \cdot t = 2 \pi R L \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\sigma_y}$$

#### Step 3 — Simplify

$$m = \frac{2 \pi \cdot P \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot R^2}{\sigma_y}$$

#### Step 4 — Isolate the Material Index

Since $P$, $L$, and $R$ are fixed by design, the only material-controllable factor is the ratio $\rho / \sigma_y$. To minimize $m$, **minimize $\rho / \sigma_y$**, or equivalently:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \text{(Strength-to-Weight Index, Pressure Vessel)}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Material Index for an Elastic Spring (Energy Storage)

**Design Problem:** A helical compression spring of mean coil diameter $D$, wire diameter $d$, and $n$ active coils must store a strain energy $W$ without exceeding the allowable shear stress $\tau_{max}$. **Minimize the volume $V$**.

#### Step 1 — Energy Storage Equation

The strain energy stored in a helical spring under axial load $F$ and deflection $\delta$ is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} F \cdot \delta = \frac{F^2 \cdot L}{2 \cdot G \cdot A}, \quad L = \pi D n, \quad A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

#### Step 2 — Express $F$ in Terms of $\tau_{max}$ and Geometry

The maximum shear stress in a helical spring:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8 \cdot F \cdot D}{\pi \cdot d^3} \cdot K_w \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot \pi \cdot d^3}{8 \cdot K_w \cdot D}$$

where $K_w$ is the Wahl correction factor.

#### Step 3 — Express $W$ in Terms of $d$, $D$, $n$

Substituting $F$ and $L$ into the energy equation:

$$W = \frac{1}{2 G A} \cdot F^2 \cdot L = \frac{1}{2 G \cdot (\pi d^2/4)} \cdot \left( \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot \pi \cdot d^3}{8 K_w D} \right)^2 \cdot \pi D n$$

After algebraic simplification (grouping powers of $d$):

$$W = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot \tau_{max}^2 \cdot n \cdot d^4}{16 \cdot G \cdot K_w^2 \cdot D}$$

Solving for $n$:

$$n = \frac{16 \cdot W \cdot G \cdot K_w^2 \cdot D}{\pi^2 \cdot \tau_{max}^2 \cdot d^4}$$

#### Step 4 — Express the Volume

$$V = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \pi D n = \frac{\pi^2 d^2 D n}{4}$$

Substituting $n$:

$$V = \frac{\pi^2 d^2 D}{4} \cdot \frac{16 W G K_w^2 D}{\pi^2 \tau_{max}^2 d^4} = \frac{4 W G K_w^2 D^2}{\tau_{max}^2 d^2}$$

#### Step 5 — Optimize $d$ (Free Variable)

For minimum $V$, set $\partial V / \partial d = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial d} = -\frac{8 W G K_w^2 D^2}{\tau_{max}^2 d^3} = 0$$

This is minimized as $d \to \infty$, so the optimum is at the **maximum allowable $d$** (often set by the **spring index $C = D/d$**, typically $C \in [4, 12]$).

#### Step 6 — Final Material Index

The volume scales as:

$$V \propto \frac{G \cdot W}{\tau_{max}^2}$$

To minimize $V$, **maximize**:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{\tau_{max}^2}{G} \quad \text{(Spring Stiffness/Specific-Energy Index)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU students:** When designing springs for *minimum mass* (not volume), the index is **$\tau_{max}^2 / (\rho \cdot G)$**. Always verify whether the question asks for *mass* or *volume* optimization.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Weighted Property Index (WPI) Numerical Worked Example

**Design Problem:** Select the best material for a structural bracket among Steel, Aluminum, and CFRP, given the following data and weights.

| Property | Steel | Aluminum | CFRP | Weight $W_i$ | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | 250 | 180 | 600 | 0.35 | Larger is better |
| Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | 7850 | 2700 | 1600 | 0.25 | Smaller is better |
| Cost (₹/kg) | 60 | 180 | 4500 | 0.40 | Smaller is better |

#### Step 1 — Normalize Each Property

Using **larger-is-better**:
$$X_{ij} = \frac{P_{ij}}{P_{i,\max}}$$

Using **smaller-is-better**:
$$X_{ij} = \frac{P_{i,\min}}{P_{ij}}$$

**Normalization (using each property's best value as reference):**

For Strength (larger better), reference = 600 MPa (CFRP):
- Steel: $X_{1,S} = 250/600 = 0.417$
- Aluminum: $X_{1,A} = 180/600 = 0.300$
- CFRP: $X_{1,C} = 600/600 = 1.000$

For Density (smaller better), reference = 1600 kg/m³ (CFRP):
- Steel: $X_{2,S} = 1600/7850 = 0.204$
- Aluminum: $X_{2,A} = 1600/2700 = 0.593$
- CFRP: $X_{2,C} = 1600/1600 = 1.000$

For Cost (smaller better), reference = 60 ₹/kg (Steel):
- Steel: $X_{3,S} = 60/60 = 1.000$
- Aluminum: $X_{3,A} = 60/180 = 0.333$
- CFRP: $X_{3,C} = 60/4500 = 0.013$

#### Step 2 — Compute Weighted Sum

$$\gamma_j = \sum_{i=1}^{3} W_i \cdot X_{ij}$$

**Steel:**
$$\gamma_{Steel} = (0.35)(0.417) + (0.25)(0.204) + (0.40)(1.000)$$
$$\gamma_{Steel} = 0.146 + 0.051 + 0.400 = 0.597$$

**Aluminum:**
$$\gamma_{Al} = (0.35)(0.300) + (0.25)(0.593) + (0.40)(0.333)$$
$$\gamma_{Al} = 0.105 + 0.148 + 0.133 = 0.386$$

**CFRP:**
$$\gamma_{CFRP} = (0.35)(1.000) + (0.25)(1.000) + (0.40)(0.013)$$
$$\gamma_{CFRP} = 0.350 + 0.250 + 0.005 = 0.605$$

#### Step 3 — Rank and Select

| Material | WPI Score $\gamma$ | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| CFRP | 0.605 | **1st (Selected)** |
| Steel | 0.597 | 2nd |
| Aluminum | 0.386 | 3rd |

**Conclusion:** **CFRP** is selected by the WPI method — its superior strength and low density outweigh its high cost under the assigned weights.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity Alert:** If cost weight $W_3$ increased to 0.70, Steel would win. WPI is **highly sensitive to weight choice** — always perform a sensitivity analysis before final selection.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Feature | Ashby | WPI | Digital Logic | AHP | Cost-per-Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quantitative? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Hybrid | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Handles qualitative criteria? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Best for screening? | ⚠️ Moderate | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⚠️ Moderate |
| Best for ranking? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Requires material chart? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Computational effort | Low | Low | Very Low | High | Low |
| Output | Optimal subset + index | Score | Pass/Fail | Weighted score | Cost ratio |
| KTU frequency | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Ashby Method Workflow (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Design Requirement"] --> B["Translate to F-M-A-G-O"]
    B --> C["Identify Free Variables"]
    C --> D["Use Constraints to Eliminate Free Variables"]
    D --> E["Derive Material Index M"]
    E --> F["Plot on Ashby Property Chart"]
    F --> G["Apply Attribute Filters"]
    G --> H{"Satisfies All Constraints?"}
    H -- No --> I["Eliminate Material"]
    H -- Yes --> J["Rank by M Value"]
    J --> K["Select Optimal Material"]
    K --> L["Verify with Prototype / FEA"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** Each step transforms qualitative requirements into quantitative indices. The Ashby Chart (step F) is the **central decision tool** — it is a 2D log-log plot of one material property against another, with bubbles representing material families (metals, polymers, ceramics, composites).

---

### 4.2 The WPI Method — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Property 1 Value"] --> N1["Normalize"]
    P2["Property 2 Value"] --> N2["Normalize"]
    P3["Property n Value"] --> Nn["Normalize"]
    W1["Weight W1"] --> M1["Multiply and Sum"]
    W2["Weight W2"] --> M1
    Wn["Weight Wn"] --> M1
    N1 --> M1
    N2 --> M1
    Nn --> M1
    M1 --> S["Final WPI Score gamma"]
    S --> R["Rank Materials"]
    R --> OUT["Best Material Selected"]
```

---

### 4.3 The AHP Hierarchy Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G["Level 1 GOAL: Select Best Material"] --> C1["Level 2 Criterion 1 Strength"]
    G --> C2["Level 2 Criterion 2 Weight"]
    G --> C3["Level 2 Criterion 3 Cost"]
    G --> C4["Level 2 Criterion 4 Corrosion Resistance"]
    C1 --> A1["Level 3 Alternative Steel"]
    C1 --> A2["Level 3 Alternative Aluminum"]
    C1 --> A3["Level 3 Alternative CFRP"]
    C2 --> A1
    C2 --> A2
    C2 --> A3
    C3 --> A1
    C3 --> A2
    C3 --> A3
    C4 --> A1
    C4 --> A2
    C4 --> A3
```

---

### 4.4 The Digital Logic Decision Matrix (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Material A"] --> T1["Test 1: T_service 200C"]
    S1 --> T2["Test 2: K_IC 15 MPa.sqrt m"]
    S1 --> T3["Test 3: Density 5000 kg/m3"]
    S2["Material B"] --> T1
    S2 --> T2
    S2 --> T3
    S3["Material C"] --> T1
    S3 --> T2
    S3 --> T3
    T1 --> AND1["AND Gate"]
    T2 --> AND1
    T3 --> AND1
    AND1 --> Result1["Pass = 1, Fail = 0"]
```

**Interpretation of the AND gate:** A material passes the screen **only if it passes *every* test**. Any single failure eliminates the material from the candidate list. The output of each material is a binary string (e.g., `101` = passes Test 1, fails Test 2, passes Test 3 → **Eliminated**).

---

### 4.5 The F-M-A-G-O Workflow (Input–Process–Output Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        F["F Function"]
        O["O Objective"]
    end
    subgraph CONSTRAINTS
        A["A Attribute Limits"]
        G["G Geometry Free Variables"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        P1["Step 1 Express Objective Function"]
        P2["Step 2 Eliminate Free Variables using Constraints"]
        P3["Step 3 Derive Material Index M"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        M["M Material Index"]
        SEL["Selected Material Subset"]
    end
    F --> P1
    O --> P1
    A --> P2
    G --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> M
    M --> SEL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualization Note:** This is the canonical "input-process-output" architecture of every modern material selection tool — from Ashby's CES EduPack to commercial software like **Granta MI** and **IHS Markit**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the "Material Index" as used in the Ashby method of material selection. Derive the material index for a light, stiff beam.**

**Model Answer:**

A **material index** is a grouping of material properties — derived by eliminating all free geometric variables from the objective function using design constraints — that quantifies the *intrinsic merit* of a material for a specific application. A material with the highest (or lowest, depending on the objective) value of the material index is the optimal choice.

For a light, stiff beam (length $L$, rectangular cross-section $b \times h$, fixed stiffness $S$):

Stiffness: $S = E b h^3 / (4 L^3)$

Mass: $m = \rho b h L$

Eliminating $b$ and optimizing with respect to $h$ gives:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}}$$

To minimize mass, **maximize $E^{1/3}/\rho$**. Materials high on the $E^{1/3}/\rho$ axis of an Ashby chart (CFRP, wood, advanced composites) are optimal.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**Explain the Weighted Property Index (WPI) method. State its main limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Weighted Property Index (WPI)** method ranks materials by computing a single merit score $\gamma$ for each candidate:

$$\gamma = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot X_i$$

where $W_i$ is the weight (importance) of property $i$ (with $\sum W_i = 1$), and $X_i$ is the normalized value of property $i$ (e.g., $X_i = P_i / P_{i,\text{ref}}$ for larger-is-better properties, or $X_i = P_{i,\text{ref}} / P_i$ for smaller-is-better).

The material with the **maximum $\gamma$** is selected.

**Main Limitation:**
WPI assumes that material properties are **independent** (additive utility). In reality, properties are strongly correlated (e.g., high $E$ correlates with high $\rho$ in metals). This causes WPI to **double-count performance** and produce **counterintuitive rankings** that violate physical intuition (e.g., selecting a "best overall" material that is simultaneously heavy, weak, and expensive).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the material index for selecting a material for a **lightweight, stiff column** that must support a buckling load $P_{cr}$ without buckling. State the design constraints, free variables, and final index.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using an Ashby chart for stiffness vs. density, identify the **three best material families** for a stiff column and justify your answer in terms of the derived material index.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — State the Function & Objective**

**Function:** Axial column support (resist buckling).
**Objective:** Minimize the mass $m$ of the column.
**Free Variable:** Cross-sectional dimensions (e.g., radius $r$ for a circular column, or width $b$ and thickness $t$ for a hollow column).
**Fixed Parameter:** Length $L$, buckling load $P_{cr}$.

**Step 2 — Buckling Constraint (Euler's Formula)**

For a slender column with pinned ends:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L^2}$$

For a solid circular cross-section of radius $r$:

$$I = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4}{4}$$

Substituting:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot \pi \cdot r^4}{4 \cdot L^2} = \frac{\pi^3 \cdot E \cdot r^4}{4 \cdot L^2}$$

Solving for $r^4$:

$$r^4 = \frac{4 \cdot P_{cr} \cdot L^2}{\pi^3 \cdot E}$$

Therefore:

$$r^2 = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot P_{cr} \cdot L^2}{\pi^3 \cdot E}} = \left( \frac{4 \cdot P_{cr} \cdot L^2}{\pi^3 \cdot E} \right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Equation**

$$m = \rho \cdot \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot L = \rho \cdot \pi \cdot L \cdot \left( \frac{4 \cdot P_{cr} \cdot L^2}{\pi^3 \cdot E} \right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the Mass Expression**

$$m = \rho \cdot \pi \cdot L \cdot \frac{(4 \cdot P_{cr})^{1/2} \cdot L}{\pi^{3/2} \cdot E^{1/2}} = \frac{\rho \cdot L^2 \cdot (4 \cdot P_{cr})^{1/2} \cdot \pi^{1-3/2}}{E^{1/2}}$$

$$m = \frac{\rho \cdot L^2 \cdot (4 \cdot P_{cr})^{1/2}}{\pi^{1/2} \cdot E^{1/2}} = \frac{2 \cdot \rho \cdot L^2 \cdot \sqrt{P_{cr}}}{\sqrt{\pi} \cdot E^{1/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the Material Index**

Grouping material-dependent terms:

$$m = \frac{2 \cdot L^2 \cdot \sqrt{P_{cr}}}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{\rho}{E^{1/2}}$$

Since $L$ and $P_{cr}$ are fixed, the only material-controllable factor is $\rho / E^{1/2}$. To minimize $m$, **minimize $\rho / E^{1/2}$**, or equivalently **maximize**:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho} \quad \text{(Stiff-Column Material Index)}}$$

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Material Index Recap**

For a stiff column: $M = E^{1/2} / \rho$

Equivalently, we seek materials with **high $E$ and low $\rho$**.

**Step 2 — Interpretation on the Ashby $E$–$\rho$ Chart**

On a log-log plot of Young's modulus $E$ (GPa, y-axis) vs. density $\rho$ (kg/m³, x-axis), a guide line of slope **1/2** (in log-log space) represents a **constant value of $M = E^{1/2}/\rho$**. The "best" materials lie on **lines of progressively higher $M$** (toward the upper-left of the chart).

**Step 3 — Top Three Material Families**

| Rank | Material Family | Typical $E$ (GPa) | Typical $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $M$ (GPa$^{1/2}$/kg·m$^{-3}$) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carbon Fiber Composites (CFRP)** | 70–200 | 1500–1800 | 215–340 | High $E$, very low $\rho$; aerospace standard |
| 2 | **Engineering Ceramics (SiC, Al₂O₃)** | 300–450 | 2800–3200 | 170–190 | Highest absolute $E$, moderate $\rho$ |
| 3 | **High-Strength Alloys (Ti, Be-Al)** | 100–290 | 1850–4500 | 145–255 | Excellent strength-to-weight ratio, aerospace applications |

**Step 4 — Justification**

On the $E$–$\rho$ Ashby chart, the slope-1/2 lines pass through:
- **CFRP region:** Located in the upper-left of the chart, indicating high $M$.
- **Ceramics region:** Highest $E$ values, but denser; still high $M$.
- **Ti alloys:** Aerospace-standard, with $M$ values comparable to ceramics.
- **Engineering polymers (e.g., PA66):** Lower $M$ due to low $E$.
- **Steels:** High $E$ but high $\rho$; $M$ is moderate.

**Conclusion:** **CFRP** offers the highest $M$ for a stiff column, followed by **engineering ceramics** and **titanium alloys**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Main Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Digital Logic Method** of material selection with a suitable example. How does it complement the **Ashby method** in a complete selection workflow?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Three candidate materials — **Al 6061-T6, AISI 1045 Steel, and CFRP T700** — are being considered for an automotive control arm. Using the **Weighted Property Index (WPI) method**, rank them given the data below. Justify the weight selection.

| Property | Al 6061-T6 | AISI 1045 Steel | CFRP T700 | Weight $W_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | 276 | 530 | 1500 | 0.30 |
| Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | 2700 | 7850 | 1600 | 0.40 |
| Cost (₹/kg) | 200 | 80 | 6000 | 0.30 |

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**The Digital Logic Method (DLM) — Explanation**

The **Digital Logic Method** is a two-stage material selection technique based on **Boolean (pass/fail) screening** followed by numerical ranking. It is *not* a ranking method itself — it is a **filter** that narrows down an extensive candidate list to a manageable subset.

**Stage 1 — Sieve (Pass/Fail Tests)**

Each design requirement (e.g., operating temperature, corrosion resistance, density limit) is converted into a **binary test**. For each material $j$ and each test $i$, the result is 1 (pass) or 0 (fail). A material **survives** the sieve if and only if it passes *all* tests:

$$\text{Status}(j) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \text{Test}_{ij}$$

where $\bigwedge$ denotes the Boolean **AND** operator. A single failure (0) eliminates the material.

**Example:**

Consider selecting a material for a marine propeller shaft. The tests might be:

| Test | Criterion | Steel | Bronze | Cast Iron |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Yield Strength $\sigma_y \geq 200$ MPa | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| T2 | Density $\rho \leq 9000$ kg/m³ | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| T3 | Corrosion resistance in saltwater | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| T4 | Cost $\leq$ ₹500/kg | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **Result** | — | **0 (Eliminated)** | **1 (Survives)** | **0 (Eliminated)** |

Only **Bronze** survives — proceeding to the ranking stage.

**Stage 2 — Ranking of Survivors**

The surviving materials are then ranked using a numerical method (WPI, Ashby, or AHP).

**How DLM Complements the Ashby Method**

| Step | Role of DLM | Role of Ashby Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Define requirements | Convert to pass/fail tests | Translate to F-M-A-G-O |
| 2. Filter candidates | Eliminate materials failing constraints | Apply attribute limits on chart |
| 3. Rank survivors | Not its function | Compute material indices, rank |
| 4. Final selection | Narrows list to ~5–10 candidates | Selects the optimal 1–2 candidates |

**In summary:** DLM is a **coarse filter**; Ashby is a **fine selector**. They are typically used **sequentially** in a complete selection workflow.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Justify the Weight Selection**

For an automotive control arm:
- **Density (0.40):** Critical because the control arm is an **unsprung mass** in the suspension. Reducing it directly improves ride quality, handling, and fuel economy.
- **Yield Strength (0.30):** Important for fatigue resistance and crash safety, but the part is not the most highly stressed.
- **Cost (0.30):** Automotive industry is **cost-sensitive**; even an excellent material is rejected if too expensive.

**Verification:** $\sum W_i = 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.30 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2 — Normalize Each Property**

**Yield Strength (larger is better), reference = 1500 MPa (CFRP):**

$$X_{1,Al} = 276/1500 = 0.184, \quad X_{1,Steel} = 530/1500 = 0.353, \quad X_{1,CFRP} = 1500/1500 = 1.000$$

**Density (smaller is better), reference = 1600 kg/m³ (CFRP):**

$$X_{2,Al} = 1600/2700 = 0.593, \quad X_{2,Steel} = 1600/7850 = 0.204, \quad X_{2,CFRP} = 1600/1600 = 1.000$$

**Cost (smaller is better), reference = 80 ₹/kg (Steel):**

$$X_{3,Al} = 80/200 = 0.400, \quad X_{3,Steel} = 80/80 = 1.000, \quad X_{3,CFRP} = 80/6000 = 0.013$$

**Step 3 — Compute Weighted Property Index**

**Al 6061-T6:**
$$\gamma_{Al} = (0.30)(0.184) + (0.40)(0.593) + (0.30)(0.400)$$
$$\gamma_{Al} = 0.0552 + 0.2372 + 0.1200 = 0.4124$$

**AISI 1045 Steel:**
$$\gamma_{Steel} = (0.30)(0.353) + (0.40)(0.204) + (0.30)(1.000)$$
$$\gamma_{Steel} = 0.1059 + 0.0816 + 0.3000 = 0.4875$$

**CFRP T700:**
$$\gamma_{CFRP} = (0.30)(1.000) + (0.40)(1.000) + (0.30)(0.013)$$
$$\gamma_{CFRP} = 0.3000 + 0.4000 + 0.0039 = 0.7039$$

**Step 4 — Rank the Materials**

| Rank | Material | WPI Score $\gamma$ |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **CFRP T700** | **0.7039** |
| 2 | AISI 1045 Steel | 0.4875 |
| 3 | Al 6061-T6 | 0.4124 |

**Step 5 — Select and Validate**

**CFRP T700** is selected. However, in a real automotive context, the *very high cost* (₹6000/kg vs. ₹80/kg for steel) and manufacturing complexity (layup, autoclave cure) may make CFRP uneconomical at scale. A **sensitivity analysis** with cost weight $W_3 = 0.60$ would likely flip the ranking to **Steel**, reflecting industry reality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — WPI Method:**
> 1. **Unit confusion (1 mark lost):** Always normalize to **dimensionless ratios**. Do not use raw MPa or kg/m³ in the WPI formula.
> 2. **Direction error (1 mark lost):** Confusing larger-is-better vs. smaller-is-better *inverts the normalization*. For density, you must use $X_i = P_{i,\min}/P_{ij}$ — *not* $P_{ij}/P_{i,\min}$.
> 3. **Weight sum verification (0.5 marks):** Always state $\sum W_i = 1$ explicitly. If weights are given as percentages, convert them to decimals first.
> 4. **No justification (1 mark lost):** State *why* each weight was chosen — KTU examiners specifically mark the **reasoning behind weight selection** (design context, industry standards, cost-sensitivity).
> 5. **Single-method bias (1 mark lost):** Always recommend a **sensitivity analysis** or **cross-validation** with another method (e.g., Ashby). WPI alone is *not sufficient* for a final design decision.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1: Evaluation Methods for Material Selection**

- **F-M-A-G-O Framework:** Every selection problem maps to Function, Material Index, Attribute limits, Geometry, Objective. **State all 5 explicitly in derivations.**
- **Ashby Method Core:** Derive material indices by **eliminating free variables** using constraints; the resulting property grouping is the index. Always specify **maximize or minimize**.
- **Index Exponents (most-tested):**
  - Tension tie / pressure vessel → $\sigma_y/\rho$
  - Stiff tie / shaft → $E/\rho$ or $G/\rho$
  - Stiff beam (free h, fixed b) → $E^{1/3}/\rho$
  - Stiff column (buckling) → $E^{1/2}/\rho$
  - Torsion bar → $G^{2/3}/\rho$
  - Spring (energy storage) → $\tau_{max}^2/G$ (volume) or $\tau_{max}^2/(\rho G)$ (mass)
- **WPI Formula:** $\gamma = \sum W_i X_i$; weights must sum to 1; **normalize all properties**; direction of "better" must be correct.
- **Digital Logic Method:** Boolean AND/OR screening; eliminates ~95% of candidates; must be paired with a ranking method.
- **AHP Consistency:** $\text{CR} = \text{CI}/\text{RI} \leq 0.10$ for valid pairwise comparisons.
- **Cost-per-Property:** Use only after screening — assumes all other properties are equivalent.
- **Ashby Chart Reading:** Log-log plot; guide lines have slope = exponent in index (e.g., $E^{1/3}/\rho$ has slope 1/3 on $E$ vs. $\rho$).
- **Common Errors to Avoid:**
  - Forgetting to square root / cube root the property in the index.
  - Confusing *stiffness* ($E$) with *strength* ($\sigma_y$).
  - Using raw units in WPI (forgetting normalization).
  - Selecting a material by raw property values instead of an index.
- **Top 3 KTU-Favorite Questions:**
  1. Derive the material index for a [lightweight component] for [given objective].
  2. Compare WPI, Digital Logic, and AHP — when is each appropriate?
  3. Given a property chart, identify the best material family and justify using the index.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "FAST-D-C":**
> - **F** – Function first
> - **A** – Attribute limits next
> - **S** – Specify free variables
> - **T** – Translate to objective
> - **D** – Derive index (Ashby) or digitize (Logic)
> - **C** – Compare & choose (or apply Cost method)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Design Principles for Manufacturability: Strength and Mechanical Factors, Mechanisms Selection, Evaluation Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Design Principles for Manufacturability: Strength and Mechanical Factors, Mechanisms Selection, Evaluation Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Design for Manufacturability (DFM)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)** is a systematic engineering approach that integrates manufacturing process considerations, material behaviour, mechanical performance, and assembly logic into the early stages of product design, with the explicit objective of producing components that satisfy functional requirements **at minimum total cost, time, and complexity**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** perspective, DFM is not an afterthought but a **concurrent engineering philosophy** — the design engineer, manufacturing engineer, and mechanism designer must think together from the conceptual sketch itself.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Tailoring a Shirt**
> Think of designing a machine component like **tailoring a custom shirt**. A tailor who plans the fabric, stitch count, button type, seam allowance, and folding strategy *before cutting* produces a durable, neat, low-cost shirt. If fabric is cut first and *then* stitching is forced onto it, you get wrinkles, weak seams, and high waste.
> In the same way, a part designed **knowing** that it will be CNC machined, cast, or injection-moulded will have:
> - Generous corner radii (not sharp 90° corners)
> - Uniform wall thickness (no thick-thin junctions)
> - Standard fasteners (not exotic sizes)
> - Draft angles for moulding
> - Accessible machining features

This is the **spirit of DFM** — design *with* the factory in mind.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of This Module

| Pillar | Meaning | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Strength & Mechanical Factors** | Will the part carry load safely? | Avoid failure → **Safety** |
| **Mechanism Selection** | Is the right motion-converter chosen? | Convert input motion efficiently → **Function** |
| **Evaluation Method** | How do we measure "good design"? | Quantify manufacturability → **Optimisation** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT522 – Module 1)**
> *"General design principles for manufacturability — strength and mechanical factors, mechanism selection, and evaluation method"* — these three threads must be interlinked. A strong part with the wrong mechanism is a failure; a perfect mechanism with poor strength is a disaster.

---

## 1.3 Why Strength and Mechanical Factors Matter

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)**
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS), $\sigma_u$** = Maximum stress a material can withstand (units: **MPa** or **N/mm²**)
> - **Yield Strength, $\sigma_y$** = Stress at which permanent deformation begins
> - **Fatigue Limit, $\sigma_e$** = Stress below which a material survives **$10^7$** cycles
> - **Young's Modulus, $E$** = Stiffness of the material (units: **GPa**)
> - **Factor of Safety, FOS (or N)** = Dimensionless ratio (typically **1.5 to 4** for static, **2 to 6** for dynamic)
> - **Poisson's Ratio, $\nu$** = Lateral strain / Axial strain (for steel ≈ **0.3**)

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — DFM Philosophy

```
+---------------------------------------------+
|        FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENT               |
|  (Load, Speed, Motion, Environment)         |
+--------------------+------------------------+
                     |
                     v
+---------------------------------------------+
|     DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURABILITY (DFM)      |
+---------------------------------------------+
|  (1) STRENGTH & MECHANICAL FACTORS          |
|  (2) MECHANISM SELECTION                    |
|  (3) EVALUATION METHOD                      |
+---------------------------------------------+
                     |
                     v
+---------------------------------------------+
|    OPTIMAL PART:  Low Cost + Safe + Easy    |
+---------------------------------------------+
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1: Strength and Mechanical Factors in Design

The strength of a component is governed by **four interacting parameters**:

### 2.1.1 Static Strength Analysis

The designer must ensure that under the **maximum expected load**, the induced stress does not exceed the **allowable stress** of the chosen material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca Criterion)**
> Used for ductile materials under combined loading. Failure is assumed when maximum shear stress in the complex state reaches the shear stress at yielding in a simple tension test.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic and Fatigue Considerations

When a part is subjected to **fluctuating or cyclic loads**, the design must account for **fatigue failure**, which occurs at stresses **below the yield strength**.

**Modified Goodman Equation** — the most common KTU-tested relation for fluctuating stress:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right) \right]$$

Where:
- $\sigma_a$ = Allowable alternating stress amplitude
- $\sigma_e$ = Endurance limit of the material
- $\sigma_m$ = Mean stress of the cycle
- $\sigma_u$ = Ultimate tensile strength

### 2.1.3 Stress Concentration

Whenever a part contains a **hole, notch, fillet, or keyway**, the local stress is amplified by a factor called the **Stress Concentration Factor, $K_t$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why corners must be rounded (DFM wisdom)**
> A sharp internal corner is a **stress raiser** and is also a **manufacturing nightmare** — it requires extra machining passes, special tooling, and creates crack-initiation sites. A generous fillet (radius ≥ **0.5 × wall thickness**) reduces both $K_t$ and tooling cost.

### 2.1.4 The Factor of Safety — The Heart of Mechanical Design

The **Factor of Safety (FOS)** is the designer's primary tool to absorb **uncertainty**.

$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{applied}}$$

For **ductile materials under static load**, FOS of **1.5 – 2.5** is common. For **brittle materials** or **dynamic/impact loading**, FOS of **3 – 6** is recommended.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **complete formula bank** you need for any numerical problem on this topic in the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Direct / Axial stress | **MPa** |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | Torsional shear stress (circular shaft) | **MPa** |
| 3 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress (flexure formula) | **MPa** |
| 4 | $\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nominal}$ | Stress at a discontinuity (notch, hole) | **MPa** |
| 5 | $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{applied}}$ | Factor of safety (static) | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right]$ | Modified Goodman for fluctuating load | **MPa** |
| 7 | $M = \sigma_y \cdot Z$ | Design moment = yield × section modulus | **N·mm** |
| 8 | $\delta_{shaft} = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | Angular twist of shaft under torsion | **radians** |
| 9 | $P_{Hertz} = \dfrac{2F}{\pi \cdot b \cdot d}$ | Hertzian contact pressure (gear/bearing) | **MPa** |
| 10 | $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **Kutzbach criterion** for planar mechanism | **dimensionless** |
| 11 | $DFM_{index} = \dfrac{1}{C_{complexity}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{score}}{N_{parts}}$ | Manufacturability rating | **score** |
| 12 | $C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{process} + C_{assembly} + C_{tooling}$ | Total manufacturing cost | **₹ or $** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> - Equation (10) — the **Kutzbach criterion** — is the gateway to validating a mechanism's mobility. If DOF is zero, the linkage is a *structure*, not a *mechanism*.
> - Equation (12) is the **bedrock of cost-driven DFM**: every design decision must be evaluated against its impact on these four cost heads.

---

## 2.3 Pillar 2: Mechanism Selection

A **mechanism** is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints (kinematic pairs) whose relative motion is **constrained and deterministic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kutzbach's Criterion (Planar Mechanisms)**
> $$DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
> where $n$ = number of links, $j_1$ = lower pairs (1 DOF), $j_2$ = higher pairs (2 DOF).
> A well-designed mechanism must have **DOF ≥ 1** for motion. DOF = 0 means a structure (no motion possible).

### 2.3.1 Classification of Common Mechanisms Used in Manufacture

| Mechanism Type | Function | Typical Manufacturing Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Four-bar linkage** | Convert rotation to oscillation | Press tools, shaper machine |
| **Slider-crank** | Convert rotation to reciprocation | IC engine piston, sewing machine |
| **Cam-follower** | Programmed motion (rise-dwell-return) | Automatic lathe feed, printing press |
| **Gear train** | Speed/torque conversion | Gearbox, milling head |
| **Belt/Chain drive** | Power transmission over distance | Spindle drives, conveyors |
| **Screw-nut** | Convert rotation to linear motion | Lead screw of lathe, jack |
| **Ratchet-pawl** | Intermittent motion / one-way drive | Indexing table, jack |

### 2.3.2 The Six Selection Criteria (KTU-Relevant)

1. **Motion requirement** — Rotation ↔ Translation ↔ Oscillation?
2. **Speed ratio** — Constant or variable?
3. **Force / Torque transmission capacity**
4. **Compactness & weight**
5. **Manufacturing feasibility** of links and joints
6. **Cost & standardisation availability**

---

## 2.4 Pillar 3: Evaluation Method

The **Evaluation Method** quantifies the "manufacturability" of a proposed design using scoring systems.

### 2.4.1 The Weighted Scoring Model (Most common in KTU)

Each design candidate is rated against $k$ evaluation criteria. Each criterion $i$ carries a **weight $w_i$** reflecting its importance, and the candidate is scored on a scale (e.g., **1 to 5**).

$$E_{design} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot s_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The candidate with the **highest $E_{design}$** is selected. This is a classic **multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM)** technique, directly aligned with KTU's Outcome-Based Education (CO) — students must demonstrate *design evaluation competency*.

### 2.4.2 DFM Index

A more design-specific metric, the **DFM Index** rewards simplicity:

$$DFM_{index} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{(manufacturing operations avoided)}}{\text{Total operations in baseline design}}$$

A DFM Index of **0.7 or higher** is considered excellent practice in industry.

---

## 2.5 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why | How |
|---|---|---|
| Apply FOS | Real loads are unpredictable; materials have defects | $FOS = \sigma_y / \sigma_{applied}$ with material-specific multiplier |
| Use rounded corners | Avoid $K_t > 3$ and enable smooth tool path | Fillet radius $r \geq 0.5 \cdot t$ |
| Check DOF | Ensure mechanism actually moves as intended | Kutzbach criterion |
| Evaluate with weights | One number cannot capture all design virtues | Weighted sum of multiple criteria scores |
| Apply DFM Index | Reward elimination of steps | Ratio of eliminated/added operations |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) Theory in Combined Loading

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we derive it:** KTU frequently tests this derivation (or its von-Mises counterpart) at the **Apply / Analyse level of Bloom's Taxonomy**. The full algebraic chain is shown below — do **not** skip steps.

### Given
A ductile shaft is subjected to:
- A **bending moment** $M$ causing normal stress $\sigma_x$
- A **torsional moment** $T$ causing shear stress $\tau_{xy}$

The principal stresses at a surface point are obtained from Mohr's circle:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left( \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

The **third principal stress** $\sigma_3 = 0$ (plane stress).

### Step 1 — Apply Tresca Failure Criterion
For ductile failure, the maximum shear stress in the combined state must not exceed the shear stress at yielding in a simple tension test:

$$\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{yield}$$

In terms of principal stresses:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq \sigma_y$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Principal Stresses
Using $\sigma_3 = 0$ and $\sigma_1$ as the larger root:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left( \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Thus the **Tresca condition** becomes:

$$\frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left( \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \leq \sigma_y$$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Stresses from a Solid Circular Shaft
For a shaft of diameter $d$:
- Bending stress: $\sigma_x = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$
- Torsional shear stress: $\tau_{xy} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$

Substituting these into the inequality, then squaring, simplifying, and rearranging leads to the **canonical Tresca design equation for a shaft under combined bending and torsion**:

$$\sqrt{\sigma_x^{\,2} + 4 \tau_{xy}^{\,2}} \leq \sigma_y$$

### Step 4 — Write the Design Equation
Equivalently, the **equivalent (von-Mises-style) stress** for shaft design using Tresca is:

$$\sigma_{eq,\,Tresca} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^{\,2} + 4 \tau_{xy}^{\,2}}$$

The designer's safety check then becomes:

$$\frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq,\,Tresca}} \geq FOS$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight**: Note the factor **4** in front of $\tau_{xy}^2$ (Tresca) versus the factor **3** that would appear in the **von-Mises** criterion. Tresca is more conservative (gives a lower FOS) and is preferred by **Indian Standard codes (IS 2825)** for shaft design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Modified Goodman for Fluctuating Stress

### Given
A rotating shaft is subjected to a **bending moment varying between $+M$ and $-M$** (fully reversed loading).

Therefore:
- $\sigma_{max} = +M c / I$
- $\sigma_{min} = -M c / I$
- $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = 0$
- $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \sigma_{max}$

### Step 1 — General Goodman Line
The Goodman line is a **straight line** on the $\sigma_a$ vs $\sigma_m$ diagram connecting:
- $(\sigma_m = 0, \; \sigma_a = \sigma_e)$ — purely reversed
- $(\sigma_m = \sigma_u, \; \sigma_a = 0)$ — purely static (with no fatigue allowance)

The equation of this line is:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} = 1$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Allowable Alternating Stress
Rearranging the linear equation:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Design Check
For safe design under fluctuating load:

$$\sigma_{a,\,applied} \leq \sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> This is the **exact equation used in the KTU syllabus's shaft design module** and in the design of **crank shafts, connecting rods, and axles** — all of which see fully reversed bending with steady torsion.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: DFM Evaluation Using Weighted Scoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Board Style):**
> Two design alternatives **A** and **B** for a machine bracket are evaluated on four criteria with weights as given. Scores (out of 5) are tabulated. Find which design is preferable.

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Score $A_i$ | Score $B_i$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Manufacturability | 0.35 | 4 | 3 |
| Strength adequacy | 0.25 | 3 | 5 |
| Cost | 0.25 | 5 | 4 |
| Assembly ease | 0.15 | 3 | 4 |

### Step 1 — Compute Weighted Score for Design A

$$E_A = (0.35)(4) + (0.25)(3) + (0.25)(5) + (0.15)(3)$$

$$E_A = 1.40 + 0.75 + 1.25 + 0.45 = 3.85$$

### Step 2 — Compute Weighted Score for Design B

$$E_B = (0.35)(3) + (0.25)(5) + (0.25)(4) + (0.15)(4)$$

$$E_B = 1.05 + 1.25 + 1.00 + 0.60 = 3.90$$

### Step 3 — Decision

Since $E_B = 3.90 > E_A = 3.85$, **Design B is selected**.

> [!NOTE]
> Even though Design A wins on *manufacturability* and *cost*, Design B's superior *strength* and *assembly* scores push it ahead — proving that **multi-criteria evaluation must consider all factors holistically**, exactly as a KTU board examiner expects.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **decision matrix** is a high-yield tool — examiners may present a scenario and ask which mechanism is optimal.

| Motion Required | Best Mechanism | DFM Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous rotation, large speed reduction | **Gear train** | Standard gears, mass-produced, high efficiency |
| Reciprocating (in-line) motion from rotation | **Slider-crank** | Simple links, low part count |
| Intermittent indexing | **Geneva mechanism** or **Ratchet-pawl** | No continuous sliding friction |
| Programmed non-uniform motion | **Cam-follower** | One cam profile = infinite motion programs |
| Power transmission over long distance | **Belt drive** | Flexible, low cost, absorbs shock |
| Heavy load, slow linear motion | **Screw-nut** | Self-locking, very high mechanical advantage |
| Oscillating output from continuous input | **Four-bar linkage** | Robust, easily cast or machined |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — DFM Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Functional Requirement] --> B{Define Load and Motion}
    B --> C[Step 1: Select Material]
    C --> D[Step 2: Apply Strength Check]
    D --> E{FOS greater than 1.5?}
    E -- No --> F[Increase Section / Change Material]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Step 3: Select Mechanism]
    G --> H{DOF equals 1?}
    H -- No --> I[Revise Linkage]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Step 4: Apply DFM Heuristics]
    J --> K[Step 5: Evaluate with Weighted Score]
    K --> L{Score greater than Threshold?}
    L -- No --> M[Redesign Loop]
    M --> J
    L -- Yes --> N[Final Design Approved]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Strength Analysis Sub-Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Load F] --> B[Compute Nominal Stress sigma_nominal]
    B --> C[Identify Stress Riser]
    C --> D[Look up Kt from Chart]
    D --> E[Compute sigma_max equals Kt times sigma_nominal]
    E --> F[Apply FOS Check]
    F --> G{Pass?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Safe Design]
    G -- No --> I[Resize / Add Fillet]
    I --> B
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Mechanism Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Required Motion] --> B{Continuous Rotation?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Need Speed Change?]
    C -- Yes --> D[Gear Train or Belt Drive]
    C -- No --> E[Direct Shaft Coupling]
    B -- No --> F{Reciprocating Motion?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Slider Crank or Cam Follower]
    F -- No --> H{Intermittent Motion?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Geneva or Ratchet Pawl]
    H -- No --> J{Oscillating Motion?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Four Bar Linkage]
    J -- No --> L[Re-evaluate Requirement]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Evaluation Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Candidate 1] --> C[Weighted Score Model]
    A2[Design Candidate 2] --> C
    A3[Design Candidate n] --> C
    C --> D[Assign Weights w sub i]
    C --> E[Assign Scores s sub i]
    D --> F[Compute E equals sum w sub i times s sub i]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Rank Candidates}
    G --> H[Select Maximum Score]
    H --> I[Final Selection Report]
```
