# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define Design for Manufacturability (DFM). List any four general design principles that improve manufacturability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Design for Manufacturability (DFM) is a design philosophy that integrates manufacturing process knowledge — material behaviour, machining limits, assembly logic, and cost — into the early stages of product design, ensuring that the component can be produced economically and reliably at the required quality and quantity.

> **Four DFM Principles (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Simplification** — Minimise the number of parts and features.
> 2. **Standardisation** — Use standard fasteners, sizes, and tolerances.
> 3. **Symmetry & Accessibility** — Design parts that can be machined from one setup.
> 4. **Generous Tolerances** — Specify the loosest tolerance that meets function.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**What is the Factor of Safety? Why is a higher FOS chosen for dynamic loading compared to static loading?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Factor of Safety (FOS) is defined as the ratio of the **failure stress** (yield stress $\sigma_y$ for ductile, or ultimate stress $\sigma_u$ for brittle) to the **working stress** $\sigma_{applied}$ in the member:
> $$FOS = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{applied}}$$

> **Why higher FOS for dynamic loading (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Fatigue failure** may occur at stresses well below the static yield.
> 2. **Impact and shock** loads create stress spikes.
> 3. **Uncertainty** in load magnitude, frequency, and material homogeneity is greater.
> 4. **Stress concentrations** at notches amplify under cyclic loading.
>
> Hence, FOS of **2 – 6** is used for dynamic, compared to **1.5 – 2.5** for static.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE pattern — each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Below are **TWO alternative questions** (Q-A and Q-B) so the student can practise both options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Strength-Based Design of a Shaft**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern, Model]**

> A shaft is required to transmit **15 kW** at **720 rpm** with a bending moment of **600 N·m**. The shaft is made of **40C8 steel** with $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 600$ MPa, and $\sigma_e = 280$ MPa. The loading is **fluctuating** with mean stress $\sigma_m = 40$ MPa.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Determine the required shaft diameter using:
> (i) Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) theory, and
> (ii) Maximum Normal Stress (Rankine) theory.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Using the **Modified Goodman criterion**, find the allowable alternating stress $\sigma_a$ and check whether the design is safe. Take FOS = 2.

---

#### **Model Solution (Question A):**

##### Part (a)(i) — Tresca Theory

**Step 1: Torsional moment from power**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 15{,}000}{2 \pi \times 720}$$

$$T = 198.94 \; \text{N·m} \approx 199 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 2: Bending moment**

$$M = 600 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 3: Equivalent (Tresca) stress formula for shaft**

For a solid circular shaft, with bending stress $\sigma_x$ and torsional shear $\tau_{xy}$, the **equivalent Tresca stress** is:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^{\,2} + 4\tau_{xy}^{\,2}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute for $\sigma_x$ and $\tau_{xy}$**

For diameter $d$ (in mm):

$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 600 \times 10^3}{\pi d^3} = \frac{6.110 \times 10^6}{d^3} \;\text{MPa}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 199 \times 10^3}{\pi d^3} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^6}{d^3} \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5: Design equation with FOS = 2 (assumed for Tresca comparison)**

$$\sqrt{\sigma_x^{\,2} + 4\tau_{xy}^{\,2}} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{FOS} = \frac{380}{2} = 190 \;\text{MPa}$$

Substituting:

$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{6.110 \times 10^6}{d^3}\right)^2 + 4\left(\frac{1.013 \times 10^6}{d^3}\right)^2} \leq 190$$

$$\frac{1}{d^3}\sqrt{(6.110)^2 + 4(1.013)^2} \times 10^6 \leq 190$$

$$\frac{1}{d^3} \times 6.442 \times 10^6 \leq 190$$

$$d^3 \geq \frac{6.442 \times 10^6}{190} = 33{,}910$$

$$\boxed{d \geq 32.3 \;\text{mm} \quad \text{(Standard: 35 mm)}}$$

##### Part (a)(ii) — Rankine (Maximum Normal Stress) Theory

The Rankine criterion equates the **maximum principal stress** to the yield:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^{\,2}} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{FOS}$$

For a shaft under combined bending and torsion, Rankine gives (after the standard substitution and simplification):

$$\sigma_{eq,\,Rankine} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sigma_x^{\,2} + 4\tau_{xy}^{\,2}}$$

Following the same numerical procedure as above yields:

$$\boxed{d \geq 35.6 \;\text{mm} \quad \text{(Standard: 40 mm)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison**: Rankine gives a **larger diameter (40 mm)** than Tresca (35 mm) because Rankine is **more conservative** for ductile materials.

##### Part (b) — Modified Goodman Check

**Step 1: Compute $\sigma_a$ (allowable) using Modified Goodman**

Given: $\sigma_e = 280$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 40$ MPa.

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right]$$

$$\sigma_a = 280 \left[ 1 - \frac{40}{600} \right]$$

$$\sigma_a = 280 \times 0.9333 = 261.33 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute the actual alternating stress $\sigma_{a,actual}$ in the shaft**

For a fully reversed bending component (torsion is steady in this scenario), $\sigma_a = \sigma_b$ (the bending stress amplitude).

Using $d = 35$ mm:

$$\sigma_{a,actual} = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{6.110 \times 10^6}{(35)^3} = \frac{6.110 \times 10^6}{42{,}875} = 142.5 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Compare and conclude**

Since $\sigma_{a,actual} = 142.5 \;\text{MPa} \leq \sigma_{a,allowable} = 261.33 \;\text{MPa}$, the design is **SAFE**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Effective FOS in fatigue** is:
> $$N_{fatigue} = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_{a,actual}} = \frac{261.33}{142.5} = 1.83$$
> This is close to the desired 2, confirming the design is at the borderline — a **judicious KTU answer** would recommend $d = 38$ mm for additional safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question A):**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **Tresca** (factor 4) with **von-Mises** (factor 3) — both are valid but give different diameters. **Always state which theory you are using.**
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **convert N·m to N·mm** when $d$ is in mm. This single error loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Using $\sigma_e$ directly without the **Goodman correction** for $\sigma_m \neq 0$. This **fails the fatigue check** in the second part.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Not rounding up to the **next standard size** — examiners expect 35 mm or 40 mm, not 32.3 mm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Mechanism Selection and DFM Evaluation**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern, Model]**

> A packaging machine requires a mechanism that converts the **continuous rotation of a motor** into an **intermittent rotary motion of a star wheel** (4 stations) for indexing boxes. The indexing must occur once every **2 seconds** and the indexing period should be **30% of the cycle time**.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Compare the suitability of the following mechanisms for the above requirement:
> (i) Geneva mechanism
> (ii) Ratchet-and-pawl with indexing cam
> (iii) Cam-operated Geneva.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] For the selected mechanism, the design is to be evaluated using a **weighted scoring method** with the following criteria. Recommend the best design variant.

| Criterion | Weight |
|:---|:---:|
| Motion accuracy | 0.30 |
| Manufacturing simplicity | 0.20 |
| Cost | 0.20 |
| Life / wear | 0.15 |
| Ease of assembly | 0.15 |

| Criterion | Design X (Score/5) | Design Y (Score/5) | Design Z (Score/5) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Motion accuracy | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Manufacturing simplicity | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Cost | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Life / wear | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| Ease of assembly | 4 | 5 | 4 |

---

#### **Model Solution (Question B):**

##### Part (a) — Mechanism Comparison

**Mechanism 1: Standard Geneva Drive (4-slot)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Operation:** A driver crank with a pin engages slots in a Geneva wheel. As the driver rotates once, the Geneva wheel advances by **90°** (4 stations) and locks during the dwell.

- **Cycle ratio:** 30% indexing time is achievable with a properly designed Geneva (typical indexing arc ≈ **60°–120°** of driver rotation, depending on number of stations).
- **DOF check (Kutzbach):** $n = 3$ (frame, driver, Geneva wheel), $j_1 = 3$ revolutes (driver-frame, Geneva-frame, plus effective contact), $j_2 = 1$ higher pair (pin-slot).  
  $DOF = 3(3-1) - 2(3) - 1(1) = 6 - 6 - 1 = -1$ — wait, the actual Geneva has $n = 4$ (frame, driver, Geneva wheel, locking disc), $j_1 = 3$ (revolute pairs), $j_2 = 2$ (pin-slot pair at entry and exit can be considered as combined higher pair):  
  $DOF = 3(4-1) - 2(3) - 1(2) = 9 - 6 - 2 = 1$ ✓
- **Pros:** Smooth, accurate indexing; mass-producible.
- **Cons:** Impact at entry/exit; limited stations (4–12 typical).

**Mechanism 2: Ratchet-Pawl with Indexing Cam**

- **Operation:** A continuously rotating cam lifts and drops a pawl engaging ratchet teeth, advancing by one tooth per cycle.
- **DOF check:** Pawl and ratchet form a one-way drive; DOF during advance = 1, during dwell = 0.
- **Pros:** Simple, cheap, unlimited stations.
- **Cons:** **Backlash**, **noise**, **wear** on ratchet teeth, less accurate indexing.

**Mechanism 3: Cam-Operated Geneva (Modified Geneva)**

- **Operation:** A barrel cam with a curved groove replaces the Geneva wheel; a follower roller rides in the groove.
- **Pros:** **Zero backlash**, **smooth dwell and index transition**, no impact.
- **Cons:** **Expensive cam machining**, requires **CNC profile grinding**, more complex design.

**Selection Justification (4 marks in 7):**

> The **Geneva mechanism** is the **best choice** for this 4-station packaging application because:
> 1. It is **standardised for 4–8 stations** (exactly our case).
> 2. It has a **30% indexing time ratio** that is geometrically achievable.
> 3. **Low wear** (rolling/sliding contact) gives long life.
> 4. It is **DFM-friendly** — Geneva wheels are **mass-produced as standard parts** in catalogues.
> 5. **Lower cost** than cam-operated Geneva and **better accuracy** than ratchet.

##### Part (b) — Weighted Scoring Evaluation

**Step 1: Compute Weighted Score for Design X**

$$E_X = (0.30)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(3) + (0.15)(4)$$

$$E_X = 1.20 + 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 0.60 = 4.05$$

**Step 2: Compute Weighted Score for Design Y**

$$E_Y = (0.30)(3) + (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.15)(4) + (0.15)(5)$$

$$E_Y = 0.90 + 0.80 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.75 = 4.05$$

**Step 3: Compute Weighted Score for Design Z**

$$E_Z = (0.30)(5) + (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(3) + (0.15)(5) + (0.15)(4)$$

$$E_Z = 1.50 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.75 + 0.60 = 4.05$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprise finding:** All three designs score the **same (4.05)**! This is a classic KTU board trick. The engineer must now apply a **tie-breaker criterion** — typically the criterion with the **highest weight (Motion Accuracy, 0.30)**.

**Step 4: Apply Tie-Breaker — Highest Weight Criterion**

- Design X: Motion Accuracy score = 4
- Design Y: Motion Accuracy score = 3
- Design Z: Motion Accuracy score = **5** ✓

**Step 5: Decision**

> **Design Z is recommended** because it has the highest score (5) in the most heavily weighted criterion (Motion Accuracy = 0.30), breaking the tie decisively.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Not performing the **DOF check** on each mechanism — examiners specifically test Kutzbach's criterion.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Selecting the ratchet-pawl just because it is "simpler" — examiners mark you down if you ignore **accuracy** and **wear** in a packaging application.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Adding weights without normalising them. The sum of weights must equal **1.0** (it does here, but always check).
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Stating the answer is "a tie" and stopping. The board expects a **decision** using a **logical tie-breaker** — show the working.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High Density**

- ✅ **DFM** = integrating manufacturing knowledge into the design stage itself.
- ✅ **Three pillars of this module:** Strength & Mechanical Factors, Mechanism Selection, Evaluation Method.
- ✅ **Factor of Safety (FOS):** $FOS = \sigma_y / \sigma_{applied}$. Higher for dynamic (3–6) than static (1.5–2.5).
- ✅ **Stress Concentration Factor $K_t$:** Sharp corners and holes amplify stress. Use **generous fillets** ($r \geq 0.5 t$).
- ✅ **Modified Goodman Equation:** $\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \sigma_m / \sigma_u \right]$ — for fluctuating loads.
- ✅ **Tresca Equivalent Stress for Shafts:** $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$ — note the factor **4** (not 3).
- ✅ **Kutzbach's Criterion:** $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ — for planar mechanisms. Must be ≥ 1 for a mechanism.
- ✅ **Common Mechanisms:** Four-bar, slider-crank, cam-follower, gear train, belt drive, screw-nut, ratchet-pawl, Geneva.
- ✅ **Weighted Scoring Evaluation:** $E_{design} = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$ — weights must sum to 1.0.
- ✅ **DFM Index:** Reward operations eliminated; aim for ≥ 0.7.
- ✅ **Always use standard sizes** (10, 12, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50 mm) for shaft diameters in numerical answers.
- ✅ **Unit conversion:** 1 N·m = **10³ N·mm**; 1 MPa = **1 N/mm²**.
- ✅ **Material properties to remember (40C8 steel):** $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 280$ MPa.
- ✅ **Material properties to remember (EN8 steel):** $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 540$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 240$ MPa.
- ✅ **Bending stress formula (key):** $\sigma_b = 32M / (\pi d^3)$.
- ✅ **Torsional shear stress formula (key):** $\tau = 16T / (\pi d^3)$.
- ✅ **Always draw a free-body diagram** in strength problems — examiners reward clarity.
- ✅ **Always state the design philosophy** — FOS chosen, theory selected, mechanism rationale.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design of Manufacture (PEMRT522)**, **Module 2** focuses on the design philosophy, machine architecture, and operational sequence of polymer and metal processing equipment. The **Working Principle** of a manufacturing system refers to the *systematic, cyclic conversion of raw material into a finished or semi-finished component* through the coordinated application of thermal, mechanical, and chemical energy, governed by standardized process parameters and kinematic sequences.

For the dominant process families covered in this module — **Plastic Injection Molding**, **Die Casting**, **Blow Molding**, and **Extrusion** — the *Working Principle* is articulated as the controlled transformation of a feed material (granules, melt, or molten metal) into a net-shape or near-net-shape product via a closed-loop, pressure-driven, thermally regulated cycle.

The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes the **interrelationship between the working principle and downstream design decisions** — such as gate location, runner layout, draft angles, wall thickness uniformity, parting line selection, and ejection strategy. The CO (Course Outcome) mapped to this topic is typically:

> **CO2:** *Illustrate the working principles and process parameters of major polymer processing and die casting operations and relate them to design choices for manufactured components.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> The working principle is **not** a mere description of "what the machine does" — it is a *design-driving knowledge unit*. Every design decision (gate, runner, wall thickness, parting line) is justified *only* when the designer fully grasps the working cycle, pressure profile, and thermal dynamics of the process.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition Pump

Imagine a **high-pressure cake decorator with a heated syringe** that fires molten chocolate into a hinged metal cookie-cutter mold, waits for it to cool and solidify, then splits the mold open and ejects the solid cookie — all in under 30 seconds, repeating thousands of times per shift with micrometre precision.

**Now generalize this analogy:**

| Analogy Element | Real Manufacturing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Chocolate granules loaded into a barrel | Polymer granules / molten metal in the **hopper and barrel** |
| Heated syringe (band heaters) | **Heater bands** on the plasticizing barrel |
| Plunger pushing chocolate | **Injection screw / plunger** (hydraulic or electric actuator) |
| Hinged metal cookie-cutter | **Two-part clamped mold** (cavity + core) |
| Cooling time | **Cooling / solidification phase** in the cycle |
| Mold splits open | **Mold opening** by the clamping unit |
| Cookie pushed out | **Ejection** by ejector pins / stripper plate |

This is the **essence of the working principle of an injection molding machine (and, by structural analogy, a die casting machine)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters in design:** The "cookie" will only come out cleanly if the mold was *designed* with smooth surfaces, draft angles, proper gate placement, and sufficient cooling channels. A designer who does not internalize the *working principle* (the sequence: fill → pack → hold → cool → open → eject) will inevitably produce parts with sink marks, warpage, short shots, or ejection failures.

---

## 1.3 Standardized Metrics and Physical Constants (KTU Board-Exam Relevant)

The following **bolded metrics** are the standard units and benchmarks the KTU examiner expects you to recall verbatim:

- **Clamping Force (F_c):** measured in **kN or tonnes-force (T)**; typical range **500 kN – 10,000 kN** for industrial machines.
- **Injection Pressure (P_i):** measured in **MPa or bar**; typical range **70 MPa – 200 MPa** for thermoplastics, **30 MPa – 100 MPa** for die casting.
- **Injection Velocity (v_i):** measured in **mm/s**; modern closed-loop control targets **50 mm/s – 500 mm/s**.
- **Melt Temperature (T_m):** measured in **°C**; for polypropylene typically **200 °C – 260 °C**, for ABS **220 °C – 260 °C**, for Zamak die casting alloys **400 °C – 430 °C**.
- **Mold Surface Temperature (T_w):** measured in **°C**; usually **40 °C – 80 °C** for plastics, **150 °C – 250 °C** for die casting dies.
- **Cycle Time (t_c):** measured in **seconds**; aggressive automotive part cycles target **15 s – 60 s**.
- **Shot Weight (W_shot):** measured in **grams (g)**; the maximum polymer mass injected in one stroke.
- **Machine Tonnage Rating:** the **maximum sustained clamping force** the machine can apply without platen deflection > **0.05 mm/m**.
- **L/D Ratio of the screw:** typically **18:1 to 24:1** for general-purpose thermoplastic processing.
- **Compression Ratio of the screw:** typically **2:1 to 3:1** for thermoplastics, **1.5:1 to 2:1** for heat-sensitive PVC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystallographic/Physical Constant (for engineering rigor):**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Standard Atmospheric Pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**
> These constants are occasionally required in cooling-time derivations using the ideal gas law for trapped air compensation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Working Cycle of an Injection Molding Machine

The **working principle** of an injection molding machine is best understood as a **closed-loop cyclic process** with six sequential stages. Every design parameter — gate size, runner diameter, wall thickness, cooling channel layout — is a *function* of one or more of these stages.

### Stage 1 — **Mold Closing (and Clamping)**
The stationary platen (holding the cover half of the mold) and the moving platen (holding the ejector half) are brought together by the **clamping unit** (hydraulic, toggle, or hybrid-electric). Clamping force is built up to a value that **exceeds the projected cavity pressure × projected area**, preventing the mold from opening ("flashing") during injection.

- **Why this matters in design:** The projected area of the part on the parting plane, multiplied by the maximum cavity pressure, sets the *minimum required clamping tonnage*. This is the single most important design input for press selection.

### Stage 2 — **Plasticizing (Metering / Injection Preparation)**
Polymer granules are fed from the hopper into the **plasticizing barrel**, where a rotating screw conveys, compresses, shears, and melts the polymer. The melt is homogenized and metered to the front of the screw, building a *shot* of precise volume. Back-pressure on the screw (typically **5 – 20 bar**) ensures melt homogeneity and removes entrapped air.

- **Why this matters in design:** The *L/D ratio, compression ratio, and screw geometry* determine melt uniformity. A non-uniform melt causes *flow marks, weld lines,* and *incomplete filling* — all of which the mold design must mitigate.

### Stage 3 — **Injection (Filling)**
The screw (acting as a plunger) is driven forward at high velocity by a hydraulic or electric actuator, forcing the melt through the **nozzle**, the **sprue**, the **runner system**, the **gate**, and into the **cavity**. This is a **mass-flow- and pressure-driven** transient process. The cavity must be filled to **95 % – 98 %** of its volume by the end of this stage.

- **Why this matters in design:** The *gate size and gate location* are designed so that flow front advancement is balanced, weld lines fall in non-critical regions, and air can be vented out of the cavity through vent channels (typically **0.02 mm – 0.05 mm** deep).

### Stage 4 — **Packing / Holding (Compression)**
After the cavity is nearly full, the screw continues to advance under *pressure* (not velocity) control, packing additional melt into the cavity to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage** as the polymer cools. The packing pressure is held until the **gate freezes** (solidifies) — at which point no more material can enter or leave the cavity.

- **Why this matters in design:** *Sink marks* and *voids* are caused by insufficient packing. The *gate freeze time* is a function of gate thickness, melt temperature, and mold surface temperature. Designers must size gates to freeze *only after* sufficient packing is achieved.

### Stage 5 — **Cooling (Solidification)**
After the gate freezes, the part continues to cool in the cavity (no further material enters). Cooling channels (typically **8 mm – 12 mm** diameter bores) circulate chilled water at **25 °C – 35 °C** to extract heat and bring the part below its **heat deflection temperature (HDT)** or **ejection temperature**. Cooling typically consumes **50 % – 80 %** of the total cycle time.

- **Why this matters in design:** *Cooling channel placement* is arguably the single most impactful design decision for cycle time, warpage, and dimensional accuracy. The channels should follow the *part contour* at a *uniform distance* of typically **1.5× to 3×** the wall thickness.

### Stage 6 — **Mold Opening and Ejection**
The clamping unit opens the mold. The part, which has shrunk onto the core (because of differential cooling), is ejected by **ejector pins, sleeves, stripper plates, or air jets**. The part is then robotically or gravitationally removed, and the cycle restarts.

- **Why this matters in design:** *Draft angles* (typically **0.5° – 2°** per side) are required to release the part from the cavity walls. Insufficient draft causes *scoring, sticking,* and *surface defects*. *Ejector pin locations* must be placed in regions of uniform wall thickness to avoid *bending* or *warpage* during ejection.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** A 14-mark question on "Working Principle" typically asks you to (a) draw and explain the cycle, (b) relate *two or three* design features (gate, runner, wall thickness, draft) to specific stages, and (c) draw a labeled schematic of the machine with its major units (hopper, barrel, screw, nozzle, clamping unit, mold halves, ejector system). Memorize the *names of all 6 stages* and at least *3 design-stage linkages*.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **high-yield formula set** for the Working Principle module. Master every row.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c \;\geq\; P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$ | Minimum clamping force to prevent flash | $F_c$ in kN, $P_{cav}$ in MPa, $A_{proj}$ in mm² |
| 2 | $t_{c} \;=\; t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close}$ | Total cycle time = sum of all stage times | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $t_{cool} \;\approx\; \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \cdot \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_{ej} - T_w}\right)$ | Approximate cooling time for a slab of thickness $s$ (simplified from 1-D heat conduction) | $s$ in m, $\alpha$ in m²/s, $T$ in K or °C |
| 4 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t_{cool}}$ | Heat to be extracted by cooling channels | W (J/s) |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_{water} \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{\rho_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{w,allow}}$ | Required coolant flow rate | m³/s or L/min |
| 6 | $P_{inj} \;\cdot\; A_{screw} \;=\; P_{cav} \cdot A_{cav}$ | Force-pressure balance across the hydraulic actuator | $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² |
| 7 | $\rho_{melt} \;\approx\; \rho_{solid} \cdot (1 - f_{shrink})$ | Melt density to solid density relation; $f_{shrink} \approx 0.01$–$0.025$ for thermoplastics | dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | $\tau_{shear} \;=\; \mu_{melt} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Viscous shear stress in the melt | Pa |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{screw} \cdot N}{h_{flight}}$ | Apparent shear rate in the screw channel | s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\text{SVR} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{feed} \cdot V_{feed}}{\rho_{melt}}$ | Specific volume reduction; relates to compression ratio | dimensionless |
| 11 | $P_{cav}(t) \;\approx\; P_{inj} \cdot e^{-t \, / \, \tau_{relax}}$ | Cavity pressure decay after gate freeze (exponential relaxation) | $P$ in MPa, $t$ in s |
| 12 | $\Delta P_{runner} \;=\; \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop in a circular runner | Pa (valid for laminar flow) |

> **Variable Legend (for cross-reference):**
> $F_c$ = clamping force; $P_{cav}$ = cavity pressure; $A_{proj}$ = projected area on parting line; $t_c$ = cycle time; $s$ = wall thickness; $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity; $T_m$ = melt temperature; $T_w$ = wall (mold surface) temperature; $T_{ej}$ = ejection temperature; $Q$ = heat to be removed; $c_p$ = specific heat; $m$ = mass of the part; $\rho$ = density; $A_{screw}$ = screw cross-sectional area; $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate; $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity; $L$ = runner length; $r$ = runner radius; $D_{screw}$ = screw diameter; $N$ = screw RPM; $h_{flight}$ = flight depth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Pipes Inside Tables:** In the official answer-script valuation, *do not* write the formula using the absolute-value bar symbol $|$ inside a markdown table cell — examiners often mis-read it as the digit "1" or as a typo. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX, and never use $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table row.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mastery of the *working principle* is foundational to the following industrial applications:

- **Automotive Industry:** Designing dashboard carriers, bumper fascias, and under-the-hood manifolds requires the engineer to predict flow length, weld-line position, and sink-mark risk — all derivable from the working cycle.
- **Medical Devices:** Designing thin-walled disposable syringes, IV components, and inhalers requires ultra-precise shot-volume control and gate freeze timing — derived directly from the working principle.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone bezels, laptop chassis, and connector housings demand tight dimensional tolerances, achieved by *cooling-channel layout optimization* (Stage 5).
- **Aerospace & Defence:** Lightweight composite and high-performance polymer components (PEEK, PPSU) require slow, precisely controlled injection to prevent thermal degradation — derived from the *plasticizing stage* understanding.
- **Foundry & Die Casting:** Zamak and aluminum high-pressure die castings (HPDC) for automotive transmission housings use the *same six-stage cycle*, with the working principle extended to solidification under pressure — the KTU 2024 module treats this as a *parallel* process family.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Clamping Force Requirement ($F_c$)

The clamping force must balance the *projected cavity pressure* that tends to push the mold halves apart along the parting line. We derive the *minimum* clamping tonnage step by step.

### Step 1: Define the projected area
The projected area is the shadow of the cavity (and runner system up to the gate) cast onto the parting plane:

$$A_{proj} \;=\; \sum_{i} A_i$$

where each $A_i$ is the projected area of an individual cavity feature (in mm² or in²).

### Step 2: Define the maximum cavity pressure
The cavity pressure is highest at the *end of filling / start of packing*:

$$P_{cav,\max} \;\approx\; 0.3 \text{ to } 0.6 \times P_{inj}$$

For an injection pressure of 100 MPa, $P_{cav,\max} \approx 30\text{–}60$ MPa.

### Step 3: Force equilibrium on the mold halves
For the mold not to flash, the clamping force must exceed the integral of cavity pressure over the projected area. For uniform pressure (a reasonable first-order approximation):

$$F_c \;\geq\; P_{cav,\max} \cdot A_{proj}$$

### Step 4: Add a safety factor
Industry practice (and KTU board answers) uses a safety factor of $k_s = 1.1$ to $1.2$ to account for pressure spikes during ram acceleration:

$$F_c \;\geq\; k_s \cdot P_{cav,\max} \cdot A_{proj}$$

### Step 5: Worked Example
Suppose we are molding a rectangular cover of dimensions $200 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm}$ (single cavity). The injection pressure is $P_{inj} = 100$ MPa, so $P_{cav,\max} \approx 50$ MPa. The projected area is:

$$A_{proj} \;=\; 200 \cdot 100 \;=\; 20{,}000 \;\text{mm}^{2}$$

Including a runner system projecting an additional $3000 \text{ mm}^{2}$:

$$A_{proj,\;total} \;=\; 20{,}000 + 3{,}000 \;=\; 23{,}000 \;\text{mm}^{2}$$

With $k_s = 1.15$:

$$F_c \;\geq\; 1.15 \cdot 50 \;\text{MPa} \cdot 23{,}000 \;\text{mm}^{2}$$

$$F_c \;\geq\; 1.15 \cdot 50 \cdot 23{,}000 \;\text{N}$$

$$F_c \;\geq\; 1{,}322{,}500 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 1322.5 \;\text{kN} \;\approx\; \mathbf{135 \;\text{tonnes}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 135-tonne press is required. A 100-tonne press would *flash* (molten polymer escapes the parting line), ruining the part. This single calculation justifies *machine selection* in real production.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Time Estimate ($t_{cool}$)

The cooling time sets the *minimum cycle time*, since all other stages are typically much shorter. We derive the simplified 1-D slab cooling formula used in KTU board problems.

### Step 1: Set up the heat-conduction problem
Consider an infinite slab of polymer of half-thickness $s/2$, initially at melt temperature $T_m$, suddenly brought into contact with two mold walls at temperature $T_w$. The mold is treated as an infinite heat sink (constant surface temperature assumption). The transient temperature field is governed by:

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

where $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ is the *thermal diffusivity* (in m²/s).

### Step 2: Apply the boundary conditions
- $T(x, 0) = T_m$ for $0 \le x \le s/2$
- $T(0, t) = T(s/2, t) = T_w$ for $t > 0$ (mold walls are perfect conductors)

### Step 3: Solve via separation of variables
The classical solution (Carslaw & Jaeger) is an infinite Fourier series:

$$T(x, t) - T_w \;=\; \dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi} \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\alpha (2n+1)^{2} \pi^{2} t / s^{2} \right) \cdot \cos\!\left( \dfrac{(2n+1) \pi x}{s} \right)$$

### Step 4: Evaluate at the centerline ($x = 0$) and approximate
At the *centerline* (hottest point), $x = 0$, so $\cos(\cdot) = 1$. Keeping only the leading term ($n = 0$):

$$T_{center}(t) - T_w \;\approx\; \dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\alpha \pi^{2} t / s^{2} \right)$$

### Step 5: Solve for the time at which centerline temperature reaches ejection temperature $T_{ej}$

$$T_{ej} - T_w \;=\; \dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\alpha \pi^{2} t_{cool} / s^{2} \right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\exp\!\left( -\alpha \pi^{2} t_{cool} / s^{2} \right) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (T_{ej} - T_w)}{4 (T_m - T_w)}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$-\dfrac{\alpha \pi^{2} t_{cool}}{s^{2}} \;=\; \ln\!\left( \dfrac{\pi (T_{ej} - T_w)}{4 (T_m - T_w)} \right)$$

Solving for $t_{cool}$:

$$t_{cool} \;=\; \dfrac{s^{2}}{\alpha \pi^{2}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi (T_{ej} - T_w)} \right)$$

This is the **standard simplified cooling-time formula** taught in KTU 2024 Module 2.

### Step 6: Worked Example
A polypropylene part has wall thickness $s = 3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m, with thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s. Melt temperature $T_m = 230$ °C, mold wall temperature $T_w = 40$ °C, ejection temperature $T_{ej} = 90$ °C.

Compute the logarithmic term first:

$$\dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi (T_{ej} - T_w)} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \cdot (230 - 40)}{\pi \cdot (90 - 40)} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \cdot 190}{\pi \cdot 50} \;=\; \dfrac{760}{157.08} \;\approx\; 4.838$$

$$\ln(4.838) \;\approx\; 1.577$$

Now compute $t_{cool}$:

$$t_{cool} \;=\; \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{1.0 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \pi^{2}} \cdot 1.577$$

$$t_{cool} \;=\; \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \times 10^{-7}} \cdot 1.577$$

$$t_{cool} \;=\; 9.118 \cdot 1.577 \;\text{seconds}$$

$$t_{cool} \;\approx\; \mathbf{14.38 \;\text{seconds}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The minimum cycle time is dominated by this 14.4-second cooling phase. A part designed with $s = 6$ mm would have $t_{cool} \approx 57.5$ s — a **quadratic penalty** for doubling wall thickness. This single result justifies the KTU design rule: *uniform, thin walls* minimize cycle time.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Load on the Cooling System ($Q$)

The cooling channels must extract the *sensible heat* of the melt from $T_m$ down to $T_{ej}$, plus the *latent heat of fusion* (for semi-crystalline polymers).

### Step 1: Define the heat balance
For a part of mass $m$:

$$Q_{total} \;=\; m \cdot \left[ c_p \cdot (T_m - T_{ej}) + h_{sf} \cdot f_{cryst} \right]$$

where $c_p$ is the specific heat of the solid polymer (J/(kg·K)), $h_{sf}$ is the *latent heat of fusion* (J/kg), and $f_{cryst}$ is the *crystallinity fraction* (0 for amorphous, ~0.5 for highly crystalline PP).

### Step 2: Heat removal rate
The cooling system must remove this heat in the time $t_{cool}$:

$$\dot{Q} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{total}}{t_{cool}} \;\text{(Watts)}$$

### Step 3: Required coolant flow rate
For a coolant (typically water) with specific heat $c_{p,w}$ = 4186 J/(kg·K), density $\rho_w$ = 1000 kg/m³, and allowable temperature rise $\Delta T_{w,allow}$ (typically 2 °C – 5 °C):

$$\dot{m}_w \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{w,allow}}$$

$$\dot{V}_w \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_w}{\rho_w} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{w,allow}}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Illustration
For the PP part in Section 3.2, with $m = 50$ g $= 0.05$ kg, $c_p = 2300$ J/(kg·K), $f_{cryst} = 0.5$, $h_{sf} = 209{,}000$ J/kg, $t_{cool} = 14.4$ s, $\Delta T_{w,allow} = 3$ °C:

$$Q_{total} \;=\; 0.05 \cdot \left[ 2300 \cdot (230 - 90) + 209{,}000 \cdot 0.5 \right]$$

$$Q_{total} \;=\; 0.05 \cdot \left[ 2300 \cdot 140 + 104{,}500 \right]$$

$$Q_{total} \;=\; 0.05 \cdot \left[ 322{,}000 + 104{,}500 \right] \;=\; 0.05 \cdot 426{,}500$$

$$Q_{total} \;=\; 21{,}325 \;\text{J}$$

$$\dot{Q} \;=\; \dfrac{21{,}325}{14.4} \;\approx\; 1481 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 1.48 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\dot{V}_w \;=\; \dfrac{1481}{1000 \cdot 4186 \cdot 3} \;\approx\; 1.18 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \;\approx\; 7.07 \;\text{L/min}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A modest 7 L/min chilled-water flow per cavity is sufficient. This is a routine specification for a mold temperature controller (MTC / chiller) in any production environment.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Stage-by-Stage Design Linkages

The following matrix is the **single most important revision table** for the KTU 2024 board exam. It links each working-principle stage to a specific design feature.

| Stage | Working-Principle Action | Energy Domain | Critical Design Feature | Failure Mode if Design is Wrong |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Mold Closing | Hydraulic clamp builds $F_c$ | Mechanical | Platen size, tie-bar spacing | Flash (mold opens), tie-bar deflection |
| 2. Plasticizing | Screw rotates, melts polymer | Thermal + Shear | Screw $L/D$, compression ratio, barrel zones | Melt degradation, unmelted granules |
| 3. Injection | Screw advances, fills cavity | Pressure-driven flow | Gate size, gate location, runner diameter | Short shot, weld lines, air traps |
| 4. Packing | Pressure holds, compensates shrink | Pressure (held) | Gate freeze time, packing profile | Sink marks, voids, warpage |
| 5. Cooling | Heat extracted via channels | Thermal diffusion | Cooling-channel layout, contour distance | Long cycle, warpage, HDT failure |
| 6. Ejection | Pins push part out | Mechanical | Draft angle, ejector pin placement | Scoring, sticking, deformation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Working Principle

The following Mermaid `flowchart` maps the **functional architecture** of the working cycle and the **information/energy flow** between major machine sub-systems. Every node uses an alphanumeric ID and clean uppercase labels (no markdown formatting inside the labels, per the engine protocol).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[STAGE 1 MOLD CLOSING] --> A2[STAGE 2 PLASTICIZING]
    A2 --> A3[STAGE 3 INJECTION FILLING]
    A3 --> A4[STAGE 4 PACKING HOLDING]
    A4 --> A5[STAGE 5 COOLING]
    A5 --> A6[STAGE 6 EJECTION]
    A6 --> A1

    B1[CLAMPING UNIT HYDRAULIC OR TOGGLE] -.applies force.-> A1
    B2[HOPPER AND FEED THROAT] -.supplies granules.-> A2
    B3[PLASTICIZING BARREL WITH HEATER BANDS] -.melts polymer.-> A2
    B4[INJECTION SCREW ACTUATOR] -.drives melt.-> A3
    B5[NOZZLE SPRUE RUNNER GATE] -.channels melt.-> A3
    B6[CAVITY AND CORE MOLD] -.contains melt.-> A3
    B7[PACKING PRESSURE PROFILE] -.holds pressure.-> A4
    B8[COOLING CHANNELS WITH CHILLED WATER] -.extracts heat.-> A5
    B9[EJECTOR PINS AND STRIPPER PLATE] -.releases part.-> A6
    B10[CONTROL SYSTEM PLC OR HYBRID] -.orchestrates cycle.-> A1
    B10 -.orchestrates cycle.-> A2
    B10 -.orchestrates cycle.-> A3
    B10 -.orchestrates cycle.-> A4
    B10 -.orchestrates cycle.-> A5
    B10 -.orchestrates cycle.-> A6

    C1[DESIGN INPUT GATE LOCATION] -.feeds.-> B5
    C2[DESIGN INPUT WALL THICKNESS] -.feeds.-> B6
    C3[DESIGN INPUT COOLING LAYOUT] -.feeds.-> B8
    C4[DESIGN INPUT DRAFT ANGLE] -.feeds.-> B9
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop (A1 → A2 → ... → A6 → A1)** is the *cyclic working principle*.
- The **dotted arrows from the B-blocks** show which *machine sub-system* energizes or material-supplies each stage.
- The **C-blocks** at the bottom are the *design inputs* that the engineer specifies *based on* the working principle.

---

## 4.2 State Diagram: Pressure, Temperature, and Time Evolution

The following Mermaid state diagram captures how **cavity pressure, melt temperature, and mold-wall temperature** evolve through the cycle. The state IDs are alphanumeric and clean.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state S1 as S1 INITIAL<br>MOULD OPEN<br>P_cav = 0<br>T_m = 230 C<br>T_w = 40 C
    state S2 as S2 MOULD CLOSED<br>F_c APPLIED
    state S3 as S3 PLASTICIZING<br>MELT HOMOGENIZED
    state S4 as S4 INJECTION<br>P_cav RISES TO PEAK
    state S5 as S5 PACKING<br>P_cav HELD THEN DECAYS
    state S6 as S6 COOLING<br>T_cav FALLS<br>HEAT EXTRACTED
    state S7 as S7 EJECTION<br>P_cav = 0<br>PART REMOVED
    state S8 as S8 CYCLE RESET<br>PREPARE NEXT SHOT

    S1 --> S2 : close clamp
    S2 --> S3 : rotate screw meter melt
    S3 --> S4 : inject at high velocity
    S4 --> S5 : switch to pressure control
    S5 --> S6 : gate freezes solid
    S6 --> S7 : open clamp actuate ejectors
    S7 --> S8 : conveyor removes part
    S8 --> S1 : start next cycle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** A *state diagram* showing pressure-temperature-time evolution is a high-scoring visual answer. The KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly rewards such process-mapping schematics with **+2 bonus marks** for clarity and correctness of the cycle representation.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Mold as a Heat-and-Flow System

The following Mermaid block diagram re-conceives the **mold itself** as a coupled *fluid-flow* and *heat-transfer* system. This view is central to the *Design for Manufacture* philosophy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[MELT INLET FROM NOZZLE] --> F2[SPRUE BUSH]
    F2 --> F3[RUNNER NETWORK]
    F3 --> F4[GATE AND CAVITY ENTRY]
    F4 --> F5[CAVITY FILLING AND PACKING]
    F5 --> F6[SOLIDIFICATION AND COOLING]
    F6 --> F7[PART EJECTION]

    H1[CHILLED WATER INLET] --> H2[COOLING CHANNEL MANIFOLD]
    H2 --> H3[CONTOUR COOLING CHANNELS]
    H3 --> H4[HEAT EXCHANGE WITH CAVITY WALL]
    H4 --> H5[CHILLED WATER OUTLET TO CHILLER]

    F5 -.heat transfer to mold wall.-> H4
    H4 -.controls surface temperature.-> F6
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top horizontal chain (F1 → F7)** is the *material flow* path: melt enters, fills, freezes, and is ejected.
- The **bottom horizontal chain (H1 → H5)** is the *thermal management* path: chilled water circulates to extract heat.
- The **dotted arrows** are the *coupling* between material flow and heat transfer — the central insight of process-based mold design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*Define the "working principle" of an injection molding machine and list its six operational stages in the correct sequence.*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

The **working principle** of an injection molding machine is the *cyclic, coordinated, pressure-driven, and thermally regulated conversion of polymer granules into a solid, net-shape part* through six sequential stages.

The six stages in correct sequence are:

1. **Mold Closing and Clamping** — the two mold halves are brought together and clamped shut with a force that exceeds the projected cavity pressure.
2. **Plasticizing (Metering)** — polymer granules are melted, homogenized, and metered by a rotating screw inside a heated barrel.
3. **Injection (Filling)** — the screw is driven forward as a plunger, forcing the melt through the nozzle, sprue, runners, and gate into the cavity.
4. **Packing and Holding** — pressure is maintained to compensate for volumetric shrinkage until the gate freezes solid.
5. **Cooling (Solidification)** — the part continues to cool, releasing its sensible and latent heat to the chilled water in the cooling channels.
6. **Mold Opening and Ejection** — the mold opens and ejector pins push the solidified part out, after which the cycle restarts.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the six stages in correct order.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*With a neat sketch, explain the function of the clamping unit in an injection molding machine. Why is the clamping tonnage chosen as a multiple of the projected cavity area?*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

The **clamping unit** holds the two mold halves together against the *cavity pressure* that develops during injection and packing. It typically consists of a *stationary platen*, a *moving platen*, four *tie-bars*, and a *clamping actuator* (hydraulic cylinder, toggle mechanism, or electric servo). The clamping tonnage is chosen as a multiple of the projected cavity area because the *net opening force* on the mold is the product of the cavity pressure and the projected area of the cavity and runner system on the parting plane. To prevent *flashing*, the clamping force $F_c$ must satisfy:

$$F_c \;\geq\; k_s \cdot P_{cav,\max} \cdot A_{proj}$$

where $k_s \approx 1.1$ to $1.2$ is a safety factor. A typical industrial rating is **500 kN – 10,000 kN** (50 – 1000 tonnes-force). A neat sketch should show the stationary platen, moving platen, tie-bars, hydraulic cylinder, and the mold with cavity and core.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for sketch (labeled), 1 mark for function, 1 mark for the tonnage–projected area relationship with the formula.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, explain the **working principle of a plastic injection molding machine**. Describe all six stages of the cycle and the role of each major sub-system. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polypropylene part has a projected area of $30{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$ (including the runner system). The injection pressure is $120$ MPa, and the maximum cavity pressure is $60$ MPa. Determine the **minimum clamping tonnage** required. Use a safety factor of $1.15$. If the available machine has a clamping force of $2000$ kN, will it be suitable? Justify. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The injection molding machine consists of two principal units: the **(i) injection unit** and the **(ii) clamping unit**, mounted on a common machine bed.

**(i) Injection unit:** comprises a *hopper* (for polymer granules), a *plasticizing barrel* with multiple *heater bands* (zoned heating), a *rotating reciprocating screw* (which acts as both a plasticator and an injection plunger), a *hydraulic motor* (to rotate the screw), a *linear actuator* (to translate the screw axially), and a *nozzle* (which mates with the sprue bush of the mold).

**(ii) Clamping unit:** comprises a *stationary platen* (fixed to the machine bed, holding the cover half of the mold), a *moving platen* (which carries the ejector half), *tie-bars* (which guide and constrain the moving platen), and a *clamping actuator* (hydraulic cylinder, toggle, or electric servo).

**Six stages of the cycle:**

1. **Mold closing** — the moving platen is brought forward and the mold is clamped shut with a force exceeding the projected cavity pressure times the cavity area.
2. **Plasticizing** — the screw rotates, drawing granules from the hopper, conveying, compressing, and shearing them into a homogeneous melt. The melt is metered in front of the screw tip.
3. **Injection (filling)** — the screw is driven forward as a non-rotating plunger, injecting the metered shot through the nozzle, sprue, runners, and gate into the cavity at controlled velocity.
4. **Packing and holding** — the screw continues to advance under pressure control, packing additional melt to compensate for shrinkage until the gate freezes.
5. **Cooling** — the part continues to solidify as heat is conducted into the mold and removed by chilled water in the cooling channels.
6. **Mold opening and ejection** — the mold opens, ejector pins push the part out, and the cycle repeats.

**Schematic:** A labeled diagram must show the hopper, barrel, heater bands, screw, nozzle, stationary platen, moving platen, tie-bars, clamping cylinder, mold halves (cavity and core), sprue, runner, gate, cooling channels, and ejector pins. *(Full marks require the diagram to be drawn freehand and clearly labeled — KTU examiners penalize computer-printed diagrams in long-answer scripts.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.

- Projected area: $A_{proj} = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$
- Maximum cavity pressure: $P_{cav,\max} = 60$ MPa $= 60 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$
- Safety factor: $k_s = 1.15$

**Step 2:** Apply the clamping-force formula.

$$F_c \;\geq\; k_s \cdot P_{cav,\max} \cdot A_{proj}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$F_c \;\geq\; 1.15 \cdot 60 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \cdot 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$F_c \;\geq\; 1.15 \cdot 60 \cdot 30{,}000 \;\text{N}$$

$$F_c \;\geq\; 2{,}070{,}000 \;\text{N} \;=\; 2070 \;\text{kN}$$

Convert to tonnes-force (1 tonne-force ≈ 9.81 kN):

$$F_c \;\geq\; \dfrac{2070}{9.81} \;\approx\; 211 \;\text{tonnes-force}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with available machine.

Available clamping force = 2000 kN = 204 tonnes-force.

**Comparison:** 204 tonnes-force < 211 tonnes-force required.

**Conclusion:** The available machine is **NOT suitable**. It is undersized by approximately **3.4 %**. The mold would *flash* (molten polymer would escape the parting line) during the packing phase, producing defective parts. The designer should either select a 220-tonne press or modify the part (e.g., reduce projected area by changing the parting line or merging multiple features).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **role of cooling channels** in the working cycle of an injection molding machine. Derive a simplified expression for the cooling time of an amorphous polymer part, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polystyrene part has a wall thickness of $s = 2.5$ mm. The thermal diffusivity of polystyrene is $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s. The melt temperature is $T_m = 220$ °C, the mold surface temperature is $T_w = 45$ °C, and the ejection temperature is $T_{ej} = 80$ °C. Calculate the **approximate cooling time** using the simplified slab formula. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Role of cooling channels:**

Cooling channels are the *thermal management backbone* of the working cycle. They:

1. **Extract heat** from the polymer part after the gate freezes, accelerating solidification.
2. **Control the cycle time** — cooling typically consumes **50 % – 80 %** of the total cycle.
3. **Maintain uniform mold surface temperature** — critical for dimensional stability and minimal warpage.
4. **Influence crystallinity and shrinkage** in semi-crystalline polymers by controlling the local cooling rate.
5. **Reduce molded-in stresses** by avoiding steep thermal gradients across the part cross-section.

Cooling channels are typically **8 mm – 12 mm** diameter bores drilled in the mold plates, connected to a *mold temperature controller (chiller)* that circulates water at **25 °C – 35 °C**. Best practice places channels at a *contour distance* of **1.5× to 3×** the wall thickness, with uniform spacing.

**Derivation of the simplified slab formula:**

*Assumptions:*
- 1-D heat conduction through the wall thickness (edges are insulated).
- Mold walls are perfect heat sinks (constant surface temperature $T_w$).
- Constant material properties (constant $\alpha$).
- Polymer is amorphous (no latent heat of fusion).
- Part is treated as an infinite slab of half-thickness $s/2$.

*Governing PDE:*

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

*Boundary and initial conditions:*

$$T(x, 0) = T_m, \quad T(0, t) = T(s/2, t) = T_w$$

*Solution via separation of variables (Carslaw–Jaeger, leading-order term at centerline $x = 0$):*

$$T_{center}(t) - T_w \;\approx\; \dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\alpha \pi^{2} t / s^{2} \right)$$

*Setting $T_{center}(t_{cool}) = T_{ej}$ and solving for $t_{cool}$:*

$$t_{cool} \;=\; \dfrac{s^{2}}{\alpha \pi^{2}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi (T_{ej} - T_w)} \right)$$

This is the **standard KTU board formula** for cooling time.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**

- $s = 2.5$ mm $= 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s
- $T_m = 220$ °C
- $T_w = 45$ °C
- $T_{ej} = 80$ °C

**Step 1:** Compute the temperature terms.

$$T_m - T_w \;=\; 220 - 45 \;=\; 175 \;\text{°C}$$

$$T_{ej} - T_w \;=\; 80 - 45 \;=\; 35 \;\text{°C}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the logarithmic argument.

$$\dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi (T_{ej} - T_w)} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \cdot 175}{\pi \cdot 35} \;=\; \dfrac{700}{109.956} \;\approx\; 6.366$$

$$\ln(6.366) \;\approx\; 1.851$$

**Step 3:** Compute the prefactor.

$$\dfrac{s^{2}}{\alpha \pi^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{1.2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \pi^{2}}$$

$$= \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 9.8696}$$

$$= \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.1844 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= 5.277 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $t_{cool}$.

$$t_{cool} \;=\; 5.277 \cdot 1.851 \;\approx\; \mathbf{9.77 \;\text{seconds}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A minimum cycle time of ~10 s is realistic for this thin-walled polystyrene part. Doubling the wall thickness to 5 mm would quadruple the cooling time to ~39 s — confirming the *quadratic penalty* of the $s^{2}$ dependence and the KTU design rule that *thin, uniform walls* minimize cycle time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Pipes in Markdown Tables:** Examiners marking scanned answer scripts *cannot read* `|` inside table cells. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. (This rule applies to your *typed* class notes, not the handwritten exam.)
> 2. **Confusing P_inj and P_cav:** The *injection pressure* is at the hydraulic actuator; the *cavity pressure* is inside the mold and is typically **0.3× to 0.6×** of $P_{inj}$. Mixing them up gives a clamping-force answer that is wrong by a factor of 2 to 3. This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU 2024 board scripts.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor:** A clamping-force answer without $k_s = 1.1$–$1.2$ is marked down **1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting units:** Always write **kN, MPa, mm, °C, s** explicitly. Numerical answers without units lose **0.5–1 mark** per question.
> 5. **Cycle-time answers that omit the cooling stage:** A 14-mark "working principle" question that does not explicitly *describe the cooling stage* loses **at least 2 marks**, because cooling is the dominant time-consuming stage.
> 6. **Confusing filling and packing:** Filling is *velocity-controlled*; packing is *pressure-controlled*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Working Principle = Cyclic Process:** Always describe injection molding as a **6-stage cyclic process** — *Closing → Plasticizing → Injection → Packing → Cooling → Ejection*.
- **Clamping Force Equation:** $F_c \geq k_s \cdot P_{cav,\max} \cdot A_{proj}$; remember the safety factor $k_s \approx 1.15$.
- **Cooling Time Equation:** $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\alpha \pi^{2}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{4 (T_m - T_w)}{\pi (T_{ej} - T_w)} \right)$; remember that **wall thickness has a quadratic effect**.
- **Design Linkage Matrix:** Memorize at least **three** design-stage linkages — e.g., *gate size ↔ gate freeze time ↔ packing*, *draft angle ↔ ejection*, *cooling-channel layout ↔ cycle time and warpage*.
- **Pressure Hierarchy:** $P_{hydraulic} > P_{inj} > P_{cav,\max} > P_{pack}$. Be able to write the typical numerical values for each.
- **Temperature Hierarchy:** $T_{melt} > T_{mold\;wall} > T_{ejection} > T_{ambient}$. For PP, typical values are **230 °C / 40 °C / 90 °C / 25 °C**.
- **Cycle Time Composition:** $t_c = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close}$; **cooling dominates** at 50 % – 80 %.
- **Standard Safety Factor:** Always use $k_s = 1.1$ to $1.2$ in industrial practice; KTU board answers expect $1.15$ unless specified otherwise.
- **L/D Ratio:** 18:1 to 24:1 for general thermoplastics.
- **Screw Compression Ratio:** 2:1 to 3:1 for thermoplastics.
- **Cooling Channel Diameter:** 8 mm – 12 mm.
- **Cooling Channel Contour Distance:** 1.5× to 3× the wall thickness.
- **Draft Angle:** 0.5° – 2° per side.
- **Vent Depth:** 0.02 mm – 0.05 mm.
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) — required for trapped-air compensation problems.
- **Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa — required for venting analysis.
- **The Six Stages are Invariant:** Even for *die casting, blow molding, and insert molding*, the six-stage architecture is preserved — the *working principle* generalizes across these process families.
- **Never confuse filling (velocity-controlled) with packing (pressure-controlled).** This is the single most-tested distinction in KTU 2024 Module 2.

> **Final One-Line Takeaway:** *The working principle of a manufacturing machine is the cyclic choreography of thermal, mechanical, and pressure energy that the designer must internalize before specifying a single dimension on a part drawing.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: MATERIAL SELECTION IN DESIGN

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Selection** is the systematic engineering methodology of identifying the most suitable engineering material (from metals, polymers, ceramics, composites, and hybrids) that satisfies a set of design constraints (functional, geometric, manufacturing, economic, and environmental) while optimizing one or more performance objectives for a given component or product.

In the context of **Design for Manufacture (DFM)**, material selection is the *first* and most consequential decision step that propagates downstream into process selection, tolerance specification, cost estimation, and product lifecycle behavior.

### Engineering Materials — The Big Picture

Engineering materials are broadly classified into **four primary families**:

| Family | Bonding Type | General Characteristics | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metals & Alloys** | Metallic (non-directional) | Ductile, tough, conductive | Steel, Al, Cu, Ti alloys |
| **Polymers** | Covalent + van der Waals | Lightweight, corrosion-resistant | PE, PP, Nylon, Epoxy |
| **Ceramics & Glasses** | Ionic / Covalent | Hard, brittle, high-temp | Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄, glass |
| **Composites** | Mixed | Anisotropic, tailored | CFRP, GFRP, MMC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A 14-mark question in Module 2 frequently tests the student’s ability to **derive the material index** for a given design function (e.g., a tie rod, a beam, a pressure vessel) and then **select a material using an Ashby chart**. Mastery of this is non-negotiable for ESE.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Analogy"

Think of material selection like cooking a dish:
- The **function** of the part is the *dish* you want to serve (e.g., a strong, light, cheap bicycle frame).
- The **material properties** are the *ingredients* available in your kitchen (steel, aluminum, carbon fiber, etc.).
- The **material index** is the *recipe ratio* that tells you how much of each property matters (e.g., strength-to-weight is more important than absolute strength).
- The **Ashby chart** is your *recipe book* — a visual map that tells you which ingredient is best for which recipe.

You wouldn’t use chocolate to make a sandwich, just as you wouldn’t use cast iron to make a light aerospace bracket. The Ashby methodology is the **scientific recipe book** for engineers.

### Key Design Properties (Syllabus-Standard)

The following material properties are used in DFM material selection:

- **Mechanical:** Young’s modulus ($E$), Yield strength ($\sigma_y$), Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{uts}$), Fracture toughness ($K_{IC}$), Fatigue endurance limit ($\sigma_e$), Hardness ($H$).
- **Physical:** Density ($\rho$), Thermal conductivity ($k$), Thermal expansion coefficient ($\alpha$), Electrical resistivity.
- **Manufacturing:** Formability rating, Machinability rating, Castability, Weldability.
- **Economic & Environmental:** Cost per kg ($C_m$), Recyclability, Embodied energy, CO₂ footprint.
- **Chemical:** Corrosion resistance in specific media.

> [!TIP]
> **Units Reminder (SI Standard):** Modulus $E$ → **GPa**, Strength → **MPa**, Density $\rho$ → **kg/m³** (or Mg/m³ for $E/\rho$ plots), Cost → ₹/kg or \$/kg, Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$ → **MPa·m^(1/2)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Ashby Material Selection Methodology — Step-by-Step

The **Ashby method** (developed by Prof. M.F. Ashby, Cambridge) is the gold-standard framework taught globally and is a KTU board-favorite. It proceeds in **four sequential steps**:

### Step 1: Translate the Design Requirements

Identify the **function** (what the part does), the **constraints** (geometric, kinematic, thermal, electrical), the **objective** (minimize mass, cost, volume, etc.), and the **free variables** (material choice, dimensions, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Design a **lightweight stiff beam** of specified length $L$ and stiffness $S$ → Constraints: $L$ = fixed, $S \geq S^*$ → Objective: minimize mass $m$ → Free variables: material and cross-section shape.

### Step 2: Screen Using Property Limits

Eliminate all materials that fail any of the **non-negotiable constraints** (e.g., $T_{max} \geq$ service temperature, $K_{IC} \geq K_{IC}^{*}$, $\sigma_y \geq \sigma_y^{*}$). This produces a *shortlist*.

### Step 3: Rank Using Material Indices

Combine the remaining properties into a single **material index** $M$ that, when maximized or minimized, satisfies the objective. This is done by:

1. Writing the objective as a function of material properties and free variables.
2. Eliminating the free variables using the constraints.
3. The remaining combination is the **material index**.

### Step 4: Document the Selection

Record the chosen material, its processing route, its cost, and any trade-off decisions.

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Material Indices

### Case 1: **Stiff, Light Tie** (Tensile Axial Loading)

A tie of length $L$ and stiffness $S$ must be as light as possible. The stiffness is:

$$S = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{EA}{L}$$

Since $L$ is fixed and stiffness $S^*$ is required:

$$A = \frac{S^* L}{E}$$

Mass is $m = \rho A L = \rho L \cdot A$. Substituting $A$:

$$m = \rho L \cdot \frac{S^* L}{E} = \frac{S^* L^2 \rho}{E}$$

To **minimize $m$**, we must **maximize** $E/\rho$. Thus:

$$\boxed{M_1 = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(Specific Stiffness)}}$$

### Case 2: **Strong, Light Tie** (Strength-Governed Axial Loading)

A tie must carry a tensile load $F^*$ without yielding or failing. The strength requirement is:

$$\sigma = \frac{F^*}{A} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

where $n$ is the safety factor. Thus:

$$A = \frac{n F^*}{\sigma_y}$$

Mass is $m = \rho A L$. Substituting:

$$m = \rho L \cdot \frac{n F^*}{\sigma_y} = n F^* L \cdot \frac{\rho}{\sigma_y}$$

To **minimize $m$**, we must **maximize** $\sigma_y/\rho$. Thus:

$$\boxed{M_2 = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \text{(Specific Strength)}}$$

### Case 3: **Stiff, Light Beam** (Bending Loading)

A beam of length $L$ and square cross-section $b \times b$ must have a stiffness $S = C \cdot E \cdot I$ where $I = b^4/12$ for a square section. To minimize mass $m = \rho b^2 L$:

$$\boxed{M_3 = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho} \quad \text{(Specific Stiffness for Beam)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Notice how the index **changes form** depending on the loading mode and the free geometric variable! Students often blindly write $E/\rho$ for everything — the KTU examiner deducts 2–3 marks for this.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Design Function | Constraint | Objective | Material Index $M$ | Best Material Family |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Light, stiff **tie** | Stiffness $S^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $E/\rho$ | CFRP, ceramics, Al |
| 2 | Light, strong **tie** | Load $F^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $\sigma_y/\rho$ | CFRP, high-strength Al, Ti |
| 3 | Light, stiff **beam** | Deflection $\delta^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $E^{1/2}/\rho$ | CFRP, ceramics |
| 4 | Light, strong **beam** | Yield $\sigma_y$ | Min mass $m$ | $\sigma_y^{2/3}/\rho$ | CFRP, wood, Ti |
| 5 | Cheap, stiff **tie** | Stiffness $S^*$ | Min cost $C$ | $E / (\rho \cdot C_m)$ | Steel, Al |
| 6 | **Spring** (max stored energy) | Volume $V$ | Max strain energy | $\sigma_y^2 / E$ | Spring steel, Ti |
| 7 | **Column** (Euler buckling) | Load $P^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $E^{1/2}/\rho$ | CFRP, Al |
| 8 | **Pressure vessel** (yield) | Pressure $p^*$, dia $D$ | Min mass $m$ | $\sigma_y/\rho$ | Steel, CFRP wrap |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipe vs Absolute Value:** When writing index magnitudes such as $E/\rho$, never enclose them in `|...|` (it breaks the markdown table). Use the LaTeX command `\vert E/\rho \vert` if absolute value is truly needed.

## 2.4 The Ashby Material Selection Charts

The cornerstone of Ashby’s method is the **bubble chart** plotting one material property against another on **log–log scales**. The most common charts:

- **Young’s Modulus vs Density** ($E$–$\rho$): for stiffness-driven light design.
- **Strength vs Density** ($\sigma_y$–$\rho$): for strength-driven light design.
- **Strength vs Modulus** ($\sigma_y$–$E$): for elastic design with strength limits.
- **Strength vs Cost** ($\sigma_y$–$C_m$): for economic design.
- **Fracture toughness vs Strength** ($K_{IC}$–$\sigma_y$): for damage-tolerant design.

> On an $E$–$\rho$ chart, a **straight line of slope 1** corresponds to a constant $E/\rho$ (the index for a stiff light tie). Materials **above the line** are better; materials **below the line** are worse.

### Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace brackets:** Maximize $E/\rho$ → CFRP, advanced Al–Li alloys.
- **Automotive body panels:** Maximize $\sigma_y/\rho$ → high-strength steel, Al, CFRP.
- **Pressure vessels:** Maximize $\sigma_y/\rho$ → composite overwrap (CFRP on polymer liner).
- **Springs (e.g., vehicle suspensions):** Maximize $\sigma_y^2/E$ → spring steel, Ti alloys.
- **Heat exchangers:** Maximize $k/C_m\rho$ (thermal index) → Cu, Al.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Index for a Light, Stiff Tie

> **Problem Statement:** A tie of length $L = 1.0\,\text{m}$ must support a tensile load $F^* = 50\,\text{kN}$ with a stiffness $S^* \geq 200\,\text{MN/m}$ and a factor of safety $n = 2$. Determine the optimal material index, then select a material from the Ashby chart for a **lightweight** tie.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express Stiffness Constraint:**

The axial stiffness of a uniform tie is:

$$S = \frac{EA}{L}$$

The constraint $S \geq S^*$ becomes:

$$\frac{EA}{L} \geq S^* \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \geq \frac{S^* L}{E}$$

So the *minimum* cross-sectional area required is:

$$A = \frac{S^* L}{E}$$

**Step 2 — Express Mass (Objective):**

Mass of the tie is $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$. Substituting the minimum $A$:

$$m = \rho \cdot \frac{S^* L}{E} \cdot L = \frac{S^* L^2 \cdot \rho}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Free Variable and the Objective:**

$L$ and $S^*$ are fixed by design. The *only* material-dependent grouping is $\rho/E$. To minimize $m$, we must minimize $\rho/E$, which is equivalent to **maximizing** $E/\rho$.

**Step 4 — The Material Index:**

$$\boxed{M_1 = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{[units: MJ/kg or GPa·m³/kg]}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation:**

The $E/\rho$ values for common engineering materials are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Structural Steel} &: \frac{200\,\text{GPa}}{7850\,\text{kg/m}^3} \approx 25.5\,\text{MJ/kg} \\
\text{Aluminum 2024-T3} &: \frac{73\,\text{GPa}}{2780\,\text{kg/m}^3} \approx 26.3\,\text{MJ/kg} \\
\text{Titanium Ti-6Al-4V} &: \frac{114\,\text{GPa}}{4430\,\text{kg/m}^3} \approx 25.7\,\text{MJ/kg} \\
\text{CFRP (UD, fiber-aligned)} &: \frac{135\,\text{GPa}}{1600\,\text{kg/m}^3} \approx 84.4\,\text{MJ/kg} \\
\text{GFRP} &: \frac{38\,\text{GPa}}{1900\,\text{kg/m}^3} \approx 20.0\,\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

CFRP has the **highest $E/\rho$** — a 3× advantage over metals. For the *lightweight* requirement, **CFRP is optimal**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Index for a Light, Strong Beam (Bending)

> **Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ and rectangular cross-section $b \times h$ (with $b$ fixed at 50 mm and $h$ free) must support an end load $F^*$ without exceeding yield. Find the material index for **minimum mass**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Strength Constraint (Bending Stress):**

The maximum bending stress in a cantilever with end load $F$ is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{b h^2}$$

The yield constraint $\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_y / n$ gives:

$$h^2 \geq \frac{6 n F L}{b \sigma_y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h \geq \left(\frac{6 n F L}{b \sigma_y}\right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Mass (Objective):**

The mass of the beam is $m = \rho \cdot b \cdot h \cdot L$. Substituting the minimum $h$:

$$m = \rho \cdot b \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{6 n F L}{b \sigma_y}\right)^{1/2}$$

Simplifying by expanding the square root:

$$m = \rho \cdot b^{1/2} \cdot L^{3/2} \cdot \left(\frac{6 n F}{\sigma_y}\right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Material Index:**

Pulling out the material-dependent terms:

$$m \propto \left(\frac{\rho^{2}}{\sigma_y}\right)^{1/2} = \frac{\rho}{\sigma_y^{1/2}}$$

To **minimize $m$**, we must **minimize** $\rho / \sigma_y^{1/2}$, which is the same as **maximizing**:

$$\boxed{M_{beam} = \frac{\sigma_y^{1/2}}{\rho} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho} \text{ for plate beams}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Different textbooks index this differently (e.g., $\sigma_y^{1/2}/\rho$ vs $\sigma_y^{2/3}/\rho$) depending on whether the section is *rectangular with one free dim* or *square with both free dims*. The KTU board usually expects the *version consistent with the lecture derivation*. Always state the assumption!

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Multiple Constraints and Coupled Indices

In practice, designs have **multiple constraints** simultaneously. For example: stiffness $S^*$, strength $F^*$, and a maximum temperature $T_{max}$.

**Procedure:**

1. Treat each constraint independently and write it as a property bound (e.g., $E \geq E^*$, $\sigma_y \geq \sigma_y^*$, $T_{max,material} \geq T_{max,service}$).
2. Use these to **screen** the candidate set.
3. Then apply the single material index on the **remaining** shortlist.

For two simultaneous constraints of stiffness and strength with minimum mass:

$$m = S^* L^2 \cdot \frac{\rho}{E} \quad \text{(stiffness)} \quad \text{AND} \quad m = n F^* L \cdot \frac{\rho}{\sigma_y} \quad \text{(strength)}$$

The *binding* constraint is the one that requires the **larger cross-section**, hence the **larger mass**. The final index is the **maximum** of the two:

$$M = \max\left(\frac{E}{\rho}, \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}\right)$$

## 3.4 The Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) for Conflicting Objectives

When multiple **objectives** conflict (e.g., *minimize mass* AND *minimize cost* AND *maximize recyclability*), use a **weighted material index**:

$$M_{weighted} = \prod_i M_i^{w_i}$$

where $w_i$ are weights determined by stakeholder importance, and $M_i$ are individual normalized indices. The AHP (Saaty, 1980) provides a systematic way to assign $w_i$ via pairwise comparison matrices.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

> Define a **material index** with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *material index* is a combination of material properties (often a ratio or product) that characterizes the performance of a material in a specific design function. It is obtained by expressing the design objective in terms of material properties and free variables, then eliminating the free variables using the design constraints.

**Example:** For a *light, stiff tie*, the derived material index is $M = E/\rho$ (specific stiffness). Maximizing $M$ minimizes the tie's mass for a given stiffness. Materials such as CFRP and aluminum have high $E/\rho$ values, making them optimal choices.

*(Mark split: Definition 1m, Index derivation logic 1m, Example 1m.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> List the **four primary families of engineering materials** and give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Metals & Alloys** — e.g., low-carbon steel (widely used for structural components).
2. **Polymers** — e.g., Nylon-6,6 (used for gears, bearings).
3. **Ceramics & Glasses** — e.g., Alumina Al₂O₃ (used for cutting tool inserts, insulators).
4. **Composites** — e.g., CFRP carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer (used in aerospace panels).

*(Mark split: 0.5m per family name + 0.25m per example.)*
