## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

> **(a)** Derive the material index for a **lightweight, stiff beam** of square cross-section where the beam length $L$ and deflection $\delta^*$ under a fixed tip load $F$ are the constraints. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a **pressure vessel** of diameter $D$ and internal pressure $p^*$, derive the material index that minimizes the vessel mass. State the index and identify which material family typically performs best on an Ashby chart. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Lightweight, Stiff Beam (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define stiffness in terms of $E$, $L$, and section size:**

For a cantilever beam of square cross-section $b \times b$, the tip deflection under load $F$ is:

$$\delta = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b^4}$$

The constraint $\delta \leq \delta^*$ gives:

$$b^4 \geq \frac{4 F L^3}{E \delta^*} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = \left(\frac{4 F L^3}{E \delta^*}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 2 — Express mass:**

The mass is $m = \rho b^2 L$. Substituting $b$:

$$m = \rho L \cdot \left(\frac{4 F L^3}{E \delta^*}\right)^{1/2} = \rho L \cdot \frac{2 \sqrt{F L^3}}{\sqrt{E \delta^*}}$$

Simplifying:

$$m = \frac{2 \rho L^{5/2} \sqrt{F}}{\sqrt{E \delta^*}} = 2 L^{5/2} \sqrt{\frac{F}{\delta^*}} \cdot \frac{\rho}{E^{1/2}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the material index:**

The material-dependent factor is $\rho / E^{1/2}$. To **minimize mass**, we **minimize** $\rho / E^{1/2}$ = **maximize** $E^{1/2}/\rho$:

$$\boxed{M_{stiff\,beam} = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- The index combines stiffness and density non-linearly.
- On an $E$–$\rho$ Ashby chart, lines of constant $E^{1/2}/\rho$ have slope **2** (since $\log E = 2 \log \rho + \text{const}$).
- Best materials: **CFRP, engineering ceramics, wood** (all high $E^{1/2}/\rho$).

*(Mark split: Expression for deflection 1.5m, b substitution 1.5m, mass derivation 2m, index identification 1.5m, chart interpretation 0.5m.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Pressure Vessel Material Index (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Hoop stress for a thin-walled cylinder:**

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p^* D}{2 t}$$

The yield constraint $\sigma_{hoop} \leq \sigma_y / n$ gives:

$$t = \frac{n p^* D}{2 \sigma_y}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of cylindrical shell (length $L$):**

$$m = \rho \cdot \pi D t \cdot L = \rho \pi D L \cdot \frac{n p^* D}{2 \sigma_y} = \frac{\pi n p^* D^2 L}{2} \cdot \frac{\rho}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 3 — Material index:**

To **minimize mass**, maximize:

$$\boxed{M_{vessel} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Best material family (Ashby chart interpretation):**

On a $\sigma_y$–$\rho$ log-log Ashby chart, the line of constant $\sigma_y/\rho$ has slope **1**. Materials **above** the line are better.

- **Best:** **CFRP** (specific strength ~200–250 kNm/kg), followed by **Ti alloys**, **high-strength Al**, then steels.
- For **mass-critical** applications (rocket tanks, aircraft): CFRP.
- For **cost-critical** (water pipes, LPG cylinders): **steel** still dominates.
- A **hybrid solution** is common: thin steel liner + CFRP overwrap (used in SpaceX and Boeing).

*(Mark split: Hoop stress 1.5m, thickness derivation 1.5m, mass derivation 2m, index identification 1m, material family selection 1m.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** Differentiate between **screening** and **ranking** as used in the Ashby material selection process. Why is each step necessary? Provide a numerical example of a screening criterion. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two materials are being compared for a **lightweight spring** that must store maximum elastic strain energy per unit volume:
> - **Material X:** $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\sigma_y = 1500\,\text{MPa}$, $\rho = 7850\,\text{kg/m}^3$, cost = ₹60/kg.
> - **Material Y:** $E = 110\,\text{GPa}$, $\sigma_y = 1000\,\text{MPa}$, $\rho = 4500\,\text{kg/m}^3$, cost = ₹1500/kg.
>
> Determine the appropriate material index and recommend a material based on:
> (i) mass minimization, and
> (ii) cost minimization for the same strain energy storage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Screening vs Ranking (7 marks)**

**Screening** is the first pass that **eliminates** materials failing any of the *hard, non-negotiable* design constraints. It uses **property limits** (e.g., $T_{max,material} \geq T_{service}$, $K_{IC} \geq K_{IC}^*$, $E \geq E^*$).

**Ranking** is the second pass that **orders** the surviving shortlist using a **material index** — a single performance metric that captures the *objective* (min mass, cost, etc.).

**Why both are necessary:**
- Screening alone leaves a *list* with no priority.
- Ranking alone may be misleading if a high-index material *fails* a critical constraint.
- Together, they narrow the field to a few candidates, then pick the *best* among them.

**Numerical example of a screening criterion:**

For a pressure vessel in marine service, the design mandates $K_{IC} \geq 50\,\text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$. Checking the database:
- Mild steel: $K_{IC} \approx 50\,\text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$ → borderline.
- Al 2024-T3: $K_{IC} \approx 30\,\text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$ → **eliminated**.
- Maraging steel: $K_{IC} \approx 80\,\text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$ → **passes**.

Hence mild and maraging steel are shortlisted; Al 2024 is screened out.

*(Mark split: Screening definition 1.5m, Ranking definition 1.5m, Why-both 2m, Numerical example 2m.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Spring Material Selection (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — The relevant material index:**

Elastic strain energy per unit volume is:

$$U_v = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

To *maximize* $U_v$, we maximize $\sigma_y^2 / E$. For the same stored energy per unit mass (mass-normalized), we use:

$$M_{spring} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{E \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 2 — Compute for Material X (Spring Steel):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_y^2}{E} &= \frac{(1500 \times 10^6)^2}{200 \times 10^9} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{11}} = 11.25 \times 10^{3}\,\text{J/m}^3 \\
\frac{\sigma_y^2}{E \rho} &= \frac{11.25 \times 10^3}{7850} = 1.433\,\text{J/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute for Material Y (Ti-6Al-4V):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_y^2}{E} &= \frac{(1000 \times 10^6)^2}{110 \times 10^9} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{15}}{1.1 \times 10^{11}} = 9.09 \times 10^{3}\,\text{J/m}^3 \\
\frac{\sigma_y^2}{E \rho} &= \frac{9.09 \times 10^3}{4500} = 2.020\,\text{J/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

| Criterion | Material X (Steel) | Material Y (Ti) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Minimize mass** ($M = \sigma_y^2 / E\rho$) | 1.433 J/kg | **2.020 J/kg** | **Ti-6Al-4V** |
| (ii) **Minimize cost** | 60 ₹/kg | 1500 ₹/kg | **Steel** |

**Recommendation:**
- For **aerospace / racing** (mass-critical): choose **Material Y – Ti-6Al-4V**.
- For **automotive suspension / general machinery** (cost-critical): choose **Material X – Spring Steel**.

*(Mark split: Index formula 2m, X calculation 1m, Y calculation 1m, Mass-based decision 1.5m, Cost-based decision 1.5m.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Skipping units in the final answer.** A bare index number without units costs 1 mark. Always write $\text{GPa·m}^3/\text{kg}$ or $\text{MJ/kg}$ (specific stiffness) and $\text{kN·m/kg}$ (specific strength).
> 2. **Wrong power on $E$ or $\sigma_y$.** A beam with one free dimension gives $E^{1/2}/\rho$; a beam with two free dimensions gives $E^{1/3}/\rho$. Mixing them up is the most common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting safety factor $n$.** Most KTU problems embed $n$ in the constraint; failing to include it gives an index that is off by a factor.
> 4. **Ignoring the Ashby chart slope convention.** The line of constant $E^{1/2}/\rho$ on a log-log $E$–$\rho$ plot has slope 2, not 1. State this explicitly to gain 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the "free variable" assumption.** For a beam, is the section rectangular with width fixed? Is it a thin tube? The answer to *that* question determines the index.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Material**

- [ ] Engineering materials are classified into **4 families**: Metals, Polymers, Ceramics, Composites.
- [ ] The **Ashby method** has 4 steps: Translate → Screen → Rank → Document.
- [ ] A **material index** is derived by writing the objective in terms of material properties and *eliminating free geometric variables* using the constraints.
- [ ] **Stiff, light tie** → $M = E/\rho$ (specific stiffness).
- [ ] **Strong, light tie** → $M = \sigma_y/\rho$ (specific strength).
- [ ] **Stiff, light beam** (1 free dim) → $M = E^{1/2}/\rho$.
- [ ] **Strong, light beam** → $M = \sigma_y^{2/3}/\rho$ (or $\sigma_y^{1/2}/\rho$ — depends on the section).
- [ ] **Spring (max strain energy)** → $M = \sigma_y^2/E$ per volume; $\sigma_y^2/(E\rho)$ per mass.
- [ ] **Pressure vessel** → $M = \sigma_y/\rho$ (from hoop-stress derivation).
- [ ] **Column (Euler buckling)** → $M = E^{1/2}/\rho$.
- [ ] **Screening** = eliminate by hard property limits (binary: pass/fail).
- [ ] **Ranking** = order survivors by a single material index.
- [ ] On a **log-log Ashby chart**, a constant-$M$ line has slope = exponent of the *denominator property* on the x-axis.
- [ ] **Units Convention:** $E$ in **GPa**, $\sigma_y$ in **MPa**, $\rho$ in **kg/m³** (or Mg/m³). $M$ comes out in **MJ/kg** or **kNm/kg**.
- [ ] **Best materials** (typically):
  - $E/\rho$ → CFRP, ceramics, Al
  - $\sigma_y/\rho$ → CFRP, Ti, maraging steel, Al-Li
  - $\sigma_y^2/E$ → spring steel, Ti
  - $K_{IC}/\sigma_y$ (damage tolerant) → Ti, maraging steel, Al alloys
- [ ] **Multiple constraints** → screen first, *then* rank on a single index.
- [ ] **Multiple objectives** → use weighted indices or the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP).
- [ ] The **final selection** must also account for processability (castability, machinability, weldability), cost, availability, and environmental impact — not just the index.
- [ ] **KTU favourite 14-mark format:** (a) Derive index [7m], (b) Apply on Ashby chart + recommend material + manufacturing process link [7m].

---

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — MATERIAL SELECTION**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Manufacture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Manufacturing** is the process of converting raw materials into finished products through the application of physical, chemical, electrical, or mechanical processes. In the context of **Design for Manufacture (DFM)**, it refers to the engineering discipline of designing parts, components, and products in a manner that optimizes their manufacturability — minimizing complexity, cost, and time of production while maximizing quality and ease of fabrication.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Module 2, the topic *Manufacture* covers the principles of **Bulk Metal Forming Processes** — namely **Forging, Extrusion, Wire Drawing, and Rolling** — and the engineering methods used to design tooling (dies) and select process parameters that ensure defect-free, economical production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology (2024 Scheme):**
> - **Forming** – A manufacturing process where the shape of a metal is changed by plastic deformation without altering its mass.
> - **Bulk Deformation** – Significant shape change characterized by high strain magnitudes (ε > 1.0).
> - **Flow Stress (Yf or σf)** – The instantaneous stress required to continue plastic deformation at a given strain, strain rate, and temperature.
> - **True Strain (ε)** – The natural logarithmic measure of deformation: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a piece of **clay or dough** in your hands.

- If you **press it down with your palm** on a table, you are essentially performing **Forging** — applying compressive force to change the shape.
- If you **push the dough through a small hole** (like squeezing toothpaste out of a tube), you are performing **Extrusion** — forcing material through a die.
- If you **pull a piece of clay through a smaller hole** to make a thin string, you are performing **Drawing**.

All these processes **do not remove material** (unlike machining). They simply **rearrange** the existing material into a new geometry by **plastic flow**. This is the essence of bulk metal forming — and the same physics govern the production of car crankshafts (forging), aluminium window frames (extrusion), and copper electrical wires (drawing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:** Bulk forming processes account for nearly **45% of all industrial forging and 70% of non-ferrous metal products** worldwide. The screws holding your smartphone, the aluminium foil in your kitchen, and the chassis of your car — all originate from forming processes.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following standard units and constants govern the analysis of bulk metal forming:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Flow Stress | $\sigma_f$ | MPa or N/mm² | Stress required to maintain plastic flow |
| True Stress | $\sigma$ | MPa | Instantaneous load per instantaneous area |
| True Strain | $\varepsilon$ | Dimensionless | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ |
| Strain Rate | $\dot{\varepsilon}$ | s⁻¹ | Rate of deformation |
| Strength Coefficient | $K$ | MPa | Material constant in Hollomon equation |
| Strain Hardening Exponent | $n$ | Dimensionless (0–0.5) | Material constant |
| Friction Factor | $m$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Interface friction parameter |
| Coefficient of Friction | $\mu$ | Dimensionless (0–0.5) | Sliding friction |
| Shear Yield Stress | $k$ | MPa | $k = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{3}$ for von Mises criterion |
| Ideal Work | $W_i$ | J or N·m | Work for homogeneous deformation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants to Remember:**
> - For **Mild Steel**: $K \approx 530$ MPa, $n \approx 0.26$
> - For **Aluminium 1100-O**: $K \approx 180$ MPa, $n \approx 0.20$
> - For **Brass (70-30)**: $K \approx 895$ MPa, $n \approx 0.33$
> - Density of Steel: **7850 kg/m³**
> - Density of Aluminium: **2700 kg/m³**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Concepts of Plastic Deformation

When a metal is subjected to a stress exceeding its **yield stress**, it undergoes **plastic deformation** permanently. The resistance offered by the metal to this deformation is called the **flow stress (Yf)**.

The flow stress is not constant — it increases as the metal is deformed (strain hardening). This relationship is mathematically captured by the **Hollomon Equation**:

$$\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

Where:
- $K$ = Strength coefficient (MPa)
- $\varepsilon$ = True strain
- $n$ = Strain hardening exponent

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Strain Hardening

When a metal is deformed plastically, **dislocations** move and multiply within the crystal lattice. These dislocations obstruct each other's motion, requiring progressively higher stress to continue deformation. This is why a metal becomes **stronger but less ductile** as it is worked.

## 2.2 Theory of Forging

**Forging** is a manufacturing process where compressive forces are applied to a billet (workpiece) to deform it plastically into the desired shape, typically using dies.

### 2.2.1 Types of Forging

1. **Open-Die Forging (Open Die Forging)**: Material is compressed between flat or simple-shaped dies. Used for large workpieces like turbine shafts.
2. **Impression-Die (Closed-Die) Forging**: Material is forced into a die cavity that contains the negative of the desired shape.
3. **Flashless Forging**: No flash is allowed; requires precise volume control.
4. **Cold Forging**: Performed at room temperature (improves dimensional accuracy and surface finish).
5. **Hot Forging**: Performed above recrystallization temperature (reduces flow stress, enables larger deformations).

### 2.2.2 Forging Force and Pressure

The **average forging pressure** for a cylindrical billet compressed between flat dies (with friction) is given by:

$$p = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{2 \cdot \mu \cdot R}{3 \cdot h}\right)$$

Where:
- $\sigma_f$ = Average flow stress (MPa)
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction
- $R$ = Initial radius of billet
- $h$ = Instantaneous height of billet

The **total forging force** is:

$$F = p \cdot A = p \cdot \pi \cdot R^2$$

### 2.2.3 Work Done in Forging (Upsetting)

The ideal work (no friction) for upsetting a cylinder is:

$$W_i = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma_f \cdot V \cdot d\varepsilon = V \cdot \sigma_{f0} \cdot \varepsilon_f$$

Where $V$ is the volume of the billet (assumed constant: $V = \pi R_0^2 h_0 = \pi R_f^2 h_f$).

## 2.3 Theory of Extrusion

**Extrusion** is a forming process in which a billet is forced through a die opening to produce a continuous product of uniform cross-section.

### 2.3.1 Types of Extrusion

1. **Direct (Forward) Extrusion**: Ram pushes the billet through the die in the same direction.
2. **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion**: Die is mounted on the ram and moves into the stationary billet — reduces friction.
3. **Hydrostatic Extrusion**: Billet is surrounded by a pressurized fluid — eliminates container friction.
4. **Impact Extrusion**: Sudden impact (cold forming of toothpaste tubes, battery cases).

### 2.3.2 Extrusion Force (Direct Extrusion with Friction)

$$F = A_0 \cdot \sigma_f \left[ \ln(R) + \frac{2 \cdot L}{D} \right]$$

For **Indirect Extrusion** (no friction with container):

$$F = A_0 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R)$$

Where:
- $A_0$ = Original cross-sectional area of billet
- $\sigma_f$ = Average flow stress of material
- $R$ = Extrusion ratio = $A_0 / A_f$
- $L$ = Length of billet remaining in container
- $D$ = Diameter of container

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Direct extrusion requires up to **30% more force** than indirect extrusion due to friction at the billet-container interface. However, direct extrusion is mechanically simpler.

## 2.4 Theory of Wire Drawing

**Drawing** is a process where a metal rod or wire is pulled through a tapered die to reduce its cross-section.

### 2.4.1 Drawing Stress (with Friction)

$$\sigma_d = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot L_d}{r}\right) \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

Or, more compactly, using **die semi-angle** $\alpha$:

$$\sigma_d = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha}\right) \cdot \ln(R)$$

Where:
- $\sigma_f$ = Average flow stress
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction at die-workpiece interface
- $L_d$ = Length of contact in die
- $r$ = Average radius of wire being drawn
- $R$ = Draw ratio = $A_0 / A_f$

### 2.4.2 Maximum Reduction per Pass

The maximum allowable reduction per pass is limited by **chuck strength**. The condition for successful drawing is:

$$\sigma_d \leq \sigma_{\text{chuck}}$$

Typically, the area reduction per pass is limited to **20–30%** for steel and up to **45%** for aluminium.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Process | Key Formula | Variables | Typical Use |
|---------|------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Hollomon Equation** | $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$ | $K$ (MPa), $n$ (dimensionless) | Flow stress vs. strain |
| **True Strain** | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0 / h_f)$ | $h_0, h_f$ (heights) | All forming processes |
| **Strain Rate** | $\dot{\varepsilon} = v / h$ | $v$ (ram speed), $h$ (height) | Forging |
| **Forging Pressure** | $p = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{2\mu R}{3h}\right)$ | $\mu, R, h$ | Upsetting |
| **Forging Force** | $F = p \cdot \pi R^2$ | $R$ (radius) | Total load |
| **Ideal Forging Work** | $W_i = V \cdot \sigma_{f0} \cdot \varepsilon_f$ | $V$ (volume) | Energy for homogeneous deformation |
| **Direct Extrusion Force** | $F = A_0 \sigma_f \left[\ln R + \frac{2L}{D}\right]$ | $L, D$ (billet) | Forward extrusion |
| **Indirect Extrusion Force** | $F = A_0 \sigma_f \ln R$ | — | Backward extrusion |
| **Drawing Stress** | $\sigma_d = \sigma_f (1 + \mu/\tan\alpha) \ln R$ | $\alpha$ (die semi-angle) | Wire drawing |
| **Extrusion Ratio** | $R = A_0 / A_f$ | — | Reduction measure |
| **Max Draw Stress** | $\sigma_d \leq 0.88 \cdot \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$ | — | Safety condition |
| **Power Requirement** | $P = F \cdot v$ | $F$ (force), $v$ (velocity) | Press capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in industry to size **hydraulic presses, hammers, and rolls**, design **dies and tooling**, estimate **energy consumption**, and predict **defects** (cracking, folding, central burst). For example, the automotive industry uses extrusion force calculations to specify **aluminium extrusion press capacities ranging from 1,000 to 5,000 tonnes** for structural components.

## 2.6 Slab Analysis Method (Upper-Bound vs. Slab Method)

In the **Slab Method of Analysis**, a small slab of material is isolated and equilibrium equations are applied considering the surrounding material as a rigid body that exerts normal and frictional stresses. This is the most common method taught in KTU Module 2 for deriving forging and extrusion pressures.

**Assumptions of Slab Method:**
1. Material is isotropic and homogeneous.
2. Plane strain conditions prevail at the die-workpiece interface.
3. Friction follows Coulomb's law: $\tau = \mu \cdot \sigma_n$.
4. Homogeneous deformation throughout the workpiece.

The slab method gives **lower-bound solutions** to the forming force, while the **upper-bound method** (using velocity fields and energy dissipation) gives **upper-bound solutions**. The true forming force lies between these two bounds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Flow Curve under Cold Working (Hollomon Equation)

### Problem Statement
Derive the relationship between true stress and true strain for a material obeying the power-law hardening model.

### Given
- Initial dimensions: $h_0, A_0$
- Final dimensions: $h_f, A_f$
- Volume constancy: $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define true strain.

The true strain is defined as the differential change in length divided by the instantaneous length. For compression (decreasing height):

$$\varepsilon = \int_{h_0}^{h_f} \frac{dh}{h} = \ln\left(\frac{h_f}{h_0}\right)$$

Since $h_f < h_0$ in compression, the true strain is **negative** when computed directly. By convention, we use absolute values:

$$\varepsilon = \left|\ln\left(\frac{h_f}{h_0}\right)\right| = \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Express true stress.

True stress is the load divided by the instantaneous area:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_f}$$

**Step 3:** Apply volume constancy.

By the law of conservation of mass (incompressible plastic flow):

$$A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_f = A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into true stress expression.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{h_f}{h_0} = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp(-\varepsilon)$$

where $\sigma_0 = F/A_0$ is the engineering stress.

**Step 5:** Apply the power-law hardening model.

The Hollomon equation states that the flow stress is related to true strain by:

$$\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

**Step 6:** Combine equations.

The **engineering stress** corresponding to this true stress is:

$$\sigma_{eng} = K \cdot \varepsilon^n \cdot \exp(-\varepsilon)$$

This shows that engineering stress first increases (due to strain hardening) and then decreases (due to area reduction), producing a **maximum value** at some intermediate strain. The strain at which necking begins is:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{neck}} = n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The strain hardening exponent $n$ tells us the maximum uniform strain a material can undergo before necking. For steel, $n \approx 0.26$, meaning necking begins at a true strain of 0.26 (about 30% reduction in area).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Forging Force in Upsetting with Friction

### Problem Statement
A cylindrical billet of radius $R$ and height $h$ is compressed between two flat dies. Derive the forging pressure and total force, accounting for friction at the die-workpiece interface.

### Given
- Initial radius: $R_0$, initial height: $h_0$
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu$ (constant)
- Material flow stress: $\sigma_f$ (constant or average)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider an elementary slab.

Consider a small element at radius $r$ from the centre of the billet. This element is acted upon by:
- Vertical stress $\sigma_z$ on the bottom
- Vertical stress $\sigma_z + d\sigma_z$ on the top
- Radial stress $\sigma_r$ on the curved surface
- Frictional shear stress $\tau = \mu \cdot \sigma_z$ opposing the outward flow on the top and bottom surfaces.

**Step 2:** Apply equilibrium in the vertical direction.

The element has volume $2\pi r \, dr \, dz$ and the differential equilibrium equation is:

$$\frac{d\sigma_z}{dz} + \frac{2 \cdot \mu \cdot \sigma_z}{z} = 0 \quad \text{(simplified for thin element)}$$

**Step 3:** Solve the differential equation.

Separating variables and integrating from $z = 0$ (mid-plane) to $z = h/2$ (top surface):

$$\int_{\sigma_z}^{\sigma_f} \frac{d\sigma_z}{\sigma_z} = -\int_0^{h/2} \frac{2\mu}{z} dz$$

Wait — for compression in forging, friction is on the die faces. The standard solution uses the **elementary friction hill** approach. Let me use a cleaner slab equilibrium:

**Step 3 (Corrected):** Friction Hill Equation.

For a thin disk element at radius $r$, with friction acting on both top and bottom die faces, the equilibrium in the radial direction gives:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = -\frac{2 \tau}{h} = -\frac{2 \mu \sigma_z}{h}$$

Using the **Tresca yield criterion** (plane strain, von Mises equivalent): $\sigma_z - \sigma_r = \sigma_f$ (where $\sigma_f = 2k$ in shear terms, but here expressed as the uniaxial flow stress for upsetting).

**Step 4:** Substitute and separate.

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = \frac{2\mu}{h}(\sigma_f + \sigma_r)$$

Wait — let me re-state carefully. The pressure at the centre equals the die pressure $p$. As we move radially outward, the radial compressive stress increases due to friction, which is why the **friction hill** forms.

Let $q = \sigma_r + \sigma_f$. Then $\frac{dq}{dr} = \frac{2\mu q}{h}$.

Integrating from centre ($r=0$, $\sigma_r = p - \sigma_f$) to the edge ($r = R$, $\sigma_r = 0$ for free surface):

$$q(R) = q(0) \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right)$$

$$0 + \sigma_f = (p - \sigma_f) \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the die pressure $p$.

$$p = \sigma_f \left[1 + \exp\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right)\right]$$

For small values of $\frac{2\mu R}{h}$ (i.e., thin billets or low friction), we can linearize the exponential:

$$\exp\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right) \approx 1 + \frac{2\mu R}{h} + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right)^2 + \ldots$$

To first order:

$$p \approx \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{2\mu R}{h}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Write the total forging force.

The total force is the average pressure (weighted by area) over the entire disc. For a constant pressure approximation:

$$F = p \cdot \pi R^2 = \pi R^2 \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{2\mu R}{3h}\right)$$

Note: The factor changes to $\frac{2\mu R}{3h}$ in the **average pressure** integration over the disc, since pressure is highest at the centre.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** This equation is the famous **"Friction Hill"** derived by Siebel and Sachs (1931). The factor $\frac{2\mu R}{3h}$ is called the **friction hill factor**. As the billet gets thinner ($h$ decreases), the friction contribution increases dramatically — which is why forging presses are sized for the **largest possible friction hill**, not just the ideal flow stress.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Extrusion Force (Direct Extrusion)

### Problem Statement
A cylindrical billet of diameter $D$ and length $L$ is extruded through a die to produce a product of area $A_f$. The extrusion ratio is $R = A_0/A_f$. Derive the ram force required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the deformation zone.

The deformation is concentrated in a small conical zone near the die entrance. We approximate the work done as homogeneous deformation through this zone.

**Step 2:** Express the ideal work (homogeneous deformation).

For an incompressible material, the volume strain energy per unit volume equals the area under the true stress-strain curve:

$$w_i = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma_f \, d\varepsilon = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K \varepsilon^n \, d\varepsilon = \frac{K \varepsilon_f^{n+1}}{n+1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the strain in extrusion.

The true strain experienced by the material is:

$$\varepsilon_f = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln(R)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ideal ram force (for frictionless extrusion).

If the work done by the ram equals the ideal work (no friction, no redundant work):

$$F_i \cdot L = w_i \cdot V_{\text{deformed}}$$

But for a continuously deforming process, the instantaneous ram force on a billet of cross-section $A_0$ extruding through a die to $A_f$ is:

$$F_i = A_0 \cdot \sigma_{f,\text{avg}} \cdot \ln(R)$$

Where $\sigma_{f,\text{avg}}$ is the **average flow stress** integrated over the strain path:

$$\sigma_{f,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f}\int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K\varepsilon^n d\varepsilon = \frac{K \varepsilon_f^n}{n+1}$$

**Step 5:** Add the friction term (direct extrusion).

In **direct (forward) extrusion**, the billet slides along the container walls. The friction force is:

$$F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot p_{\text{avg}} \cdot \pi D \cdot L = \mu \cdot \frac{\sigma_{f,\text{avg}}}{2} \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

Wait — the container wall pressure is approximately $\sigma_{f,\text{avg}}$ (the billet is being pushed against the wall), so the friction force per unit length is $\mu \sigma_{f,\text{avg}} \pi D$.

**Step 6:** Total extrusion force (Direct).

Combining ideal and friction forces, and using $A_0 = \pi D^2/4$:

$$F = A_0 \cdot \sigma_{f,\text{avg}} \left[\ln(R) + \frac{2L}{D}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The term $\frac{2L}{D}$ is dimensionless and represents the **friction contribution**. For a long billet, this term dominates the total force, which is why **indirect extrusion** (no container friction) is preferred for high extrusion ratios.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Reduction in Wire Drawing

### Problem Statement
Determine the maximum possible reduction per pass in wire drawing, given that the drawing stress cannot exceed the ultimate tensile strength of the wire (otherwise it will break in the die).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Condition for safe drawing.

The drawing stress must be less than the **chuck stress** (the stress that the wire can withstand after exiting the die):

$$\sigma_d \leq \sigma_{\text{chuck}}$$

For a safety factor, we use:

$$\sigma_d \leq 0.88 \cdot \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$$

**Step 2:** Use the drawing stress equation (frictionless, for simplicity).

$$\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Define reduction $r$ (area reduction).

$$r = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{R}$$

So $R = \frac{1}{1-r}$ and $\ln(R) = -\ln(1-r)$.

**Step 4:** Apply yield criterion.

For a perfectly plastic material (no strain hardening): $\sigma_f = \sigma_y$.

$$\sigma_y \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-r}\right) \leq 0.88 \cdot \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for maximum reduction $r_{\text{max}}$.

If we assume $\sigma_y \approx \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$ for a perfectly plastic approximation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{1-r_{\text{max}}}\right) \leq 0.88$$

$$\frac{1}{1-r_{\text{max}}} \leq e^{0.88} \approx 2.41$$

$$1 - r_{\text{max}} \geq \frac{1}{2.41} \approx 0.415$$

$$r_{\text{max}} \leq 0.585 = 58.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Result:** The **theoretical maximum reduction** in a single draw pass is about **63%** (when $\sigma_d = \sigma_f$). In practice, friction and strain hardening reduce this to about **20–35%** for steel and **40–45%** for aluminium alloys per pass.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A cylindrical billet of **mild steel** (initial diameter $D_0 = 50$ mm, height $h_0 = 100$ mm) is upset in an open-die forging operation to a final height $h_f = 50$ mm. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.2$. The flow curve is given by $\sigma_f = 530 \varepsilon^{0.26}$ MPa. Calculate:
1. The **true strain**.
2. The **average flow stress**.
3. The **maximum forging force** at the final stage.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute true strain.

$$\varepsilon_f = \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{100}{50}\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$

**Step 2:** Compute the average flow stress.

$$\sigma_{f,\text{avg}} = \frac{K \varepsilon_f^n}{n+1} = \frac{530 \cdot (0.693)^{0.26}}{1.26}$$

Compute $(0.693)^{0.26}$:
- $\ln(0.693) \approx -0.367$
- $0.26 \times (-0.367) = -0.0954$
- $e^{-0.0954} \approx 0.909$

So:
$$\sigma_{f,\text{avg}} = \frac{530 \cdot 0.909}{1.26} = \frac{481.8}{1.26} \approx 382.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the final radius (volume constancy).

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Since $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$:

$$A_f = A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_f} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2 = 3.927 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Final radius:
$$R_f = \sqrt{\frac{A_f}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.927 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{1.250 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0354 \text{ m} = 35.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Compute maximum forging force.

The maximum force occurs at the **final stage** (largest $R/h$ ratio, hence largest friction hill).

$$\frac{2\mu R_f}{3h_f} = \frac{2 \times 0.2 \times 35.4}{3 \times 50} = \frac{14.16}{150} = 0.0944$$

$$p = \sigma_{f,\text{avg}} \left(1 + 0.0944\right) = 382.4 \times 1.0944 \approx 418.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$F = p \cdot A_f = 418.5 \times 10^6 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-3} = 1.643 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{1643 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** The maximum forging force required is approximately **1.64 MN (164 tonnes-force)**. A press of at least **200 tonnes capacity** would be selected for this job (with safety margin).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Comparison Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram classifies the major **bulk metal forming processes** covered in KTU Module 2 (Manufacture), along with their key process parameters and typical products.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[BULK METAL FORMING] --> B[COMPRESSIVE]
    A --> T[TENSILE]
    A --> S[SHEAR BASED]
    A --> M[MULTIAXIAL]

    B --> B1[FORGING]
    B --> B2[ROLLING]
    B --> B3[EXTRUSION]
    B --> B4[UPSETTING]

    T --> T1[WIRE DRAWING]
    T --> T2[BAR DRAWING]
    T --> T3[TUBE DRAWING]

    S --> S1[ROTARY SWAGING]
    S --> S2[RING ROLLING]

    M --> M1[BULK HEADING]
    M --> M2[COINING]
    M --> M3[SPINNING]

    B1 --> P1[Output: CRANKSHAFTS, GEARS]
    B2 --> P2[Output: SHEETS, PLATES, RAILS]
    B3 --> P3[Output: TUBES, CHANNELS, WIRES]
    B4 --> P4[Output: BOLTS, RIVET HEADS]
    T1 --> P5[Output: ELECTRICAL WIRES, CABLES]
    M1 --> P6[Output: SCREWS, BOLTS, FASTENERS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Bulk forming processes are primarily classified by the **dominant stress state**: compression (forging, rolling), tension (drawing), shear (swaging), or multi-axial (heading, coining). The product examples illustrate the **industrial relevance** of each process.

## 4.2 Forging Process Architecture Diagram

The following block-level schematic illustrates the **functional architecture of a closed-die forging system**, mapping the interactions between billet, dies, press, and finished part.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[BILLET INPUT: Heated Cylindrical Stock] --> heat[HEATING FURNACE<br/>1100 to 1250 degree C]
    heat --> transfer[TRANSFER MECHANISM<br/>Conveyor or Robot]
    transfer --> placement[DIE PLACEMENT<br/>Lower Die Cavity]
    placement --> press[HYDRAULIC PRESS<br/>500 to 5000 tonnes]
    press --> stage1[STAGE 1: BULGING<br/>Material spreads in die]
    stage1 --> stage2[STAGE 2: FILLING<br/>Material flows into corners]
    stage2 --> stage3[STAGE 3: FLASH FORMATION<br/>Excess material escapes]
    stage3 --> trim[TRIMMING OPERATION<br/>Flash removed by die]
    trim --> cool[COOLING<br/>Air or Water Quench]
    cool --> inspect[INSPECTION<br/>Dimensional and NDT]
    inspect --> output[FINISHED FORGED PART]

    inspect -.Defect.-> reject[SCRAP or REWORK]
    inspect -.Accept.-> output
```

## 4.3 Extrusion Process Flow Diagram

The following diagram details the **direct and indirect extrusion processes** and their functional differences.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DIRECT_EXTRUSION
    A1[BILLET IN CONTAINER] --> B1[RAM APPLIES FORCE]
    B1 --> C1[MATERIAL FLOWS THROUGH DIE]
    C1 --> D1[PRODUCT EXITS]
    A1 -.FRICTION.-> F1[Container Wall Friction]
    F1 -.Increases Force.-> B1
    end

    subgraph INDIRECT_EXTRUSION
    A2[STATIONARY BILLET] --> B2[DIE MOUNTED ON HOLLOW RAM]
    B2 --> C2[RAM MOVES INTO BILLET]
    C2 --> D2[PRODUCT EXITS BACKWARDS]
    A2 -.NO FRICTION.-> F2[No Container Contact]
    end

    DIRECT_EXTRUSION -.Versus.-> INDIRECT_EXTRUSION
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In **direct extrusion**, the friction between the billet and container wall (red arrow) increases the required ram force by up to 30%. In **indirect extrusion**, the die moves into a stationary billet, eliminating container friction — but the geometry of the hollow ram limits the maximum product cross-section.

## 4.4 Drawing Process Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    WireInput[WIRE ROD INPUT<br/>Coil or Straight] --> PreClean[SURFACE PREPARATION<br/>Pickling and Lubrication]
    PreClean --> DieMount[DIE ASSEMBLY<br/>Tungsten Carbide]
    DieMount --> Pointing[POINTING<br/>Ends tapered for entry]
    Pointing --> DieEntry[ENTRY ZONE<br/>Reduction begins]
    DieEntry --> WorkingZone[WORKING ZONE<br/>Plastic Deformation]
    WorkingZone --> SizingZone[SIZING ZONE<br/>Final dimension]
    SizingZone --> DieExit[EXIT ZONE]
    DieExit --> Capstan[CAPSTAN PULL<br/>Provides Tension]
    Capstan --> TakeUpSpool[TAKE-UP SPOOL]
    DieMount -.Lubricant Film.-> WorkingZone
    WorkingZone -.Die Wear.-> DieMount
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pointer:** The **capstan** (or drawing block) is critical — it provides the pulling force that draws the wire through the die. The **back tension** and **die angle (semi-angle α)** are key design parameters affecting the **maximum reduction per pass** and the **product quality** (residual stresses, surface finish).

## 4.5 Defect Mode Matrix in Forming

The following diagram maps common defects in bulk forming to their root causes and process parameters.

```mermaid
graph TD
    DefectBase[FORMING DEFECTS] --> D1[CRACKING]
    DefectBase --> D2[FOLDING]
    DefectBase --> D3[CENTRAL BURST]
    DefectBase --> D4[FLASH DEFECTS]

    D1 --> D1Cause[Excess Reduction, Low Ductility]
    D1Cause --> D1Fix[Reduce Reduction, Preheat, Anneal]

    D2 --> D2Cause[Improper Die Design, Sharp Corners]
    D2Cause --> D2Fix[Use Generous Fillets and Draft Angles]

    D3 --> D3Cause[Excess Tensile Stress in Centre]
    D3Cause --> D3Fix[Optimize Die Angle, Use Back Pressure]

    D4 --> D4Cause[Excess Material or Poor Flash Design]
    D4Cause --> D4Fix[Precise Volume Control, Flash Gutter Design]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** **Central burst (chevron cracking)** is a classic defect in **wire drawing** that occurs when the die angle is too large. The material at the centre of the wire experiences tensile stress that exceeds the local ductility, leading to internal cracking. The remedy is to use an **optimum die semi-angle** (typically 6–12°).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "Flow Stress" in metal forming. How is it different from yield stress?**

**Model Answer:**

**Flow stress (σf or Yf)** is defined as the **instantaneous true stress required to continue plastic deformation of a metal at a given strain, strain rate, and temperature**.

While **yield stress (σy)** is the stress at which plastic deformation *begins*, the flow stress is the stress required to *sustain* the deformation as the strain increases. For a strain-hardening material, the flow stress increases continuously with strain, following the Hollomon equation:

$$\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

**Key Differences:**
- **Yield stress** is a single value at the onset of yielding (yield point).
- **Flow stress** is a function of strain (and strain rate and temperature) — it changes throughout the deformation process.
- Yield stress is used in **strength of materials** analysis; flow stress is used in **metal forming** analysis.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: Distinguish between direct and indirect extrusion processes with a suitable sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Direct Extrusion | Indirect Extrusion |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Direction of metal flow** | Same as ram direction | Opposite to ram direction |
| **Die location** | Fixed at container end | Mounted on hollow ram |
| **Friction at container wall** | Yes (significant) | No (billet is stationary) |
| **Ram force required** | 20–30% higher | Lower |
| **Product length limitation** | None (theoretical) | Limited by hollow ram length |
| **Defect risk** | Dead-metal zone in billet | No dead-metal zone |

In **direct extrusion**, the ram pushes the billet through a stationary die. In **indirect extrusion**, the die is mounted on a hollow ram and is pushed into a stationary billet, so the product flows backwards through the hollow ram. Indirect extrusion is preferred when billet-container friction dominates the force requirement.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A cylindrical billet of aluminium alloy (initial diameter 80 mm, initial height 100 mm) is to be forged in an open-die compression operation to a final height of 50 mm. The flow curve is $\sigma_f = 180 \varepsilon^{0.20}$ MPa. The coefficient of friction at the die-workpiece interface is $\mu = 0.25$.**

**Determine:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The **average flow stress**, the **true strain**, and the **ideal forging work** (assume no friction, homogeneous deformation).

**(b) [7 Marks]** The **maximum forging force** at the final stage of the operation, considering friction (use the friction hill approach).

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Average Flow Stress, True Strain, and Ideal Work

**Step 1: True Strain**

$$\varepsilon_f = \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{100}{50}\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$$

**Step 2: Average Flow Stress**

The average flow stress for a power-law material is:

$$\sigma_{f,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f}\int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K\varepsilon^n \, d\varepsilon = \frac{K \varepsilon_f^n}{n+1}$$

Substituting values:
- $K = 180$ MPa
- $\varepsilon_f = 0.6931$
- $n = 0.20$

Compute $\varepsilon_f^n$:
- $\ln(0.6931) = -0.3665$
- $n \cdot \ln(\varepsilon_f) = 0.20 \times (-0.3665) = -0.0733$
- $\varepsilon_f^n = e^{-0.0733} = 0.9293$

So:
$$\sigma_{f,\text{avg}} = \frac{180 \times 0.9293}{1.20} = \frac{167.27}{1.20} = 139.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Ideal Forging Work**

Volume of the billet (constant):
$$V = \pi R_0^2 h_0 = \pi (0.040)^2 (0.100) = 5.027 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

The ideal work for homogeneous deformation is:
$$W_i = \sigma_{f,\text{avg}} \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon_f = 139.4 \times 10^6 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.6931$$

$$W_i = 139.4 \times 5.027 \times 0.6931 \times 10^2 = 4.857 \times 10^4 \text{ J} \approx \mathbf{48.6 \text{ kJ}}$$

#### Part (b) — Maximum Forging Force with Friction

**Step 1: Final Radius of the Billet**

By volume constancy: $R_0^2 h_0 = R_f^2 h_f$

$$R_f = R_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 40 \sqrt{2} = 56.57 \text{ mm} = 0.05657 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Final Area of the Billet**

$$A_f = \pi R_f^2 = \pi (0.05657)^2 = 1.005 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Friction Hill Factor**

$$\frac{2\mu R_f}{3h_f} = \frac{2 \times 0.25 \times 0.05657}{3 \times 0.050} = \frac{0.02829}{0.150} = 0.1886$$

**Step 4: Maximum Pressure**

The maximum pressure occurs at the final stage of compression (largest friction hill):

$$p_{\text{max}} = \sigma_{f,\text{avg}} \left(1 + \frac{2\mu R_f}{3h_f}\right) = 139.4 \times (1 + 0.1886)$$

$$p_{\text{max}} = 139.4 \times 1.1886 = 165.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Maximum Forging Force**

$$F_{\text{max}} = p_{\text{max}} \cdot A_f = 165.7 \times 10^6 \times 1.005 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$F_{\text{max}} = 1.665 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{1.67 \text{ MN} \approx 170 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Final Answer for Question A:**
- (a) True strain = 0.693; Average flow stress = 139.4 MPa; Ideal work = 48.6 kJ
- (b) Maximum forging force = **1.67 MN (≈ 170 tonnes-force)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **wire drawing process**. Derive an expression for the **drawing stress** considering the effect of friction. What is the condition for the **maximum reduction per pass**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **brass rod** with initial diameter of 10 mm is to be drawn through a die of semi-angle $\alpha = 8°$ to a final diameter of 7.5 mm. The average flow stress of brass is 350 MPa, and the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.10$. Determine the **drawing force** and the **power required** if the drawing speed is 2 m/s.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Wire Drawing Process, Stress Derivation, and Maximum Reduction

**Diagram Description:** A wire drawing setup consists of (1) a **pay-off spool** holding the input wire, (2) a **die assembly** with a tungsten carbide insert, (3) a **lubricant bath** or spray system, (4) a **capstan (drawing block)** that provides the pulling force, and (5) a **take-up spool** for the drawn product.

**Derivation of Drawing Stress:**

Consider a small element of the wire inside the die, at distance $x$ from the die entry. The element is in equilibrium between:
- Axial tension $F$ on the trailing face
- Axial tension $F + dF$ on the leading face
- Friction shear stress $\tau = \mu \sigma_n$ on the die wall

By applying the **slab equilibrium** and using the **von Mises yield criterion** (with die semi-angle $\alpha$):

$$\sigma_d = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha}\right) \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Maximum Reduction Condition:**

The drawing stress cannot exceed the **chuck strength** (≈ 0.88 × UTS for safety):

$$\sigma_d \leq 0.88 \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$$

For a perfectly plastic material with $\sigma_f \approx \sigma_y$ and no friction:

$$\sigma_y \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)_{\text{max}} = 0.88 \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$$

So:
$$r_{\text{max}} = 1 - e^{-0.88} \approx 58.5\%$$

#### Part (b) — Drawing Force and Power for Brass Rod

**Given:**
- Initial diameter $D_0 = 10$ mm = 0.010 m
- Final diameter $D_f = 7.5$ mm = 0.0075 m
- Die semi-angle $\alpha = 8°$
- Average flow stress $\sigma_f = 350$ MPa
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.10$
- Drawing speed $v = 2$ m/s

**Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.010)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi D_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the Extrusion (Draw) Ratio and True Strain**

$$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-5}}{4.418 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.778$$

$$\ln(R) = \ln(1.778) \approx 0.5754$$

**Step 3: Compute the Friction Multiplier**

$$\frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} = \frac{0.10}{\tan 8°} = \frac{0.10}{0.1405} = 0.7117$$

**Step 4: Compute the Drawing Stress**

$$\sigma_d = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha}\right) \ln(R)$$

$$\sigma_d = 350 \times (1 + 0.7117) \times 0.5754$$

$$\sigma_d = 350 \times 1.7117 \times 0.5754 = 344.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Drawing Force**

The drawing force acts on the **final cross-section** (exit area):

$$F = \sigma_d \cdot A_f = 344.7 \times 10^6 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$F = 1.523 \times 10^4 \text{ N} = \mathbf{15.23 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 6: Compute the Power Required**

$$P = F \cdot v = 1.523 \times 10^4 \times 2 = 3.046 \times 10^4 \text{ W} = \mathbf{30.5 \text{ kW}}$$

**Final Answer for Question B:**
- (a) Drawing stress formula: $\sigma_d = \sigma_f (1 + \mu/\tan\alpha) \ln(A_0/A_f)$; Maximum reduction ≈ 58.5%
- (b) Drawing force = **15.23 kN**; Power = **30.5 kW**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse the area for force calculation** — In wire drawing, the drawing force acts on the **exit cross-section ($A_f$)**, not the entry. Using $A_0$ gives an answer that is **wrong by the draw ratio (1.78× in this case)**. This is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU marking schemes.
> 2. **Always include friction in the formula** — Many students write $\sigma_d = \sigma_f \ln R$ (without friction), which is incomplete. The friction term $\mu/\tan\alpha$ adds **~71%** in our example and is essential for industrial accuracy.
> 3. **For forging force calculations, the pressure varies with position** (friction hill) — students often forget the $\frac{2\mu R}{3h}$ factor and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Units matter** — Convert mm to m for SI consistency, or MPa to Pa. Mixing mm² with N gives incorrect answers.
> 5. **Direction of force in extrusion** — Direct extrusion has an **additional friction term** $\frac{2L}{D}$ that indirect extrusion does not. Writing the wrong formula costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 – Manufacture)**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Forming** reshapes metal by **plastic deformation** without removing material.
- ☐ **Flow stress** ($\sigma_f$) increases with strain (strain hardening), described by the **Hollomon equation**: $\sigma_f = K\varepsilon^n$.
- ☐ **True strain** for compression: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$.
- ☐ **Volume constancy**: $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$ (incompressible plastic flow).

### Forging
- ☐ **Friction hill** is the radial pressure distribution; maximum at the centre.
- ☐ **Forging pressure (with friction)**: $p = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{2\mu R}{3h}\right)$.
- ☐ **Forging force**: $F = p \cdot A$.
- ☐ Maximum force occurs at the **final stage** (largest $R/h$).
- ☐ **Ideal work**: $W_i = \sigma_{f,\text{avg}} \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon_f$.

### Extrusion
- ☐ **Direct extrusion** has **container friction**; **Indirect extrusion** does not.
- ☐ **Direct force**: $F = A_0 \sigma_f [\ln R + 2L/D]$.
- ☐ **Indirect force**: $F = A_0 \sigma_f \ln R$ (no friction term).
- ☐ **Extrusion ratio**: $R = A_0 / A_f$.
- ☐ **Hydrostatic extrusion** eliminates both container and die friction.

### Wire Drawing
- ☐ **Drawing stress (with friction)**: $\sigma_d = \sigma_f (1 + \mu/\tan\alpha) \ln R$.
- ☐ **Max reduction per pass**: $\sigma_d \leq 0.88 \sigma_{\text{UTS}}$.
- ☐ The **drawing force** acts on the **exit area** $A_f$, not $A_0$.
- ☐ **Central burst** is caused by an excessive die angle.

### Defects to Watch
- ☐ **Cracking** → reduce strain per pass, anneal intermediate.
- ☐ **Folding** → improve die design, fillets, draft angles.
- ☐ **Flash** → control billet volume precisely.
- ☐ **Central burst** (chevron crack) → optimize die angle (6°–12°).

### Numerical Constants (Always Carry These)
- ☐ Mild Steel: $K = 530$ MPa, $n = 0.26$
- ☐ Aluminium 1100-O: $K = 180$ MPa, $n = 0.20$
- ☐ Brass 70-30: $K = 895$ MPa, $n = 0.33$
- ☐ $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$, $\ln(3) \approx 1.0986$
- ☐ Density of steel: **7850 kg/m³**

### Examiner's Quick-Answer Pattern (for KTU 2024)
- ☐ **3-mark question** → Define the term + write the governing equation + state one practical example.
- ☐ **7-mark sub-question** → State assumption, derive the formula step-by-step, and provide a numerical illustration.
- ☐ **14-mark full question** → Always includes a **diagram** (worth 2 marks in KTU), the **derivation** (5–6 marks), the **numerical calculation** (4–5 marks), and a **conclusion/statement** (1 mark).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design - Possible Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design - Possible Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEMRT522 - Design of Manufacture**, the "Design – Possible Solutions" phase (also referred to as the **Concept Generation / Ideation Phase**) is defined as the critical stage within the **Product Design Process (PDP)** wherein the design team systematically generates, develops, and analyses multiple alternative technical solutions that satisfy the **Product Design Specification (PDS)** derived during the problem definition stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Design - Possible Solutions* is the systematic, iterative engineering process of generating a broad spectrum of conceptual design alternatives using structured creativity methods, followed by their preliminary evaluation, screening, and selection of the most viable concept(s) that best meet the functional requirements, constraints, and design objectives of the product.

This phase sits between **Problem Definition (Module 1)** and **Detailed Design (Module 3)** in the standard KTU design process flow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Chef's Recipe Dilemma"**
>
> Imagine you are a chef who must prepare a dish (the *product*) for a customer with specific dietary needs — say, a **low-sodium, high-protein, vegetarian dinner for 4 people within a budget of ₹500 in 30 minutes** (the *PDS / Design Brief*).
>
> You don't immediately start cooking. Instead, you think of **many possible recipes** (palak paneer, chickpea curry, mushroom stir-fry, lentil stew, tofu tikka masala, etc.) — this is the **"Possible Solutions" generation phase**.
>
> Then, you mentally **score each recipe** against the constraints (protein content, sodium, time, cost, taste) — this is **Concept Evaluation (Pugh Matrix / Weighted Scoring)**.
>
> Finally, you **pick the best 1–2 recipes** to actually try in the kitchen — this is **Concept Selection**.
>
> If the chosen recipe fails in the trial (prototype), you go back to your list and try the next best one. This iterative loop is exactly what the **Design – Possible Solutions** phase represents in engineering!

In engineering terms, every "recipe" is a **conceptual design solution** that fulfills the same functional need, but uses different working principles, configurations, materials, or manufacturing routes.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU-Aligned Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Concept** | An abstract description of a possible solution idea, expressed in terms of working principle and configuration |
| **Working Principle** | The fundamental physical, chemical, or biological effect that enables the function (e.g., Archimedes' principle, Bernoulli's principle) |
| **Configuration** | The geometric layout, arrangement, and interconnection of components |
| **PDS** | Product Design Specification — the documented list of requirements, constraints, and targets |
| **Concept Generation** | The creative process of producing many diverse solution ideas |
| **Concept Screening** | Quickly eliminating infeasible or weak concepts to narrow the field |
| **Concept Scoring** | Rigorously ranking the surviving concepts against weighted criteria |
| **FRS** | Functional Requirement Specification |
| **QFD** | Quality Function Deployment — translates customer needs into engineering characteristics |

### 1.4 Why This Phase Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board examiner typically awards **7–14 marks** to questions covering the design process stages, concept generation methods, and evaluation matrices. Students who can name **at least 3 systematic methods** (e.g., Brainstorming, Morphological Chart, TRIZ) and explain **one evaluation technique** (Pugh Matrix or Weighted Scoring) with a worked example consistently score in the top band.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Design – Possible Solutions Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

The phase unfolds in **four sequential sub-stages**:

#### **Stage A — Clarify the Problem (Function Analysis)**

Before generating solutions, the team decomposes the overall product function into **sub-functions** using a **Function Analysis System Technique (FAST) diagram** or **Function Structure** tree.

**Why this step?** It ensures solutions are generated for *specific sub-functions*, not vague "make a better mousetrap" goals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For a *table lamp*, the primary function is "convert electrical energy to directed light." Sub-functions: (i) provide power, (ii) support bulb, (iii) direct light, (iv) allow switching, (v) provide stability.

#### **Stage B — Concept Generation (Divergent Thinking)**

The goal is to generate **a large quantity of diverse concepts** without judging them. Standard KTU-recognized methods include:

1. **Brainstorming** — Group ideation with 4 rules: defer judgment, quantity over quality, build on others' ideas, wild ideas welcome.
2. **Brainwriting (6-3-5 Method)** — 6 members write 3 ideas each in 5 rounds; reduces dominance by loud members.
3. **Morphological Analysis (Zwicky Box)** — Decompose problem into independent parameters, list alternatives for each, then combine systematically.
4. **SCAMPER Technique** — Acronym: **S**ubstitute, **C**ombine, **A**dapt, **M**odify, **P**ut to other use, **E**liminate, **R**earrange.
5. **TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)** — Uses 40 inventive principles and contradiction matrix to resolve design conflicts.
6. **Biomimicry / Analogies** — Borrow solutions from nature (e.g., lotus-leaf self-cleaning surfaces → hydrophobic coatings).
7. **Synectics** — Use forced analogies (direct, personal, symbolic, fantasy) to provoke novel ideas.
8. **Lateral Thinking (de Bono)** — Provocative operations to break fixed patterns.

**Why this step?** Without structured divergence, teams converge prematurely on familiar solutions ("design fixation").

#### **Stage C — Concept Screening (Convergent Thinking — First Pass)**

A **Pugh Concept Screening Matrix** is used:
- Choose a **datum (baseline) concept**, often the industry-standard or previous-generation design.
- Score each alternative as **+1 (better), 0 (same), –1 (worse)** against each selection criterion.
- Sum the scores; **eliminate concepts with strongly negative totals**.

#### **Stage D — Concept Scoring & Selection (Convergent Thinking — Final)**

A **Weighted Scoring Matrix (Decision Matrix)** is used:
- Each selection criterion is assigned a **weight $w_i$** (importance, $0 \le w_i \le 1$, sum = 1).
- Each concept is scored $s_{ij}$ (typically 1–10 scale).
- The **weighted score** of concept $j$ is computed.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 14-mark Part B questions and in 3-mark Part A definition/formula questions.**

**Table 2.1 — Core Evaluation Formulas for Design – Possible Solutions**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Purpose | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Weighted Score of Concept $j$** | $\displaystyle S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ij}$ | Final ranking score | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Sum of Weights (Normalization)** | $\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$ | Ensures criterion weights form a probability distribution | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Relative Score (Normalized)** | $\displaystyle R_j = \dfrac{S_j}{S_{\max}}$ | Compare concepts on 0–1 scale | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Pugh Score (sum of $\pm 1$ votes)** | $\displaystyle P_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_{ij}, \quad v_{ij} \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ | Quick screening | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Number of Combinations (Morphological Box)** | $\displaystyle N_{comb} = \prod_{k=1}^{m} a_k$ | Total concepts combinable | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Feasibility Index** | $\displaystyle F_j = \alpha \cdot \text{Technical}_j + \beta \cdot \text{Economic}_j + \gamma \cdot \text{Legal}_j$ | Weighted feasibility | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Concept Diversity Index (Shannon)** | $\displaystyle H = -\sum_{k} p_k \ln p_k$ | Measures variety of generated concepts | nats |
| 8 | **Expected Value of Concept** | $\displaystyle EV_j = \sum_{s} p_s \cdot V_{js}$ | Decision under uncertainty | Currency (₹) |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE:** The absolute-value-like notation in the Pugh Score row uses `v_{ij} ∈ {−1, 0, +1}` and is **not** placed inside a table cell with vertical bars, preserving markdown integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Design – Possible Solutions phase is the **highest-leverage moment in product development** because:

- It is **10–100× cheaper** to reject a poor concept on paper than to scrap a physical prototype.
- It is where **patentable novelty** is born (the 40 TRIZ principles have generated > 90% of modern engineering patents).
- Industry data (e.g., Wheelwright & Clark, *Harvard Business Review*) shows that projects with **rigorous concept screening** are **3× more likely to meet launch schedule** and **50% less likely to be cancelled** mid-development.
- Automotive OEMs (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki) generate **> 50 concept sketches per new model** before selecting 3 for clay modelling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weighted Scoring Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Derivation:** Why does the weighted score formula $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot s_{ij}$ make sense? We will derive it from first principles of multi-criteria decision making.

**Step 1 — Single Criterion Baseline**

Suppose a single design criterion $i$ has importance weight $w_i$ (between 0 and 1). If a concept $j$ receives raw score $s_{ij}$ on that criterion, the **criterion-weighted contribution** is simply the product:

$$C_{ij} = w_i \cdot s_{ij}$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiplying the raw score by the weight amplifies strong performance on important criteria and dampens strong performance on unimportant ones.

**Step 2 — Aggregation Across Multiple Criteria**

For $n$ independent criteria, the **total merit** of a concept is the sum of its criterion-weighted contributions. By the **additive utility assumption** (valid when criteria are mutually preference-independent, as in most KTU-scope design problems):

$$S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{ij} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ij}$$

**Conversion logic:** Total goodness is built additively from independent parts — this is the classical **linear additive weighting (LAW)** model from multi-attribute utility theory (Keeney & Raiffa, 1976).

**Step 3 — Weight Normalization Constraint**

For the scores to be comparable across teams, weights must sum to unity:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$

**Step 4 — Ranking**

The optimal concept $j^*$ is the one with maximum weighted score:

$$j^* = \arg\max_{j} S_j$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A KTU student team is designing a **low-cost electric kettle for hostel students** (Target cost < ₹800, capacity 1 L, boil time < 4 min, ABS plastic body, Boil-Dry protection, ISI mark required). Three concepts (A, B, C) have been generated. Evaluate using the weighted scoring method.

**Step 1 — Define Criteria and Weights**

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Rationale |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $C_1$ : Manufacturing cost | 0.30 | Hostel budget is the strongest constraint |
| $C_2$ : Boil time | 0.20 | Functional performance |
| $C_3$ : Safety features | 0.20 | Boil-dry, ISI mark |
| $C_4$ : Ease of manufacturing | 0.15 | Single-semester project |
| $C_5$ : Aesthetics | 0.10 | Lowest importance |
| $C_6$ : Energy efficiency | 0.05 | Lowest importance |

**Step 2 — Verify Weight Sum**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i = 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Score Each Concept (Scale 1–10)**

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Score $A$ ($s_{iA}$) | Score $B$ ($s_{iB}$) | Score $C$ ($s_{iC}$) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $C_1$ Cost | 0.30 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| $C_2$ Boil time | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| $C_3$ Safety | 0.20 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| $C_4$ Ease of mfg | 0.15 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| $C_5$ Aesthetics | 0.10 | 5 | 9 | 7 |
| $C_6$ Efficiency | 0.05 | 6 | 7 | 7 |

**Step 4 — Compute Weighted Scores**

For **Concept A**:

$$S_A = (0.30)(8) + (0.20)(7) + (0.20)(6) + (0.15)(9) + (0.10)(5) + (0.05)(6)$$

$$S_A = 2.40 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.50 + 0.30$$

$$S_A = 7.15$$

For **Concept B**:

$$S_B = (0.30)(6) + (0.20)(9) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(9) + (0.05)(7)$$

$$S_B = 1.80 + 1.80 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.90 + 0.35$$

$$S_B = 7.50$$

For **Concept C**:

$$S_C = (0.30)(9) + (0.20)(6) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(7) + (0.05)(7)$$

$$S_C = 2.70 + 1.20 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.35$$

$$S_C = 7.55$$

**Step 5 — Rank and Select**

| Concept | Weighted Score $S_j$ | Rank |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| A | 7.15 | 3rd |
| B | 7.50 | 2nd |
| **C** | **7.55** | **1st ✓** |

**Conclusion:** Concept **C** is selected for detailed design. Note that Concept C wins on **cost** (its dominant criterion), confirming the model's adherence to the most-weighted requirement.

### 3.3 Morphological Box Example

**Problem:** Generate all possible combinations of a **bicycle warning bell** with 3 functional sub-systems.

| Sub-function | Option 1 | Option 2 | Option 3 | Option 4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Power source** | Manual spring | Battery | Dynamo | Solar |
| **Sound generation** | Mechanical clapper | Electronic buzzer | Piezo speaker | Whistle |
| **Mounting** | Handlebar clamp | Strap-on | Bolt-on bracket | Integrated grip |

**Total combinations:**

$$N_{comb} = a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 = 4 \cdot 4 \cdot 4 = 64 \text{ concepts}$$

Most will be infeasible, but the method forces exploration of non-obvious pairings (e.g., *solar + piezo + integrated grip*).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Generation Methods

| Method | Output Volume | Novelty | Group Friendly | KTU Exam Favourite |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Brainstorming | High | Medium | Yes | ★★★★★ |
| Brainwriting 6-3-5 | High | Medium-High | Yes | ★★★★ |
| Morphological Box | Very High | High | Partially | ★★★★★ |
| SCAMPER | Medium | Medium | Yes | ★★★★ |
| TRIZ | Medium | Very High | No | ★★★ |
| Biomimicry | Low | Very High | Partially | ★★★ |
| Synectics | Medium | High | Yes | ★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Design – Possible Solutions Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Problem Definition / PDS"] --> nodeB["Function Analysis FAST Diagram"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Sub-function Decomposition"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Concept Generation Methods"}
    nodeD --> nodeE1["Brainstorming"]
    nodeD --> nodeE2["Brainwriting 6-3-5"]
    nodeD --> nodeE3["Morphological Box"]
    nodeD --> nodeE4["SCAMPER"]
    nodeD --> nodeE5["TRIZ"]
    nodeD --> nodeE6["Biomimicry"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF["Pool of N Concepts"]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF
    nodeE3 --> nodeF
    nodeE4 --> nodeF
    nodeE5 --> nodeF
    nodeE6 --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Pugh Screening Matrix"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Eliminate Poor Concepts"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Weighted Scoring Matrix"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Select Top 1 to 3 Concepts"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Detailed Design Stage"]
    nodeK --> nodeL{"Prototype Test Pass?"}
    nodeL -- No --> nodeF
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM["Final Product"]
    style nodeA fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style nodeF fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style nodeJ fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style nodeM fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Weighted Scoring Calculation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeW1["Criterion C1 with Weight w1"] --> nodeAdd[Weighted Sum Aggregator]
    nodeW2["Criterion C2 with Weight w2"] --> nodeAdd
    nodeW3["Criterion C3 with Weight w3"] --> nodeAdd
    nodeWN["Criterion Cn with Weight wn"] --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd --> nodeScore["Final Score S_j for Concept j"]
    nodeScore --> nodeRank["Rank against all concepts"]
    nodeRank --> nodePick["Select j star with max S_j"]
    style nodeAdd fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style nodeScore fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style nodePick fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Morphological Box Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP1["Sub-function A"] --> nodeC1A["Option A1"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeC1B["Option A2"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeC1C["Option A3"]
    nodeP2["Sub-function B"] --> nodeC2A["Option B1"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeC2B["Option B2"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeC2C["Option B3"]
    nodeP3["Sub-function C"] --> nodeC3A["Option C1"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeC3B["Option C2"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeC3C["Option C3"]
    nodeC1A --> nodeComb["Combinatorial Assembly N = a x b x c"]
    nodeC2B --> nodeComb
    nodeC3A --> nodeComb
    nodeComb --> nodeSet["Set of Feasible Concepts"]
    style nodeComb fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style nodeSet fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Decision Logic for Concept Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Concept Pool"] --> nodeS1["Apply Pugh Matrix"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2{"Score greater than zero?"}
    nodeS2 -- No --> nodeElim["Eliminate Concept"]
    nodeS2 -- Yes --> nodeS3["Apply Weighted Scoring"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Compute S_j = Sum w_i s_ij"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Rank all surviving concepts"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6{"Sensitivity Check"}
    nodeS6 -- Robust --> nodeFinal["Select j star"]
    nodeS6 -- Fragile --> nodeS1
    style nodeFinal fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style nodeElim fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the term "Concept Generation" in the design process. List any two structured methods used for concept generation.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Concept Generation** is the *divergent phase* of the design process in which a large number of diverse conceptual solutions are produced to satisfy the functions and constraints defined in the Product Design Specification (PDS), without premature judgment.

Two structured methods:
1. **Brainstorming** — unstructured group ideation following the 4 rules (defer judgment, quantity over quality, build on ideas, wild ideas welcome).
2. **Morphological Analysis (Zwicky Box)** — systematic enumeration of all combinations of independent sub-function alternatives.

**Q2. What is a Pugh Matrix? State its purpose in concept selection.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> The **Pugh Matrix** (or Pugh Concept Screening Matrix) is a **qualitative, datum-based** evaluation tool in which each alternative concept is compared against a chosen **datum (reference) concept** using a rating scale of $+1$ (better), $0$ (same), or $-1$ (worse) for each selection criterion.
>
> **Purpose:** It rapidly screens a large pool of concepts to identify the strongest candidates for more rigorous weighted scoring, eliminating infeasible or weak ideas before detailed analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

> **Q. (a)** Explain the **Morphological Analysis** method of concept generation with a suitable example. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **Q. (b)** A product design team has shortlisted three concepts (P, Q, R) for a low-cost water purifier. Using the weighted scoring data below, determine the concept to be selected for detailed design. *(7 Marks)*

| Criterion | Weight | Score P | Score Q | Score R |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Filtration efficiency | 0.35 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Cost | 0.25 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| Ease of use | 0.20 | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| Maintenance | 0.15 | 5 | 8 | 6 |
| Aesthetics | 0.05 | 6 | 7 | 8 |

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Morphological Analysis (Fritz Zwicky, 1948)** is a structured method that systematically explores the **entire solution space** by:

1. **Identifying independent parameters** (sub-functions) of the problem.
2. **Listing all feasible alternatives** for each parameter.
3. **Forming a matrix** (the *Zwicky Box*) of parameters × alternatives.
4. **Selecting one alternative from each row** to form a complete concept.
5. **Evaluating each combination** for feasibility.

**Example: Design of a Domestic Ladder**

| Sub-function | Option 1 | Option 2 | Option 3 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Material | Wood | Aluminium | Steel |
| Folding | Fixed | A-frame | Telescopic |
| Feet | Rubber | Plastic | Metallic spike |

**Number of combinations:**

$$N = 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27 \text{ concepts}$$

A typical promising combination: *Aluminium + A-frame + Rubber feet*. The method's strength is the **forced exposure to unconventional pairings** (e.g., steel telescopic with rubber feet), promoting novelty.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

Verify weights:

$$\sum w_i = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.05 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

Compute weighted score for **Concept P**:

$$S_P = (0.35)(8) + (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(5) + (0.05)(6)$$

$$S_P = 2.80 + 1.50 + 1.40 + 0.75 + 0.30 = 6.75$$

Compute weighted score for **Concept Q**:

$$S_Q = (0.35)(7) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(6) + (0.15)(8) + (0.05)(7)$$

$$S_Q = 2.45 + 2.25 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 0.35 = 7.45$$

Compute weighted score for **Concept R**:

$$S_R = (0.35)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(6) + (0.05)(8)$$

$$S_R = 3.15 + 1.75 + 1.60 + 0.90 + 0.40 = 7.80$$

**Ranking:**

| Concept | Score | Rank |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| P | 6.75 | 3rd |
| Q | 7.45 | 2nd |
| **R** | **7.80** | **1st ✓** |

**Decision:** **Concept R** is selected for detailed design.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

> **Q. (a)** Describe the **SCAMPER** technique for concept generation. Apply it to redesign a conventional **bicycle water bottle holder**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **Q. (b)** Explain the **TRIZ** methodology. List any 5 of the 40 inventive principles. *(7 Marks)*

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**SCAMPER** (Bob Eberle, 1971) is a checklist-based ideation method. Each letter is a *prompt operator*:

| Letter | Prompt | Applied to Bicycle Water Bottle Holder |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **S** | **Substitute** | Substitute plastic with bamboo composite |
| **C** | **Combine** | Combine holder with a small tool kit compartment |
| **A** | **Adapt** | Adapt adjustable fishing-rod clamp mechanism |
| **M** | **Modify** | Modify shape to a triangular cross-section for stiffness |
| **P** | **Put to other use** | Use as a phone/camera mount in emergencies |
| **E** | **Eliminate** | Eliminate the top strap; use magnetic grip |
| **R** | **Rearrange** | Rearrange to a side-mounted swing-out position |

This systematic probing surfaces at least 7 new concepts, ensuring **diverse design exploration**.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**TRIZ** (Russian: *Теория решения изобретательских задач* — *Teoriya Resheniya Izobretatelskikh Zadatch*), developed by **Genrich Altshuller** (1946) and his colleagues, is a problem-solving methodology based on the analysis of **2 million patents** to extract universal principles of invention.

**Core Idea:** Most engineering problems contain **contradictions** (improving one parameter worsens another). TRIZ provides:
- **40 Inventive Principles** to resolve contradictions.
- **Contradiction Matrix** mapping 39 engineering parameters to recommended principles.
- **Laws of Technical System Evolution.**

**Five of the 40 Inventive Principles (commonly examined):**

1. **Segmentation** — Divide an object into independent parts. *(e.g., modular furniture)*
2. **Taking Out** — Extract the disturbing part. *(e.g., muffler in exhaust)*
3. **Local Quality** — Change structure from uniform to non-uniform. *(e.g., varying thickness in a spoon)*
4. **Asymmetry** — Replace symmetry with asymmetry. *(e.g., asymmetric gear teeth)*
5. **Merging / Consolidation** — Combine identical or similar objects. *(e.g., USB-C ports doing multiple jobs)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Design – Possible Solutions" Questions**
>
> 1. **Naming methods without explaining their procedure** — A bare list ("Brainstorming, TRIZ, SCAMPER") fetches 1 mark; the procedure/rule fetches 5–6 marks. Always describe *how* the method is executed.
> 2. **Skipping the Functional Analysis step** — Most students jump from PDS directly to concepts. Examiners expect a mention of *function decomposition* before concept generation.
> 3. **Forgetting weight normalization** — $\sum w_i = 1$ must be explicitly verified; failure to do so loses 1 mark in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Confusing Pugh Matrix with Weighted Scoring** — Pugh is qualitative ($\pm 1$); Weighted Scoring is quantitative (0–10 scale). Markers specifically watch for this mix-up.
> 5. **Selecting concepts without justification** — A concept cannot be selected based on gut feeling; you must show the *highest weighted score* and the *arg max* decision rule.
> 6. **Ignoring sensitivity** — Top-level examiners award 1–2 marks for a *sensitivity comment* (e.g., "If cost weight increases to 0.40, Concept Q would win instead of R").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design: Possible Solutions**
>
> ✅ The phase sits between **Problem Definition** and **Detailed Design** in the product design process.
> ✅ Always begin with **Function Analysis / FAST diagram** to decompose the product into sub-functions.
> ✅ **Divergent phase** = generate many concepts; **Convergent phase** = screen and select.
> ✅ Memorize **at least 5 concept generation methods**: Brainstorming, Brainwriting 6-3-5, Morphological Box, SCAMPER, TRIZ, Biomimicry.
> ✅ **Pugh Matrix** uses $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ against a datum concept — used for *screening*.
> ✅ **Weighted Scoring Matrix** uses $S_j = \sum w_i s_{ij}$ with $\sum w_i = 1$ — used for *final selection*.
> ✅ The optimal concept is $j^* = \arg\max_j S_j$.
> ✅ Morphological Box combinations: $N = \prod a_k$ — use this to demonstrate completeness of search.
> ✅ Always perform a **sensitivity check** — change one weight by ±20% and re-rank.
> ✅ Real-world impact: ~10–100× cheaper to reject a concept on paper than in prototype.
> ✅ In KTU 14-mark answers, structure as: (1) Define phase, (2) List methods, (3) Apply ONE method to given product, (4) Compute scores, (5) Select and justify.
