- **$C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\,\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$** — actual capability, accounts for drift. **Most important index for KTU exams.**
- **Shift factor** $k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - T \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2}$; relationship: $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$.
- **$P_p$ and $P_{pk}$** use **sample** standard deviation (overall variation).
- **$C_p$ and $C_{pk}$** use **within-subgroup** standard deviation (common-cause variation).
- **Industry benchmarks:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable), $\geq 1.67$ (highly capable / safety-critical), $\geq 2.00$ (Six-Sigma).
- **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ — loss is quadratic, incurred even within tolerance.
- **Machine vs. Process:** Machine capability is short-term/ideal; process capability is long-term/real-world.
- **Tolerance types:** Bilateral, Unilateral, and Geometric (GD&T per ASME Y14.5).
- **Normality assumption:** All capability indices assume a **normal distribution** — verify with Anderson-Darling or Shapiro-Wilk test before computing.
- **Sample size rule:** Minimum **25 to 30** consecutive parts for a reliable capability study; IATF 16949 mandates **125 parts** for PPAP.
- **Gage R&R prerequisite:** The measurement system must have **Gage R&R < 10%** of tolerance before capability indices are computed — otherwise, measurement error inflates σ artificially.
- **Six-Sigma philosophy:** $C_{pk} = 2.0$ implies only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, the gold standard of modern quality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Tolerances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Geometric Tolerances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Tolerance (GT)** is the general term used to describe the permissible variation in **form, profile, orientation, location, and run-out** of a feature of a part. It is a symbolic language defined by **ASME Y14.5-2018** (and the older **ISO 1101**) used on engineering drawings to communicate design intent precisely to manufacturing and inspection.

In other words, a **Geometric Tolerance** is *not* about *how big* a feature is (that is **dimensional tolerance**), but about *what shape, what angle, what position, and what orientation* a feature is allowed to have relative to a true geometric ideal.

The **tolerance zone** for any geometric characteristic is the **three-dimensional region** within which the entire considered feature must lie.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Parking Slot Intuition"

Imagine a car in a **parking slot** that is 2.5 m wide and 5 m long.

- **Dimensional tolerance** tells you: *"Make the car between 1.4 m and 1.6 m wide."*
- **Geometric tolerance** tells you: *"The car body must be straight (form), the windscreen must be vertical (orientation), the steering axis must lie on the centerline (location), and the wheels must rotate without wobble (run-out)."*

So while dimensional tolerance controls the **size**, geometric tolerance controls the **geometry** — *how perfectly the part looks and behaves* in assembly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard References for KTU 2024:**
> - **ASME Y14.5-2018** (American Society of Mechanical Engineers) — primary reference.
> - **ISO 1101:2017** — international counterpart.
> - All symbols in this module are aligned with **ASME Y14.5** notation as prescribed in the KTU PEMRT522 syllabus.

---

## 1.3 Why Geometric Tolerances are Needed

- **Functional requirements** — a part must fit, mate, and move as designed (e.g., a shaft must be *round* and *straight* to rotate in a bearing).
- **Interchangeability** — parts from different batches must assemble without rework.
- **Manufacturing economy** — over-constraining a part forces expensive precision; GD&T allows *just enough* precision.
- **Inspection clarity** — gives unambiguous language between designer, manufacturer, and quality inspector.

> [!TIP]
> Always remember the **golden rule of GD&T**: **"Form is controlled by form, orientation is controlled relative to a datum, and location is controlled relative to a datum."**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Geometric Characteristics (ASME Y14.5)

Geometric tolerances are grouped into **five families** based on what aspect of geometry they control. The order also represents increasing complexity and information content.

| # | Family | What it controls | Symbol Examples | Datum Required? |
|---|--------|------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Form** | The shape of an individual feature (in isolation) | Straightness, Flatness, Circularity, Cylindricity | **No** |
| 2 | **Profile** | 2-D or 3-D outline of a feature | Profile of a Line, Profile of a Surface | Optional |
| 3 | **Orientation** | Tilt / angle of a feature relative to a datum | Parallelism, Perpendicularity, Angularity | **Yes** |
| 4 | **Location** | True position of features | True Position, Concentricity, Symmetry | **Yes** |
| 5 | **Run-out** | Composite form + orientation / location | Circular Run-out, Total Run-out | **Yes** |

---

## 2.2 The Five Form Tolerances (No Datums)

| Symbol | Name | Tolerance Zone | Typical Application |
|--------|------|----------------|----------------------|
| — | **Straightness** | Two parallel straight lines / a cylindrical zone of diameter $\phi t$ | Shaft axes, edges of plates |
| ⏚ | **Flatness** | Two parallel planes separated by $t$ | Sealing faces, base plates |
| ○ | **Circularity (Roundness)** | Two concentric circles separated radially by $t$ | Shaft journals, bearing seats |
| ⌭ | **Cylindricity** | Two coaxial cylinders separated radially by $t$ | Hydraulic cylinder bores |

> [!NOTE]
> Form tolerances are the only geometric tolerances that **do not require a datum reference** because they are *intrinsic* properties of the feature itself.

---

## 2.3 Orientation Tolerances (Datums Required)

| Symbol | Name | Definition |
|--------|------|------------|
| ∥ | **Parallelism** | Tolerance zone is parallel to a datum plane or axis |
| ⊥ | **Perpendicularity** | Tolerance zone is at 90° to a datum |
| ∠ | **Angularity** | Tolerance zone is at a specified angle (≠ 0° or 90°) to a datum |

---

## 2.4 Location Tolerances (Datums Required)

| Symbol | Name | Notes |
|--------|------|-------|
| ⊕ | **True Position** | The most-used location symbol; cylindrical or parallel-plane zone |
| ◎ | **Concentricity / Coaxiality** | Tolerance zone is a cylinder/circle centered on a datum axis |
| ⌯ | **Symmetry** | Tolerance zone is symmetrically disposed about a datum plane/axis |

---

## 2.5 Run-out Tolerances (Datums Required)

| Symbol | Name | Applies to |
|--------|------|------------|
| ↗ | **Circular Run-out** | Single circular cross-section during one full revolution |
| ↗↗ | **Total Run-out** | Entire surface over multiple revolutions |

---

## 2.6 The Feature Control Frame (FCF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Feature Control Frame (FCF)** is the rectangular symbol box that conveys the complete GD&T specification in a single, ordered sequence.

**Order of compartments (left to right):**
1. **Geometric characteristic symbol**
2. **Diameter symbol (⌀)** if the zone is cylindrical
3. **Tolerance value**
4. **Material condition modifier** (Ⓜ MMC, Ⓛ LMC, or omitted for RFS)
5. **Datum letters** (primary, secondary, tertiary — left to right)

**Example:**

$$
\boxed{\;|\;\phi\;0.2\;\text{M}\;\;A\;\;B\;\;C\;|\;}
$$

Means: *The axis of this hole must lie within a cylindrical tolerance zone of diameter 0.2 mm, at **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)**, relative to datums A (primary), B (secondary), C (tertiary).*

---

## 2.7 Datums and the Datum Reference Frame (DRF)

- A **datum** is a theoretically exact reference (a point, line, or plane) derived from a real **datum feature** on the part.
- The **datum identifier** is a letter inside a square box, connected to the datum feature by a leader line or extension line, with a **filled or unfilled triangle** (filled = on the surface, unfilled = on the extension line / offset).
- A **Datum Reference Frame (DRF)** uses **3 mutually perpendicular datums** (3-2-1 principle) to fully constrain the part in space.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3-2-1 Principle:**
> - **3** primary points (or a plane) — Datum A
> - **2** secondary points (or a line) — Datum B
> - **1** tertiary point (or a point) — Datum C

---

## 2.8 Material Condition Modifiers (MMC, LMC, RFS)

| Modifier | Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Use |
|----------|--------|---------|-----------------|
| **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** | Ⓜ | Feature contains the *most* material (largest shaft, smallest hole) | Allows **bonus tolerance** as the feature departs from MMC — used for *gauging* |
| **Least Material Condition (LMC)** | Ⓛ | Feature contains the *least* material (smallest shaft, largest hole) | Used for *minimum wall thickness* control |
| **Regardless of Feature Size (RFS)** | *(no symbol)* | Tolerance applies at *any* produced size | Default in ASME; no bonus tolerance |

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Tolerance** = (Actual produced size − MMC size) for external features, or (MMC size − Actual produced size) for internal features.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Virtual Condition (VC) — External Feature (shaft, pin)** | $VC = MMC - \text{Geometric Tolerance}$ | mm |
| 2 | **Virtual Condition (VC) — Internal Feature (hole, slot)** | $VC = MMC + \text{Geometric Tolerance}$ | mm |
| 3 | **Bonus Tolerance — External Feature** | $\text{Bonus} = \text{Actual Size} - MMC$ | mm |
| 4 | **Bonus Tolerance — Internal Feature** | $\text{Bonus} = MMC - \text{Actual Size}$ | mm |
| 5 | **Resultant (Total) Tolerance** | $T_{\text{total}} = \text{Specified GT} + \text{Bonus}$ | mm |
| 6 | **Circular Run-out** | $R = \Delta r_{\max}$ measured by dial indicator during 1 revolution | mm |
| 7 | **Total Run-out** | $TR = \Delta r_{\max}$ over the *entire* surface in multiple revolutions | mm |
| 8 | **Cylindrical Tolerance Zone Diameter (Position)** | $Z_{\text{cyl}} = \phi t$ | mm |
| 9 | **Position Tolerance — Bonus at LMC Ⓛ** | $T_{\text{actual}} = T_{\text{spec}} + (LMC - \text{Actual})$ | mm |
| 10 | **Projected Tolerance Zone height** | $H_p = \text{specified projection distance}$ | mm |

> **Real-world utility of these formulas** — used heavily in **CNC machining, CMM inspection, gauge design (Go/No-Go plug gauges), aerospace assembly, and automotive tolerancing stacks**. Mastering VC and bonus tolerance is essential for KTU ESE problems.

---

## 2.10 The 14 Standard GD&T Symbols (Quick-Reference Table)

| Symbol | Name | Category |
|--------|------|----------|
| — | Straightness | Form |
| ⏚ | Flatness | Form |
| ○ | Circularity | Form |
| ⌭ | Cylindricity | Form |
| ⌒ | Profile of a Line | Profile |
| ⌒ (with surface) | Profile of a Surface | Profile |
| ∥ | Parallelism | Orientation |
| ⊥ | Perpendicularity | Orientation |
| ∠ | Angularity | Orientation |
| ⊕ | Position | Location |
| ◎ | Concentricity | Location |
| ⌯ | Symmetry | Location |
| ↗ | Circular Run-out | Run-out |
| ↗↗ | Total Run-out | Run-out |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through the **canonical derivations** that appear in KTU ESE question papers. Each derivation is fully expanded — no steps skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Virtual Condition of an Internal Feature (Hole)

**Statement:** A hole is specified at $\phi 20_{0}^{+0.4}$ mm with a **position tolerance of $\phi 0.3$ mm at MMC (Ⓜ)** relative to datums A and B. Compute the **Virtual Condition (VC)** for the hole.

### Step 1 — Identify the feature type and limits

- Feature: **Internal** (hole)
- Nominal diameter: $\phi 20$ mm
- Upper limit (MMC of hole = smallest hole): $D_{\text{MMC}} = 20.0$ mm
- Lower limit (LMC of hole = largest hole): $D_{\text{LMC}} = 20.4$ mm

> [!NOTE]
> For an **internal feature**, MMC corresponds to the **smallest** allowable size (most material, hardest to insert mating part).

### Step 2 — Write the formula for VC of an internal feature

$$
VC_{\text{internal}} = MMC + \text{Geometric Tolerance}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the values

$$
\begin{aligned}
VC_{\text{internal}} &= D_{\text{MMC}} + T_{\text{position}} \\
&= 20.0 \text{ mm} + 0.3 \text{ mm} \\
&= 20.3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Physical interpretation

> The **virtual condition** of $20.3$ mm represents the *worst-case boundary* that a perfect mating pin would encounter. It is the dimension of the smallest *cylindrical envelope* that the hole's axis must lie within, taking into account both the position tolerance and the size variation.

### Step 5 — Engineering significance

A **Go plug gauge** of diameter $20.3$ mm would just pass through the hole under worst-case conditions — this is the basis of **functional gauging** in Taylor's Principle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Virtual Condition of an External Feature (Shaft)

**Statement:** A shaft is specified at $\phi 30_{-0.3}^{0}$ mm with **perpendicularity tolerance of $\phi 0.2$ mm at MMC (Ⓜ)** relative to datum A. Compute the **Virtual Condition (VC)** of the shaft.

### Step 1 — Identify feature limits

- Feature: **External** (shaft)
- Maximum size (MMC of shaft = largest shaft): $D_{\text{MMC}} = 30.0$ mm
- Minimum size (LMC of shaft = smallest shaft): $D_{\text{LMC}} = 29.7$ mm

### Step 2 — Apply the external VC formula

$$
VC_{\text{external}} = MMC - \text{Geometric Tolerance}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
VC_{\text{external}} &= D_{\text{MMC}} - T_{\text{perpendicularity}} \\
&= 30.0 \text{ mm} - 0.2 \text{ mm} \\
&= 29.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3a — Verification (Sanity Check)

> An external feature (shaft) is most material at its **largest** size. The geometric tolerance *shrinks* the effective boundary that a mating hole must accommodate. So $30.0 - 0.2 = 29.8$ mm is correct: the largest possible *envelope* the shaft can occupy is $29.8$ mm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bonus Tolerance Calculation

**Statement:** The shaft in Derivation 2 is **actually produced at $29.75$ mm** during inspection. What is the **bonus tolerance** available, and what is the **total permissible position/orientation tolerance**?

### Step 1 — Compute bonus tolerance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bonus} &= \text{Actual Size} - MMC \\
&= 29.75 \text{ mm} - 30.0 \text{ mm} \\
&= -0.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative** bonus tolerance is *impossible* for MMC applications! This tells us the produced size ($29.75$ mm) is *below* the LMC ($29.7$ mm)... wait, $29.75 > 29.7$, so the value $29.75$ mm is valid. Re-check: for an **external** feature, MMC = $30.0$ mm (largest). Actual size $29.75$ mm is *less* than MMC, so the bonus is *negative* — but the part is still within size limits. The sign just indicates the feature is **departing from MMC in the LMC direction**, which is **not** the favorable direction for MMC bonus.

### Step 2 — Reinterpret for the correct scenario

For a **shaft produced at $29.85$ mm** (i.e., larger than the LMC, departs from MMC toward MMC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bonus} &= \text{Actual Size} - MMC \\
&= 29.85 \text{ mm} - 30.0 \text{ mm} \\
&= -0.15 \text{ mm (negative, departs from MMC in the wrong direction for external)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an **external feature at MMC**, "departure from MMC" means *going smaller*; the size must be **less than MMC** to gain bonus. Let us re-choose an **actual size of $29.90$ mm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual} &= 29.90 \text{ mm (less than MMC = 30.0)} \\
\text{Bonus} &= 29.90 - 30.0 = -0.10 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is still negative. The convention is: **for an external feature, the bonus is defined as MMC − Actual Size**, so the absolute value of departure is the bonus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correct convention to memorize for KTU:**
> - **External feature bonus** = $MMC - \text{Actual Size}$ (always positive when Actual < MMC)
> - **Internal feature bonus** = $\text{Actual Size} - MMC$ (always positive when Actual > MMC)

### Step 2 (Corrected)

Using the corrected convention with **Actual Size = $29.90$ mm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bonus} &= MMC - \text{Actual} \\
&= 30.0 \text{ mm} - 29.90 \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.10 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute total permissible geometric tolerance

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{specified}} + \text{Bonus} \\
&= 0.2 \text{ mm} + 0.10 \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.30 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The shaft, produced at $29.90$ mm (i.e., $0.10$ mm below MMC), can now have a perpendicularity deviation of up to $0.30$ mm instead of the originally specified $0.20$ mm. This is the famous **"bonus tolerance"** that loosens inspection as the part departs from MMC.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resultant Condition for a Projected Tolerance Zone

**Statement:** A pin of $\phi 12_{-0.2}^{0}$ mm is located in a slot with position tolerance $\phi 0.1$ at MMC Ⓜ, and a **projected tolerance zone of height 5 mm** is specified. Compute the VC for the **functional gauge** that engages the pin at the projection.

### Step 1 — Identify values

- Pin MMC (external, largest): $12.0$ mm
- Position tolerance: $0.1$ mm
- Projection height: $H_p = 5$ mm

### Step 2 — Standard formula

For a **projected** zone on an external feature, the virtual condition becomes:

$$
VC_{\text{projected, external}} = MMC - (T_{\text{position}} + H_p \cdot \theta)
$$

> When the projection is **measured linearly** (e.g., for clearance with a fastened plate), the relevant condition is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VC_{\text{effective}} &= MMC - T_{\text{position}} - (\text{angular deviation} \times H_p)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **purely translational** projection (the common KTU case):

$$
\begin{aligned}
VC_{\text{projected}} &= MMC - T_{\text{position}} \\
&\quad \text{(at the projected plane, angular effect of } \theta = 0 \text{ for axial position)} \\
&= 12.0 - 0.1 = 11.9 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Engineering meaning

The **projected tolerance zone** extends the controlled boundary of the feature *in the direction of projection* — used typically for **threaded fasteners** where the mating hole clearance must be guaranteed at the *far* side of the engaged length, not at the surface.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Composite Position Tolerance (Pattern-Locating vs. Feature-Relating)

> [!NOTE]
> A **composite FCF** has *two* tolerance values separated by a slash — the first controls **pattern location** (e.g., the 4-hole bolt circle as a whole), the second controls each feature within the pattern.

**Example FCF:** $\;|\;\phi\;0.5\;\text{M}\;A\;B\;\;/\;\;\phi\;0.2\;\text{A}\;B\;|$

### Interpretation

- **First segment ($\phi 0.5$ at MMC, A, B):** The *derived median plane / axis of the entire pattern* must lie within a $\phi 0.5$ cylindrical zone relative to A-B.
- **Second segment ($\phi 0.2$ relative to A-B):** Each individual feature must lie within $\phi 0.2$ **relative to the pattern's true position** (not just to A-B).

> This is heavily used in **bolt-pattern tolerancing** for engine blocks, flanges, and aerospace ribs — directly relevant to **PEMRT522 manufacturing design problems**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Input Parameters | Core Formula | Output |
|------------|------------------|--------------|--------|
| VC — Internal (Hole) | $D_{\text{MMC}}, T_{\text{GT}}$ | $VC = MMC + T$ | Envelope diameter |
| VC — External (Shaft) | $D_{\text{MMC}}, T_{\text{GT}}$ | $VC = MMC - T$ | Envelope diameter |
| Bonus — External | $D_{\text{MMC}}, D_{\text{actual}}$ | $\text{Bonus} = MMC - D_{\text{actual}}$ | Extra allowance |
| Bonus — Internal | $D_{\text{MMC}}, D_{\text{actual}}$ | $\text{Bonus} = D_{\text{actual}} - MMC$ | Extra allowance |
| Total Position Tolerance | $T_{\text{spec}}, \text{Bonus}$ | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{spec}} + \text{Bonus}$ | Maximum allowable deviation |
| Projected Zone | $MMC, T, H_p$ | Geometry-dependent | Functional envelope at projection plane |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are **Mermaid** block-level functional architecture flows that map the conceptual structure of Geometric Tolerances — the same way a textbook sketch would. They are used because Mermaid cannot render a literal 2-D drawing of a tolerance zone, but the *flow of information* is faithfully represented.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Classification of GD&T Symbols

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["GD&T Symbols ASME Y14.5"]:::root --> B1["Form Tolerances"]
    A --> B2["Profile Tolerances"]
    A --> B3["Orientation Tolerances"]
    A --> B4["Location Tolerances"]
    A --> B5["Runout Tolerances"]

    B1 --> C1["Straightness"]
    B1 --> C2["Flatness"]
    B1 --> C3["Circularity"]
    B1 --> C4["Cylindricity"]

    B2 --> D1["Profile of a Line"]
    B2 --> D2["Profile of a Surface"]

    B3 --> E1["Parallelism"]
    B3 --> E2["Perpendicularity"]
    B3 --> E3["Angularity"]

    B4 --> F1["True Position"]
    B4 --> F2["Concentricity"]
    B4 --> F3["Symmetry"]

    B5 --> G1["Circular Runout"]
    B5 --> G2["Total Runout"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Feature Control Frame Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Compartments of FCF"]:::head --> B1["1 Geometric Symbol"]
    A --> B2["2 Diameter Symbol"]
    A --> B3["3 Tolerance Value"]
    A --> B4["4 Material Modifier"]
    A --> B5["5 Primary Datum"]
    A --> B6["6 Secondary Datum"]
    A --> B7["7 Tertiary Datum"]

    B4 --> C1["MMC symbol"]
    B4 --> C2["LMC symbol"]
    B4 --> C3["No Symbol RFS"]

    classDef head fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Virtual Condition Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start Identify Feature Type"]:::s --> B{"Internal or External"}
    B -- Internal --> C1["Use MMC of Internal feature Smallest Size"]
    B -- External --> C2["Use MMC of External feature Largest Size"]
    C1 --> D1["Apply VC Internal formula"]
    C2 --> D2["Apply VC External formula"]
    D1 --> E1["VC equals MMC plus Geometric Tolerance"]
    D2 --> E2["VC equals MMC minus Geometric Tolerance"]
    E1 --> F["Result Worst Case Envelope Diameter"]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G["Output Virtual Condition"]

    classDef s fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Datum Reference Frame 3-2-1 Principle

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Part Placement in Inspection"]:::start --> B["Step 1 Establish Primary Datum A"]
    B --> C["3 Contact Points form a Plane"]
    C --> D["Step 2 Establish Secondary Datum B"]
    D --> E["2 Contact Points form a Line"]
    E --> F["Step 3 Establish Tertiary Datum C"]
    F --> G["1 Contact Point forms a Point"]
    G --> H["Complete 3-2-1 Datum Reference Frame DRF"]

    classDef start fill:#bf360c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — MMC vs LMC vs RFS Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Choose Material Condition Modifier"]:::m --> B{"What is the design intent"}
    B -- Assure assembly under worst case --> C1["MMC symbol"]
    B -- Guarantee minimum wall thickness --> C2["LMC symbol"]
    B -- No size based reasoning needed --> C3["RFS no symbol"]
    C1 --> D1["Bonus grows as feature departs from MMC"]
    C2 --> D2["Bonus grows as feature departs from LMC"]
    C3 --> D3["Tolerance is fixed at any size"]

    classDef m fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question, PEMRT522]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"Differentiate between dimensional tolerance and geometric tolerance with two suitable examples."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Dimensional Tolerance | Geometric Tolerance |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Permissible variation in the *size* of a feature | Permissible variation in *form, orientation, location, or run-out* of a feature |
| Controls | Length, diameter, angle (as a size) | Shape, tilt, position, concentricity, etc. |
| Datum required? | No | Yes (except form tolerances) |
| Example 1 | Shaft diameter $\phi 20 \pm 0.1$ mm | Straightness of shaft axis 0.05 mm |
| Example 2 | Hole diameter $\phi 12^{+0.2}_{+0.1}$ mm | Perpendicularity of hole axis 0.05 mm to face A |
| Standard | ISO 286, ASME Y14.5 | ASME Y14.5, ISO 1101 |

*(Any equivalent 3-mark answer with definition, contrast, and one example each is awarded full marks.)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"List the five form tolerances as per ASME Y14.5 and state one industrial application for each."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Straightness** — Used to control the *bow* of long shafts, e.g., line shaft in a rolling mill.
2. **Flatness** — Used on **mating flanges** of pressure vessels and on machine tool beds.
3. **Circularity (Roundness)** — Used on **bearing journals** of electric motors.
4. **Cylindricity** — Used on **hydraulic cylinder bores** and piston sealing surfaces.
5. *(Note: ASME Y14.5 actually lists 4 form tolerances; if the question says "five," it may include a fifth like "Profile of a Line" treated as a form tolerance. State the 4 + clarify.)*

**Refined (as per ASME Y14.5 — 4 form tolerances):**

1. Straightness
2. Flatness
3. Circularity
4. Cylindricity

*(Examiner note: Always specify 4 form tolerances. Profile is a separate family.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question, PEMRT522 Module 2]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choose either A or B)*

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> Explain the concept of **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** and **Least Material Condition (LMC)** with neat sketches. A shaft is specified at $\phi 30_{-0.3}^{0}$ mm with a position tolerance of $\phi 0.2$ mm at MMC relative to datum A. If the shaft is produced at $29.80$ mm, calculate the **bonus tolerance** and the **total permissible geometric tolerance**.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (4 marks):**

- **MMC:** The condition in which a feature contains the *maximum* amount of material. For a shaft = **largest size**; for a hole = **smallest size**. It is the boundary of *worst-case interference* during assembly.
- **LMC:** The condition with the *least* material. Shaft = smallest size; hole = largest size. Used to control **minimum wall thickness** and **clearance**.

*(A sketch showing a shaft with its MMC and LMC envelopes must be drawn — boxes around the feature indicating the size limits and the cylindrical tolerance zone.)*

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given:

- Shaft MMC = $30.0$ mm (largest, since external)
- Actual size = $29.80$ mm
- Specified position tolerance $T_{\text{spec}} = 0.2$ mm

Step 1 — Bonus tolerance (external feature):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bonus} &= MMC - \text{Actual Size} \\
&= 30.0 - 29.80 \\
&= 0.20 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Total permissible tolerance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{spec}} + \text{Bonus} \\
&= 0.2 + 0.20 \\
&= 0.40 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Bonus = **$0.20$ mm**, Total tolerance = **$0.40$ mm**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> Define **Virtual Condition (VC)**. Compute the VC of the shaft in part (a). Also, compute the VC if the same shaft had been a **hole** instead of a shaft, with all other values identical.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Virtual Condition is a *theoretical boundary* of perfect form generated by the collective effect of MMC size and the applicable geometric tolerance. It defines the **worst-case envelope** that must clear or be cleared by a mating part.

**VC of the shaft (external) — 2.5 marks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VC_{\text{external}} &= MMC - T_{\text{position}} \\
&= 30.0 - 0.2 \\
&= 29.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**VC of the same as a hole (internal) — 2.5 marks:**

For a hole, MMC = smallest = $30.0$ mm (assume same limits).

$$
\begin{aligned}
VC_{\text{internal}} &= MMC + T_{\text{position}} \\
&= 30.0 + 0.2 \\
&= 30.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The internal virtual condition is **larger** than the external one because the geometric tolerance *adds* to the envelope for a hole but *subtracts* for a shaft.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative choice)*

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **Feature Control Frame (FCF)**. Construct an FCF for a hole of $\phi 25^{+0.3}_{+0.1}$ mm with a position tolerance of $\phi 0.15$ mm at MMC relative to datums A, B, and C. Also state the **Virtual Condition** of this hole.

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram of FCF (3 marks):**

> A rectangular box divided into **compartments** as:
> `| Symbol | ⌀ | Tolerance | Modifier | Datum A | Datum B | Datum C |`

**Construction (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{\;|\;\oplus\;\;\phi\;0.15\;\;\text{M}\;\;A\;\;B\;\;C\;|\;}
$$

Where:
- $\oplus$ = True Position symbol
- $\phi$ = Cylindrical tolerance zone
- $0.15$ = Tolerance value
- $\text{M}$ = Maximum Material Condition modifier
- $A$ = Primary datum, $B$ = Secondary datum, $C$ = Tertiary datum

**Virtual Condition (2 marks):**

- Hole MMC (smallest) = $25.1$ mm
- $T_{\text{position}} = 0.15$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
VC_{\text{hole}} &= MMC + T_{\text{position}} \\
&= 25.1 + 0.15 \\
&= 25.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> What is **run-out tolerance**? Differentiate between **circular run-out** and **total run-out** with diagrams. A grinding wheel is tested on a spindle: the dial indicator reads a maximum deviation of $0.08$ mm over one revolution. Is the wheel acceptable if the specified circular run-out is $0.05$ mm?

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Run-out is a composite geometric tolerance that simultaneously controls **form, orientation, and location** of a feature relative to a datum axis, as the part is rotated about that axis.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Circular Run-out | Total Run-out |
|--------|------------------|---------------|
| Controls | Single circular cross-section (form) | Entire surface (form + orientation + location) |
| Revolution | One revolution of the part | Multiple revolutions (one per cross-section) |
| Use | Quick checks, bearings | High-precision shafts, turbine rotors |
| Symbol | Single arrow (↗) | Double arrow (↗↗) |

**Numerical (2 marks):**

- Measured run-out: $0.08$ mm
- Allowed: $0.05$ mm

$$
0.08 > 0.05 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Part is NOT acceptable (Rejected).}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE (Module 2 — Geometric Tolerances):**
> 1. **Mixing up MMC and LMC** — for an external feature, MMC = *largest*; for an internal feature, MMC = *smallest*. Many students write the wrong sign in the VC formula.
> 2. **Forgetting to add the diameter symbol (⌀) in FCF** for cylindrical zones — the KTU model answer key explicitly checks for $\phi$ before the tolerance value.
> 3. **Sign errors in bonus tolerance** — always use the absolute departure from MMC, not raw subtraction.
> 4. **Treating form tolerances as requiring datums** — form tolerances are datum-less by definition.
> 5. **Confusing parallelism with position** — parallelism orients the *axis*; position locates the *point* along the axis.
> 6. **Missing units in the final answer** — every numerical answer must include the unit (mm) to fetch the final mark.
> 7. **Skipping the sketch in part (a)** — at least 2–3 marks are reserved for the diagram in any FCF / MMC / run-out question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density, rapid-revision checklist:**

- **GD&T standard for KTU 2024:** **ASME Y14.5-2018** (with awareness of ISO 1101).
- **5 Families:** Form, Profile, Orientation, Location, Run-out — in that complexity order.
- **4 Form tolerances** (no datum needed): Straightness, Flatness, Circularity, Cylindricity.
- **3 Orientation tolerances** (datum needed): Parallelism ∥, Perpendicularity ⊥, Angularity ∠.
- **3 Location tolerances** (datum needed): True Position ⊕, Concentricity ◎, Symmetry ⌯.
- **2 Run-out tolerances** (datum needed): Circular ↗, Total ↗↗.
- **Feature Control Frame order:** Symbol → ⌀ (if cylindrical) → Tolerance → Modifier (Ⓜ/Ⓛ/none) → Datum A → Datum B → Datum C.
- **VC External:** $VC = MMC - T$.
- **VC Internal:** $VC = MMC + T$.
- **Bonus External:** $MMC - \text{Actual}$.
- **Bonus Internal:** $\text{Actual} - MMC$.
- **Total Tolerance:** $T_{\text{spec}} + \text{Bonus}$.
- **DRF = 3-2-1 principle** (3 primary points, 2 secondary, 1 tertiary).
- **MMC modifier** = maximum material = worst-case assembly interference.
- **LMC modifier** = least material = minimum wall thickness / maximum clearance.
- **RFS** = default in ASME, no bonus tolerance.
- **Projected tolerance zone** = extends the controlled boundary in the direction of projection (used for threaded fasteners).
- **Composite FCF** = two segments separated by `/` for pattern-locating + feature-relating.
- **Run-out measurement** uses a dial indicator with the part rotated about a datum axis.
- **Always draw a sketch** of the FCF, MMC/LMC envelopes, or tolerance zone in KTU answers — it earns 2–3 easy marks.
- **Always state units (mm)** in the final answer.
- **GD&T controls *how* a part is shaped; dimensional tolerance controls *how big* a part is.** Never confuse the two.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Assembly Limits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assembly Limits** are the extreme permissible values (maximum and minimum) of a resultant dimension — typically a **clearance** or an **interference** — that is obtained when two or more toleranced components are assembled together. They define the functional boundary of the assembled product and are derived from the individual tolerances of the mating parts.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Assembly Limits* is the gateway concept connecting **Dimensional Metrology (Module 1)** with **Tolerance Stack-Up Analysis and Statistical Tolerancing (Module 2)**. Every mating pair (e.g., a shaft inside a hole) yields a resultant dimension known as the **assembly clearance** or **assembly interference**, and its upper and lower bounds are the *assembly limits*.

Mathematically, for a simple shaft-in-hole pair:

$$\text{Assembly Clearance} \; C = D_{\text{hole}} - d_{\text{shaft}}$$

The **limits** are:

$$C_{\max} = D_{\max} - d_{\min} \quad ; \quad C_{\min} = D_{\min} - d_{\max}$$

where:
- $D_{\max}, D_{\min}$ are the maximum and minimum hole diameters
- $d_{\max}, d_{\min}$ are the maximum and minimum shaft diameters

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT522 – Module 2):** The student must be able to compute assembly limits under both **Arithmetic (Worst-Case) Tolerancing** and **Statistical Tolerancing** philosophies, and predict the resulting **type of fit** (Clearance, Interference, or Transition) before manufacture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket and Tumbler" Picture

Imagine you have a **bucket (hole)** and a **tumbler lid (shaft)** that must fit into the bucket. The bucket is manufactured with a diameter between 50.00 mm and 50.10 mm. The tumbler is manufactured with a diameter between 49.90 mm and 50.00 mm. 

- If the bucket is at its **largest (50.10)** and the tumbler is at its **smallest (49.90)**, the lid falls in loosely — this is the **maximum clearance (0.20 mm)**.
- If the bucket is at its **smallest (50.00)** and the tumbler is at its **largest (50.00)**, they touch with **zero clearance**.
- Any other combination gives clearance between 0.00 and 0.20 mm.

These two extreme outcomes are the **Assembly Limits** of the system. The designer must ensure that *any* combination of tolerances within the manufactured population still produces a functional assembly — whether that means a sliding fit, a press fit, or a locational fit.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The three most important categorical metrics for assembly limits are:

1. **Fundamental Deviation** — the deviation of the limit nearest to the basic size (defines the position of the tolerance zone).
2. **Tolerance Grade (IT)** — specified in **ISO 286-1** as IT01, IT0, IT1 … IT18 (in microns). The KTU Module 2 design problems most frequently use **IT6, IT7, IT8, and IT11**.
3. **Hole-Basis vs Shaft-Basis System** — the universal convention of fixing either the hole or the shaft as the reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **Hole-Basis System**, the hole is always the **H-type** (lower deviation = 0). In the **Shaft-Basis System**, the shaft is always the **h-type** (upper deviation = 0). For KTU 2024 problems, the **Hole-Basis System** is the default unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Assembly Limit Calculation

The fundamental question answered by assembly limit analysis is:

> *"Given the individual tolerances of components, what is the range of variation in the assembled dimension?"*

This is solved through **tolerance chain (stack-up) analysis** in two distinct philosophical frameworks.

### 2.1.1 Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Tolerance Method

This is the conservative, deterministic method preferred for **safety-critical assemblies** (e.g., brake assemblies, aerospace components, biomedical implants).

**Logic Steps:**
1. Identify the resultant dimension $R$ (clearance, gap, or interference).
2. Express $R$ as a linear algebraic function of individual component dimensions $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$.
3. The **maximum resultant** is obtained by adding the **maximum** values of the *increasing* dimensions and the **minimum** values of the *decreasing* dimensions.
4. The **minimum resultant** is obtained by the reverse operation.

For a generic dimension chain:

$$R = x_1 - x_2 + x_3 - x_4 + \dots$$

$$R_{\max} = x_{1,\max} - x_{2,\min} + x_{3,\max} - x_{4,\min} + \dots$$

$$R_{\min} = x_{1,\min} - x_{2,\max} + x_{3,\min} - x_{4,\max} + \dots$$

The total tolerance on the assembly is:

$$T_R = R_{\max} - R_{\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert t_i \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** The worst-case method guarantees **100 % functional assemblies** — no mating part will ever violate the design intent. The trade-off is **higher manufacturing cost** because each component must be held to a tighter individual tolerance.

### 2.1.2 Statistical (Probabilistic) Tolerance Method

This is the economical, probability-based method used for **mass-produced assemblies** (e.g., consumer electronics, automotive sub-assemblies).

**Logic Steps:**
1. Assume the dimensions of each manufactured component follow a **normal (Gaussian) distribution** with mean $\mu_i$ and standard deviation $\sigma_i$.
2. The resultant dimension is also normally distributed.
3. For **independent** random dimensions, variances add.

For the **Root-Sum-Squares (RSS)** approach:

$$\sigma_R^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2$$

$$\sigma_R = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$$

The assembly tolerance is then expressed at a chosen confidence level $k$ (e.g., $k = 3$ corresponds to **99.73 %** of assemblies under the $\pm 3\sigma$ rule):

$$T_R = 2k\sigma_R = 2k\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus explicitly mandates that students **derive and apply** the RSS equation. It is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark derivation problems in the University Examination.

## 2.2 Resultant Type of Fit — The KTU Decision Tree

Once the assembly limits are computed, the type of fit is determined by the relationship between $C_{\max}$ and $C_{\min}$:

| Condition | Type of Fit | Functional Use |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{\min} > 0$ | **Clearance Fit** | Sliding, rotating, easy assembly |
| $C_{\max} < 0$ (i.e., negative) | **Interference Fit** | Permanent joints, press-fits |
| $C_{\min} \leq 0 \leq C_{\max}$ | **Transition Fit** | Locational, light interference / clearance |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Assembly Clearance (basic) | $C = D_{\text{hole}} - d_{\text{shaft}}$ | mm |
| 2 | Maximum Clearance (Worst Case) | $C_{\max} = D_{\max} - d_{\min}$ | mm |
| 3 | Minimum Clearance (Worst Case) | $C_{\min} = D_{\min} - d_{\max}$ | mm |
| 4 | Total Clearance Tolerance | $T_C = C_{\max} - C_{\min} = t_D + t_d$ | mm |
| 5 | Maximum Interference | $I_{\max} = d_{\max} - D_{\min}$ | mm |
| 6 | Minimum Interference | $I_{\min} = d_{\min} - D_{\max}$ | mm |
| 7 | Multi-component Worst-Case Tolerance | $T_R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert t_i \vert$ | mm |
| 8 | Standard Deviation of Resultant (RSS) | $\sigma_R = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$ | mm |
| 9 | Statistical Assembly Tolerance | $T_R = 2k\sigma_R$ | mm (k = 3 for 99.73 %) |
| 10 | Relation between $\sigma$ and tolerance $t$ (uniform dist.) | $\sigma_i = t_i / \sqrt{12}$ | mm |
| 11 | Relation between $\sigma$ and tolerance $t$ (normal dist., $\pm 3\sigma$) | $\sigma_i = t_i / 6$ | mm |
| 12 | Hole-Basis Lower Deviation (H-type) | $EI = 0$ | mm |
| 13 | Shaft-Basis Upper Deviation (h-type) | $es = 0$ | mm |
| 14 | ISO Tolerance Grade (IT grade) | $T = a \cdot i$ | microns (i = standard tolerance unit) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Assembly limit analysis is the *backbone* of Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA). In the automotive industry, statistical tolerancing saves millions of dollars annually by allowing wider, cheaper individual tolerances while still maintaining overall assembly function. In aerospace, worst-case tolerancing is mandatory for life-critical joints.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Two Methods — A Designer's Dilemma

A student often asks: *"Which method should I use?"* The answer depends on three engineering constraints:

1. **Safety requirement** → If failure causes injury, use *Worst-Case*.
2. **Production volume** → If > 10,000 units/year, statistical savings dominate.
3. **Component interchangeability** → Both methods assume that any component from the population must work with any other component from the population (**complete interchangeability**).

A **combined approach** is also used: statistical tolerance for routine dimensions, and worst-case for critical functional dimensions (e.g., the fit between a piston pin and the connecting rod).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Worst-Case Assembly Limits for a Multi-Component Dimension Chain

**Problem Setup:** Consider a linear assembly consisting of $n$ components arranged in series. Let the basic size of component $i$ be $N_i$, with upper deviation $\Delta_i^{U}$ and lower deviation $\Delta_i^{L}$. Define the **deviation variable** $\delta_i = \Delta_i^{U} - \Delta_i^{L} = t_i$ (tolerance of the $i$-th component).

**Objective:** Derive the maximum and minimum values of the resultant dimension $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i$.

### Step 1 — Write the resultant in terms of limits

The resultant dimension $R$ takes the form:

$$R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i$$

The maximum possible value occurs when every component is at its **upper limit** $N_i + \Delta_i^{U}$:

$$R_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (N_i + \Delta_i^{U})$$

The minimum possible value occurs when every component is at its **lower limit** $N_i + \Delta_i^{L}$:

$$R_{\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (N_i + \Delta_i^{L})$$

### Step 2 — Subtract to obtain the total assembly tolerance

The total tolerance on $R$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_R &= R_{\max} - R_{\min} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (N_i + \Delta_i^{U}) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} (N_i + \Delta_i^{L}) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ (N_i + \Delta_i^{U}) - (N_i + \Delta_i^{L}) \right] \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\Delta_i^{U} - \Delta_i^{L}) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each $N_i$ cancels because the basic size is common to both the upper and lower limit expressions. What remains is the sum of the individual tolerances.

### Step 3 — Generalise to a chain with mixed signs

If the resultant is $R = x_1 - x_2 + x_3 - x_4 + \dots$, then:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= x_{1,\max} - x_{2,\min} + x_{3,\max} - x_{4,\min} + \dots \\
R_{\min} &= x_{1,\min} - x_{2,\max} + x_{3,\min} - x_{4,\max} + \dots
\end{aligned}$$

The total tolerance is still:

$$T_R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert t_i \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** The total assembly tolerance is **additive in magnitude**. Adding more components to a chain **widens** the assembly limits — a fundamental reason why modular designs with shorter tolerance chains are preferred.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Statistical Assembly Limits using Root Sum of Squares (RSS)

**Problem Setup:** Suppose each component dimension $x_i$ is an independent random variable with mean $\mu_i$ and standard deviation $\sigma_i$. We want the statistical assembly limits of the resultant $R = x_1 - x_2 + x_3 - x_4 + \dots$

### Step 1 — Recall the property of variance under addition/subtraction

For **independent** random variables, variances add regardless of the sign of the coefficient:

$$\text{Var}(x_1 \pm x_2) = \text{Var}(x_1) + \text{Var}(x_2) \quad \text{(if independent)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the property to the resultant

The variance of the resultant $R$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_R^2 &= \text{Var}(R) \\
&= \text{Var}(x_1) + \text{Var}(x_2) + \text{Var}(x_3) + \text{Var}(x_4) + \dots \\
&= \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 + \sigma_4^2 + \dots \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Take the square root to obtain the resultant standard deviation

$$\sigma_R = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$$

This is the **Root-Sum-Squares (RSS)** formula.

### Step 4 — Express the assembly limits in terms of a confidence factor $k$

The mean of the resultant is:

$$\mu_R = \mu_1 - \mu_2 + \mu_3 - \mu_4 + \dots$$

The statistical assembly limits are:

$$R_{\max} = \mu_R + k\sigma_R \quad ; \quad R_{\min} = \mu_R - k\sigma_R$$

The total statistical tolerance is:

$$T_R = 2k\sigma_R = 2k\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$$

### Step 5 — Convert individual tolerances to standard deviations

If a component's tolerance is $t_i$ and the distribution is **uniform** across the tolerance band:

$$\sigma_i = \frac{t_i}{\sqrt{12}}$$

If the distribution is **normal** within the tolerance band, applying the **$\pm 3\sigma$ rule** (covering 99.73 % of the population):

$$\sigma_i = \frac{t_i}{6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $\pm 3\sigma$ rule?** Empirical studies in manufacturing show that the actual distribution of dimensions is closer to a normal distribution truncated at the tolerance limits. The $\pm 3\sigma$ model gives a reasonable, industry-standard estimate.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU 2024 Style

**Problem Statement:**

A hole of nominal diameter $D = 50.000$ mm is to mate with a shaft. The hole has a tolerance of $+0.025 / 0.000$ mm (H7 fit). The shaft has a tolerance of $-0.025 / -0.050$ mm (k6 fit). 

*(a) Determine the assembly limits (clearance/interference) and identify the type of fit.*

*(b) If three additional spacers of nominal length 20.000 mm, each with a tolerance of $\pm 0.05$ mm, are added in series with the shaft, compute the new total assembly limits using: (i) Worst-case method, (ii) Statistical method (assume $\pm 3\sigma$ distribution).*

### Solution Part (a) — Simple Two-Part Assembly

**Step 1 — Identify the limits of the hole:**

$$D_{\max} = 50.000 + 0.025 = 50.025 \text{ mm}$$
$$D_{\min} = 50.000 + 0.000 = 50.000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the limits of the shaft:**

$$d_{\max} = 50.000 - 0.025 = 49.975 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_{\min} = 50.000 - 0.050 = 49.950 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum clearance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\max} &= D_{\max} - d_{\min} \\
&= 50.025 - 49.950 \\
&= 0.075 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the minimum clearance (which is actually the maximum interference):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\min} &= D_{\min} - d_{\max} \\
&= 50.000 - 49.975 \\
&= +0.025 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

Since $C_{\min} = +0.025$ mm **and** $C_{\max} = +0.075$ mm, **both clearance values are positive**. This is therefore a **Clearance Fit** with assembly limits of **+0.025 mm to +0.075 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** A positive value for $C$ means the hole is larger than the shaft at that combination — clearance. A negative value would have meant interference. Here, even the worst combination (smallest hole, largest shaft) still leaves 0.025 mm of clearance.

### Solution Part (b) — Multi-Component Assembly

The three spacers each have:
$$L_{\max} = 20.000 + 0.05 = 20.050 \text{ mm} \quad ; \quad L_{\min} = 20.000 - 0.05 = 19.950 \text{ mm}$$

The total spacer stack length:
- Basic: $L_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 20.000 = 60.000$ mm
- Spacer tolerance: $t_L = 3 \times 0.10 = 0.30$ mm (per spacer $\pm 0.05$)

#### (i) Worst-Case Method

The total assembly is now $D - d - L_{\text{total}}$. Using the additive rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{assembly}} &= t_D + t_d + t_{L_{\text{total}}} \\
&= 0.025 + 0.025 + 0.300 \\
&= 0.350 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

The basic assembly dimension (clearance before spacers) is $C = 50.000 - 50.000 = 0.000$ mm. But since the spacers are inserted in series with the shaft, they reduce the available clearance by their total length variability.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Resultant} &= D - d - L_{\text{total}} \\
\text{Basic value} &= 50.000 - 50.000 - 60.000 = -60.000 \text{ mm (interference-like reference)}
\end{aligned}$$

The limits of the resultant:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= D_{\max} - d_{\min} - L_{\text{total,min}} = 50.025 - 49.950 - 59.850 = -59.775 \text{ mm} \\
R_{\min} &= D_{\min} - d_{\max} - L_{\text{total,max}} = 50.000 - 49.975 - 60.150 = -61.125 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Total assembly tolerance: $T_R = -59.775 - (-61.125) = 1.350$ mm.

> **Conversion Logic:** The spacer stack is a *subtractive* term in the chain, so the spacer tolerance adds to the assembly tolerance but in the "interference-direction". The negative signs confirm that the absolute length of the assembly is now ~60 mm, and the assembly tolerance is 1.350 mm.

#### (ii) Statistical Method (using $\pm 3\sigma$)

For each component, $\sigma_i = t_i / 6$:

$$\sigma_D = \frac{0.025}{6} = 0.00417 \text{ mm}$$
$$\sigma_d = \frac{0.025}{6} = 0.00417 \text{ mm}$$
$$\sigma_L = \frac{0.300}{6} = 0.05000 \text{ mm}$$

Apply RSS formula with $k = 3$ (99.73 % confidence):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_R &= \sqrt{\sigma_D^2 + \sigma_d^2 + \sigma_L^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(0.00417)^2 + (0.00417)^2 + (0.05000)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.0000174 + 0.0000174 + 0.0025000} \\
&= \sqrt{0.0025348} \\
&= 0.05035 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Total statistical assembly tolerance:

$$T_R^{\text{stat}} = 2k\sigma_R = 2 \times 3 \times 0.05035 = 0.3021 \text{ mm}$$

> **Comparison Insight:** The statistical method yields $T_R = 0.302$ mm, which is **less than one-quarter** of the worst-case value (1.350 mm). However, the statistical result covers **99.73 %** of assemblies, meaning 0.27 % of assemblies may fall outside the limits. This trade-off — cost vs. yield — is the heart of statistical tolerancing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Does Each Method Apply?

| Criterion | Worst-Case | Statistical (RSS) |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical basis | Deterministic / arithmetic | Probabilistic / variance sum |
| Coverage of population | 100 % | $2k\sigma$ (typically 99.73 %) |
| Total tolerance | Sum of individual tolerances | $\approx 1/\sqrt{n}$ of worst-case for equal $t_i$ |
| Cost of manufacture | Higher (tighter parts needed) | Lower (wider parts acceptable) |
| Best application | Safety-critical, low volume | High volume, non-critical |
| KTU exam weightage | Always tested | Heavily tested in 14-mark derivations |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Assembly Limit Computation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Mating Pair / Dimension Chain] --> B{Identify Resultant Dimension R}
    B --> C[R = Hole Dia - Shaft Dia = Clearance]
    B --> D[R = Shaft Dia - Hole Dia = Interference]
    B --> E[R = Linear Sum of N Components]
    C --> F[Extract Dmax, Dmin, dmax, dmin from Drawing]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Extract N_i Upper and Lower Limits]
    F --> H{Choose Tolerance Method}
    G --> H
    H --> I[Worst-Case Arithmetic]
    H --> J[Statistical RSS]
    I --> K[Compute Rmax and Rmin by adding or subtracting extremes]
    J --> L[Compute sigma_R = sqrt sum sigma_i squared]
    L --> M[Apply k factor, typically k = 3]
    M --> N[Compute Statistical Rmax = mu_R + k sigma_R]
    N --> O[Compute Statistical Rmin = mu_R - k sigma_R]
    K --> P{Determine Type of Fit}
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Cmin greater than 0: Clearance Fit]
    P --> R[Cmax less than 0: Interference Fit]
    P --> S[Cmin less than or equal to 0 less than or equal to Cmax: Transition Fit]
    Q --> T[Document Assembly Limits and Functional Check]
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Tolerance Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Tolerances]
        X1[Component 1: x1 with t1]
        X2[Component 2: x2 with t2]
        X3[Component 3: x3 with t3]
        XN[Component N: xN with tN]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Tolerance Stack Processor]
        WC[Worst-Case Engine: T_R = sum t_i]
        ST[Statistical Engine: sigma_R = sqrt sum sigma_i squared]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Assembly Limits Output]
        LO[Lower Limit Rmin]
        HI[Upper Limit Rmax]
        TT[Total Assembly Tolerance T_R]
        FT[Type of Fit Identifier]
    end
    X1 --> WC
    X2 --> WC
    X3 --> WC
    XN --> WC
    X1 --> ST
    X2 --> ST
    X3 --> ST
    XN --> ST
    WC --> LO
    WC --> HI
    WC --> TT
    WC --> FT
    ST --> LO
    ST --> HI
    ST --> TT
    ST --> FT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Type of Fit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Cmax and Cmin] --> B{Cmin greater than 0?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Clearance Fit: Cmin to Cmax all positive]
    B -- No --> D{Cmax less than 0?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Interference Fit: Cmax to Cmin all negative]
    D -- No --> F[Transition Fit: Cmin less than or equal to 0 less than or equal to Cmax]
    C --> G1[Example: Sliding Shaft in Bearing]
    E --> G2[Example: Press-Fit Gear on Shaft]
    F --> G3[Example: Locational Pin in Plate]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — A Multi-Component Tolerance Chain

```
   <-------- Resultant R = D - d - L1 - L2 - L3 -------->
   
   +---------+   +-------+   +-------+   +-------+   +-------+
   |   HOLE  | - | SHAFT | - | SPACER| - | SPACER| - | SPACER|
   |   (D)   |   |  (d)  |   |  (L1) |   |  (L2) |   |  (L3) |
   +---------+   +-------+   +-------+   +-------+   +-------+
     ±t_D/2       ±t_d/2      ±t_L/2      ±t_L/2      ±t_L/2
     
   Worst-case:  T_R = t_D + t_d + 3 * t_L
   Statistical: sigma_R = sqrt(sigma_D² + sigma_d² + 3 * sigma_L²)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The ASCII schematic above illustrates the canonical **5-component tolerance chain** that frequently appears in KTU Module 2 university exam questions. Recognising this chain pattern is the first step to rapid problem-solving.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
Define the term *Assembly Limits*. Differentiate between *maximum clearance* and *minimum clearance* in a shaft-hole assembly with a suitable diagram.

**Model Answer:**

*Assembly limits* are the extreme permissible values of a resultant dimension (typically a clearance or interference) obtained when toleranced components are assembled.

- **Maximum clearance ($C_{\max}$):** Occurs when the hole is at its **largest** permissible size and the shaft is at its **smallest** permissible size. $C_{\max} = D_{\max} - d_{\min}$
- **Minimum clearance ($C_{\min}$):** Occurs when the hole is at its **smallest** permissible size and the shaft is at its **largest** permissible size. $C_{\min} = D_{\min} - d_{\max}$

A simple sketch shows the shaft nested inside the hole, with the radial gap between them being the clearance.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
What is the fundamental difference between *worst-case (arithmetic)* tolerancing and *statistical* tolerancing in the context of assembly limits?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Worst-Case | Statistical |
|---|---|---|
| Total tolerance | $T_R = \sum \vert t_i \vert$ | $T_R = 2k\sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ |
| Population coverage | 100 % of assemblies | $2k\sigma$ (typically 99.73 %) |
| Manufacturing cost | High (tight individual tolerances) | Low (looser individual tolerances) |
| Mathematical basis | Deterministic | Probabilistic (assumes normal distribution) |

The worst-case method **guarantees** functional assemblies, while the statistical method **trades off a small yield loss** for significant cost reduction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Original Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the statistical assembly tolerance of a multi-component dimension chain using the **Root-Sum-Squares (RSS)** method, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of nominal diameter 60.000 mm is to be fitted into a hole. The hole tolerance is $+0.030 / 0.000$ mm and the shaft tolerance is $+0.020 / +0.002$ mm. Determine:
- (i) The maximum and minimum clearance / interference.
- (ii) The type of fit obtained.
- (iii) The total tolerance on the assembly. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Derivation

**Assumption 1:** Each component dimension $x_i$ is a **random variable** following a **normal (Gaussian) distribution** with mean $\mu_i$ and standard deviation $\sigma_i$.

**Assumption 2:** The component dimensions are **statistically independent** of one another.

**Assumption 3:** The resultant dimension $R$ is a linear function:

$$R = \pm x_1 \pm x_2 \pm \dots \pm x_n$$

**Variance Addition Rule:** For independent random variables, the variance of a sum (or difference) is the sum of the individual variances.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(R) &= \text{Var}(\pm x_1) + \text{Var}(\pm x_2) + \dots + \text{Var}(\pm x_n) \\
\sigma_R^2 &= \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \dots + \sigma_n^2 \\
\sigma_R &= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}
\end{aligned}$$

The **mean** of the resultant is:

$$\mu_R = \pm \mu_1 \pm \mu_2 \pm \dots \pm \mu_n$$

The **statistical assembly limits** at a confidence level of $k$ standard deviations:

$$R_{\max} = \mu_R + k\sigma_R \quad ; \quad R_{\min} = \mu_R - k\sigma_R$$

The **statistical assembly tolerance** is:

$$\boxed{T_R = R_{\max} - R_{\min} = 2k\sigma_R = 2k\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}}$$

Typically, $k = 3$ is used in industry, corresponding to a 99.73 % confidence interval.

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical

**Step 1 — Extract limits:**

Hole: $D_{\max} = 60.030$ mm, $D_{\min} = 60.000$ mm
Shaft: $d_{\max} = 60.020$ mm, $d_{\min} = 60.002$ mm

**Step 2 — Compute (i) Maximum clearance:**

$$C_{\max} = D_{\max} - d_{\min} = 60.030 - 60.002 = +0.028 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute (i) Minimum clearance:**

$$C_{\min} = D_{\min} - d_{\max} = 60.000 - 60.020 = -0.020 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute (ii) Type of fit:**

Since $C_{\min} = -0.020$ mm (negative = interference) and $C_{\max} = +0.028$ mm (positive = clearance), the limits **straddle zero**. This is a **Transition Fit**.

**Step 5 — Compute (iii) Total tolerance:**

$$T_R = C_{\max} - C_{\min} = 0.028 - (-0.020) = 0.048 \text{ mm}$$

Equivalently: $T_R = t_D + t_d = 0.030 + 0.018 = 0.048$ mm. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Original Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the three types of fits — **clearance fit**, **interference fit**, and **transition fit** — and state one engineering application of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A precision assembly consists of **four components in series** with basic sizes 30.000 mm, 20.000 mm, 15.000 mm, and 25.000 mm. The tolerances on these components are $\pm 0.04$ mm, $\pm 0.03$ mm, $\pm 0.02$ mm, and $\pm 0.05$ mm respectively. Calculate the assembly limits by:
- (i) The **worst-case (arithmetic)** method.
- (ii) The **statistical (RSS)** method using $k = 3$ and the $\pm 3\sigma$ distribution model. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Three Types of Fits

**1. Clearance Fit (Loose Fit):**

The clearance is **always positive** for every possible combination of hole and shaft sizes. The smallest hole is still larger than the largest shaft.

$$C_{\min} = D_{\min} - d_{\max} > 0$$

*Sketches:*
```
   HOLE    |<- D ->|
   ________  
  |        |   SHAFT    |<- d ->|
  |        |   ______ 
  |        |  |      |
  |________|  |______|
       <--- C = D - d --->
```
**Engineering application:** Sliding fit between a piston pin and the gudgeon pin in an internal combustion engine; bearing sleeves on shafts.

---

**2. Interference Fit (Force / Shrink Fit):**

The clearance is **always negative** for every possible combination. The largest shaft is larger than the smallest hole — they must be forced together.

$$C_{\max} = D_{\max} - d_{\min} < 0 \quad (\text{interference} \; I_{\min} = d_{\min} - D_{\max} > 0)$$

**Engineering application:** Mounting of a gear or a railway wheel on a shaft; permanent bearing assemblies in turbochargers.

---

**3. Transition Fit:**

The clearance **changes sign** depending on the actual manufactured dimensions. Some assemblies will have clearance; others will have interference.

$$C_{\min} \leq 0 \leq C_{\max}$$

**Engineering application:** Locational fits where accurate centering is required, with the ability to assemble and disassemble — e.g., a coupling hub on a motor shaft, dowel pins in jigs and fixtures.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Multi-Component Calculation

**Given data:**

| Component | Basic Size (mm) | Tolerance (mm) | $\sigma_i = t_i / 6$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30.000 | $\pm 0.04$ | $0.04/6 = 0.00667$ |
| 2 | 20.000 | $\pm 0.03$ | $0.03/6 = 0.00500$ |
| 3 | 15.000 | $\pm 0.02$ | $0.02/6 = 0.00333$ |
| 4 | 25.000 | $\pm 0.05$ | $0.05/6 = 0.00833$ |

**Total basic size:**
$$N_R = 30 + 20 + 15 + 25 = 90.000 \text{ mm}$$

#### (i) Worst-Case Method

The maximum and minimum resultant dimensions:

$$R_{\max} = 90.000 + 0.04 + 0.03 + 0.02 + 0.05 = 90.140 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_{\min} = 90.000 - 0.04 - 0.03 - 0.02 - 0.05 = 89.860 \text{ mm}$$

Total tolerance:

$$T_R^{\text{WC}} = \sum t_i = 0.04 + 0.03 + 0.02 + 0.05 = \boxed{0.140 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each component is independent, so the maximum of the sum is the sum of the maxima, and the minimum of the sum is the sum of the minima.

#### (ii) Statistical Method (RSS, $k = 3$)

**Step 1 — Sum of variances:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_R^2 &= (0.00667)^2 + (0.00500)^2 + (0.00333)^2 + (0.00833)^2 \\
&= 0.0000444 + 0.0000250 + 0.0000111 + 0.0000694 \\
&= 0.0001499 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Take the square root:**

$$\sigma_R = \sqrt{0.0001499} = 0.01225 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the confidence factor $k = 3$:**

$$T_R^{\text{stat}} = 2 \times 3 \times 0.01225 = \boxed{0.0735 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Comparison:** The statistical tolerance (0.0735 mm) is approximately **52.5 %** of the worst-case tolerance (0.140 mm). This represents the cost-saving potential of statistical tolerancing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Assembly Limit Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in Minimum Clearance:** Students frequently compute $C_{\min}$ as $D_{\min} - d_{\min}$ (incorrect) instead of $D_{\min} - d_{\max}$ (correct). This leads to a wrong fit classification. **Always remember:** to *minimise* the clearance, make the hole *smallest* and the shaft *largest*.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Interference vs Clearance:** A negative "clearance" value is actually an **interference** value. Some students lose 1-2 marks by mislabelling the result. State explicitly: *"The value is negative, indicating interference of magnitude … mm."*
> 
> 3. **Mixing Up Statistical and Worst-Case Formulas:** In RSS, you add **variances** ($\sigma^2$), not standard deviations. Squaring is non-negotiable. $\sigma_R = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$, not $\sum \sigma_i$.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the Factor of 2:** Statistical assembly tolerance = $2k\sigma_R$, not $k\sigma_R$. Both the upper and lower limits are $k\sigma_R$ away from the mean — total spread is twice that.
> 
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** ISO tolerance grades (IT6, IT7…) are in **microns** in the standard tables, but your diameter is in **mm**. Always convert. KTU 2024 valuation strictly checks unit conversions.
> 
> 6. **Skipping Assumptions in Derivations:** In the 7-mark RSS derivation, **explicitly list the three assumptions** (normal distribution, independence, linear chain). Examiners award 1-2 marks specifically for assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Assembly Limits**
> 
> ✅ **Core Definitions:**
> - Assembly limits = extreme values of a resultant dimension after assembly.
> - $C_{\max} = D_{\max} - d_{\min}$ (maximum clearance).
> - $C_{\min} = D_{\min} - d_{\max}$ (minimum clearance / can be negative = interference).
> - $I_{\max} = d_{\max} - D_{\min}$ (maximum interference).
> - $I_{\min} = d_{\min} - D_{\max}$ (minimum interference).
> 
> ✅ **Worst-Case Formula:**
> - $T_R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert t_i \vert$ → 100 % population coverage.
> 
> ✅ **Statistical RSS Formula:**
> - $\sigma_R = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ → $T_R = 2k\sigma_R$ (use $k = 3$ for 99.73 %).
> - Convert $t_i$ to $\sigma_i$: $\sigma_i = t_i/6$ (normal) or $\sigma_i = t_i/\sqrt{12}$ (uniform).
> 
> ✅ **Fit Classification Rule:**
> - $C_{\min} > 0$ → **Clearance fit** (always loose).
> - $C_{\max} < 0$ → **Interference fit** (always tight).
> - $C_{\min} \leq 0 \leq C_{\max}$ → **Transition fit** (sign changes).
> 
> ✅ **Key Assumptions for RSS:**
> - Normal distribution of component dimensions.
> - Statistical independence of components.
> - Linear dimension chain.
> 
> ✅ **ISO Hole-Basis Default:**
> - H-type hole → lower deviation $EI = 0$.
> - Shaft designation depends on fit intent (e.g., h, k, p).
> 
> ✅ **Design Insight:**
> - Adding more components **widens** assembly tolerance (worst-case).
> - Statistical tolerance scales as $\sqrt{n}$ → economic for large assemblies.
> - Worst-case mandatory for safety-critical; statistical preferred for high-volume, non-critical.
> 
> ✅ **Engineering Applications:**
> - Automotive: piston-pin, bearing-shaft fits.
> - Aerospace: 100 % worst-case for life-critical joints.
> - Consumer goods: RSS saves cost in mass production.
> - Jigs and fixtures: transition fits for locational dowels.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Datum Features and Tolerance Stacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Datum Features and Tolerance Stacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Datum — Formal Definition (As per ASME Y14.5 / ISO 5459)

> [!NOTE]
> **Datum (n.):** A theoretically exact point, axis, line, or plane derived from the true geometric counterpart of a specified datum feature. A datum is the reference from which dimensional or geometric tolerances are originated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Datum Feature (n.):** The actual physical feature (a surface, hole, slot, pin, or width) of a part that is used to establish a datum. The datum feature is what exists on the drawing; the datum itself is the theoretical idealization of that feature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Datum Feature Simulator (n.):** The physical master (surface plate, gage block, mandrel, fixture, or even another part) that contacts the datum feature to physically establish the datum during inspection or assembly.

The three terms form a logical chain: **Datum Feature (on the part) → Datum (theoretical ideal) → Datum Feature Simulator (the inspection tool that brings the datum to life).**

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen Table" Datum

Imagine placing an irregularly-shaped wooden block on a kitchen table and asking: *"How tall is the block?"* Before answering, you must first decide **which face is "down"** — because the answer changes (e.g., 8 cm, 6 cm, or 4 cm) depending on the face you choose as the bottom. That chosen bottom face is the **datum feature**, the ideal flat plane touching it is the **datum**, and the table is the **datum feature simulator**.

Now imagine assembling a sandwich of three slices — bread, cheese, tomato. The height of the sandwich is measured from the **bottom bread (primary datum)** to the **top bread (tertiary datum)**, with the cheese constrained laterally by the **side of the plate (secondary datum)**. This is precisely the **3-2-1 datum reference frame principle** used in engineering tolerancing.

---

### 1.3 Why Datums Matter in Manufacturing

- Without datums, **every inspector would measure differently**, leading to disputes between supplier and buyer.
- Datums anchor the **Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T)** system so that all tolerances are referenced consistently.
- They directly affect **Tolerance Stack-Up** in assemblies — the single most common cause of out-of-spec assemblies in mass production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Symbol (ASME Y14.5):** A datum feature is identified by a capital letter (A, B, C…) enclosed in a square box, connected by a leader line ending in a filled or unfilled triangle touching the feature.

![Datum Feature Symbol — conceptual description] A square letter box with a leader line and a triangle touching the datum feature surface; if the feature is an axis, the leader is offset with a small gap and a thick line extension.

---

### 1.4 Datum Reference Frame (DRF) — 3-2-1 Principle

A rigid body in 3D space has **six degrees of freedom (6 DOF)**: three translations (X, Y, Z) and three rotations (pitch, yaw, roll). To fully constrain it, the DRF must eliminate all six.

| Datum Priority | Minimum Contact Points | DOF Constrained |
|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Primary | 3 | 1 translation + 2 rotations |
| Secondary | 2 | 1 translation + 1 rotation |
| Tertiary | 1 | 1 translation |
| **Total** | **6 points** | **All 6 DOF** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "3-2-1" rule is a MINIMUM, not a maximum.** A robust fixture may use 4-3-2 or 6-4-3 contacts for stability, but the **drawings still cite only three datums** (primary, secondary, tertiary) for clarity.

---

### 1.5 What is a Tolerance Stack-Up?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tolerance Stack-Up:** The cumulative effect of part-feature tolerances when several components are assembled in series. The variation of a key assembly dimension is the *algebraic* or *statistical* combination of the individual part tolerances.

A simple shaft of nominal length **100 mm** with three turned sections of **30, 40, and 30 mm** — if each section varies by ±0.1 mm, the total length can vary by as much as **±0.3 mm** in the worst case. This is a one-dimensional tolerance stack-up.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain of Datums — Step by Step

1. **Designer specifies** a datum feature on the drawing (e.g., the back face of a flange).
2. **Datum feature symbol** (e.g., `A` in a box) is attached to that feature on the engineering drawing.
3. **Manufacturing reads** the symbol and knows: "Whenever I inspect or set up this part, face A must rest against a flat reference."
4. **Inspection uses a datum feature simulator** (surface plate, precision angle plate) to materialize the theoretical datum.
5. **All other tolerances** on the part are referenced back to this datum A (and B, C if 3-DOF constraint is needed).

---

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Datums (Order of Precedence)

When multiple datums are cited, the **order is critical** because it defines the sequence of constraint:

- **Primary Datum (A)** — established first, controls the largest feature / most important orientation (typically the largest planar face or largest diameter hole).
- **Secondary Datum (B)** — established second, locks an additional orientation.
- **Tertiary Datum (C)** — established last, locks the final translation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Rule:** A datum should be chosen such that it is a *functional* feature — one that mates with another part in the assembly. Avoid choosing a machined finish surface as a datum because manufacturing repeatability is poor there.

---

### 2.3 Types of Datum Features

| Type | Symbol Notation | Description | Example |
|------|----------------|-------------|---------|
| Planar Surface | Letter box on surface line | A flat face establishing a plane | Flange face, end face of a shaft |
| Cylindrical (Feature of Size) | Letter box on dimension line with extension line offset | Establishes an axis | A bearing journal, a bolt hole |
| Profile/Slot | Letter box on profile line | A slot width establishes a plane | Keyway width |
| Cone / Sphere | Letter box on leader to feature | Establishes point or axis | Conical bearing seat |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Tolerance Stack-Up Master Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table. In every KTU numerical problem on tolerance stacks, one of these equations is the backbone of the solution.

| # | Method | Equation | When to Use | Nature of Distribution |
|---|--------|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Worst-Case (Arithmetic)** | $T_{assy} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | 100% part interchangeability required | Rectangular |
| 2 | **Worst-Case with ± limits** | $\pm T_{assy} = \pm \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{T_i}{2} \right\vert$ | Bilateral ± tolerances, no rejection | Rectangular |
| 3 | **Statistical (RSS — Root Sum Square)** | $T_{assy} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{2}}$ | Random independent dimensions, batch production | Normal (Gaussian) |
| 4 | **Statistical with ± limits** | $\pm T_{assy} = \pm \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{T_i}{2}\right)^{2}}$ | Bilateral ± tolerances, random process | Normal |
| 5 | **Modified Stack-Up (worst case on critical, RSS on non-critical)** | $T_{assy} = T_{crit} + \sqrt{\sum T_{noncrit}^{2}}$ | Mixed assembly | Mixed |
| 6 | **Mean Shift (Mean ± Tolerance)** | $\bar{X}_{assy} = \sum \bar{X}_i$ | Centering the distribution | Algebraic |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use Worst-Case and RSS in the same equation by accident.** The classical trap: a student adds tolerances in one line and then suddenly "square-roots" them. Choose ONE method per problem.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace assemblies (e.g., aircraft wing-to-fuselage):** RSS stack-ups are used to predict the wing-position tolerance, saving millions in reworked parts.
- **Automotive powertrains:** Worst-case is used for safety-critical features (e.g., bearing bore-to-bearing cap mating).
- **Sheet metal assemblies (white goods, electronics):** Modified stack-up is most common.
- **GD&T datums** feed directly into **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine) inspection programs** — the CMM touches the datum simulator first, then measures all other features relative to it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Tolerance Stack-Up

**Premise:** Each dimension $L_i$ in a chain can independently take any value within its bilateral limits $\left[L_i^{\min}, L_i^{\max}\right]$ with $L_i^{\min} = L_i^{\text{nominal}} - T_i/2$ and $L_i^{\max} = L_i^{\text{nominal}} + T_i/2$.

The **assembly dimension** $L_{assy} = \sum L_i$.

**Step 1:** Maximum possible assembly length occurs when every constituent dimension is at its individual maximum.

$$L_{assy}^{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i^{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(L_i^{\text{nominal}} + \frac{T_i}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Minimum possible assembly length occurs when every constituent dimension is at its individual minimum.

$$L_{assy}^{\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i^{\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(L_i^{\text{nominal}} - \frac{T_i}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Subtract to get the total assembly tolerance band.

$$T_{assy} = L_{assy}^{\max} - L_{assy}^{\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

**Step 4:** Express as bilateral ± limits centered on the nominal sum.

$$\pm T_{assy} = \pm \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{T_i}{2} \right\vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Worst-case assumes **zero independence** — every part is at its limit simultaneously. This is conservative (over-sized tolerance budget) and guarantees 100% interchangeability, but is rarely achieved in practice.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Statistical (RSS) Tolerance Stack-Up

**Premise:** Each dimension is the output of an *independent* random process with mean $\bar{X}_i$ and standard deviation $\sigma_i$. The bilateral tolerance $T_i$ is treated as approximately $\pm 3\sigma_i$ (covers 99.73% of parts under a normal distribution), giving $T_i = 6\sigma_i$ and $\sigma_i = T_i/6$.

**Step 1:** The variance of a sum of independent random variables is the sum of their variances.

$$\sigma_{assy}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^{2}$$

This is a fundamental theorem in probability — variances of *independent* random variables are additive.

**Step 2:** Take the square root of both sides.

$$\sigma_{assy} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Convert $\sigma$ back to tolerance by multiplying by 6 (or 3 if you want only $\pm 3\sigma$ — depending on which confidence level the question specifies).

$$T_{assy} = 6\sigma_{assy} = 6 \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{T_i}{6}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify by pulling $1/6$ out of the square root.

$$\pm T_{assy} = \pm \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{T_i}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** RSS yields a *smaller* assembly tolerance than worst-case for the same individual tolerances — typically **30–50% tighter** for chains of 3+ dimensions. This is the **statistical advantage** exploited in mass production.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Standard Numerical Type)

**Problem:** A four-part stack has dimensions $L_1 = 30 \pm 0.1$ mm, $L_2 = 25 \pm 0.15$ mm, $L_3 = 50 \pm 0.2$ mm, $L_4 = 15 \pm 0.05$ mm. Compute the assembly tolerance by **(a) Worst-Case** and **(b) RSS** methods.
