For deeper pockets, use a *long-reach* end mill or design the feature in **two steps** (a wide outer pocket + a narrow deeper slot).

#### 2.2.5 Provide Cutter Clearance
The cutter body has **diametral clearance (radial relief)** of about **0.5–1°** on the periphery. For *side-milling* or *slotting* on a vertical wall, the designer should allow a **small draft angle (1°–2°)** on the wall to avoid rubbing.

#### 2.2.6 Use Standard Fillet Radii on External Edges
External fillet radii should match **standard end-mill radii**. This enables the use of a single cutter for both roughing and finishing, and improves surface finish (no manual blending required).

---

### 2.3 Design Features to Facilitate Keyway Cutting

A keyway is a **rectangular or semicircular groove** cut into a shaft or hub to transmit torque via a key. There are three standard profiles:

#### 2.3.1 Types of Keyways

| Profile | Cutter Used | Strength | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Square Keyway** | Square-end mill or keyway broach | High | General purpose, balanced stress |
| **Rectangular (Flat) Keyway** | Slitting saw + end mill, or Woodruff cutter | Medium | Hub-mounted, single-direction drive |
| **Woodruff Keyway** | Woodruff cutter (semicircular) | Lower (deep stress concentration) | Light-duty, tapered shafts |

#### 2.3.2 Standard Keyway Dimensions
Keyways are standardised in **ISO 773, ISO 2491, BS 46, DIN 6885**. For a shaft of diameter **D**, the keyway width **b** and depth **t₁** (in shaft) / **t₂** (in hub) are:

| Shaft Diameter Range (mm) | Key Width b (mm) | Shaft Depth t₁ (mm) | Hub Depth t₂ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 – 8 | 2 | 1.2 | 0.5 |
| 8 – 10 | 3 | 1.8 | 0.9 |
| 10 – 12 | 4 | 2.5 | 1.1 |
| 12 – 17 | 5 | 3.0 | 1.4 |
| 17 – 22 | 6 | 3.5 | 1.7 |
| 22 – 30 | 8 | 4.0 | 2.0 |
| 30 – 38 | 10 | 5.0 | 2.4 |
| 38 – 44 | 12 | 5.0 | 2.4 |
| 44 – 50 | 14 | 5.5 | 2.9 |
| 50 – 58 | 16 | 6.0 | 3.4 |
| 58 – 65 | 18 | 7.0 | 3.4 |
| 65 – 75 | 20 | 7.5 | 3.9 |
| 75 – 85 | 22 | 9.0 | 4.4 |
| 85 – 95 | 25 | 9.0 | 4.4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** Always select the next higher *standard* shaft diameter if the calculated size is borderline. Specifying a non-standard keyway width forces the workshop to grind a special cutter, increasing cost and lead time.

#### 2.3.3 Keyway Run-Out and Relief
- **Keyway run-out**: Provide a **relief groove** (a small annular undercut) at the end of the keyway so that the cutter can exit cleanly. The relief length is typically **1.5 × b**.
- **Keyway end fillets**: At each end of a profile (square/rectangular) keyway, specify a **fillet radius = keyway width / 2** to avoid stress concentration.

#### 2.3.4 Keyway on a Shaft with a Fillet
A shaft-to-collar transition normally has a fillet (to reduce stress). The keyway must **not extend into the fillet region** because the cutter cannot machine the curved fillet surface. The safe distance is:

$$L_{safe} = r_{fillet} + L_{keyway}$$

The designer must indicate this **clearance from the fillet** in the drawing.

#### 2.3.5 Woodruff Keyway Considerations
- Woodruff keys are **semicircular** discs (e.g., 403, 404, 505, 606 series per ANSI B17.1).
- The cutter is a small **circular milling cutter**, so the keyway is *naturally arced*.
- **Advantage:** Self-aligning, easy to cut on a milling machine.
- **Disadvantage:** Deep groove weakens the shaft; the bottom is curved, not flat, so load distribution is poor.

#### 2.3.6 Effect of Keyway on Shaft Strength
The keyway introduces a **stress concentration factor (Kt)** that reduces the fatigue strength of the shaft. The approximate reduction is:

$$K_t \approx 2.0 - 2.5 \text{ (for rectangular keyway)}$$

$$K_t \approx 2.5 - 3.0 \text{ (for Woodruff keyway)}$$

The **end-mill keyway** (closed rectangular) has the highest strength, followed by the **tapered key**, and then the **Woodruff key**.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| # | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Min. edge distance (drilled hole) | $E_{min} = 1.0 \times D$ to $1.5 \times D$ | mm | D = drill diameter |
| 2 | Min. spacing between holes | $S_{min} \geq 1.5 \times D_{larger}$ | mm | For equal diameter holes |
| 3 | Spacing between unequal holes | $S_{min} = D_1 + D_2 + 2C$ | mm | C = clearance (1–2 mm) |
| 4 | Max. pocket depth (end mill) | $L_{max} = 1.5 \times D_{cutter}$ | mm | For full-diameter cut |
| 5 | Cutter clearance for side milling | Draft $= 1°–2°$ | degrees | Avoids body rubbing |
| 6 | Keyway width b | From ISO 773 / DIN 6885 table | mm | Function of D |
| 7 | Keyway relief length | $L_{relief} = 1.5 \times b$ | mm | Allows cutter exit |
| 8 | Keyway end fillet | $r = b / 2$ | mm | Stress relief |
| 9 | Stress concentration (rect. keyway) | $K_t \approx 2.0–2.5$ | – | Fatigue reduction |
| 10 | Stress concentration (Woodruff) | $K_t \approx 2.5–3.0$ | – | Fatigue reduction |
| 11 | Drill standard point angle | $118°$ | degrees | General purpose |
| 12 | Drill standard helix angle | $30°$ | degrees | General purpose |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Markdown:** All absolute value notations within equations have been replaced with `\vert` and `\geq` / `\leq` for proper rendering without breaking the table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These design rules are applied every day in industries ranging from **automotive gearboxes** (keyways on drive shafts) to **aerospace structural brackets** (drilled and milled pockets) to **consumer electronics enclosures** (milled slots for connectors). The cumulative effect of following these rules:
- Reduces **tool inventory cost** by 20–30%.
- Reduces **cycle time** by 10–25%.
- Improves **first-time-right yield** from ~70% to >95%.
- Enables **standardised CNC programming** with no special tool paths.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Wall Thickness for Drilling Clearance

When a deep hole is to be drilled near an internal wall, the designer must ensure that the drill body (which has a *slightly smaller* diameter than its cutting lips, but still substantial) does not rub against the wall.

**Step 1:** Define the drill geometry.
Let the drill diameter be $D$, and the drill body diameter (after the margin) be:

$$D_{body} = D - 2 \cdot m$$

where $m$ is the **margin width** (typically $0.05$ to $0.15$ mm per side for general-purpose drills).

**Step 2:** Define the cavity wall offset.
For the drill to clear the wall during deep-hole drilling, the centre of the drill must be at a horizontal distance of at least:

$$X_{c} = D/2 + c$$

where $c$ is the **operational clearance** (set by the designer based on drill length and material). Common values:
- For drill depth $\leq 2D$: $c = 0.5$ mm
- For drill depth $2D$ to $4D$: $c = 1.0$ mm
- For drill depth $> 4D$: $c = 1.5$ mm

**Step 3:** The minimum wall thickness (distance from drill centre to nearest wall) is:

$$t_{wall,min} = \frac{D}{2} + c$$

**Step 4:** Substitution and final rule.

$$\boxed{t_{wall,min} = \frac{D_{drill}}{2} + c}$$

**Example (Worked Out):** A designer wants to drill a **Ø 20 mm** hole to a depth of **80 mm** in a steel block. The drill is a standard 20 mm jobber drill.

- Drill depth ratio: $L/D = 80/20 = 4.0$ (boundary case).
- Operational clearance: $c = 1.0$ mm.
- Minimum wall thickness: $t_{wall,min} = 20/2 + 1.0 = 11.0$ mm.
- **Conclusion:** The designer must keep at least **11 mm of material** between the hole centre and the nearest wall. If the existing wall is only 9 mm, the designer must *either* reduce the drill depth, *or* enlarge the cavity, *or* shift the hole.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Keyway Depth Ratio for Torque Transmission

A key transmits torque $M$ between a shaft (diameter $D$) and a hub. The key is sheared along its length $L$ and crushed on its bearing face.

**Step 1:** Define the torque capacity.
For a square key ($b = h$), the shear stress in the key is:

$$\tau = \frac{2M}{b \cdot L \cdot D}$$

The crushing (bearing) stress on the key face is:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{2M}{h \cdot L \cdot D}$$

**Step 2:** Equate shear and bearing for an *optimum* design (so neither fails first).
For a square key, $b = h$, so $\tau = \sigma_c$. This is the design optimum.

**Step 3:** For a rectangular key (flat key), the standard ratio is $h \approx 0.75 \cdot b$, giving:

$$\frac{\sigma_c}{\tau} = \frac{b}{h} = \frac{1}{0.75} = 1.33$$

So the **bearing stress is 33% higher** than the shear stress — a flat key fails by crushing first, which is the *standard* design philosophy (crushing is a gradual, detectable failure; shear is sudden).

**Step 4:** Required key length for a given torque.

$$L = \frac{2M}{b \cdot \tau_{allowable} \cdot D}$$

**Example (Worked Out):** A shaft of diameter $D = 50$ mm must transmit torque $M = 800$ N·m. The material is medium-carbon steel with allowable shear stress $\tau_{allow} = 50$ MPa for the key.

- From the table, key width $b = 16$ mm (for $D = 50$).
- Substituting:

$$L = \frac{2 \times 800 \times 10^3}{16 \times 50 \times 50} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^6}{40{,}000} = 40 \text{ mm}$$

- **Conclusion:** The key must be at least **40 mm long**. The designer should specify **45 mm or 50 mm** (a safety margin and a standard length).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Decision Table

The following matrix helps a KTU student decide **which machining approach** to recommend for a given design feature:

| Design Feature | Preferred Operation | Tool Required | Standard Sizes to Use | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Through hole (cylindrical) | Drilling | Twist drill | ISO 235 metric, ANSI number/letter | Non-standard Ø |
| Tapered entry for screw | Countersinking | Countersink (60°–120°) | 90° most common | Wrong angle |
| Recess for bolt head | Counterboring | End mill or counterbore tool | Standard head diameters | Under-depth |
| Deep hole (> 4D) | Deep-hole drilling | Gun drill, ejector drill | Standard Ø + pilot Ø | No pilot/relief |
| Open-ended slot | Slitting + end mill | Slitting saw + end mill | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm | Non-standard width |
| Closed pocket | End milling + relief | End mill (corner radius = R) | 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm | Sharp internal corners |
| Keyway (rectangular) | End milling | Keyway end mill (Woodruff optional) | ISO 773 widths | Crossing fillet region |
| Keyway (Woodruff) | Milling | Woodruff cutter | 403, 404, 505, 606 | Deep weakening |
| External fillet | End milling | Ball-end or corner-radius end mill | Standard radii | Non-standard radius |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Redesigning a Shaft-Hub Assembly for DFM

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A student designs a shaft of **Ø 42 mm** with a **rectangular keyway 13 mm wide × 4.5 mm deep**, terminating **6 mm from a shoulder fillet of radius 2 mm**. The shaft transmits torque to a hub using a standard key.

**Step 1: Check keyway standard.**
From the table, for $D = 42$ mm, the standard key width is **12 mm**, not 13 mm. The designer should reduce $b$ to **12 mm**.

**Step 2: Check keyway depth.**
For $b = 12$ mm, the standard shaft depth is $t_1 = 5.0$ mm. The proposed 4.5 mm is too shallow — change to **5.0 mm**.

**Step 3: Check keyway-to-fillet distance.**
The keyway ends 6 mm from the shoulder, but the fillet radius is 2 mm. The fillet zone occupies about 2 mm of length. The keyway end is therefore $6 - 2 = 4$ mm from the *tangent point* of the fillet. A standard end-mill keyway cutter typically has a diameter of $b = 12$ mm, so it needs a run-out of at least 6 mm (half the cutter diameter). Therefore the keyway must be **truncated** at least 8 mm from the shoulder.

**Step 4: Revised Drawing Specification.**
- Shaft diameter: Ø **42 mm** (unchanged).
- Keyway: **b = 12 mm, t₁ = 5.0 mm, L = 30 mm** (specified length).
- Keyway relief at each end: **1.5 × b = 18 mm** undercut.
- Distance from keyway end to shoulder fillet tangent: **≥ 8 mm**.
- Fillet radius at shoulder: **2 mm** (standard, unchanged).

**Conclusion:** Three design changes were required to make the part machinable with standard tooling. The KTU examiner expects the student to identify *all three* in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Design Rule Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Drawing Received] --> B{Identify Machining Features}
    B --> C[Holes]
    B --> D[Pockets / Slots]
    B --> E[Keyways]
    C --> C1[Check Standard Drill Size]
    C1 --> C2[Check Edge Distance]
    C2 --> C3[Check Inter-Hole Spacing]
    C3 --> C4[Check Surface Flatness]
    C4 --> F{All Rules Satisfied?}
    D --> D1[Check Standard Cutter Diameter]
    D1 --> D2[Check Internal Corner Radius]
    D2 --> D3[Check Pocket Depth]
    D3 --> D4[Check Wall Clearance]
    D4 --> F
    E --> E1[Check ISO 773 Key Width]
    E1 --> E2[Check Keyway Depth]
    E2 --> E3[Check Fillet Clearance]
    E3 --> E4[Check Relief Length]
    E4 --> F
    F -->|Yes| G[Approved for Production]
    F -->|No| H[Redesign Feature]
    H --> A
```

### 4.2 Architecture Flow: Keyway Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shaft Diameter D Selected] --> B{Load Direction}
    B -->|Reversible| C[Use Square Key]
    B -->|One Way| D[Use Flat Key]
    B -->|Tapered Shaft| E[Use Woodruff Key]
    C --> F[Select b from ISO 773]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Select Woodruff Series 403 to 1010]
    F --> H[Set t1 and t2 from Table]
    G --> I[Verify Hub Depth Compatibility]
    H --> J[Add Fillet Relief at Ends]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Verify Distance from Shoulder Fillet]
    K --> L{All Checks Pass?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Release to Workshop]
    L -->|No| N[Re-select Shaft Diameter or Key]
    N --> A
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hole Spacing Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hole 1 Diameter D1] --> B[Centre to Centre Distance S]
    C[Hole 2 Diameter D2] --> B
    B --> D{Minimum Spacing Rule}
    D --> E[D1 + D2 + 2C]
    E --> F[Edge Distance Rule 1.5D]
    F --> G[Final Layout Approved]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description: Keyway with Relief

A standard keyway on a shaft is drawn in three zones:

1. **Main keyway length (L):** the full flat-bottomed rectangular groove that transmits torque.
2. **Relief grooves (L_relief):** short, shallower undercuts at each end of the keyway, of length **1.5 × b** and depth slightly less than **t₁**. These allow the end-mill cutter to exit without leaving a step on the shaft.
3. **Run-out zone:** the section of shaft between the relief groove and the shoulder fillet, where the cutter cannot reach. This zone must be at least **half the cutter diameter** long.

The drawing must explicitly call out all three zones with dimensions, because omitting the relief is a common cause of scrap parts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six design features that facilitate drilling on a component.*

**Model Answer:**
1. Flat, perpendicular entry surface (spotface or counterbore if needed).
2. Standard drill diameter from ISO 235 or ANSI tables.
3. Minimum edge distance of 1.0D to 1.5D from the hole to any free edge.
4. Minimum centre-to-centre spacing of 1.5D between adjacent holes.
5. Pilot hole for deep holes exceeding 4D in depth.
6. Adequate clearance for the drill body and chip evacuation (especially for deep holes).
7. Provision for jig bushings if multiple holes are to be drilled in a single setup.

*(Any six of the above carry full 3 marks. KTU expects 1 mark for each correctly named feature.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why should internal corners in a milled pocket be designed with a radius equal to the end-mill radius? Explain briefly.*

**Model Answer:**
Internal corners in a milled pocket must be designed with a radius equal to the end-mill radius because:
1. A rotating end-mill produces a **circular arc** at every internal corner — sharp corners are physically unmachinable with a cylindrical tool.
2. Matching the corner radius to the cutter radius enables the use of a **standard off-the-shelf end mill** without needing a special tapered cutter.
3. It reduces **stress concentration** at the corner, improving fatigue life of the part.
4. It simplifies **CNC tool-path programming** — the cutter can follow the corner directly without complex retract-and-plunge cycles.

*(Each of the above points carries partial credit. A 3-mark answer should include 3–4 points with one-line justifications.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, PEMRT522]**

A machine tool spindle has a section of diameter Ø 45 mm on which a hub is mounted using a rectangular key. The torque to be transmitted is 1200 N·m, and the allowable shear stress in the key is 60 MPa. The shoulder fillet at the end of the shaft has a radius of 2.5 mm.

**(a)** Select a suitable standard keyway from the ISO 773 table, and calculate the required key length. (7 marks)

**(b)** Sketch the keyway with proper relief, and specify the dimensional rules for the relief groove and the run-out zone near the fillet. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1: From the ISO 773 / DIN 6885 table, for shaft diameter D = 45 mm, the nearest standard key width is **b = 14 mm** (covering the 44–50 mm range). The corresponding shaft depth is **t₁ = 5.5 mm** and hub depth **t₂ = 2.9 mm**.

Step 2: For a rectangular (flat) key, the height is $h \approx 0.75 \times b = 0.75 \times 14 = 10.5$ mm. We use the standard depth ratio from the table.

Step 3: Required key length from shear formula:

$$L = \frac{2M}{b \cdot \tau_{allow} \cdot D}$$

$$L = \frac{2 \times 1200 \times 10^3}{14 \times 60 \times 45}$$

$$L = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{37{,}800} = 63.49 \text{ mm}$$

Step 4: Apply a safety margin and round to a standard length. Recommended key length: **L = 70 mm** (the next available standard length). The actual shear stress in the key is then:

$$\tau_{actual} = \frac{2 \times 1200 \times 10^3}{14 \times 70 \times 45} = 54.2 \text{ MPa} < 60 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

**Part (b):**

Step 1: Relief groove length at each end:

$$L_{relief} = 1.5 \times b = 1.5 \times 14 = 21 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Relief groove depth (slightly less than $t_1$ to avoid step on the shaft surface): **4.5 mm** (compared to $t_1 = 5.5$ mm).

Step 3: Run-out zone between relief end and shoulder fillet tangent:

The keyway cutter has diameter equal to $b = 14$ mm. The cutter needs at least half its diameter to exit, so the run-out must be at least:

$$L_{runout} = D_{cutter}/2 = 14/2 = 7 \text{ mm}$$

Step 4: Fillet clearance. The shoulder fillet has $r = 2.5$ mm. The total axial clearance from the *end of the main keyway* to the *start of the fillet curve* is:

$$L_{clearance} = L_{relief} + L_{runout} = 21 + 7 = 28 \text{ mm}$$

Step 5: Final keyway drawing call-outs:
- Main keyway: **14 mm wide × 5.5 mm deep × 70 mm long**.
- Relief at each end: **21 mm long × 4.5 mm deep**.
- Total machined zone: **70 + 2 × 21 = 112 mm**.
- Distance from keyway end (with relief) to shoulder fillet tangent: **≥ 28 mm**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, PEMRT522]**

A cast iron bracket requires a closed pocket 80 mm × 60 mm × 15 mm deep, with two Ø 12 mm through-holes for fasteners, and a Ø 25 mm central bore. The bracket has a minimum wall thickness of 10 mm.

**(a)** Identify the design modifications required to make the pocket machinable with a standard end mill. (7 marks)

**(b)** Specify the minimum edge distance for the Ø 25 mm bore, the spacing rule for the two Ø 12 mm holes, and recommend a standard drill size for the central bore. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1: The pocket is 15 mm deep, which is greater than the standard $1.5 \times D_{cutter}$ rule for full-diameter cutting. For a 10 mm end mill, the recommended depth is 15 mm — feasible with a standard end mill. For a 12 mm end mill, max depth is 18 mm — also feasible. So a **standard Ø 10 mm or Ø 12 mm end mill** can be used.

Step 2: The pocket is *closed* (surrounded by walls). The designer must specify **relief holes** at the four internal corners of diameter ≥ cutter diameter (i.e., **Ø 10 mm or larger**).

Step 3: Internal corner radii must equal the end-mill radius: **R = 5 mm** (for a Ø 10 mm cutter) or **R = 6 mm** (for a Ø 12 mm cutter). The drawing must replace the four sharp corners with these arcs.

Step 4: The 10 mm wall thickness is just sufficient — verify that the end mill body has clearance from the adjacent wall when cutting the opposite side. With a 10 mm cutter, the centre of the cutter must be at least 5 + 0.5 = 5.5 mm from the wall. A 10 mm wall satisfies this with margin.

**Part (b):**

Step 1: **Edge distance for Ø 25 mm bore** (in cast iron, a brittle material, so use the lower bound):

$$E_{min} = 1.0 \times D = 1.0 \times 25 = 25 \text{ mm}$$

The drawing must show a minimum of 25 mm from the bore centre to any free edge of the bracket.

Step 2: **Spacing between the two Ø 12 mm holes:**

$$S_{min} = D_1 + D_2 + 2C = 12 + 12 + 2 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ mm}$$

(use $C = 1.5$ mm for CNC access). Round up to a clean dimension of **30 mm centre-to-centre**.

Step 3: **Standard drill size for Ø 25 mm bore:**
- 25 mm is a whole-millimetre size, available as a **standard ISO 235 jobber drill** (in HSS or solid carbide).
- The designer should note in the drawing: *"Drill Ø 25 (standard)*. *Ream to Ø 25 H7 if tolerance < ± 0.05 mm."*
- For depths exceeding 4D (i.e., > 100 mm), specify a **pilot hole of Ø 5 mm** at the entry to guide the main drill.

Step 4: **Summary of all design changes:**
1. Replace four sharp internal corners with **R = 5 mm arcs**.
2. Add **four Ø 10 mm relief holes** at the corners.
3. Specify **edge distance ≥ 25 mm** for the central bore.
4. Specify **centre-to-centre spacing ≥ 30 mm** for the two Ø 12 holes.
5. Specify the **drill as a standard Ø 25 mm** ISO 235 size.
6. Note in the drawing: *"Use standard end mill Ø 10 mm, 4-flute, HSS/coated."*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing drill diameter and drill body diameter** — students often forget the body clearance, especially for deep holes.
> 2. **Ignoring the relief groove** on keyways — a very common reason for marks deduction (loses 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question).
> 3. **Specifying non-standard internal corner radii** — students often write "R = 4.5 mm" or "R = 7 mm" without realising these are non-standard. Always round to 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm.
> 4. **Forgetting the fillet clearance** for keyways — the keyway must terminate *before* the shoulder fillet begins, not at the fillet tangent.
> 5. **Not specifying tolerances** — the KTU examiner will deduct a mark if the keyway width is given without a tolerance (e.g., **b = 12 N9** per ISO 773).
> 6. **Confusing t₁ and t₂** — $t_1$ is the depth in the **shaft**, $t_2$ is the depth in the **hub**. Mixing them up is a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Drilling design** demands a **flat, perpendicular entry surface** — provide a spotface or counterbore if the natural surface is curved.
- [ ] **Standard drill sizes** must be used: ISO 235 (metric), ANSI number (1–80), ANSI letter (A–Z), or fractional (1/64" steps).
- [ ] **Minimum edge distance** for a drilled hole is **1.0D to 1.5D**, depending on material ductility.
- [ ] **Minimum hole-to-hole spacing** is **1.5D** for equal-diameter holes, and **D₁ + D₂ + 2C** for unequal.
- [ ] **Milling pockets** must always have **internal corner radii equal to the end-mill radius** (3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm).
- [ ] **Closed pockets** are difficult to machine; provide **relief holes** at internal corners if a closed pocket is unavoidable.
- [ ] **Maximum pocket depth** with a standard end mill is **1.5 × D_cutter** for full-diameter cuts.
- [ ] **Keyways** must be selected from **ISO 773 / DIN 6885** standard tables; never invent a custom width.
- [ ] **Keyway depth** has two values: **t₁ (in shaft)** and **t₂ (in hub)** — never confuse them.
- [ ] **Keyway relief grooves** (length **1.5 × b**, depth slightly less than t₁) must be specified at both ends.
- [ ] **Keyways must not extend into shoulder fillets** — maintain a run-out zone of at least **D_cutter / 2** from the fillet tangent.
- [ ] **Woodruff keyways** weaken the shaft the most (K_t ≈ 2.5–3.0); use them only for light-duty, tapered-shaft applications.
- [ ] **Stress concentration** at a keyway reduces fatigue life — specify a **fillet radius = b/2** at the keyway ends to mitigate.
- [ ] **Countersink standard angles**: 90° (flat-head screws), 60°, 82°, 100°, 110°, 120° (per ISO 7721).
- [ ] **Standard drill point angle**: 118° (general purpose); 135° for hard materials.
- [ ] **The drawing is the contract** — every design feature must be unambiguous, dimensioned, and tolerant enough to be produced with standard tooling.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Scheme exam tip:** Always show the *modifications* to a given drawing, not just a list of rules. A drawing revision is worth 2–3 marks on its own.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Takeaway:** A design that requires a special tool is a *design failure*. Always ask: *Can this feature be produced with a standard catalogue tool?* If the answer is no, redesign the feature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doweling Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Doweling Procedures # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Doweling Procedure** is a precision locating technique used in the design of jigs, fixtures, press tools, dies, and machine assemblies, wherein hardened cylindrical pins (called **dowels** or **dowel pins**) are inserted into mating components to ensure accurate, repeatable, and unambiguous location of parts relative to one another. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "A dowel is a hardened, ground cylindrical pin of standard tolerance, press-fitted into one component and slip-fitted into the mating component, serving to locate, align, and prevent lateral/rotational displacement of mating parts." A dowel pin transfers **shear loads** (and occasionally small tensile/compressive loads) across the joint interface while allowing the joint to be disassembled for maintenance, replacement of wear parts, or tool sharpening. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Furniture Dowel:** > When you assemble an IKEA bookshelf, you insert small wooden pegs (dowels) into pre-drilled holes to align the two panels perfectly before driving a screw or bolt. Without the dowel, the two panels could shift sideways during fastening, causing misalignment. The dowel acts as a **"positional memory"** — it remembers the exact position for you. In manufacturing, the same idea applies, but the **dowels are made of hardened steel**, ground to micrometer tolerances, and used in tooling that must repeat the same location thousands of times without wear-induced drift. **Three everyday engineering equivalents:** - The alignment pegs on a **mobile phone charging port**. - The locating pins on a **microwave oven door hinge**. - The two guide posts on a **plastic injection mould**. --- ## 1.3 Primary Functions of Dowels | # | Function | Engineering Significance | |---|----------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Location** | Establishes datum relationship between two parts | | 2 | **Alignment** | Re-establishes the same position after each disassembly | | 3 | **Load Transfer** | Carries shear (and sometimes bearing) load across joint | | 4 | **Anti-rotation** | Prevents twisting between mating parts | | 5 | **Anti-translation** | Locks the two parts in the X-Y plane | --- ## 1.4 Classification of Dowel Pins ```text DOWEL PINS ├── By Geometry │ ├── Straight (Parallel) Dowels ← MOST COMMON in jigs │ ├── Tapered Dowels ← For self-aligning joints │ └── Special (Grooved, Spring, Elongated) │ ├── By Installation │ ├── Press-Fit (Permanent) Dowels │ ├── Slip-Fit (Removable) Dowels │ └── Pull-Out (Extractable) Dowels │ └── By Standardization ├── ISO 8734 (Hardened Parallel Pins, Type A) ├── ISO 8734 (Hardened Parallel Pins, Type B) ├── DIN 6325 (Hardened Dowel Pins) └── IS 6193 (Indian Standard) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Focus:** In Part B questions, students are routinely asked to *justify the choice of press-fit vs. slip-fit* in a given jig design. Remember: **one half is press-fit (in the locator body), the other half is slip-fit (in the mating component)** — this is the universal rule of doweling. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logic of Dowel Installation — "The Golden Rule" Every doweled joint has **two mating holes of different character**: | Hole Location | Fit Type | Tolerance Class | Reason | |---------------|----------|-----------------|--------| | Hole in the **fixed/locator body** (e.g., jig base) | **Press Fit** | H7/r6 or similar interference | Dowel stays locked; defines the reference datum | | Hole in the **movable/inserted component** (e.g., bush plate) | **Slip Fit** | H7/g6 or H7/h6 clearance | Allows easy assembly/disassembly with hand force | > [!NOTE] > If both holes are press-fit, you can never assemble the parts. If both holes are slip-fit, the dowel falls out. The **interference/lock side defines the location**, and the **clearance side permits assembly** — this asymmetry is the heart of every dowel joint. --- ## 2.2 Design Steps for a Dowel Joint (Engineering Methodology) 1. **Identify the locating surfaces** that need to be repeated. 2. **Decide the number of dowels** — minimum 2 (to prevent rotation); 3 or 4 for heavy tools. 3. **Decide dowel diameter (d)** — typically **1/3 to 1/2 of the bolt diameter** it accompanies. 4. **Decide dowel length (L)** — typically **1.5 d to 2.5 d** embedded depth on the press-fit side. 5. **Select standard material** — **EN8 / EN31 / AISI 52100** bearing steel, hardened to **58–62 HRC**. 6. **Choose surface finish** — Ground to **Ra 0.2–0.4 µm**. 7. **Specify tolerances** — Use **ISO 8734** standards. 8. **Provide extraction provision** — A small slot, tapped hole, or shoulder for pull-out dowels. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are the **complete mathematical toolkit** required to solve any KTU numerical on dowel design. Memorise these as a unit. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Dowel Diameter Selection | $d = (0.3 \text{ to } 0.5) \cdot d_{bolt}$ | mm | Empirical, based on adjacent bolt size | | 2 | Standard Dowel Length | $L = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \cdot d$ | mm | Embedded length on press-fit side | | 3 | Shear Stress in Dowel | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{F}{\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}} = \dfrac{4F}{\pi d^{2}}$ | N/mm² | Single shear plane (most common) | | 4 | Bearing (Crushing) Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{F}{d \cdot L_{contact}}$ | N/mm² | Force on projected bearing area | | 5 | Dowel Quantity Check (No. of Dowels) | $n = \dfrac{F_{total}}{F_{allowable\_per\_dowel}}$ | integer | Round up to next whole number | | 6 | Allowable Shear Load (Single Dowel) | $F_{allow} = \tau_{allow} \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | N | $\tau_{allow} \approx 0.4 \cdot \sigma_{y}$ for hardened steel | | 7 | Extraction Force (Pull-Out Dowel) | $F_{ext} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot p_{friction}$ | N | Where $p_{friction} \approx 10\text{–}20$ MPa interference pressure | | 8 | Spacing Between Dowels | $S_{min} = 2d$ to $3d$ | mm | Avoid interference of stress fields | | 9 | Dowel Distance from Edge | $E \geq 1.5 d$ | mm | To prevent edge failure | | 10 | Tapered Dowel Taper | $1:50$ (standard) | — | Self-locking taper angle | --- ## 2.4 Material Selection for Dowels > [!TIP] > **Board-Exam Favourite:** "Why are dowels hardened?" — Because they must **resist wear** over thousands of assembly cycles, and they must **not deform** under the press-fit installation stress. If the dowel deforms, the locating accuracy is permanently lost. | Material | Hardness (HRC) | Typical Use | |----------|---------------|-------------| | **AISI 52100 (bearing steel)** | 60–62 | Precision jigs, dies | | **EN31 (equivalent)** | 58–62 | Indian tool-room standard | | **EN8 (mild-medium carbon)** | 30–35 (case-hardened) | Low-precision fixtures | | **Carbide** | 70+ | High-wear, abrasive environments | --- ## 2.5 Tolerances — Standard ISO 8734 Specification For a **10 mm nominal dowel**: ```text Type A (press-fit both ends): Ø10 m6 → Ø10.012 to 10.018 Type B (press/slip): Ø10 m6 / h7 → press: +0.012/+0.018; slip: 0.000/+0.015 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"P-Press / S-Slip"** — The **P**in goes in the body with **P**ress fit, the **S**liding component receives it with **S**lip fit. The body's hole is **smaller (H7) with r6/m6 interference pin**, the moving component's hole is **larger (H7) with g6/h6 clearance pin**. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Demonstrations ## 3.1 Derivation: Shear Stress in a Dowel Pin A dowel pin is loaded in **single shear** when two mating parts try to slide across each other in the plane perpendicular to the dowel axis. Consider a dowel of diameter $d$ subjected to a transverse force $F$. The cross-sectional area resisting shear is the circular cross-section: $$ A_{shear} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} $$ By definition of shear stress: $$ \tau = \frac{\text{Shear Force}}{\text{Resisting Area}} $$ Substituting the area: $$ \tau = \frac{F}{A_{shear}} = \frac{F}{\dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}} $$ Therefore, the **governing shear stress equation** is: $$ \boxed{\tau = \frac{4F}{\pi d^{2}}} $$ **Rearranged for design** (to find required diameter for a given load and allowable stress): $$ \boxed{d = \sqrt{\frac{4F}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning:** Doubling the diameter quadruples the load capacity — because area scales with the *square* of diameter. This is why KTU examiners love this formula: small increases in $d$ yield big increases in $F_{allow}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Bearing (Crushing) Stress The dowel also bears against the hole wall along its embedded length $L$. The projected bearing area is: $$ A_{bearing} = d \cdot L $$ The bearing stress is: $$ \boxed{\sigma_{b} = \frac{F}{d \cdot L}} $$ For a hardened dowel in a softer jig body, the **body** is the weaker element. Hence, $L$ must be sufficient such that: $$ \sigma_{b} \leq \sigma_{b,\text{allow}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L \geq \frac{F}{d \cdot \sigma_{b,\text{allow}}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style) > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]:** > A drill jig plate is to be located on the jig base using dowel pins. The cutting force during drilling is $F = 3500$ N. The dowel material is hardened EN31 steel with allowable shear stress $\tau_{allow} = 90$ MPa and allowable bearing stress $\sigma_{b,allow} = 140$ MPa. The adjacent bolt is M12. > > **Design the dowel pin and determine:** > 1. Required number of dowels and diameter > 2. Required embedded length > 3. Spacing and edge distance > 4. Specify the fit type for both holes. ### 🔹 Step 1: Decide number of dowels A drill jig typically uses **2 dowels** (placed diagonally) to prevent rotation. Use 2 dowels; each carries half the load. $$ F_{per\_dowel} = \frac{3500}{2} = 1750 \text{ N} $$ ### 🔹 Step 2: Diameter from shear consideration $$ d = \sqrt{\frac{4 F_{per\_dowel}}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}} $$ $$ d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1750}{\pi \times 90}} = \sqrt{\frac{7000}{282.74}} = \sqrt{24.76} $$ $$ d = 4.976 \text{ mm} \approx 5 \text{ mm} $$ ### 🔹 Step 3: Verify against bolt-based empirical rule For M12 bolt: $$ d_{empirical} = 0.4 \times 12 = 4.8 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{use } 5 \text{ mm standard.} $$ ### 🔹 Step 4: Diameter from bearing consideration (cross-check) $$ L = \frac{F_{per\_dowel}}{d \cdot \sigma_{b,allow}} = \frac{1750}{5 \times 140} = 2.5 \text{ mm} $$ But this is the *minimum* bearing length. In practice, we use the standard **L = 1.5 d to 2.5 d** rule: $$ L = 2.0 \times 5 = 10 \text{ mm} $$ So we use **L = 10 mm**, which safely exceeds the 2.5 mm bearing requirement. ✅ ### 🔹 Step 5: Spacing and edge distance $$ S_{min} = 2.5 d = 2.5 \times 5 = 12.5 \text{ mm} $$ $$ E_{min} = 1.5 d = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5 \text{ mm} $$ ### 🔹 Step 6: Specify fits (KTU 14-mark gold) - **Hole in jig base** (the fixed body): **H7 hole with m6 dowel** → **Press fit** - **Hole in jig plate** (the inserted component): **H7 hole with g6 dowel** → **Slip fit** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Design Summary (write this in the exam):** > - Number of dowels: **2** > - Diameter: **Ø5 mm** > - Length: **10 mm** > - Material: **EN31, hardened to 60 HRC** > - Base hole: **Ø5 H7 (press) + Ø5 m6 dowel** > - Plate hole: **Ø5 H7 (slip) + Ø5 g6 dowel** --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Dowel Selection Quick-Reference | Bolt Size | Recommended Dowel Ø | Standard Length (mm) | Min. Spacing (mm) | Min. Edge Distance (mm) | |-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------------|--------------------------| | M6 | 3 mm | 6, 10, 16 | 9 | 5 | | M8 | 4 mm | 8, 12, 20 | 12 | 6 | | M10 | 5 mm | 10, 16, 24 | 15 | 8 | | M12 | 6 mm | 12, 20, 30 | 18 | 9 | | M16 | 8 mm | 16, 25, 40 | 24 | 12 | | M20 | 10 mm | 20, 30, 50 | 30 | 15 | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Dowel Joint Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ASSEMBLY SYSTEM]:::sys --> B[Fixed Body e.g. Jig Base]:::fixed A --> C[Moving Component e.g. Bush Plate]:::moving B --> B1["Hole in Jig Base
H7 Tolerance"]:::fixed B1 --> B2["Press Fit
Dowel Section AB"]:::press B2 --> D["DOWEL PIN
EN31 / 52100 Steel
Hardened 60 HRC"]:::dowel C --> C1["Hole in Bush Plate
H7 Tolerance"]:::moving C1 --> C2["Slip Fit
Dowel Section BC"]:::slip C2 --> D D --> E["Functions"]:::func E --> E1[Location]:::func E --> E2[Alignment]:::func E --> E3[Anti-Rotation]:::func E --> E4[Shear Load Transfer]:::func D --> F["Design Equations"]:::eq F --> F1["Tau = 4F over pi d squared"]:::eq F --> F2["Sigma b = F over d times L"]:::eq F --> F3["L = 1.5 d to 2.5 d"]:::eq classDef sys fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef fixed fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef moving fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef press fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef slip fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef dowel fill:#9333ea,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef func fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef eq fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Dowel Design Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1: Identify locating surfaces and applied load F"]:::step S2["STEP 2: Decide number of dowels N = 2 to 4"]:::step S3["STEP 3: Compute load per dowel F per dowel = F over N"]:::step S4["STEP 4: Compute d shear = sqrt of 4F over pi tau allow"]:::step S5["STEP 5: Compute d bolt = 0.3 to 0.5 times bolt diameter"]:::step S6["STEP 6: Select standard d greater than max of d shear and d bolt"]:::step S7["STEP 7: Compute length L = 1.5 d to 2.5 d"]:::step S8["STEP 8: Check bearing stress Sigma b = F over dL"]:::step S9["STEP 9: Specify fits P press for base S slip for plate"]:::step S10["STEP 10: Specify dowel material EN31 hardened 60 HRC"]:::step S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Dowel Pin Anatomy (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR P[DOWEL PIN GEOMETRY]:::pin --> R1["Chamfer End 1
30 degree for entry"]:::part P --> R2["Press Fit Zone
Length 1.5 d to 2.0 d"]:::part P --> R3["Slip Fit Zone
Length equal to mating thickness"]:::part P --> R4["Chamfer End 2
30 degree for entry"]:::part P --> R5["Optional Extraction Slot
0.5 mm wide by 1 mm deep"]:::part R2 --> M1["Material EN31 or AISI 52100"]:::mat R2 --> M2["Surface Finish Ground Ra 0.2 to 0.4 micrometer"]:::mat R2 --> M3["Hardness 58 to 62 HRC"]:::mat classDef pin fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef part fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef mat fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Define a dowel pin. State any two functions of a dowel pin in a jig or fixture.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A dowel pin is a hardened, precision-ground cylindrical steel pin used to locate and align two mating parts in a jig, fixture, die, or machine assembly. It is press-fitted into one component and slip-fitted into the other. **Two functions:** 1. **Location and alignment** — ensures that components reassemble in the identical position after every disassembly, preserving the machined datum. 2. **Shear load transfer** — carries transverse (shear) loads across the joint interface, preventing lateral movement of the mating parts during operation. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two functions = 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Why is one end of a dowel pin press-fitted and the other end slip-fitted? What would happen if both ends were press-fitted?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **press-fit end** is installed in the fixed/locator body and defines the **master datum position**. The **slip-fit end** is in the movable component, which allows the operator to slide the part on and off the dowel for repeated assembly/disassembly. If both ends were press-fitted, the parts could never be assembled without damaging the dowel or the holes (or would require unrealistic press force), making the joint **non-detachable and non-functional** for a removable jig/fixture application. *(1 mark for press-fit purpose + 1 mark for slip-fit purpose + 1 mark for consequence of both press-fit = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) ### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q8(a,b) modified]** **(a)** A pillar-type drill jig is to be designed with two dowel pins to locate a steel bush plate on the cast-iron base. The maximum drilling thrust is **4.2 kN**. The dowel material is **hardened EN31 (60 HRC)** with $\tau_{allow} = 95$ MPa and $\sigma_{b,allow} = 150$ MPa. Adjacent bolts are **M16**. Design the dowel pin: determine diameter, length, fit specifications, edge distance and spacing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the classification of dowel pins with neat sketches. List the Indian/ISO standards applicable to dowel pins. **(7 marks)** #### 🔸 Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Load per dowel:** $$ F_{per} = \frac{4200}{2} = 2100 \text{ N} $$ **Step 2 — Diameter by shear:** $$ d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2100}{\pi \times 95}} = \sqrt{\frac{8400}{298.45}} = \sqrt{28.15} = 5.30 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Empirical diameter from bolt:** $$ d_{bolt-based} = 0.4 \times 16 = 6.4 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4 — Select standard diameter (use larger):** $d = 8$ mm (next standard above 6.4 mm). **Step 5 — Length:** $$ L = 2.0 \times 8 = 16 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 6 — Bearing check:** $$ \sigma_{b} = \frac{2100}{8 \times 16} = 16.4 \text{ MPa} \ll 150 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 7 — Spacing and edge distance:** $$ S = 2.5d = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad E = 1.5d = 12 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 8 — Fits:** - Base hole: **H7 with m6 dowel → press fit** - Plate hole: **H7 with g6 dowel → slip fit** **Final Design:** 2 dowels, Ø8 mm × 16 mm, EN31, 60 HRC, ground, H7/m6 + H7/g6. #### 🔸 Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Classification of Dowel Pins** (write as 4 points × ~1.5 marks each = 6 marks, + 1 mark for standards): 1. **Straight (parallel) dowels** — cylindrical, hardened, most common in jigs. 2. **Tapered dowels** — with standard taper 1:50, used in self-aligning joints. 3. **Grooved / spring dowels** — have longitudinal grooves for slight compression (to absorb tolerance variation). 4. **Pull-out (removable) dowels** — have a slot/head to extract easily for maintenance. 5. **Semi-permanent dowels** — for components that need frequent removal but not daily. **Applicable standards** (1 mark): - **IS 6193** (Indian Standard) - **ISO 8734** (Type A — both ends press-fit; Type B — press/slip) - **DIN 6325** (German standard) > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to verify the empirical rule** $d = 0.3$–$0.5 d_{bolt}$ alongside the shear-based $d$. Many students compute only the shear diameter and miss the bolt-relation check, losing **1–2 marks**. > 2. **Reversing the fit specification** — writing "slip-fit in the base" loses full marks for fit specification. **Always: press-fit in the FIXED (body) part, slip-fit in the MOVABLE (plate) part.** > 3. **Not rounding up to standard sizes** — KTU expects you to use a standard dowel diameter (5, 6, 8, 10 mm), not a calculated odd value like 5.3 mm. > 4. **Missing the bearing check** — only doing the shear check is incomplete design. --- ### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **press-fit and slip-fit arrangement** of dowel pins in a drill jig. Why are dowels always made of hardened steel? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **two-dowel locating arrangement** must resist a transverse load of **$F = 6000$ N**. The allowable shear stress is **$\tau_{allow} = 80$ MPa**, and the bolt size is **M20**. Determine the dowel diameter, length, and the number of dowels required. Specify fits. **(7 marks)** #### 🔸 Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Sketch (describe):** A 2D cross-section of a drill jig base and bush plate joined by two dowels. Annotate: "H7/m6 — press fit (left part)" and "H7/g6 — slip fit (right part)". **Hardened steel reasoning** (5 key points × ~1.4 marks each): 1. **Wear resistance** — dowels are repeatedly inserted/removed; hardened surface resists abrasion. 2. **Dimensional stability** — hardened dowels do not deform under press-fit installation stress, preserving location accuracy. 3. **Load-carrying capacity** — hardened steel has high shear and bearing strength. 4. **Long service life** — the jig/die must last thousands of cycles; soft dowels would wear and shift. 5. **Grinding compatibility** — hardened steel can be ground to micron-level tolerances (Ra 0.2 µm). #### 🔸 Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1:** Number of dowels: minimum 2 (problem states 2-dowel arrangement). **Step 2:** Load per dowel: $$ F_{per} = \frac{6000}{2} = 3000 \text{ N} $$ **Step 3:** Shear-based diameter: $$ d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3000}{\pi \times 80}} = \sqrt{\frac{12000}{251.33}} = \sqrt{47.75} = 6.91 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4:** Bolt-based diameter: $$ d_{bolt} = 0.4 \times 20 = 8 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5:** Select standard: $d = 8$ mm (governs). **Step 6:** Length: $$ L = 2.0 \times 8 = 16 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 7:** Fits: - Base: **H7/m6 press fit** - Plate: **H7/g6 slip fit** **Final design:** 2 dowels of **Ø8 mm × 16 mm**, hardened EN31, 60 HRC. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > 🎯 **Read this block 5 times the night before the exam — these are the 12 most-likely "exam-winning" points:** - ✅ A dowel pin is a **hardened, ground, cylindrical locating pin** — never soft, never rough. - ✅ One end is **press-fit (H7/m6)** in the **fixed body**, the other is **slip-fit (H7/g6)** in the **movable part**. - ✅ Minimum **2 dowels** are used to prevent rotation; 1 dowel cannot prevent rotation, so 1 is meaningless. - ✅ Diameter selection: use the **larger** of (a) $d = \sqrt{4F/\pi\tau}$ and (b) $d = 0.3$–$0.5 \cdot d_{bolt}$. - ✅ Length selection: $L = 1.5d$ to $2.5d$ on the press-fit side. - ✅ **Spacings:** $S \geq 2.5d$, **Edge distance:** $E \geq 1.5d$. - ✅ Materials: **EN31 / AISI 52100**, hardened to **58–62 HRC**, surface finish **Ra 0.2–0.4 µm**. - ✅ Standards: **ISO 8734** (international), **IS 6193** (Indian), **DIN 6325** (German). - ✅ Tapered dowel taper: **1:50** (self-locking). - ✅ Dowels carry **shear load** primarily; bolts carry **tension/clamping load** — they are *complementary, not redundant*. - ✅ Pull-out dowels have a **slot/head** for extraction with a screw or pin punch. - ✅ **Never** reuse a press-fit dowel without re-checking diameter — press-fit installation slightly expands the pin and the receiving hole. > [!WARNING] > **Last-Month KTU Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark Part B question on dowel design, you can secure **at least 10–11 marks** by: > (1) drawing the assembly sketch with press/slip marked, > (2) writing 3 design steps: shear, length, fits, > (3) writing the final specification table. > Don't waste time writing reams of theory — the steps and the *final numbers* are what examiners reward.

topics.title: Reduction of Machined Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reduction of Machined Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduction of Machined Area** is a fundamental principle of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)** that focuses on minimizing the **surface area (and volumetric material) that must be removed through machining operations** during the production of a component. It aims to redesign parts so that only the **functional surfaces** (those that genuinely require tight dimensional tolerances, superior surface finish, or specific geometric features) are machined, while non-functional surfaces are left in the as-formed state from primary shaping processes such as **casting, forging, powder metallurgy, or extrusion**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Reduction of Machined Area is classified under **Design for Machining (DFM-3.2)** and is treated as a **cost-driving design parameter**, because the **machining cost is directly proportional to the volume of material removed** and the **machining time required**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sculpture Principle":**
> Imagine you are a sculptor asked to create a **statue of a human figure** from a large block of marble. A *poor sculptor* would carve away huge chunks of marble, even from the back, bottom, and sides of the statue that no one will ever see. This wastes enormous effort, marble dust, time, and energy. A *skilled sculptor* (an efficient designer) starts with an **approximate pre-shaped marble form** (like a rough figure roughly shaped by another craftsman first) and only **carves the visible, functional surfaces** — the face, hands, and front-facing details.
>
> In manufacturing, the **"rough pre-shape"** is what you get from casting, forging, or powder metallurgy, and the **"final sculpting"** is machining. **Reduction of Machined Area = "Don't carve what nobody will ever see!"**

The same philosophy applies to engineering parts:
- A **mating surface** that must mate precisely → **machine it** ✓
- A **decorative or hidden boss surface** → **don't machine it** ✗
- A **bearing seat** requiring tight tolerance → **machine it** ✓
- A **flat back surface** of a bracket hidden inside a machine → **leave it as-cast** ✗

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

> [!NOTE]
> The key engineering metrics associated with Reduction of Machined Area are:
> - **Machined Area (A_m)** — measured in **mm²** or **cm²**
> - **Volume of Material Removed (V_r)** — measured in **mm³** or **cm³**
> - **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — measured in **mm³/s** or **cm³/min**
> - **Machining Time (T_m)** — measured in **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)**
> - **Specific Machining Energy (U)** — typically **0.4 to 3.0 W·s/mm³** for common engineering materials
> - **Cost per unit volume removed (C_v)** — measured in **₹/cm³** or **$/in³**

The guiding principle can be written qualitatively as:

$$\text{Total Machining Cost} \propto A_m \;\times\; \text{Depth of Cut} \;\times\; \text{Machining Time}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Engineering Logic — Why Reduce Machined Area?

The strategic rationale for minimizing machined area is rooted in **four pillars of manufacturing economics**:

1. **Direct Cost Reduction** — Machining is the **most expensive shaping process** per unit mass removed. A typical turning operation costs **5 to 10 times more** per kg of material removed than a casting or forging operation.
2. **Energy Conservation** — Specific machining energy is high; reducing material removed conserves electrical energy.
3. **Tool Life Preservation** — Every mm³ of material removed gradually wears the cutting tool. Less material removal → **longer tool life** → fewer tool changes.
4. **Cycle Time Reduction** — Machining time is a primary component of the total part cycle time. Reducing it directly improves **production throughput**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic for Reduction of Machined Area

The design engineer should follow this **structured 6-step logic** when applying the principle:

- **Step 1 — Identify Functional Surfaces:**
  Catalog every surface of the part and ask: *Does this surface require a specific tolerance, surface finish, or geometric form?* Only surfaces that pass this test are candidates for machining.

- **Step 2 — Classify Surfaces into Three Tiers:**
  - **Tier-A (Critical):** Bearing seats, mating bores, datum surfaces, threaded holes → **Must be machined**
  - **Tier-B (Semi-critical):** External diameters for press-fits, locating surfaces → **Machined only where necessary**
  - **Tier-C (Non-critical):** External ribs, decorative surfaces, hidden bosses → **Never machine**

- **Step 3 — Specify the Primary Shaping Process:**
  Select a near-net-shape process (casting, forging, MIM, extrusion) that delivers the **as-formed shape as close to the final shape as economically possible**.

- **Step 4 — Redesign Bulk Sections into Ribs/Webs:**
  Replace **solid bulky sections** with **ribbed, webbed, or cored structures** to reduce both the material in the part and the area that might otherwise be machined.

- **Step 5 — Standardize on Stock Forms:**
  Use standard round bars, standard plates, standard tubes, and standard channels so that the **raw stock is already close to the final cross-section**, minimizing material to be removed.

- **Step 6 — Use Bosses with Controlled Heights and Avoid Machining Both Sides:**
  Design bosses such that the *machined face is the only functional face*. Avoid designs where both sides of a wall require machining.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Reduction of Machined Area principle is extensively applied in:
> - **Automotive industry** — Engine blocks, cylinder heads, transmission housings (use cast iron / aluminum castings with minimal post-machining)
> - **Aerospace industry** — Titanium and aluminum alloy structural components use **near-net-shape forging** to minimize expensive, slow machining of difficult-to-cut alloys
> - **Pump and valve manufacturing** — Pump bodies and valve bonnets are designed as **castings with machined bores and sealing faces only**
> - **Consumer electronics** — Laptop and phone chassis use **die-casting + minimal CNC milling** for cosmetic surfaces
> - **Hydraulic systems** — Cylinder blocks, manifolds — cast iron bodies with **precisely machined port faces and bores only**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all the **high-yield formulas** a KTU student must memorize for problems on Reduction of Machined Area and the related machining-time/cost trade-offs.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{MRR} = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material Removal Rate (turning/milling) | mm³/s |
| 2 | $T_m = \dfrac{V_r}{\text{MRR}} = \dfrac{V_r}{v \cdot f \cdot d}$ | Machining time from removed volume | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $V_r = A_m \cdot \Delta r$ | Removed volume = Machined area × Radial depth | mm³ |
| 4 | $C_m = C_v \cdot V_r$ | Machining cost proportional to removed volume | ₹ / $ |
| 5 | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{setup}} + C_v \cdot V_r$ | Total cost = fixed setup + variable removal cost | ₹ / $ |
| 6 | $\text{Saving \%} = \dfrac{V_{r,\text{old}} - V_{r,\text{new}}}{V_{r,\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Percentage volume reduction saving | % |
| 7 | $T_{m,\text{new}} = T_{m,\text{old}} \times \dfrac{A_{m,\text{new}}}{A_{m,\text{old}}}$ | New machining time after area reduction | s |
| 8 | $E_m = U \cdot V_r$ | Machining energy = Specific energy × Removed volume | Joules (J) |
| 9 | $P = U \cdot \text{MRR}$ | Cutting power required | Watts (W) |
| 10 | $C_{\text{machining}} = C_m + C_l + C_t$ | Machining cost = material + labor + tool cost | ₹ / $ |

**Where:**
- $v$ = cutting speed (m/min or mm/s)
- $f$ = feed rate (mm/rev or mm/tooth)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)
- $A_m$ = machined area (mm²)
- $\Delta r$ = radial infeed / depth removed (mm)
- $V_r$ = volume of material removed (mm³)
- $U$ = specific machining energy (W·s/mm³)
- $C_m, C_l, C_t$ = material, labor, tool cost components
- $C_v$ = cost per unit volume removed (₹/mm³ or ₹/cm³)

## 2.5 Key Design Heuristics for KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> **Ten KTU-Favorite Design Heuristics for Reducing Machined Area:**
> 1. Use **castings/forgings** with ribs instead of solid blocks
> 2. Specify **standard stock sizes** (round bar, hex bar)
> 3. **Avoid machining both sides** of a part wall
> 4. Use **cored holes** in castings instead of drilled/bored holes
> 5. **Limit boss height** so that standard tools reach the face
> 6. Use **partial-depth machining** — machine only the bearing seat width, not the full length
> 7. Replace **separate flanges** with **integrally cast flanges** in one piece
> 8. Avoid **large flat surfaces** that need extensive face milling
> 9. Use **powder metallurgy** for complex small parts to eliminate machining almost entirely
> 10. Specify **looser tolerances** on non-functional surfaces (ISO 2768-mK or fine-grade)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Machining Time as a Function of Machined Area

We start from the **fundamental definition of Material Removal Rate (MRR)** in a turning operation on a cylindrical workpiece:

$$\text{MRR} = v \cdot f \cdot d$$

where:
- $v$ = cutting velocity (mm/s)
- $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)

The **total volume of material removed** $V_r$ can be expressed in terms of the **machined area** $A_m$ and the **radial infeed** $\Delta r$:

$$V_r = A_m \cdot \Delta r$$

The **machining time** is defined as the **ratio of removed volume to material removal rate**:

$$T_m = \frac{V_r}{\text{MRR}}$$

Substituting the expressions for $V_r$ and MRR:

$$T_m = \frac{A_m \cdot \Delta r}{v \cdot f \cdot d}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Since $d = \Delta r$ in turning (the radial depth of cut equals the radial infeed), the formula simplifies to:

$$T_m = \frac{A_m}{v \cdot f}$$

This shows **directly and cleanly** that:
> **Machining time is *linearly proportional* to the machined area when cutting parameters are held constant.**

Therefore, if we redesign the part such that the **machined area is reduced from $A_{m,1}$ to $A_{m,2}$**, the new machining time becomes:

$$T_{m,2} = T_{m,1} \cdot \frac{A_{m,2}}{A_{m,1}}$$

And the **percentage saving in machining time** is:

$$\eta_{\text{saving}} = \frac{T_{m,1} - T_{m,2}}{T_{m,1}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{\text{saving}} = \left(1 - \frac{A_{m,2}}{A_{m,1}}\right) \times 100\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost Savings Due to Reduced Machined Area

The **machining cost** $C_m$ is:

$$C_m = C_v \cdot V_r = C_v \cdot A_m \cdot \Delta r$$

The **direct cost saving** when machined area is reduced from $A_{m,1}$ to $A_{m,2}$ is:

$$\Delta C = C_v \cdot (A_{m,1} - A_{m,2}) \cdot \Delta r$$

The **percentage cost saving** is:

$$\% \text{ Cost Saving} = \frac{A_{m,1} - A_{m,2}}{A_{m,1}} \times 100\%$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — A KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A flanged coupling hub is currently designed as a **solid cylindrical block of diameter 80 mm and length 120 mm** (rough forging). The current design requires the **entire external cylindrical surface to be turned** to a final diameter of 75 mm, along with one end face and a keyway.
>
> An alternative design proposes to use a **cored (hollow) forging** with an outer diameter of 80 mm, an **internal bore of 50 mm diameter (cast core, no machining required)**, and the same external turning of 75 mm final diameter.
>
> Calculate the **percentage saving in machining time and cost** for the redesigned hub. Take cutting speed $v = 200$ m/min, feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev, and assume the axial length to be turned is $L = 120$ mm.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Original Machined Area $A_{m,1}$

The original hub is a solid cylinder. The machined area consists of:
- **External cylindrical surface** (turned): $A_{\text{cyl,1}} = \pi D L = \pi \times 80 \times 120$
- **One end face** (faced): $A_{\text{face,1}} = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi \times 80^2 / 4$

But for a fair comparison of *area* (not volume), and focusing on the cylindrical turning, we use:

$$A_{m,1} = \pi D_o L = \pi \times 80 \times 120$$

$$A_{m,1} = 30,159.29 \;\text{mm}^2$$

> (We will compare the *cylindrical machined area* only, since the internal bore is not machined in either design, except — wait, in the original solid design, no bore exists, but in the new design, the bore is a *cast core* so it is not machined either. The key comparison is the *external cylindrical area*, which is the same. So we must look at the **radial infeed** for a fair comparison.)

### Step 1 (Corrected) — Compare the Radial Infeed (Volume of Removal)

The **radial infeed** is the change in radius from rough to finished diameter.

**Original design:** Rough Ø = 80 mm, Finish Ø = 75 mm
$$\Delta r_1 = \frac{80 - 75}{2} = 2.5 \;\text{mm}$$

**Redesigned cored hub:** The external rough Ø is still 80 mm, finish Ø is still 75 mm
$$\Delta r_2 = \frac{80 - 75}{2} = 2.5 \;\text{mm}$$

For a fair "machined area" comparison on the **outside**, both are identical. The real saving is **not in the external turning**, but rather in the **internal machining**.

Let us consider that in the **original design** (a hypothetical alternative), the hub required an **internal bore to be machined from solid (drilled + bored) from Ø 0 to Ø 50 mm**, while in the **redesigned cored version**, this bore is already a **cast core** and requires no machining.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Volume Removed in Internal Machining (Original Design)

$$V_{r,\text{internal}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L = \frac{\pi \times 50^2}{4} \times 120$$

$$V_{r,\text{internal}} = \frac{\pi \times 2500}{4} \times 120 = \pi \times 625 \times 120$$

$$V_{r,\text{internal}} = 235,619.45 \;\text{mm}^3$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Machining Time Saved

Convert cutting speed to mm/s:
$$v = 200 \;\text{m/min} = \frac{200 \times 1000}{60} = 3333.33 \;\text{mm/s}$$

Feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev. The depth of cut in boring equals the radial infeed, which is $25$ mm (since we bore from Ø 0 to Ø 50, each side cuts 25 mm — but typically done in multiple passes). For a single-pass approximation:
$$d = 25 \;\text{mm}$$

Material Removal Rate:
$$\text{MRR} = v \cdot f \cdot d = 3333.33 \times 0.3 \times 25 = 25,000 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Time saved by avoiding internal boring:
$$T_{\text{saved}} = \frac{V_{r,\text{internal}}}{\text{MRR}} = \frac{235,619.45}{25,000} = 9.42 \;\text{seconds}$$

### Step 4 — Express as Percentage of Original Machining Time

The original total external turning time:
$$T_{m,\text{ext}} = \frac{\pi D_o L}{v \cdot f} = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 120}{3333.33 \times 0.3} = \frac{30,159.29}{1000} = 30.16 \;\text{s}$$

Percentage saving:
$$\% \text{ Time Saved} = \frac{9.42}{30.16 + 9.42} \times 100 = \frac{9.42}{39.58} \times 100 = 23.8\%$$

> **Conclusion:** By using a **cored forging** (cast internal bore), approximately **23.8% of the total machining time is saved** without any compromise on the functional surfaces.

### Step 5 — Cost Impact

If the shop rate is ₹ 5 per second of machining time:
$$C_{\text{saved}} = 9.42 \times 5 = \text{₹ } 47.10 \text{ per part}$$

For a batch of 10,000 parts per year:
$$\text{Annual Saving} = 47.10 \times 10,000 = \text{₹ } 4,71,000$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how a *single design change* (introducing a cast core) eliminates a *complete boring operation* — this is the **highest-leverage design intervention** in DFM, because it removes an *entire process step*, not just reduces its duration.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Before vs. After Comparison

| Design Parameter | Original Design (Solid Block) | Redesigned (Cored Forging) | Effect on Machined Area |
|------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| External Turning | Required (Ø 80 → 75) | Required (Ø 80 → 75) | **No change** |
| Internal Boring | Required (solid → Ø 50) | **Eliminated** (cast core) | **Major reduction** |
| End Facing | Required | Required | **No change** |
| Material Used | 603,186 mm³ | 376,991 mm³ | **37.5% less** |
| Number of Machining Operations | 3 (turn, bore, face) | 2 (turn, face) | **One less operation** |
| Machining Time | 39.58 s | 30.16 s | **23.8% faster** |
| Tool Changes | More frequent | Fewer | **Tool life extended** |
| Energy Consumed | Higher | Lower | **Sustainable manufacturing** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reduction of Machined Area Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Part Design] --> B{Identify All Surfaces of the Part}
    B --> C[Classify Each Surface]
    C --> D1[Tier A: Critical - Must Machine]
    C --> D2[Tier B: Semi-Critical - Selective Machining]
    C --> D3[Tier C: Non-Critical - No Machining]
    D1 --> E[Apply Standard Machining Process]
    D2 --> F{Net-Shape Process Feasible?}
    D3 --> G[Specify As-Cast or As-Forged Surface]
    F -- Yes --> H[Use Casting or Forging]
    F -- No --> E
    H --> I[Verify Tolerance and Finish on Critical Surfaces Only]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    I --> J{Quality Checks Passed?}
    J -- No --> K[Reiterate Design - Reduce Machined Area Further]
    K --> B
    J -- Yes --> L[Finalize Design and Release to Production]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Good vs Bad Design Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BAD[Bad Design: Excessive Machined Area]
        A1[Solid Cylindrical Block] --> A2[Machine Entire External Surface]
        A2 --> A3[Drill and Bore Internal Hole from Solid]
        A3 --> A4[Face Both Ends]
        A4 --> A5[Result: High Cost, Long Cycle Time]
    end

    subgraph GOOD[Good Design: Minimized Machined Area]
        B1[Near-Net-Shape Forging with Cast Core] --> B2[Turn Only Outer Functional Surface]
        B2 --> B3[Face Only One End]
        B3 --> B4[Internal Bore Already Formed - No Machining]
        B4 --> B5[Result: Low Cost, Short Cycle Time]
    end

    BAD -. Contrast .- GOOD
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a DFM-Reduced Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Final Part] --> S1[Surface 1: Bearing Seat - Machined]
    P --> S2[Surface 2: Mounting Flange Face - Machined]
    P --> S3[Surface 3: Outer Rib - As-Cast]
    P --> S4[Surface 4: Internal Bore - Cast Core]
    P --> S5[Surface 5: Decorative Boss - As-Cast]

    M[Manufacturing Process] --> M1[Machining Operation 1: Turn OD]
    M --> M2[Machining Operation 2: Face Flange]
    M --> M3[No Machining for S3, S4, S5]

    M1 --> P
    M2 --> P
    M3 --> P
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Impact Hierarchy (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reduce Machined Area] --> R1[Less Material to Remove]
    R1 --> R2[Lower Material Removal Rate Required]
    R2 --> R3[Shorter Machining Time]
    R3 --> R4[Lower Direct Labor Cost]
    R3 --> R5[Lower Machine Hour Cost]
    R3 --> R6[Lower Energy Cost]
    R2 --> R7[Less Tool Wear]
    R7 --> R8[Longer Tool Life]
    R8 --> R9[Lower Tool Replacement Cost]
    R --> R10[Use Near-Net-Shape Primary Process]
    R10 --> R11[Lower Material Cost]
    R10 --> R12[Lower Energy in Primary Forming]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to *illustrate* a design improvement, draw a **before-after sketch** with clear labels: e.g., a solid boss vs. a ribbed boss, or a through-bored hole vs. a cored hole. Examiners reward **clear, labeled, dimensioned** sketches.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *Define the term "Reduction of Machined Area" in the context of Design for Manufacture. List **any four** design guidelines to reduce machined area in a component.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Reduction of Machined Area is a DFM principle that aims to **minimize the surface area and volume of material that must be removed through machining operations** by designing parts so that only the **functional, tolerance-critical, and finish-critical surfaces** are machined, while all other surfaces are left in the as-formed state from primary shaping processes like casting, forging, or powder metallurgy.

**Four Design Guidelines:**
1. **Use near-net-shape processes** (casting, forging, MIM) to deliver the part as close to final shape as possible.
2. **Replace solid bulky sections with ribbed or webbed structures** to reduce both material and the area that could potentially be machined.
3. **Use standard stock forms** (round bars, hex bars, standard plates) so the raw material is already close to the final cross-section.
4. **Avoid machining both faces of a wall** — design features so that only one face of any wall requires machining.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Explain with a sketch how the use of a **cast core** in a casting can eliminate the need for internal machining and thereby reduce the overall machined area.*

**Model Answer:**

A **cast core** is a sand core placed inside the mould cavity during casting to form an **internal cavity, hole, or undercut** in the finished casting. When a designer specifies a part with an internal bore or cavity, the casting can be made *with* the cavity already formed by the core, eliminating the need to drill, bore, or ream the hole on a machine tool.

**Sketch Description:**
- **Left side (Bad Design):** A solid cylindrical block. To create an internal Ø 50 mm bore, the manufacturer must (a) drill a pilot hole, (b) bore to size, and (c) possibly hone or ream. This adds **multiple machining operations** and **large machined area** on the internal cylindrical surface.

- **Right side (Good Design):** The same hub is produced as a **cored casting**. The internal Ø 50 mm bore is formed by the sand core during the casting process itself. The bore emerges with a usable surface finish and tolerance, and may require **no further machining** or only a light reaming pass.

**Significance:** The cast core approach **eliminates an entire machining operation**, reduces cycle time, lowers tool consumption, and removes the volume of chips that would otherwise have been produced.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose marks in this topic because they:
> 1. **List guidelines without justification** — Examiners expect the *why* behind each guideline.
> 2. **Forget the link between machined area and machining time** — Always connect area reduction to **time, cost, and tool life**.
> 3. **Miss the cast core and standard stock** points — These are the *most frequently tested* sub-concepts.
> 4. **Do not include a sketch** — A clear before-after sketch is worth **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Confuse "reduction of machined area" with "reducing tolerances"** — These are *related but distinct* DFM principles.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Discuss in detail the various **design guidelines for reducing the machined area** in a component. Support your answer with **at least four well-labeled sketches** showing before-and-after redesigns. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* A **bracket** is currently produced by machining **all six faces** of a 60 mm × 60 mm × 80 mm solid block of mild steel. As a DFM redesign, it is proposed to produce the same bracket as a **casting** with ribs, requiring machining of only the **two mounting faces and one mating face** (each 60 mm × 60 mm). The rough casting is 70 mm × 70 mm × 90 mm. Calculate the **percentage reduction in machined area** and the **corresponding reduction in machining cost** if the shop rate is ₹ 0.05 per mm² of machined area. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Design Guidelines for Reducing Machined Area:**

1. **Use Near-Net-Shape Primary Processes:**
   Use castings, forgings, extrusions, or powder metallurgy parts whose as-formed shape is already very close to the final geometry. The less material that has to be removed, the less machined area is required.

2. **Replace Solid Sections with Ribs/Webs:**
   A solid 30 mm-thick wall can be replaced with a ribbed structure (3 mm face sheet + 5 mm ribs), reducing weight by 60% and eliminating the possibility of machining a thick face.

3. **Standardize on Stock Forms:**
   Use standard round bar (Ø 50 mm, Ø 75 mm) or standard hex bar stock so that the raw material is already close to the final cross-section.

4. **Limit Boss Heights and Avoid Large Flat Machined Faces:**
   Design bosses to a standard height so that standard cutters can reach; avoid large flat surfaces that require extensive face milling.

5. **Use Cast Cores for Internal Features:**
   Whenever an internal bore, oil passage, or undercut is required, specify it as a **cored feature** in the casting rather than machining it from solid.

6. **Avoid Machining Both Sides of a Wall:**
   If a wall has functional features on both sides, redesign with stepped features or a partition so that only one side needs to be machined.

7. **Specify Appropriate Tolerances:**
   Use ISO 2768-mK or similar general tolerance standards for non-critical surfaces, and reserve tight tolerances only for functional surfaces.

8. **Combine Multiple Operations into One Setup:**
   Use multi-axis CNC machines, special tooling, or formed cutters to reduce the number of separate machining passes.

**Sketches (described in text form for the answer script):**

- **Sketch 1:** A solid block vs. a ribbed plate — show the rib structure with cross-hatching.
- **Sketch 2:** A drilled through-hole vs. a cored hole in a casting.
- **Sketch 3:** A solid cylindrical hub vs. a cored hub with internal bore already cast.
- **Sketch 4:** A long solid shaft vs. a stepped shaft using standard bar stock with reduced turned length.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Calculate Original Machined Area $A_{m,1}$:**

The original bracket is a 60 × 60 × 80 mm solid block with all **6 faces machined**.

$$A_{m,1} = 2(60 \times 60) + 2(60 \times 80) + 2(60 \times 80)$$

$$A_{m,1} = 2(3600) + 2(4800) + 2(4800)$$

$$A_{m,1} = 7200 + 9600 + 9600 = 26,400 \;\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate New Machined Area $A_{m,2}$:**

The new design requires machining of:
- **2 mounting faces** (each 60 mm × 60 mm)
- **1 mating face** (60 mm × 60 mm)

$$A_{m,2} = 3 \times (60 \times 60) = 3 \times 3600 = 10,800 \;\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Reduction in Machined Area:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{A_{m,1} - A_{m,2}}{A_{m,1}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{26,400 - 10,800}{26,400} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{15,600}{26,400} \times 100 = 59.09\%$$

**Step 4 — Original Machining Cost:**

$$C_{m,1} = 0.05 \times 26,400 = \text{₹ } 1,320$$

**Step 5 — New Machining Cost:**

$$C_{m,2} = 0.05 \times 10,800 = \text{₹ } 540$$

**Step 6 — Cost Saving:**

$$\Delta C = 1,320 - 540 = \text{₹ } 780$$

$$\% \text{ Cost Saving} = \frac{780}{1320} \times 100 = 59.09\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The redesigned bracket achieves a **59.09% reduction in both machined area and machining cost**, demonstrating the powerful impact of DFM on manufacturing economics.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Explain the **principle of reduction of machined area** as a DFM guideline. With a neat sketch, show how a **stepped shaft design** can reduce the machined area compared to a uniform-diameter shaft of the same maximum diameter. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* An **aluminum housing** requires a **circular pocket of diameter 100 mm and depth 25 mm** to be machined. Two design alternatives are considered:
> - **Option 1:** Machine the pocket from a solid blank of thickness 30 mm.
> - **Option 2:** Cast the housing with the pocket already formed (cast core) — only a **light skimming pass of 1 mm depth** is required on the pocket floor to achieve the final tolerance.
>
> Calculate the **percentage saving in machining volume** and **machining time** if MRR for the skimming pass is 5000 mm³/s. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Principle of Reduction of Machined Area:**
The principle states that **only those surfaces of a part that genuinely require tight dimensional control, superior surface finish, or specific geometric features (such as bearing seats, threaded bores, mating flanges) should be machined**. All other surfaces should be left in their as-formed state from a primary shaping process. Reducing the machined area directly reduces **machining time, energy consumption, tool wear, and cost**.

**Stepped Shaft vs. Uniform Shaft — Sketch Description:**

- **Uniform Shaft:** A solid cylinder of Ø 50 mm and length 200 mm. If the entire length must be turned to a tight tolerance of ±0.05 mm, the entire lateral surface area $\pi \times 50 \times 200 = 31,416 \;\text{mm}^2$ is machined.

- **Stepped Shaft:** The same shaft is redesigned with three diameters — Ø 50 mm for 30 mm (bearing seat), Ø 45 mm for 100 mm (standard stock, no machining), and Ø 40 mm for 70 mm (another bearing seat). Only the **two stepped portions** are machined, totaling $2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 30 = 9,425 \;\text{mm}^2$ — a **70% reduction** in machined area.

The middle Ø 45 mm portion is produced by **drawing standard 45 mm bar stock** — no turning required.

#### Model Solution to (b):
