**Step 1 — Calculate Machined Volume in Option 1 (Solid Blank):**

The pocket is a cylinder of Ø 100 mm and depth 25 mm. The entire volume of the pocket is removed from the solid blank.

$$V_{r,1} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot h = \frac{\pi \times 100^2}{4} \times 25$$

$$V_{r,1} = \frac{\pi \times 10,000}{4} \times 25 = \pi \times 2500 \times 25$$

$$V_{r,1} = 196,349.54 \;\text{mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Machined Volume in Option 2 (Cored Casting + Skim):**

Only a 1 mm skimming pass over the **floor of the pocket** is required. The floor is a circular area of Ø 100 mm.

$$A_{\text{skim}} = \frac{\pi \times 100^2}{4} = 7853.98 \;\text{mm}^2$$

$$V_{r,2} = A_{\text{skim}} \times 1 = 7853.98 \;\text{mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Volume Saving:**

$$\% \text{ Volume Saved} = \frac{V_{r,1} - V_{r,2}}{V_{r,1}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Volume Saved} = \frac{196,349.54 - 7853.98}{196,349.54} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Volume Saved} = \frac{188,495.56}{196,349.54} \times 100 = 96.0\%$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time in Option 1:**

Assume MRR for the rough pocketing operation is **10,000 mm³/s** (typical for aluminum roughing).

$$T_{m,1} = \frac{V_{r,1}}{\text{MRR}} = \frac{196,349.54}{10,000} = 19.63 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Machining Time in Option 2:**

Given MRR for skimming pass = 5000 mm³/s.

$$T_{m,2} = \frac{V_{r,2}}{\text{MRR}} = \frac{7853.98}{5000} = 1.57 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage Time Saving:**

$$\% \text{ Time Saved} = \frac{T_{m,1} - T_{m,2}}{T_{m,1}} \times 100 = \frac{19.63 - 1.57}{19.63} \times 100 = 92.0\%$$

> **Conclusion:** By using a **cored casting with a single skimming pass**, the machining volume is reduced by **96%** and the machining time by **92%** — a dramatic demonstration of the power of the Reduction of Machined Area principle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Reduction of Machined Area":**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** Minimize the surface area/volume that must be removed by machining — machine only functional, tolerance-critical, and finish-critical surfaces.
>
> ✅ **Why It Matters:** Machining is the **most expensive shaping process per unit mass**; less material removed = lower cost, less time, less energy, longer tool life.
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
> - $T_m = \dfrac{A_m}{v \cdot f}$ (machining time ∝ machined area)
> - $V_r = A_m \cdot \Delta r$ (removed volume)
> - $C_m = C_v \cdot V_r$ (machining cost)
> - $\% \text{ Saving} = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{m,2}}{A_{m,1}}\right) \times 100$
>
> ✅ **Top 8 Design Guidelines:**
> 1. Use near-net-shape primary processes
> 2. Use ribs/webs instead of solid bulky sections
> 3. Use cast cores for internal features
> 4. Use standard stock forms (round, hex, plate)
> 5. Avoid machining both sides of a wall
> 6. Step shafts and stepped features
> 7. Limit boss heights to standard tool reach
> 8. Specify loose tolerances on non-functional surfaces
>
> ✅ **Common Exam Traps:**
> - Confusing *machined area* (mm²) with *machined volume* (mm³)
> - Forgetting to convert cutting speed to consistent units (m/min → mm/s)
> - Using **area ratios** for time problems when the problem is actually about **volume ratios** (e.g., when depth of cut also changes)
>
> ✅ **High-Yield Buzzwords for KTU Answers:**
> - "Near-net-shape"
> - "Cast core"
> - "Standard stock form"
> - "Functional vs. non-functional surface"
> - "As-cast tolerance"
> - "ISO 2768 general tolerance"
>
> ✅ **Numericals Always Require:**
> 1. State the given data with units
> 2. Calculate original machined area / volume
> 3. Calculate new machined area / volume
> 4. Apply the percentage saving formula
> 5. Conclude with a **practical engineering interpretation** ("This saves ₹ X per part and Y% cycle time")

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplification by Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simplification by Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simplification by Separation** is a fundamental principle of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)** in which a complex, multifunctional component is deliberately divided into two or more simpler, single-function sub-components. These sub-components are subsequently manufactured using the most suitable process for each, and finally joined (assembled) to deliver the identical end-function of the original monolithic part.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (aligned with NEP 2020 and Outcome-Based Education), simplification by separation is taught as a **top-down design rationalization strategy**. It is one of the three classical pillars of design simplification, the other two being:

1. **Simplification by Substitution** (replacing complex geometry with standard forms)
2. **Simplification by Refinement** (optimizing tolerances, materials, and processes)
3. **Simplification by Separation** (decomposing assemblies into simpler parts) ← *This Module*

The governing design philosophy states:

$$
\text{Manufacturability Index} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Part Complexity Factor (PCF)}}
$$

Where **PCF** is reduced when a complex part is separated into simpler ones, each carrying a single, well-defined function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Swiss Army Knife vs. a Toolbox**

Imagine you need to perform four different tasks: cut a wire, open a bottle, screw a bolt, and saw a small piece of wood. A **Swiss Army Knife** combines all four functions into a single, intricate, complex multi-tool. Manufacturing it requires multiple precision operations, exotic materials, and tight tolerances. Repairing one broken function means replacing the whole knife.

Now compare this to a **standard toolbox** containing a wire cutter, a bottle opener, a screwdriver, and a small saw. Each tool is a **separated, single-function sub-component**. Manufacturing each is simple (drop forging for the cutter, stamping for the opener, etc.). If one breaks, you only replace that single tool. The toolbox still delivers the same end-functions as the Swiss Army Knife, but each individual part is far simpler to design, produce, and maintain.

**This is the essence of Simplification by Separation.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The key engineering metrics used to evaluate the effectiveness of separation are:

- **DFA Index (%)** — Boothroyd–Dewhurst Design for Assembly efficiency metric
- **Minimum Number of Parts Criterion** — $N_{min}$
- **Handling Code (h)** — ease of part handling
- **Insertion Code (i)** — ease of part insertion/joining
- **Symmetry / Rotation Factor**
- **Standard Time per Assembly Operation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA Index** is a key KTU Board Exam metric, and students must remember that the theoretical maximum achievable DFA index is **100 %**, which represents a perfectly designed single-part product (no assembly required).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Decision for Separation

A KTU examiner expects students to know *when* to separate and *when not to*. The decision is governed by the following three principles:

### Pillar 1: Functional Necessity
A part should be separated only if it performs a **distinct, independent function** that cannot be efficiently integrated with another function. The famous Boothroyd question is:

> *"Does the part move relative to all other parts during the operation of the product?"*

If **Yes** → The part **must** be separate (cannot be merged).
If **No** → We must check the next pillar before deciding.

### Pillar 2: Manufacturing Necessity
Even if a part does not move, it may require:
- A **different material** (e.g., a soft rubber gasket bonded to a metal flange)
- A **different manufacturing process** (e.g., a polymer insert molded into a metal bracket)
- **Different tolerance grades** on adjacent features

In such cases, separation is justified.

### Pillar 3: Assembly Disadvantage Criterion
Every additional part in an assembly introduces:
- One extra **handling operation** (time $t_h$)
- One extra **insertion/joining operation** (time $t_i$)
- One extra **fastener/joining element** (cost $C_f$)

If the manufacturing cost saving from separation exceeds the assembly cost penalty, separation is economically justified.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for KTU Board Exams on this topic. Special characters have been escaped to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Term | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DFA Index | $D = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100$ | Assembly efficiency | \% |
| 2 | Total Assembly Time | $T_A = \sum_{k=1}^{N} (t_{h,k} + t_{i,k})$ | Time to assemble N parts | seconds |
| 3 | Manufacturing Cost of a Monolithic Part | $C_M = C_{machine} + C_{tooling} + C_{scrap}$ | Single-part cost | ₹ (INR) |
| 4 | Cost After Separation | $C_S = \sum_{j=1}^{m} C_{M,j} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} C_{A,k}$ | Sum of sub-part + assembly cost | ₹ (INR) |
| 5 | Net Cost Saving | $\Delta C = C_M - C_S$ | Justification for separation | ₹ (INR) |
| 6 | Tolerance Stack-up Rule | $T_{assembly} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}$ (statistical) or $T_{assembly} = \sum T_i$ (worst case) | Cumulative tolerance | mm |
| 7 | Minimum Parts Criterion (Boothroyd) | $N_{min}$ = parts that must move + parts requiring different material/process | Theoretical lower bound | count |
| 8 | Handling Difficulty (Symmetry) | $H = \frac{1}{\text{Number of orientations required}}$ | $H = 1$ for symmetric part, $0.5$ for 2-orientation, etc. | dimensionless |
| 9 | Separation Efficiency | $\eta_{sep} = \frac{\text{Cost saved by simpler processes}}{\text{Added assembly cost}}$ | Ratio deciding if separation is beneficial | dimensionless |
| 10 | Standard Time | $T_{std} = T_{normal} \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$ | Time standard for one assembly operation | seconds |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principle of Simplification by Separation is the backbone of:

- **Automotive Industry** — Engine blocks (monolithic) vs. modular sub-assemblies (cylinder head, crankcase, sump separated)
- **Aerospace** — Composite skin panels separated from internal ribs and stringers for ease of inspection and replacement
- **Electronics** — PCB sub-modules separated into power, analog, and digital sections for easier manufacture and repair
- **Consumer Appliances** — Modular washing machines where the motor, drum, and control panel are separate, serviceable units
- **Heavy Machinery** — Gears separated from shafts to allow independent material selection (e.g., hardened gear + mild steel shaft)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In design viva voce, always cite a **real industrial example** when explaining separation. Examiners reward applied understanding over rote definitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The DFA Index Formula

The **Design for Assembly (DFA) Index** is the most frequently asked formula in KTU examinations on this topic. Below is the full derivation.

### Step 1: Define the Theoretical Minimum Number of Parts

The minimum number of parts, $N_{min}$, is the smallest count of components that can deliver the desired function, considering only the unavoidable separations (moving parts + parts requiring different material or process).

$$
N_{min} = N_{moving} + N_{different\,material} + N_{different\,process}
$$

### Step 2: Define the Actual Number of Parts

The actual number of parts, $N_{actual}$, is the count of components in the **as-designed** product before simplification.

$$
N_{actual} = \text{count of all distinct components in the BOM}
$$

### Step 3: Construct the Efficiency Ratio

The DFA Index is defined as the ratio of minimum possible parts to the actual number of parts. Since we want this to be expressed as a percentage:

$$
D = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\,\%
$$

### Step 4: Interpret the Result

$$
D = \begin{cases} 100\% & \text{Perfect design (no excess parts)} \\ 50\% & \text{Twice as many parts as strictly necessary (typical of un-simplified products)} \\ <30\% & \text{Poorly designed; massive simplification opportunity} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 5: Worked Example for Clarity

A product requires 1 moving shaft, 2 parts needing different material (a rubber seal and a metal housing), and 1 part requiring a different process (a knurled knob). Therefore:

$$
N_{min} = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 \text{ parts}
$$

However, the original design has 9 distinct components. Then:

$$
D = \frac{4}{9} \times 100 \approx 44.4\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The DFA index is a **target metric** during simplification. A good KTU Board answer should always conclude with a recommendation: *"By separating the unnecessary 5 parts, the index can be improved to 100%."*

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost-Based Justification for Separation

A complete 14-mark KTU question often asks: *"Should the part be separated or not? Justify using cost analysis."*

### Step 1: Cost of the Monolithic Part

The monolithic (single complex) part requires expensive multi-axis machining, special tooling, and high scrap. Its cost is:

$$
C_M = C_{material} + C_{machining} + C_{tooling} + C_{inspection} + C_{scrap}
$$

### Step 2: Cost After Separation

After separation, the part is split into $m$ sub-parts and $n$ assembly operations. The new cost is:

$$
C_S = \sum_{j=1}^{m} C_{M,j}^{\,simple} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} C_{A,k}
$$

Where:
- $C_{M,j}^{\,simple}$ is the cost of each simpler sub-part (now manufactured using optimal processes)
- $C_{A,k}$ is the cost of each assembly operation (handling + insertion + fastening)

### Step 3: Net Economic Decision

$$
\Delta C = C_M - C_S
$$

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \Delta C > 0 & \text{Separation is ECONOMICALLY justified} \\ \Delta C = 0 & \text{Indifferent; decide on functional grounds} \\ \Delta C < 0 & \text{Separation is NOT justified; keep monolithic} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A complex bracket currently costs ₹480 as a single CNC-milled part. It is proposed to split it into 2 simpler sub-parts (costing ₹160 and ₹120 respectively) joined by 2 bolted connections (₹30 each). The annual production volume is 5000 units. Determine the economic feasibility of separation.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

Compute cost of monolithic design per unit:

$$
C_M = \text{₹}480
$$

Compute cost of separated design per unit:

$$
C_S = C_{M,1} + C_{M,2} + C_{A,1} + C_{A,2}
$$

$$
C_S = 160 + 120 + 30 + 30 = \text{₹}340
$$

Compute net saving per unit:

$$
\Delta C = C_M - C_S = 480 - 340 = \text{₹}140 \text{ per unit}
$$

Compute annual saving (production volume $V = 5000$):

$$
\text{Annual Saving} = \Delta C \times V = 140 \times 5000 = \text{₹}7{,}00{,}000
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since $\Delta C = \text{₹}140 > 0$, **separation is economically justified**, saving ₹7,00,000 per year at the given production volume.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tolerance Stack-Up After Separation

A critical (and often overlooked) consequence of separation is **tolerance accumulation**. Every added interface introduces a new tolerance. The two standard models are:

### Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Stack-Up

$$
T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i
$$

This is the **safe, conservative** approach used when dimensions are not statistically independent.

### Statistical (Root Sum Square) Stack-Up

$$
T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}
$$

This is the **realistic** approach when tolerances are statistically independent (typical of random manufacturing variation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The board expects students to **state both models** and explain when each is used. The worst-case is preferred for **safety-critical assemblies** (aerospace, medical), while RSS is used for **general mechanical assemblies**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart: Should We Separate?

The following Mermaid flowchart encapsulates the complete decision logic for applying simplification by separation, as taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Complex Monolithic Part] --> B{Does part move relative to others during operation?}
    B -- Yes --> C[MUST separate - moving part criterion]
    B -- No --> D{Does it require a different material?}
    D -- Yes --> E[MUST separate - material criterion]
    D -- No --> F{Does it require a different manufacturing process?}
    F -- Yes --> G[MUST separate - process criterion]
    F -- No --> H{Can it be assembled using existing components?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Reconsider: possibly merge]
    H -- No --> J{Does separation reduce total cost Delta C greater than zero?}
    J -- Yes --> K[SEPARATE - economically justified]
    J -- No --> L[Keep monolithic - no net benefit]
    C --> M[Final Design: Multiple Sub-Parts]
    E --> M
    G --> M
    K --> M
    L --> N[Final Design: Single Part]
    I --> N
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pre- and Post-Separation

The diagram below illustrates the architectural transformation when a complex part is separated into simpler sub-components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Before Separation - Monolithic Design]
        direction TB
        A1[Complex Single Part] --> A2[Function A: Structural Support]
        A1 --> A3[Function B: Bearing Surface]
        A1 --> A4[Function C: Sealing Interface]
        A1 --> A5[Function D: Electrical Contact]
    end

    subgraph P2[After Separation - Modular Design]
        direction TB
        B1[Sub-Part 1: Body] --> B2[Function A: Structural Support]
        B3[Sub-Part 2: Insert] --> B4[Function B: Bearing Surface]
        B5[Sub-Part 3: Gasket] --> B6[Function C: Sealing Interface]
        B7[Sub-Part 4: Terminal] --> B8[Function D: Electrical Contact]
    end

    P1 -- Simplification by Separation --> P2
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Applying Separation in a Design Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Functional Analysis] --> S2[Step 2: Identify All Functions of the Part]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Group Functions by Material Need]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Group Functions by Process Need]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply Boothroyd Criteria]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute DFA Index Before]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Propose Separation]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compute DFA Index After]
    S8 --> S9{Improvement Significant?}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Adopt Separated Design]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Retain Monolithic Design]
    S10 --> S12[Step 9: Validate via Cost Analysis]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13[Step 10: Final Design Documentation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

> Define **Simplification by Separation** in the context of design for manufacture. State any **two advantages** and **one limitation** of this principle.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Simplification by Separation is a design rationalization technique in which a complex, multifunctional component is divided into two or more simpler sub-components. Each sub-component performs a single, well-defined function, is manufactured using the most suitable process, and is later assembled to deliver the original end-function.

**Two Advantages:**
1. Each sub-part can be manufactured using a **different, optimal process** (e.g., forging the body, molding the seal), reducing overall manufacturing cost.
2. **Maintenance and repair** become easier — a damaged sub-part can be replaced without discarding the entire assembly.

**One Limitation:**
1. Separation introduces **additional assembly operations** (handling, insertion, fastening), each adding time, cost, and a potential failure interface (tolerance stack-up).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

> List the **three Boothroyd criteria** that determine whether a part must be separated in an assembly. Give a one-line justification for each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Movement Criterion:** *"Does the part move relative to all other parts during the operation of the product?"* — If yes, it **must** be a separate part.
2. **Material Criterion:** *"Does the part require a different material than other parts due to functional or chemical reasons?"* — If yes, it **must** be a separate part.
3. **Process Criterion:** *"Does the part require a different manufacturing process (e.g., molding vs. machining) for feasible production?"* — If yes, it **must** be a separate part.

If none of the three criteria apply, the part is a candidate for **merging** rather than separation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose marks by **defining without applying**. Always end with a numerical or graphical illustration.
> - In cost-justification questions, **do not forget to multiply by production volume** when computing annual savings.
> - Tolerance stack-up after separation is a **favourite trap question**. State both worst-case and statistical models.
> - Drawing the **decision flowchart** earns at least 2 bonus marks in any separation-related question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

> **(a)** Explain the **principle of simplification by separation** in design for manufacture. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** with a suitable industrial example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A complex housing is currently manufactured as a single CNC-milled aluminum part at ₹620 per unit. The design team proposes to separate it into **three simpler sub-parts** (a body costing ₹180, a cover costing ₹140, and an internal bushing costing ₹90), joined using **two bolts at ₹25 each and one gasket at ₹15**.
> The annual production volume is **8000 units**.
> - **(i)** Compute the cost per unit after separation. **(2 marks)**
> - **(ii)** Determine the **net cost saving per unit and per year**. **(3 marks)**
> - **(iii)** State whether **separation is economically justified**, with justification. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Principle:** Simplification by Separation is a DFM principle where a complex monolithic component is decomposed into simpler sub-components, each with a single function, manufactured by optimal processes, and later assembled. The principle is rooted in Boothroyd's three criteria (movement, material, process) and is governed by the DFA Index.

**Industrial Example:** The automotive **engine cylinder head** is separated from the engine block. The block is cast iron (high mass, vibration damping), while the head is often aluminum (lightweight, better heat dissipation). They require different materials, different processes (sand casting vs. die casting), and are bolted together for serviceability.

**Advantages:**
- Optimal process selection for each sub-part
- Easier maintenance and replacement
- Better tolerance control on smaller parts
- Reduced machining time per sub-part

**Limitations:**
- Increased assembly time and cost
- Tolerance stack-up at interfaces
- Additional fasteners and joining elements
- More parts in the Bill of Materials (BOM)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Cost per unit after separation:**

$$
C_S = C_{body} + C_{cover} + C_{bushing} + 2 \times C_{bolt} + C_{gasket}
$$

$$
C_S = 180 + 140 + 90 + 2(25) + 15
$$

$$
C_S = 180 + 140 + 90 + 50 + 15 = \text{₹}475 \text{ per unit}
$$

**(ii) Net cost saving per unit:**

$$
\Delta C = C_M - C_S = 620 - 475 = \text{₹}145 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Annual saving** for production volume $V = 8000$:

$$
\text{Annual Saving} = 145 \times 8000 = \text{₹}11{,}60{,}000
$$

**(iii) Justification:**

Since $\Delta C = \text{₹}145 > 0$ per unit, and the annual saving is ₹11,60,000, **separation is economically justified** at the given production volume. The saving significantly exceeds any tooling re-investment cost, making the separated design the recommended alternative.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

> **(a)** With the help of a **flowchart**, describe the **systematic procedure** for applying simplification by separation to a complex product. List the **Boothroyd criteria** used in the process. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The DFA study of a product reveals that the **minimum theoretical number of parts** is 5, while the **actual number of parts** in the current design is 12.
> - **(i)** Compute the **DFA Index** of the current design. **(2 marks)**
> - **(ii)** If separation reduces the actual count to 7 parts, what is the **new DFA Index**? **(2 marks)**
> - **(iii)** Discuss **two engineering consequences** of excessive part count and explain how separation improves them. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Systematic Procedure (as flowchart shown in Section 4.1):**

1. **Functional Analysis** — Decompose the product into its elementary functions.
2. **Material & Process Mapping** — Tag each function with its required material and process.
3. **Apply Boothroyd Criteria** — Check movement, material, and process requirements.
4. **DFA Index Computation** — Calculate $D = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$.
5. **Cost Validation** — Verify economic benefit via $\Delta C$ analysis.
6. **Tolerance Validation** — Check stack-up at new interfaces.
7. **Documentation** — Update the Bill of Materials and assembly drawings.

**Boothroyd Criteria:** *(See Part A Question 2 above for full answer.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Current DFA Index:**

$$
D_{current} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100 = \frac{5}{12} \times 100 \approx 41.67\,\%
$$

**(ii) DFA Index after separation:**

$$
D_{new} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{new}} \times 100 = \frac{5}{7} \times 100 \approx 71.43\,\%
$$

**(iii) Two Engineering Consequences of Excessive Part Count:**

1. **Increased Assembly Time and Cost:** More parts mean more handling ($t_h$) and insertion ($t_i$) operations. Total assembly time $T_A = \sum (t_h + t_i)$ grows linearly with part count, raising labour cost and production cycle time. Separation, when applied correctly, **eliminates redundant parts**, reducing $T_A$ and hence assembly cost.

2. **Tolerance Stack-Up and Quality Risk:** Every interface introduces a tolerance. With 12 parts, the worst-case stack-up is $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{12} T_i$, which often exceeds the design's functional tolerance window, leading to rejection and rework. Separation, by **eliminating non-functional interfaces**, reduces the stack-up to a manageable value, improving first-pass yield and reducing warranty costs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- **Definition:** Separation = splitting one complex part into multiple simpler sub-parts, each with a single function.
- **Origin:** Part of the **DFMA (Design for Manufacture and Assembly)** methodology developed by **Boothroyd and Dewhurst**.
- **Three Boothroyd Criteria:** Movement, Material, Process. If any one applies, separation is **mandatory**.
- **DFA Index Formula:** $D = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\,\%$. Target = **100%**.
- **Cost Justification Rule:** Separate **only if** $\Delta C = C_M - C_S > 0$.
- **Tolerance Stack-Up:**
  - Worst-case: $T_{total} = \sum T_i$ (use for safety-critical)
  - Statistical (RSS): $T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ (use for general assemblies)
- **Advantages:** Optimal process per sub-part, easier serviceability, better tolerance control, lower per-part machining time.
- **Limitations:** Extra assembly operations, more fasteners, tolerance accumulation, larger BOM.
- **Industrial Examples to Memorize:**
  - Engine block vs. cylinder head (automotive)
  - Composite skin + ribs + stringers (aerospace)
  - Motor + drum + control panel (washing machine)
  - PCB sub-modules (electronics)
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing *separation* with *modular design*. Separation is a **manufacturing-driven** decomposition, not a product-platforming strategy.
- **Examiner's Favourite Question Type:** *"Given the cost data and production volume, justify whether separation is recommended."* Always end with a clear **Yes/No** and a numerical proof.
- **Diagram to Draw:** The decision flowchart (Section 4.1) — earns easy 2–3 marks if reproduced in the answer sheet.
- **Linked Outcomes (CO Mapping as per KTU 2024):** This topic primarily maps to **CO3 — Apply design simplification principles to manufacturing problems**, assessed at **Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplification by Amalgamation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simplification by Amalgamation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simplification by Amalgamation** is a systematic method-improvement technique used in the *Develop* stage of Method Study (after Recording and Critical Examination) in which two or more elementary operations, activities, or procedural steps are **combined (merged) into a single, consolidated operation** to be performed at one workplace, by one operator (or one machine), in a single motion sequence — thereby reducing the total number of process elements, cycle time, handling, and the cost of manufacture.

In the standard KTU syllabus treatment, this technique is grouped alongside three sibling techniques that together form the *Develop* stage of the Method Study procedure (often remembered with the acronym **E-C-R-S**: Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify). **Amalgamation is the "Combine" tool.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Position (Module 3 — Method Study):**
> After the present method is critically examined using questioning techniques (Purpose? Place? Sequence? Person? Means?), the analyst uses **Amalgamation** to redesign the workflow by merging split-up activities that logically belong together.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are making a cup of tea. The **old method** has these steps:

1. Walk to the kitchen (15 s)
2. Boil water (90 s)
3. Walk back to the room (15 s)
4. Pour water into cup (10 s)
5. Walk to the sugar jar (10 s)
6. Add sugar (5 s)
7. Walk back (10 s)
8. Stir (5 s)

**Total = 160 s, 8 steps, 6 unnecessary "Walk" steps.**

If you **amalgamate** intelligently — bring the sugar jar and cup to the kitchen *first*, boil water, pour, add sugar, stir **all in the same place** — the new method becomes:

1. Carry cup + sugar to kitchen (15 s)
2. Boil water, pour, add sugar, stir — all in one location (110 s)

**Total = 125 s, 2 logical steps.** The "walks" have been **amalgamated** with the productive operations, eliminating wasted motion and transportation.

> This is the essence of **Simplification by Amalgamation** — *merging what was separated so it happens at the same place, at the same time, by the same agent.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used

| Metric (bolded) | Standard Unit | Purpose in Amalgamation |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Time (T<sub>b</sub>)** | **minutes (min)** or **seconds (s)** | Time for the new amalgamated operation at standard rating |
| **Standard Time (T<sub>s</sub>)** | **min** | $T_s = T_b \times (1 + Allowances)$ |
| **Number of Operations (N)** | **dimensionless integer** | Reduced from N<sub>old</sub> to N<sub>new</sub> |
| **Workplace Count (W)** | **workplaces** | Fewer workplaces after amalgamation |
| **Cost Saving (ΔC)** | **₹ / piece** | Difference between old and new method cost |

> [!NOTE]
> The governing KTU Board convention is to compute savings in **cost per piece** and **time per piece**, then annualise using the **production volume** constant $Q$ (pieces/year).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — The "Why" and "How"

The logic of simplification by amalgamation rests on **three core engineering observations**:

1. **Observation 1 — The "Place" economy principle**
   When two operations are done at different workplaces, the *transporter* (man/material) is the third unnecessary element. Combining them at one workplace **eliminates the transport** that links them.

2. **Observation 2 — The "Simultaneity" principle**
   Many manual activities that are sequenced can be made *parallel*. A worker can **hold the part with one hand while aligning with the other** — these are "amalgamated" within a single therblig group.

3. **Observation 3 — The "Information/Decision" economy**
   Two quality checks done at two stations by two inspectors can be amalgamated into one inspection point with one inspector (if skill permits) — saving the second inspection set-up and motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Heuristic:** A perfect amalgamation answer must show *both* — the **reduction in number of operations** AND the resulting **time / cost saving** with numerical evidence.

### 2.2 The Six Questioning Techniques — Where Amalgamation Fits

In critical examination, the analyst asks:

| Question | Technique Triggered |
|---|---|
| **What is done? Why?** | Elimination |
| **Where is it done? Why there?** | Rearrangement / Amalgamation |
| **When is it done? Why then?** | Rearrangement / Amalgamation |
| **Who does it? Why that person?** | Amalgamation / Simplification |
| **How is it done? Why that way?** | Simplification / Amalgamation |
| **With what means? Why?** | Simplification |

**Amalgamation is triggered** when the answer to *Where? When? Who?* reveals that splitting the job is illogical.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{s,\text{new}} = T_{b,\text{new}} \times (1 + A)$ | Standard time of amalgamated method | min |
| 2 | $T_{s,\text{old}} = T_{b,\text{old}} \times (1 + A)$ | Standard time of original method | min |
| 3 | $\Delta T = T_{s,\text{old}} - T_{s,\text{new}}$ | Time saved per piece | min |
| 4 | $\Delta C = \Delta T \times \text{Labour rate} \times \text{minutes/hour factor}$ | Cost saved per piece | ₹/piece |
| 5 | $\text{Annual Saving} = \Delta C \times Q$ | Total yearly saving | ₹/year |
| 6 | $\% \text{ Saving} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{s,\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Percentage reduction in time | % |
| 7 | $N_{\text{reduction}} = N_{\text{old}} - N_{\text{new}}$ | Number of operations eliminated by amalgamation | integer |
| 8 | $\text{Efficiency Gain} = \dfrac{T_{b,\text{old}}}{T_{b,\text{new}}}$ | Ratio of productivity | dimensionless |

> Where $A$ = allowance fraction (typically 0.10 to 0.20), and $Q$ = annual production volume in pieces.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machining Cells:** Amalgamating roughing + semi-finishing into one toolpath.
- **Assembly Lines:** Combining sub-assemblies A + B into a single station (modular amalgamation).
- **Hospital Workflows:** Merging patient registration + initial vitals check at one desk.
- **Garment Manufacturing:** Combining cut + mark operations at one table.
- **Software/Hardware Production:** Combining QC + packaging for export-grade items.

In all these domains the **engineering utility** is the same: *fewer hand-offs, fewer queues, lower WIP, faster throughput.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The Standard KTU 14-Mark Problem Template

A typical KTU 14-mark problem on "Simplification by Amalgamation" follows **exactly this 7-step analytical template**. You must reproduce all 7 steps in the exam to score full marks.

> **Step 1:** Record the present method using an *Outline Process Chart* or *Two-Hand Process Chart* or *Flow Process Chart*.
> **Step 2:** Compute the *basic time* and *standard time* of the present method.
> **Step 3:** Apply the questioning techniques → identify the operations that can be amalgamated.
> **Step 4:** Develop the *proposed (amalgamated) method* and re-draw the chart.
> **Step 5:** Compute the *basic time* and *standard time* of the proposed method.
> **Step 6:** Compute time saving $\Delta T$, cost saving $\Delta C$, and annual saving.
> **Step 7:** Comment on the feasibility / recommendation.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Derivation (Canonical KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement (Model):**
> In a small component assembly, the present method has **8 operations** performed by **3 different operators** at **3 different workplaces**. The standard time of the present method is **12.00 min/piece**. After critical examination, the analyst proposes to amalgamate the operations so that **2 operators** do the job at **2 workplaces** in **6 operations**. The proposed standard time is **8.40 min/piece**. The labour rate is **₹ 4.00 per minute** and the annual production is **50,000 pieces**. Compute (a) time saved per piece, (b) cost saved per piece, (c) total annual saving, and (d) percentage saving.

#### Step 1 — Identify Given Data (Write on the answer sheet)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{s,\text{old}} &= 12.00 \text{ min/piece} \\
T_{s,\text{new}} &= 8.40 \text{ min/piece} \\
\text{Labour rate} &= \text{₹ } 4.00 \text{ per minute} \\
Q &= 50{,}000 \text{ pieces/year} \\
N_{\text{old}} &= 8 \text{ operations at 3 workplaces} \\
N_{\text{new}} &= 6 \text{ operations at 2 workplaces}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *This block reads:* "Old standard time is 12 minutes, new is 8.4 minutes, labour costs ₹4 every minute, 50,000 items per year, eight operations compressed to six across three workplaces compressed to two."

#### Step 2 — Compute Time Saved per Piece (Part a)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{s,\text{old}} - T_{s,\text{new}} \\
        &= 12.00 - 8.40 \\
        &= 3.60 \text{ min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *This block reads:* "Subtract the new time from the old — 12 minus 8.4 equals 3.6 minutes saved on every single piece."

#### Step 3 — Compute Cost Saved per Piece (Part b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= \Delta T \times \text{Labour rate} \\
        &= 3.60 \text{ min} \times \text{₹ } 4.00/\text{min} \\
        &= \text{₹ } 14.40 \text{ per piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *This block reads:* "Multiply the saved time by the labour rate — 3.6 minutes times ₹4 per minute equals ₹14.40 saved per piece."

#### Step 4 — Compute Total Annual Saving (Part c)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Saving} &= \Delta C \times Q \\
                    &= 14.40 \times 50{,}000 \\
                    &= \text{₹ } 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *This block reads:* "Multiply per-piece saving by annual volume — 14.4 times 50,000 equals ₹7,20,000 saved every year."

#### Step 5 — Compute Percentage Saving (Part d)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Saving} &= \frac{\Delta T}{T_{s,\text{old}}} \times 100 \\
                 &= \frac{3.60}{12.00} \times 100 \\
                 &= 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *This block reads:* "Divide the saved time by the original time, then multiply by 100 — 3.6 over 12 gives 0.30, which is 30 percent."

#### Step 6 — Operations Reduced Check (Amalgamation Validation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{reduction}} &= N_{\text{old}} - N_{\text{new}} \\
                     &= 8 - 6 = 2 \text{ operations}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *This block reads:* "Two operations were successfully merged into existing ones — a clear amalgamation outcome."

#### Step 7 — Recommendation Statement (Mandatory for KTU 14-mark)

> **Recommendation:** The amalgamation of two operations reduces cycle time by **30 %**, yields an annual saving of **₹ 7,20,000**, and is therefore **recommended for implementation**, subject to operator training on the combined task and ergonomic re-layout of the workplace.

### 3.3 Sensitivity / Sanity Check

We verify the result by computing the *efficiency gain*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{gain}} &= \frac{T_{b,\text{old}}}{T_{b,\text{new}}} = \frac{12.00}{8.40} \approx 1.4286
\end{aligned}
$$

> *This block reads:* "The new method is approximately 1.43 times faster than the old — consistent with the 30 % time reduction calculated above."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Amalgamation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Record Present Method] --> B[Outline Process Chart]
    B --> C[Critical Examination]
    C --> D{Ask 5 W 1 H}
    D --> E[Identify Splittable Operations]
    E --> F[Apply Amalgamation Rules]
    F --> G[Re-draw Proposed Chart]
    G --> H[Compute New Standard Time]
    H --> I[Compute Savings]
    I --> J[Compare and Recommend]
    J --> K[Implement New Method]
    K --> L[Maintain Improved Method]
```

> *This diagram reads:* "Record the current method, draw its outline process chart, critically examine it, ask the five-W one-H questions, identify which operations are split, apply amalgamation rules, redraw the new chart, compute the new time, calculate savings, compare and recommend, implement, and maintain."

### 4.2 Before vs After Amalgamation — Process Element Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OLD[Present Method - 8 Ops]
        O1[Op 1 Workplace A]:::oldnode
        O2[Transport]:::oldnode
        O3[Op 2 Workplace B]:::oldnode
        O4[Transport]:::oldnode
        O5[Op 3 Workplace C]:::oldnode
        O6[Transport]:::oldnode
        O7[Op 4 Workplace C]:::oldnode
        O8[Inspect]:::oldnode
        O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> O4 --> O5 --> O6 --> O7 --> O8
    end

    subgraph NEW[Amalgamated Method - 6 Ops]
        N1[Op 1 plus 2 Workplace A]:::newnode
        N2[Op 3 plus 4 Workplace B]:::newnode
        N3[Inspect combined]:::newnode
        N1 --> N2 --> N3
    end

    OLD ==> NEW

    classDef oldnode fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef newnode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
```

> *This diagram reads:* "The old method has eight process elements with three transport moves; the new amalgamated method has six elements with zero transport because ops 1+2 are merged at workplace A and ops 3+4 are merged at workplace B."

### 4.3 Decision Logic — When to Amalgamate vs Not

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the operator skilled in both tasks?} -->|Yes| Q2{Distance between workplaces > 3 m?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A1[Strong Candidate for Amalgamation]:::good
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Does it reduce cycle time > 15 percent?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A1
    Q3 -->|No| A2[Do NOT Amalgamate]:::bad
    Q1 -->|No| Q4{Can training be done in 1 week?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| A1
    Q4 -->|No| A2

    classDef good fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
```

> *This diagram reads:* "If the operator is skilled and the workplace is far — amalgamate. If the time gain is below 15 percent — do not amalgamate. If training takes more than a week — do not amalgamate."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Define the term "Simplification by Amalgamation" as applied in the Develop stage of Method Study. State any two situations where this technique is most beneficial.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Simplification by Amalgamation is a method-improvement technique in which **two or more operations that are presently performed separately are combined into a single operation** carried out at one workplace, by one operator, in one continuous motion sequence, so as to reduce the number of process elements, eliminate transport, and shorten the standard time.

**Two situations where it is most beneficial:**
1. When the same component is handled repeatedly between two nearby workplaces — combining the operations removes the inter-workplace transport.
2. When two simple manual checks are done by two inspectors at two stations — they can be merged into one inspection point with one multi-skilled inspector.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write "elimination" or "rearrangement" instead of "combination" — the KTU marker deducts 1 mark for confusing the four sibling techniques (Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*List the seven steps of Method Study and identify the step in which Simplification by Amalgamation is performed.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The seven steps of Method Study are:
1. Select the work to be studied
2. Record the present method
3. Examine the recorded facts (Critical Examination)
4. **Develop the improved method** ← *Amalgamation is performed here*
5. Define the new method
6. Install the new method
7. Maintain the new method

Amalgamation is applied in **Step 4 — Develop**, where the analyst synthesises a better workflow from the critical-examination findings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

In a sheet-metal fabrication shop, a rectangular cover plate is produced. The present method has **10 process steps** across **3 workplaces** with a **standard time of 18.00 min/piece**. After critical examination, the analyst proposes an amalgamated method with **7 process steps** across **2 workplaces** and an estimated **standard time of 13.50 min/piece**. The labour rate is **₹ 3.50 per minute**, and the annual production is **40,000 plates**.

**(a)** Compute the time saved per piece, cost saved per piece, and the total annual saving. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the percentage time reduction and the operations eliminated. Comment on the recommendation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Given data block (write first, earns 1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{s,\text{old}} &= 18.00 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,\text{new}} &= 13.50 \text{ min} \\
R &= \text{₹ } 3.50 \text{ per min} \\
Q &= 40{,}000 \text{ pieces/year} \\
N_{\text{old}} &= 10,\quad W_{\text{old}} = 3 \\
N_{\text{new}} &= 7,\quad W_{\text{new}} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Time saved per piece**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{s,\text{old}} - T_{s,\text{new}} \\
        &= 18.00 - 13.50 = 4.50 \text{ min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cost saved per piece**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= \Delta T \times R \\
        &= 4.50 \times 3.50 = \text{₹ } 15.75 \text{ per piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total annual saving**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= \Delta C \times Q \\
                  &= 15.75 \times 40{,}000 = \text{₹ } 6{,}30{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 4: Percentage time reduction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Saving} &= \frac{\Delta T}{T_{s,\text{old}}} \times 100 \\
                 &= \frac{4.50}{18.00} \times 100 = 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Operations eliminated**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{reduction}} &= N_{\text{old}} - N_{\text{new}} = 10 - 7 = 3 \text{ operations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Workplace reduction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{reduction}} &= 3 - 2 = 1 \text{ workplace eliminated}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Recommendation**
With a **25 % time reduction**, **3 operations amalgamated**, **1 workplace eliminated**, and an annual saving of **₹ 6,30,000**, the proposed method is **highly recommended for implementation**, subject to (i) operator multi-skilling and (ii) ergonomic re-layout of the remaining two workplaces.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Question A:** Students frequently forget to multiply the cost saving by the annual production $Q$. Marks are split — **3 marks for per-piece values, 4 marks for annualised saving**. Forgetting the multiplication loses 4 marks.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

A small assembly line produces a mechanical sub-assembly. The **present method** has **6 operations** performed by **4 operators** at **4 workplaces**, with a **standard time of 9.60 min/piece**. The **proposed amalgamated method** combines the work into **4 operations** performed by **2 operators** at **2 workplaces**, with a **standard time of 6.72 min/piece**. The labour rate is **₹ 5.00 per minute** and the company manufactures **60,000 sub-assemblies per year**.

**(a)** Compute the per-piece time saving, per-piece cost saving, and total annual monetary saving. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the percentage reduction in time, the number of operations reduced, and the number of workplaces saved. Justify whether the amalgamation should be adopted. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Given data block (write first, earns 1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{s,\text{old}} &= 9.60 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,\text{new}} &= 6.72 \text{ min} \\
R &= \text{₹ } 5.00 \text{ per min} \\
Q &= 60{,}000 \text{ pieces/year} \\
N_{\text{old}} &= 6,\quad W_{\text{old}} = 4,\quad Op_{\text{old}} = 4 \\
N_{\text{new}} &= 4,\quad W_{\text{new}} = 2,\quad Op_{\text{new}} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Time saved per piece**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{s,\text{old}} - T_{s,\text{new}} \\
        &= 9.60 - 6.72 = 2.88 \text{ min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cost saved per piece**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= \Delta T \times R \\
        &= 2.88 \times 5.00 = \text{₹ } 14.40 \text{ per piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total annual saving**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= \Delta C \times Q \\
                  &= 14.40 \times 60{,}000 = \text{₹ } 8{,}64{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 4: Percentage reduction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Saving} &= \frac{2.88}{9.60} \times 100 = 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Operations reduced**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{reduction}} &= 6 - 4 = 2 \text{ operations amalgamated}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Workplaces saved**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{reduction}} &= 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ workplaces saved}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Operator reduction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Op_{\text{reduction}} &= 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ operators redeployable}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Justification**
A **30 % time reduction** combined with a **₹ 8,64,000 annual saving**, **2 fewer workplaces**, and **2 redeployable operators** makes the amalgamation **strongly justified**. The break-even point is reached within the first month of operation. Recommendation: **Adopt immediately**, with a parallel multi-skilling training plan for the retained operators.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Question B:** A common student error is forgetting to convert the per-piece saving to **annual saving** by multiplying with $Q$. The 14-mark problem explicitly tests whether you remember the production-volume multiplier. **Losing this multiplication = losing 4 of 14 marks.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU revision):**

- **Definition:** Amalgamation = *combining* two or more operations into one at one workplace, by one operator.
- **Position in Method Study:** Belongs to **Step 4 — Develop** (not Record, not Examine).
- **Sibling Techniques (E-C-R-S):** **E**liminate, **C**ombine (Amalgamation), **R**earrange, **S**implify.
- **Trigger Questions:** *Where? When? Who?* — if the answer reveals a logical split, amalgamate.
- **Time Saved:** $\Delta T = T_{s,\text{old}} - T_{s,\text{new}}$.
- **Cost Saved per Piece:** $\Delta C = \Delta T \times R$ (where $R$ = ₹/min).
- **Annual Saving:** $S = \Delta C \times Q$ (where $Q$ = pieces/year).
- **Percentage Saving:** $\%\text{Saving} = (\Delta T / T_{s,\text{old}}) \times 100$.
- **Always validate** amalgamation by showing (i) operations reduced, (ii) workplaces reduced, (iii) operators reduced.
- **Killer pitfalls to avoid:**
  1. Confusing Amalgamation with Elimination (they are different).
  2. Forgetting to multiply by $Q$ for annual saving.
  3. Skipping the recommendation/justification statement (mandatory for 14-mark answers).
  4. Not drawing the before/after process chart when the question asks for "develop the new method."
- **Real-world examples to memorise:** Tea-making analogy, sheet-metal cut+mark, garment cut+bundle, hospital registration+vitals, assembly sub-A + sub-B.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Mantra for this topic:**
> *"Record → Examine → Amalgamate → Compute ΔT → Compute ΔC → Multiply by Q → Recommend."*
> Write these seven verbs at the top of your answer sheet, and your 14-mark answer is guaranteed to score above 12.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Machinability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design for Machinability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design for Machinability (DFM-Mach)** is a systematic, rule-based design philosophy integrated within the broader *Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)* framework that governs the geometric, dimensional, and tolerance specifications of a component to **minimize manufacturing cost, machining time, tool wear, and energy consumption** during subtractive (material removal) operations such as turning, drilling, milling, grinding, and broaching.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Design for Machinability encompasses the following governing principles:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Design for Machinability is the practice of designing parts so that they can be produced efficiently and economically by available machining processes, while simultaneously ensuring adequate tool life, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and operator safety.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wood Carver's Rule"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Sculpting a Statue from Marble:**
> Imagine a sculptor asked to carve a statue from a block of marble. If the design demands *deep undercuts*, *sharp internal corners*, and *complex cavities*, the sculptor will:
> - Use **more chisels** (more tool changes)
> - Take **longer hours** (higher cycle time)
> - Risk **chipping the marble** (poor surface finish, scrap)
> - Need **special curved chisels** (custom tooling = higher cost)
>
> Now, if the sculptor had *redesigned* the statue with **flat surfaces, generous radii, and accessible faces**, the same job would be done *five times faster* with *standard tools*. This is exactly what **Design for Machinability** does for an engineer designing a metal component.

**The Machinability of a material is quantitatively expressed as the Machinability Rating (MR):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:**
> **AISI/SAE B1112** (free-machining resulfurized steel) is assigned a baseline **Machinability Rating = 100%**. All other workpiece materials are rated relative to this benchmark based on a standardized cutting test (typically measured by tool life at a fixed cutting speed, or by cutting speed required to obtain a standard **20-minute tool life**).

### 1.3 Scope of Design for Machinability (DFM-Mach)

The scope covers four intersecting engineering domains:

| Domain | Design Concern |
|---|---|
| **Workpiece Material** | Selection of free-machining grades; hardness range HB 160–220 for steels |
| **Part Geometry** | Corner radii, pocket depth, wall thickness, hole depths, standard sizes |
| **Tolerances & Surface Finish** | Specifying the *loosest* tolerance consistent with function |
| **Process Selection** | Choosing the most economical machining process for the geometry |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in Machining

The following metrics are evaluated when judging a design's "machinability":

- **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — measured in **mm³/s** or **cm³/min**
- **Tool Life (T)** — measured in **minutes** of actual cutting
- **Cutting Force (Fc)** — measured in **Newtons (N)**
- **Specific Cutting Energy (U)** — measured in **N·mm/mm³ = J/mm³**
- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** — measured in **micrometres (µm)**
- **Machinability Rating (%)** — dimensionless ratio
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taylor's Tool Life Equation — The Heart of Machinability

**Frederick Winslow Taylor (1907)** established the foundational empirical relationship between **cutting speed (V)** and **tool life (T)**. This equation is the single most important formula in the entire DFM-Mach syllabus.

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

Where:
- **V** = Cutting Speed (m/min)
- **T** = Tool Life (minutes)
- **n** = Taylor's Exponent (tool-life-slope constant, dimensionless)
- **C** = Taylor's Constant (depends on tool-workpiece combination, feed, depth of cut)

### 2.2 Linearized Form (Log-Log Plot)

Taking the logarithm of both sides:

$$\log V + n \cdot \log T = \log C$$

This is a **straight line** when plotted on log-log axes, with:
- **y-intercept** = log C
- **slope** = -n

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Values of 'n' (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **n = 0.25** for cemented carbide tools on steel (most common)
> - **n = 0.125** for HSS tools on steel
> - **n = 0.30 to 0.50** for ceramic tools
> - **n = 0.083 to 0.125** for coated carbides

### 2.3 Expanded Form — Effect of Feed and Depth of Cut

A more complete form incorporating feed (f) and depth of cut (d):

$$V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = K$$

The exponents **a** and **b** are typically:
- **a ≈ 0.5 to 0.7** (feed has a strong effect on tool life)
- **b ≈ 0.1 to 0.2** (depth of cut has weak effect on tool life)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A designer can **increase depth of cut almost freely** without much tool-life penalty, but **doubling feed rate reduces tool life by ~40%** for a given cutting speed. Therefore, designs that allow larger depths and smaller feeds are preferred.

---

## 2.4 Material Removal Rate (MRR) — The Throughput Metric

For **Turning Operation:**

$$\text{MRR} = f \times d \times V \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

For **Drilling Operation:**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \times f_r \times N \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

For **Milling Operation:**

$$\text{MRR} = f \times d \times v \times w \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

Where:
- **f** = feed (mm/rev)
- **d** = depth of cut (mm)
- **V** = cutting speed (m/min)
- **D** = drill diameter (mm)
- **f_r** = feed per revolution (mm/rev)
- **N** = spindle speed (rev/min)
- **w** = width of cut (mm)

---

## 2.5 Cutting Force, Power, and Specific Cutting Force

### Cutting Force (Fc):

$$F_c = K_s \times f \times d \quad [\text{N}]$$

Where **K_s** = **Specific Cutting Force** (N/mm²), a material-dependent constant. Typical values:
- **Low Carbon Steel: K_s ≈ 1500–2000 N/mm²**
- **Aluminum Alloys: K_s ≈ 600–900 N/mm²**
- **Stainless Steel: K_s ≈ 2400–2800 N/mm²**
- **Cast Iron: K_s ≈ 1000–1400 N/mm²**

### Cutting Power (P):

$$P = \frac{F_c \times V}{60{,}000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

Substituting F_c:

$$P = \frac{K_s \times f \times d \times V}{60{,}000 \times 1000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

### Specific Cutting Energy (U):

$$U = \frac{P}{\text{MRR}} = \frac{K_s \times f \times d \times V / (60{,}000)}{f \times d \times V} = \frac{K_s}{1000} \quad [\text{N·m/mm}^3]$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Taylor's Tool Life | $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ | V: m/min, T: min | All machining |
| 2 | Linearized Taylor | $\log V + n \log T = \log C$ | log units | Graphical analysis |
| 3 | Expanded Taylor | $V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = K$ | m/min | Multi-variable analysis |
| 4 | MRR — Turning | $\text{MRR} = f \cdot d \cdot V$ | mm³/min | Production rate |
| 5 | MRR — Drilling | $\text{MRR} = (\pi D^2 / 4) \cdot f_r \cdot N$ | mm³/min | Hole production |
| 6 | MRR — Milling | $\text{MRR} = f \cdot d \cdot v \cdot w$ | mm³/min | Slot production |
| 7 | Cutting Force | $F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$ | N | Force calculations |
| 8 | Cutting Power | $P = (K_s \cdot f \cdot d \cdot V)/60{,}000{,}000$ | kW | Motor sizing |
| 9 | Spindle Speed | $N = (1000 \cdot V) / (\pi \cdot D)$ | rev/min | Turning/Drilling |
| 10 | Feed Rate | $f_m = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | Turning |
| 11 | Machinability Rating | $MR = (V_{mat} / V_{B1112}) \times 100$ | % | Material rating |
| 12 | Cost per Piece | $C_{pc} = C_{tool}/N_{pieces} + (C_m \cdot T_m)$ | ₹/pc | Economic analysis |

---

## 2.7 Factors Affecting Machinability (KTU Frequently Asked)

The designer has direct or indirect control over these factors:

### A. Workpiece Material Properties
- **Hardness:** Optimum range **HB 160–220** for steels
- **Microstructure:** Ferrite + Pearlite (free-machining) is best
- **Chemical Composition:** Presence of **S, Pb, Mn, P** improves machinability
- **Inclusion Shape:** Sulfides act as **internal stress raisers** → chip breaks easily

### B. Tool Material
- **HSS** → low speed, low cost
- **Carbide (WC-Co)** → medium-high speed, general purpose
- **Coated Carbide (TiN, Al₂O₃, TiAlN)** → high speed, long life
- **Ceramics & CBN** → high speed on hard materials

### C. Cutting Parameters
- **Speed:** Most influential (exponent n in Taylor)
- **Feed:** Second most influential
- **Depth of Cut:** Least influential on tool life

### D. Cutting Fluids
- Reduce friction → reduce heat → increase tool life by 2–5×

### E. Rigidity of System
- **Machine tool, fixture, tool holder, and workpiece** rigidity
- Chatter and vibration are major machinability reducers

### F. Design Geometry
- This is the **designer's primary lever** — covered in next section.

---

## 2.8 Design Guidelines for Improved Machinability (DFM-Mach Rules)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 10 Golden DFM-Mach Rules (KTU High-Yield):**

| Rule # | Design Guideline | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Avoid deep pockets** (> 4× width) | Tool deflection, chatter |
| 2 | **Use standard hole diameters** | Stock drills available |
| 3 | **Use standard thread sizes** | Standard taps/dies available |
| 4 | **Provide generous corner radii** | Standard end-mills available |
| 5 | **Avoid sharp internal corners** | Tool cannot reach; radii mandatory |
| 6 | **Use flat surfaces instead of curved** | Reduces complex 5-axis toolpaths |
| 7 | **Use uniform wall thicknesses** | Reduces distortion, uniform cutting |
| 8 | **Hole depth ≤ 4× diameter** | Standard drills; deeper needs gun drills |
| 9 | **Avoid surfaces inside enclosed spaces** | Tool accessibility |
| 10 | **Specify loosest tolerance consistent with function** | Reduces grinding/finishing cost |

### Effect of Machinability on Cost — Empirical Rule (NTMA, USA):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tightening tolerance from ±0.5 mm to ±0.05 mm (10× tighter) increases machining cost by approximately 4×.**
> A designer should **never specify tighter tolerance than the functional requirement demands.**

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **CNC Programming:** Design for Standard Cycles (G81 drilling, G83 peck drilling)
2. **Cost Estimation:** MRR × Specific Energy → Power → Cost per hour
3. **Tool Selection:** Tool catalogs use Taylor's equation for life vs. speed curves
4. **Fixture Design:** Designer must ensure clamping doesn't obstruct tool path
5. **DFMA Software:** Modern DFMA tools (e.g., Boothroyd, DFMPro) flag DFM-Mach violations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Taylor's Tool Life to Operating Cutting Speed

**Problem Setup:**
A high-speed steel tool has the following Taylor equation parameters on AISI 1040 steel:
- n = 0.125
- C = 250 m/min (for f = 0.25 mm/rev, d = 2.5 mm)
- Required tool life: **T = 60 minutes**

**Step 1 — Start with the Taylor Equation**

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values of n, T, and C**

$$V \cdot (60)^{0.125} = 250$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential term**

$$(60)^{0.125} = e^{0.125 \cdot \ln(60)} = e^{0.125 \cdot 4.0943} = e^{0.5118} = 1.6683$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the evaluated value back**

$$V \cdot 1.6683 = 250$$

**Step 5 — Solve for V**

$$V = \frac{250}{1.6683} = 149.85 \approx 150 \text{ m/min}$$

**Conclusion:** For a 60-minute tool life, the designer should set the **cutting speed V ≈ 150 m/min**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Doubling Cutting Speed on Tool Life

**Problem:** Using the same Taylor equation, find tool life when V is doubled from 150 to 300 m/min.

**Step 1 — Start with original tool life**

$$V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = C \Rightarrow 150 \cdot (60)^{0.125} = 250$$

**Step 2 — Express C in terms of V₁ and T₁**

$$C = V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = 150 \cdot (60)^{0.125}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to new speed V₂ = 300 m/min**

$$V_2 \cdot T_2^{n} = C = 150 \cdot (60)^{0.125}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for T₂**

$$T_2^{n} = \frac{150 \cdot (60)^{0.125}}{300} = 0.5 \cdot (60)^{0.125}$$

**Step 5 — Take both sides to power 1/n**

$$T_2 = \left[0.5 \cdot (60)^{0.125}\right]^{1/0.125} = \left[0.5 \cdot 1.6683\right]^{8}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the bracket**

$$0.5 \cdot 1.6683 = 0.8342$$

**Step 7 — Raise to 8th power**

$$T_2 = (0.8342)^{8} = 0.194 \text{ minutes} \approx 11.6 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conclusion (Killer Insight):** *Doubling cutting speed reduces tool life from 60 min to about 0.2 min — a **300× reduction**. This is the reason why machinability ratings are so sensitive to cutting speed.*

$$\boxed{\text{Speed-up factor: } \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{1/n} = (0.5)^{8} = \frac{1}{256}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: MRR for a Multi-Pass Turning Operation

**Problem:** A shaft of diameter 80 mm is turned down to 75 mm in 2 passes.
- Cutting speed V = 120 m/min
- Feed f = 0.3 mm/rev
- Depth per pass = (80 − 75)/2 = 2.5 mm

**Step 1 — Calculate MRR for one pass**

$$\text{MRR} = f \times d \times V = 0.3 \times 2.5 \times 120 = 90 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mm³/min**

$$\text{MRR} = 90 \times 60 = 5400 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate volume of material removed**

$$V_{removed} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D^2 - d^2) \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (80^2 - 75^2) \times L$$

For L = 200 mm:

$$V_{removed} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (6400 - 5625) \times 200 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 775 \times 200$$

$$V_{removed} = 0.7854 \times 155{,}000 = 121{,}737 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Calculate time for one pass**

$$t_{pass} = \frac{V_{removed}}{2 \times \text{MRR}} = \frac{121{,}737}{2 \times 5400} = 11.27 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate cutting power**

$$P = \frac{F_c \times V}{60{,}000} = \frac{K_s \times f \times d \times V}{60{,}000{,}000}$$

For AISI 1040, K_s ≈ 1800 N/mm²:

$$P = \frac{1800 \times 0.3 \times 2.5 \times 120}{60{,}000{,}000} = 0.0027 \text{ kW} \times 1000 = 2.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Spindle Speed**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 120}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{120{,}000}{251.33} = 477.5 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate Feed Rate**

$$f_m = f \times N = 0.3 \times 477.5 = 143.25 \text{ mm/min}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Machinability of Common Engineering Materials

| Material | Hardness (HB) | Cutting Speed (m/min) HSS | Carbide V (m/min) | K_s (N/mm²) | Machinability Rating (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AISI B1112 (free-machining steel) | 130 | 75 | 200 | 1500 | **100** |
| AISI 1018 low-carbon steel | 130 | 50 | 150 | 1700 | 70 |
| AISI 1040 medium-carbon steel | 200 | 35 | 110 | 1800 | 50 |
| AISI 4140 alloy steel (annealed) | 220 | 25 | 85 | 2400 | 35 |
| AISI 304 stainless steel | 180 | 20 | 70 | 2500 | 25 |
| Gray Cast Iron (class 30) | 200 | 30 | 90 | 1100 | 45 |
| Aluminum 6061-T6 | 95 | 120 | 300 | 750 | 300 |
| Brass C36000 | 80 | 90 | 250 | 850 | 100 |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | 330 | 12 | 45 | 2200 | 20 |
| Inconel 718 | 380 | 8 | 30 | 3500 | 12 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the Matrix:**
> - **Aluminum** has MR = 300% (3× easier to machine than B1112)
> - **Inconel** has MR = 12% (8× harder to machine than B1112) → very expensive
> - **AISI B1112** is the **reference benchmark** for all machinability ratings

---

## 3.5 Cost Optimization Using DFM-Mach

**Problem:**
A designer has two alternative geometries for a mounting bracket:
- **Design A:** Tight tolerance ±0.05 mm, surface finish Ra 0.8 µm, requires grinding
- **Design B:** Looser tolerance ±0.25 mm, surface finish Ra 3.2 µm, achievable by turning only

Assume:
- Machining rate = ₹500/hour
- Grinding rate = ₹800/hour
- Grinding adds 2 minutes per part

**Step 1 — Time for Design A**
$$T_A = T_{turning} + T_{grinding} = 5 \text{ min} + 2 \text{ min} = 7 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Cost for Design A**
$$C_A = (7/60) \times 500 + (2/60) \times 800 = 58.33 + 26.67 = 85 \text{ ₹/part}$$

**Step 3 — Time for Design B**
$$T_B = 5 \text{ min (turning only, no grinding)}$$

**Step 4 — Cost for Design B**
$$C_B = (5/60) \times 500 = 41.67 \text{ ₹/part}$$

**Step 5 — Savings per part**
$$\Delta C = 85 - 41.67 = 43.33 \text{ ₹/part}$$

**Step 6 — Annual savings for 10,000 parts**
$$\text{Annual Savings} = 43.33 \times 10{,}000 = 4{,}33{,}300 \text{ ₹}$$

**Conclusion:** The looser tolerance of Design B saves over ₹4.3 lakhs per year on this single part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The DFM-Mach principle in action:** *Specifying the loosest tolerance consistent with function is a direct cost-saving decision made at the design stage — long before manufacturing begins.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
