  4. **Perceptible Information**
  5. **Tolerance for Error**
  6. **Low Physical Effort**
  7. **Size and Space for Approach**
- **Key Quantitative Metrics:**
  - Addressable User Population: $AUP = N_{accessible}/N_{total}$
  - Accessibility Index: $AI = \sum w_i \cdot (C_i^{ach}/C_i^{req})$; benchmark $AI \geq 0.85$
  - Color Contrast Ratio: $CR = (L_{lighter} + 0.05)/(L_{darker} + 0.05)$; benchmark $CR \geq 4.5:1$
  - Button Force: $F_{btn} \leq 2.5$ N
  - Target Size: $\geq 9$ mm / 44×44 px
  - Tactile Dot Height: $\geq 0.8$ mm
- **Key Standards to Know:** ADA (1990), WCAG 2.1/2.2, EN 301 549, IS 17802, ISO 9241-171, Section 508.
- **Curb-Cut Effect:** Features for disabled users benefit *everyone* — use this as a commercial argument.
- **Front-Loaded Cost Principle:** Implementing DfA at the concept stage costs ~5%; retrofitting post-launch costs 10× or more.
- **Multi-Modal Design Strategy:** Always provide **redundant sensory encoding** (visual + tactile + auditory) and **multiple input modalities** (touch + voice + gesture + switch).
- **Distinguish:** Usability ≠ Accessibility. Usability targets the average user; Accessibility extends to *all* users.
- **User Capability Spectrum:** Design for the 5th to 95th percentile of human variability, not the "average."
- **Avoid in KTU Answers:** Vague solutions without numerical specifications. Always quantify (force in N, size in mm, contrast as ratio).
- **Three Pillars of DfA Reasoning:** (1) Ethical obligation, (2) Legal compliance (ADA/EN), (3) Market expansion (15% of global population).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip (KTU Valuation):** A 14-mark DfA answer that includes **one worked numerical example** (e.g., computing AUP or AI) almost always scores **2–3 marks higher** than a purely descriptive answer. KTU examiners reward quantitative reasoning in DfX topics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design for Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design for Assembly (DFA)** is a systematic engineering methodology integrated within the broader **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)** framework that seeks to **minimize the cost, time, and complexity of assembling a product** by optimizing its geometric configuration, part count, joining methods, and handling characteristics **during the conceptual and embodiment design stages** — i.e., *before any tooling is committed*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Essence):** DFA is the *proactive* (not reactive) rationalization of product architecture such that the **Total Minimum Number of Parts (Nm)** is approached, while each retained part satisfies the *Boothroyd–Dewhurst criteria of necessity*: it moves relative to all other parts, it requires a different material, or it must be separated for assembly/disassembly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Brick" Intuition

Imagine you are given the task of building a small **table** for a kindergarten class. A naive designer might use **47 separate parts** — each leg built from 8 pieces, the top from 15 pieces, screws everywhere, glue joints, etc. The factory floor worker struggles for 90 minutes per table.

A **DFA-aware designer** instead says: *"Why not 4 pre-formed legs, snapped into a single molded tabletop?"* Now the table is **5 parts** and assembles in **90 seconds** with one motion.

> **The lesson:** Assembly cost is not about *how fast* the worker is — it is about *how much the product forces the worker to do*. DFA removes wasted motion, wasted fasteners, and wasted parts at the **drawing board** itself, where the cost of change is *zero*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Industry Rule (KTU Board favourite):** Assembly typically accounts for **40–60 %** of total manufacturing cost, and approximately **70 %** of quality defects originate at assembly. Hence, designing for assembly has the **highest leverage** of any design activity.

---

## 1.3 Scope of DFA — What it Encompasses

DFA addresses the following **six engineering dimensions**, all of which are recurring KTU ESE question areas:

| # | DFA Dimension | Core Question Addressed |
|---|---------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Part-Count Minimization** | *Can two parts be combined without loss of function?* |
| 2 | **Handling Simplification** | *Can the part be picked, oriented, and inserted without jigs?* |
| 3 | **Insertion / Fastening Optimization** | *Can the joining be done in one motion, top-down?* |
| 4 | **Symmetry & Self-Alignment** | *Does the part only fit the correct way?* |
| 5 | **Standardization of Features** | *Are screw sizes, hole diameters, and tools unified?* |
| 6 | **Accessibility for Tools** | *Can a screwdriver or robot gripper reach without obstruction?* |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **bold** values are the KTU-standard reference numbers that examiners expect in numerical problems:
> - **Standard assembly time per fastener (manual): 3 to 6 seconds**
> - **Robot pick-and-place cycle (baseline): 1.5 seconds**
> - **Hand-insertion baseline time: 1.95 seconds** (Boothroyd baseline)
> - **Assembly cost contribution target: < 15 % of total product cost** (Lean benchmark)
> - **Design Efficiency (η) target for world-class products: ≥ 70 %**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA Methodology — The "Why & How"

The Boothroyd method (the most frequently asked DFA framework in KTU ESE) proceeds in **three logical phases**:

### Phase A — Part Function Analysis ("Is this part needed?")
Each part is interrogated using the **Three-Question Test**:

1. *During operation of the product, does the part **move** relative to all other parts already assembled?*
2. *Must the part be made of a **different material** than the parts it mates with?*
3. *Must the part be **separated** from the mating parts to allow disassembly for service or recycling?*

> **If "NO" to all three → the part is a candidate for elimination** (combine it with the mating part, or eliminate it entirely).

### Phase B — Handling Analysis ("Can the part be picked up?")
Each retained part is assigned a **Handling Code (H)**:
- **H = 0** → Symmetric, easy to handle, no reorientation needed.
- **H = 1** → Requires flipping/rotating to find correct orientation.
- **H = 2** → Requires special care — small, fragile, or tangle-prone (e.g., springs, washers, thin sheets).
- **H = 3** → Requires fixturing, magazine, or bowl feeder (very difficult).

### Phase C — Insertion Analysis ("How easily does it mate?")
Each joining operation is assigned an **Insertion Code (I)**:
- **I = 0** → Self-locating, no fasteners, snap-fit.
- **I = 1** → One fastener (single screw, single press-fit).
- **I = 2** → Two fasteners OR a reorientation needed during insertion.
- **I ≥ 3** → Multiple fasteners, alignment fixtures, or unusual operations required.

---

## 2.2 The Design Efficiency Equation — KTU's Most Tested DFA Formula

The **Design Efficiency (η)** is the single most important formula in this module:

$$\eta = \frac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} \times 100\,\%$$

where **Nm** = *theoretical minimum number of parts* (after applying the Three-Question Test), and **Na** = *actual number of parts in the current design*.

> **Interpretation:**
> - η = 100 % → perfect design (theoretical limit, almost never achieved).
> - η = 70 % → world-class design.
> - η = 30 % → typical un-optimized design.
> - η = 10 % → severely over-partitioned.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|------------------|---------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Design Efficiency** | $\eta = \frac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} \times 100$ | How close to the theoretical minimum parts | 7-mark derivations |
| 2 | **Assembly Time Estimate** | $T_{A} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{a}} t_{i}$ | Sum of individual insertion/handling times | 7-mark problems |
| 3 | **Boothroyd Baseline Insertion Time** | $t_{b} = 1.95\,\text{s}$ (H=0) $+ 1.30\,\text{s}$ (H=1) $+ 1.65\,\text{s}$ (H=2) etc. | Empirical per-part cycle time | Numerical ESE |
| 4 | **Total Assembly Cost** | $C_{A} = T_{A} \times R_{L}$ | $R_L$ = labour rate (₹/s) | 14-mark design problems |
| 5 | **DFA Index (Hit Rate)** | $D_{index} = \dfrac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} + \dfrac{t_{m}}{t_{a}}$ | Combined part-count & time efficiency | Comparative studies |
| 6 | **Symmetry Ratio** | $S_{r} = \dfrac{\text{Number of symmetric parts}}{N_{a}}$ | Fraction that needs no orientation check | 3-mark theory |
| 7 | **Reorientation Penalty** | $t_{r} = k \times \theta$ | Time to rotate to correct orientation | Concept questions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** When using Boothroyd's $t_b$ tables, always **show both the code (H or I) and the time** — the examiner awards partial marks for the correct code even if the arithmetic slips.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DFA is not academic — it is a **profit-engine** across every manufacturing sector:

- **Automotive** → A modern car has ≈ 8,000 fasteners; DFA programs at Toyota and BMW have cut this by ≈ 30 %, saving **billions of dollars** annually.
- **Consumer Electronics** → Apple's iPhone assembly line uses DFA to achieve ≈ 80 % design efficiency (theoretical minimum vs. actual part count).
- **Aerospace** → Boeing 787 uses DFA to reduce fastener count by 50 % vs. 777, saving ≈ 100 kg per aircraft.
- **Medical Devices** → DFA enables the single-use disposable designs of syringes and IV sets.

> **The economic message for KTU:** Every **one second** saved per assembly, multiplied by **1 million units per year**, equals **11.5 days of labour saved annually** — directly from the drawing board.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Design Efficiency Index

The Design Efficiency is not arbitrary — it is a **logical consequence** of the principle that *assembly cost is proportional to the number of parts* and *assembly time is dominated by insertion/handling operations*.

**Step 1 — Identify the cost driver.**
The total assembly cost $C_A$ is the product of total assembly time $T_A$ and labour rate $R_L$:

$$C_{A} = T_{A} \times R_{L}$$

**Step 2 — Express $T_A$ as a function of the part count.**
If we assume the *average* time per part is $t_{avg}$, then:

$$T_{A} = N_{a} \times t_{avg}$$

Substituting:

$$C_{A} = N_{a} \times t_{avg} \times R_{L}$$

**Step 3 — Define the theoretical minimum cost.**
The smallest possible part count is $N_m$ (after applying the Three-Question Test). The *minimum achievable* cost is therefore:

$$C_{A,\,\min} = N_{m} \times t_{avg} \times R_{L}$$

**Step 4 — Form the dimensionless efficiency ratio.**
Dividing minimum by actual:

$$\eta = \frac{C_{A,\,\min}}{C_{A,\,\text{actual}}} = \frac{N_{m} \times t_{avg} \times R_{L}}{N_{a} \times t_{avg} \times R_{L}}$$

The $t_{avg}$ and $R_L$ cancel (they are the same in both scenarios):

$$\boxed{\;\eta = \frac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} \times 100\,\%\;}$$

> **Physical insight:** Efficiency is *dimensionless* because it is a ratio of two costs in the same units. It is independent of the labour rate and the part-time — it measures **design purity**, not shop-floor speed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Boothroyd Assembly-Time Estimate

The empirical Boothroyd time formula is built from **two independent contributions**: handling ($t_H$) and insertion ($t_I$):

$$t_{i} = t_{H}(H_{i}) + t_{I}(I_{i})$$

**Step 1 — Handling time table (Boothroyd, empirical fit to PERT data):**

$$t_{H}(H) = \begin{cases} 1.95\,\text{s} & H = 0 \\ 1.30 + 0.65\,\text{s} & H = 1 \\ 1.65 + 1.30\,\text{s} & H = 2 \\ 3.55 + 1.40\,\text{s} & H = 3 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Insertion time penalty (added per part):**

$$t_{I}(I) = I \times 1.30\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Total assembly time** is the sum across all $N_a$ parts:

$$T_{A} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{a}} \left[ t_{H}(H_{i}) + I_{i} \times 1.30 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Cost conversion** (final step for design economics problems):

$$C_{A} = T_{A} \times R_{L} \quad \text{(₹ per unit)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When the problem gives you a "modified" design with fewer parts, you must **re-evaluate** the handling code of the combined part. Combining two parts almost always *raises* the handling code (e.g., from H=0 to H=1) because the combined part is harder to orient.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Boothroyd DFA on a 4-Part Bracket Assembly

A bracket is currently assembled from **4 parts**: a base plate, a U-bracket, 2 screws, and 2 washers (total **Na = 6 separate items**). Apply the Three-Question Test:

| Part | Q1 (Moves?) | Q2 (Different material?) | Q3 (Must separate?) | Retain? |
|------|-------------|--------------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Base plate | No (fixed to housing) | — | — | Necessary (frame) |
| U-bracket | Yes (rotates) | Yes (steel vs. aluminium housing) | Yes (for service) | Necessary |
| Screw 1 | No | — | — | **Eliminate — snap-fit candidate** |
| Screw 2 | No | — | — | **Eliminate** |
| Washer 1 | No | — | — | **Eliminate** |
| Washer 2 | No | — | — | **Eliminate** |

**Theoretical minimum Nm = 2** (base + bracket).

**Design Efficiency of original:**

$$\eta_{\text{orig}} = \frac{2}{6} \times 100 = 33.3\,\%$$

**Proposed design:** Snap-fit the U-bracket directly to the base (one-piece molded or one-piece machined). Then **Na = 2** and:

$$\eta_{\text{new}} = \frac{2}{2} \times 100 = 100\,\%$$

> **However**, the snap-fit may raise the handling code (the combined part is bulkier, harder to orient). So **practical efficiency** is usually < 100 %. KTU expects you to state this caveat.

---

## 3.4 Sequential Assembly Analysis Matrix (Template for ESE Answers)

| Step # | Part Name | Handling Code (H) | Insertion Code (I) | $t_H$ (s) | $t_I$ (s) | $t_i$ Total (s) | Notes |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | Base plate | 0 | 0 | 1.95 | 0 | 1.95 | Self-locating boss |
| 2 | U-bracket | 1 | 1 | 1.30 | 1.30 | 2.60 | Reorient + one screw |
| 3 | Screw A | 2 | 1 | 1.65 | 1.30 | 2.95 | Small part |
| 4 | Screw B | 2 | 1 | 1.65 | 1.30 | 2.95 | Small part |
| 5 | Washer A | 2 | 0 | 1.65 | 0 | 1.65 | |
| 6 | Washer B | 2 | 0 | 1.65 | 0 | 1.65 | |
| **Total** | — | — | — | — | — | **13.75 s** | $\eta = 33.3\,\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always tabulate the matrix. Examiners give **2 marks** for the correct format, **2 marks** for the codes, and the remaining marks for the calculation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — DFA Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product Concept] --> B[Generate Initial Bill of Materials BOM]
    B --> C[Part A: Apply Three-Question Test]
    C --> D{Does Part Pass All Three Questions?}
    D -->|No - Eliminate Candidate| E[Combine or Delete Part]
    D -->|Yes - Retain Part| F[Assign Handling Code H]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Is Handling Code 0 or 1?}
    G -->|No - H 2 or 3| H[Redesign for Easier Handling]
    H --> F
    G -->|Yes| I[Assign Insertion Code I]
    I --> J{Is Insertion Code 0 or 1?}
    J -->|No - I 2 or 3| K[Simplify Fastening or Reorient]
    K --> I
    J -->|Yes| L[Compute Part Time tH plus tI]
    L --> M[Sum All Times to get Total Assembly Time TA]
    M --> N[Compute Design Efficiency eta]
    N --> O{Is eta Greater than 70 percent?}
    O -->|No| P[Iterate: Redesign for Assembly]
    P --> C
    O -->|Yes| Q[Finalize Design and Lock BOM]
    Q --> R[End: DFA-Optimized Product]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DFA Tool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Product CAD Model]
        A2[Bill of Materials BOM]
        A3[Material Specification]
    end
    subgraph CORE[DFA Analysis Engine]
        B1[Three-Question Test Module]
        B2[Handling Code Classifier]
        B3[Insertion Code Classifier]
        B4[Boothroyd Time Library]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        C1[Part Elimination List]
        C2[Assembly Time Matrix]
        C3[Design Efficiency Report]
        C4[Redesign Recommendations]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Assembly Direction Strategy (Vertical Insertion Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assembly Direction Strategy] --> B[Preferred: Top-Down Vertical Insertion]
    A --> C[Acceptable: Horizontal Slide-In with Locating Features]
    A --> D[Avoid: Sideways with Fixturing Required]
    B --> B1[All parts drop into place under gravity]
    B --> B2[No reorientation needed]
    B --> B3[Robot-friendly single axis]
    C --> C1[Requires chamfers and lead-ins]
    C --> C2[Symmetric or keyed features mandatory]
    D --> D1[Operator error prone]
    D --> D2[Increases cycle time by 40 percent]
    D --> D3[DFA penalty: Higher I code]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "Block Architecture" instead of physical drawings?** DFA is a *process* and *decision-flow* discipline, not a part-geometry one. The KTU examiner rewards logical flow diagrams far more than perspective sketches, which often fail to communicate the methodology clearly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define Design for Assembly (DFA). List any four general design guidelines for improving assemblability.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Design for Assembly is a *systematic design methodology* that seeks to *simplify the product structure* and *reduce the cost, time, and effort* of assembly by minimizing the number of parts, simplifying handling and insertion operations, and standardizing fastening and tooling — *all during the design stage*.

**Any Four Design Guidelines (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Minimize the number of parts** — eliminate every part that does not pass the Three-Question Test.
2. **Use modular and standardized components** — adopt off-the-shelf fasteners, bearings, and seals.
3. **Provide symmetry and self-alignment features** — make parts only fit in the *correct* orientation.
4. **Design for top-down (gravity-fed) assembly** — avoid side-mounted or upward-facing fasteners.
5. **Ensure tool accessibility** — allow straight-line insertion of a screwdriver or robot gripper.
6. **Use snap-fits, press-fits, or captive fasteners** to eliminate loose parts.
7. **Standardize fastener sizes** so that one driver works for all joints.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Estimated Pattern)]**
*Explain the concept of "Design Efficiency" in Boothroyd's DFA method. What is its formula and the ideal value for a world-class product?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks: 1 + 1.5 + 0.5):**

**Concept (1 mark):** Design Efficiency is a *dimensionless metric* that quantifies how close a product's part count is to the *theoretical minimum* achievable after applying the Three-Question Test. It is a measure of **design purity** — independent of labour rate or part-time.

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} \times 100\,\%$$

where $N_m$ = minimum number of parts theoretically required, and $N_a$ = actual number of parts in the current design.

**Ideal Value (0.5 marks):**
- **Theoretical maximum: 100 %** (achieved only when every part is necessary — practically never).
- **World-class benchmark: η ≥ 70 %** (achievable in mature products like the Apple iPhone or Toyota engines).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Recommended Choice: Design-Oriented Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**(a)** Explain the Boothroyd Three-Question Test for part elimination in Design for Assembly. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A product consists of **15 parts** as currently designed. After applying the Three-Question Test, the theoretical minimum number of parts is found to be **6**. The total assembly time, measured using the Boothroyd time table, is **42 seconds** per unit. Propose **three (3) specific design changes** that would reduce the part count from 15 to 10 while keeping the minimum part count unchanged, and calculate the *new design efficiency*. Comment on whether the new design qualifies as "world-class". **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):**

The Boothroyd Three-Question Test (also called the *necessity test*) is applied to every part in the BOM. A part is **considered necessary** only if it answers "YES" to **at least one** of the three questions. Otherwise, it is a candidate for elimination.

| # | Question | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|----------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | *Does the part **move** relative to all other parts already assembled during the operation of the product?* | Relative motion implies a kinematic requirement — the part carries out a function that no other part can. |
| 2 | *Must the part be made of a **different material** than the parts it mates with?* | Material difference implies a function (insulation, wear surface, sealing) unique to this part. |
| 3 | *Must the part be **separated** from the mating parts to allow disassembly for service, maintenance, or recycling?* | Serviceability and end-of-life separation are mandatory in modern products. |

**Procedure (1 mark):** The designer goes through the entire BOM and for each part asks the three questions. A *single "NO" to all three* triggers elimination. A *"YES" to any one* justifies retention.

**Typical results from a case study (2 marks):** A 30-part consumer product typically reduces to ≈ 8–10 essential parts after a rigorous Three-Question Test. For example, in a hair dryer, the **mains cord (part of a single molded strain-relief)** and the **two-piece handle** can be combined into one injection-molded shell — saving ≈ 4 parts.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Combining parts may *raise* the handling code (the new part is larger, heavier, harder to orient). It may also create *recycling problems* (mixed-material parts are harder to recycle). DFA must therefore be balanced against Design for Disassembly (DFD) and Design for Recycling (DFR).

---

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Original part count: $N_a = 15$
- Theoretical minimum: $N_m = 6$
- Original total assembly time: $T_A = 42$ s

**Step 1 — Compute the original design efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{\text{orig}} = \frac{6}{15} \times 100 = 40\,\%$$

> **Diagnosis:** This is a *typical un-optimized* design (below the 70 % world-class benchmark).

**Step 2 — Propose three specific design changes (3 marks — 1 each):**

| # | Proposed Change | Parts Eliminated | Engineering Justification |
|---|------------------|------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Combine the 4 separate housing panels into a single molded clamshell** | 3 parts saved | All four panels are stationary and made of the same polymer. Snap-locks replace screws. |
| 2 | **Replace 2 separate metal brackets + 4 screws with 1 integrated plastic clip** | 5 parts saved (1 bracket, 4 screws) | Brackets are stationary, same material as housing, and not separable for service. |
| 3 | **Eliminate the 2 external label stickers by molding the part number directly into the housing** | 2 parts saved | Labels are passive, non-moving, and can be embossed during molding. |

**Total parts eliminated:** 3 + 5 + 2 = **10 parts eliminated** (more than required, we will keep 5 retained). New part count:

$$N_{a,\text{new}} = 15 - 5 = 10 \text{ (after retaining the integrated clip)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new design efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{\text{new}} = \frac{N_{m}}{N_{a,\text{new}}} \times 100 = \frac{6}{10} \times 100 = 60\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Comment on "world-class" status (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{\text{new}} = 60\,\% < 70\,\%$$

> **The new design is *improved* (a 20-percentage-point gain) but is still *not* world-class.** The designer should attempt a *second iteration* by re-examining the remaining 10 parts — particularly by asking whether the theoretical minimum $N_m = 6$ itself can be reduced. For example, if the integrated plastic clip can be molded as part of the clamshell, $N_m$ drops to 5 and $\eta = 5/9 = 55.6\,\%$ — paradoxically *worse*! The lesson: **do not lower $N_m$ unless the part is truly eliminable.**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice: Theory-and-Case-Study*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the concepts of *Handling Code* and *Insertion Code* as used in Boothroyd's DFA method. Explain how the total assembly time is estimated from these codes. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A simple pen consists of **8 parts**: a barrel, a cap, a click button, a spring, an ink refill, a tip cone, a clip, and a logo ring. Apply the Boothroyd Three-Question Test to evaluate which of these parts are *essential* and which are *candidates for elimination*. Justify each decision with one sentence. Compute the design efficiency both *before* and *after* your recommended redesign. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):**

**Handling Code H (3 marks):**
The Handling Code quantifies *how difficult it is to pick the part up and present it in the correct orientation* to the assembly station. Boothroyd's classification:

| H | Difficulty | Typical Parts |
|---|------------|----------------|
| 0 | **Easy** — symmetric, no reorientation | Cylinders, spheres, square blocks |
| 1 | **Moderate** — requires flip/rotation | Asymmetric but still graspable, e.g., a rectangular block with one slot |
| 2 | **Hard** — small, fragile, or tangle-prone | Springs, washers, thin sheets, threads |
| 3 | **Very hard** — requires magazine or bowl feeder | Subminiature parts, oily parts, tangled parts |

**Insertion Code I (2 marks):**
The Insertion Code quantifies *how many discrete operations* are required to mate the part with the sub-assembly.

| I | Operation |
|---|-----------|
| 0 | Self-locating, no fastener (snap-fit, drop-in) |
| 1 | One fastener OR one reorientation |
| 2 | Two fasteners OR two reorientations |
| ≥ 3 | Multiple fasteners, fixturing, or non-standard operations |

**Total Assembly Time (2 marks):**
For each part $i$, the per-part time is $t_i = t_H(H_i) + I_i \times 1.30$ s, and the **total** is:

$$T_{A} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{a}} \left[ t_{H}(H_{i}) + I_{i} \times 1.30 \right]$$

This time is then multiplied by the labour rate $R_L$ to obtain the per-unit assembly cost.

---

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):**

**Three-Question Test on the Pen (5 marks — 0.5 each + 0.5 for design efficiency):**

| # | Part | Q1 (Moves?) | Q2 (Different Material?) | Q3 (Must Separate?) | Verdict |
|---|------|-------------|--------------------------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | Barrel | No (housing) | — | — | **Retain** (structural shell) |
| 2 | Cap | Yes (slides on/off) | Yes (often different colour/finish) | Yes (to refill) | **Retain** |
| 3 | Click button | Yes (depresses) | Yes (rigid plastic, tactile) | Yes (replaces refill) | **Retain** |
| 4 | Spring | Yes (compresses) | Yes (metal vs. plastic) | Yes (spring replacement) | **Retain** |
| 5 | Ink refill | Yes (axial) | Yes (ink is the function) | Yes (consumable) | **Retain** |
| 6 | Tip cone | No (fixed) | — | — | **Eliminate — combine with barrel** |
| 7 | Clip | No (passive) | — | — | **Eliminate — combine with cap** |
| 8 | Logo ring | No (decorative) | — | — | **Eliminate — mold logo into barrel** |

**Theoretical minimum after test: Nm = 5** (barrel, cap, button, spring, refill).

**Design Efficiency (Before):**

$$\eta_{\text{before}} = \frac{5}{8} \times 100 = 62.5\,\%$$

**Design Efficiency (After redesign — combining the three eliminated parts with their mating parts so Na = 5):**

$$\eta_{\text{after}} = \frac{5}{5} \times 100 = 100\,\%$$

> **Caveat (essential for full marks):** The 100 % efficiency is *theoretical*. In practice, the combined barrel-with-cone part has a *higher handling code* (H=1, slightly asymmetric) and the integrated cap-clip is *larger*. A *pragmatic* estimate would be η ≈ 85–90 % — still world-class.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in DFA Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing Nm and Na.** $N_m$ is the *theoretical minimum after the Three-Question Test*, not the *reduced* part count of your proposed design. If your proposed design has 10 parts and the minimum is 6, $N_a = 10$, **not** 6.
> 2. **Forgetting to re-evaluate Handling Codes after combining parts.** Combining two H=0 parts almost always gives an H=1 combined part. Mark loss = –1 to –2 per part.
> 3. **Writing "η = 100 %" without the caveat.** Real designs never reach 100 % practically; examiners expect the *combined-handling-code* discussion.
> 4. **Mixing up assembly *time* and assembly *cost*.** $T_A$ is in seconds; $C_A$ is in ₹ (or $). Always state units explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the table format.** KTU Board examiners award **2 marks** for the structured assembly-analysis matrix; free-text answers lose this.
> 6. **Applying DFA without checking Design for Disassembly (DFD).** A snap-fit that cannot be serviced at end-of-life is *not* an acceptable DFA solution in modern product design (especially for electronics under E-waste rules).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DFA = proactive, drawing-board optimization** of part count, handling, and insertion.
- **Boothroyd Three-Question Test:** movement? material? separation? → *If "NO" to all three, eliminate the part.*
- **Design Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{N_m}{N_a} \times 100\,\%$. **World-class target: η ≥ 70 %.**
- **Handling Code H** ranges 0 (easy) to 3 (magazine-fed); **Insertion Code I** is the number of fastening/reorientation operations.
- **Total Assembly Time:** $T_A = \sum (t_H + I \times 1.30\,\text{s})$.
- **Total Assembly Cost:** $C_A = T_A \times R_L$.
- **Top-down (gravity-fed) assembly is the gold standard** — it minimizes H and I codes.
- **Symmetry & self-alignment** reduce H to 0 and I to 0 — the *DFA ideal*.
- **Standardization of fasteners** (one screw size across the product) reduces tooling cost and operator training.
- **Snap-fits and captive fasteners** eliminate the "loose screw" problem in service and disassembly.
- **Combine parts carefully:** part consolidation *raises* the handling code — always re-evaluate.
- **DFA must be balanced with DFD** (Design for Disassembly) and **DFR** (Design for Recycling) — modern E-waste regulations make serviceability mandatory.
- **Assembly typically accounts for 40–60 %** of total manufacturing cost — DFA is the **highest-leverage** design activity.
- **Memorize these constants** (KTU numericals use them): $t_b(\text{H=0}) = 1.95$ s; fastener penalty = **1.30 s** per operation; $\eta_{\text{world-class}} \geq 70\,\%$.
- **Always present the analysis as a table** (Part, H, I, $t_H$, $t_I$, $t_i$, Notes) — this is the KTU-expected format.
- **End the answer with a "world-class?" comment** — examiners reward the critical evaluation as a 1-mark "thinking" component.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Disassembly
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design for Disassembly

# Design for Disassembly (DFD)

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> *Design for Disassembly (DFD)* is a proactive product design methodology where a product is engineered from the conceptual stage itself to be efficiently, safely, and economically taken apart into its constituent components, sub-assemblies, and materials at the end of its useful life. DFD is a critical sub-discipline of **Design for Environment (DFE)** and **Design for Sustainability (DFS)**, supporting the goals of the **circular economy**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child building a toy with **Lego bricks** versus a toy that has been **glued and welded together**.

- The **Lego toy** can be taken apart piece-by-piece, sorted by color, and reused to build something new. Each brick is recoverable.
- The **glued toy**, if broken, must be thrown away because separating the parts is impossible or destroys them.

**DFD essentially asks designers to treat every product as if it were a Lego model.** When the product reaches its "end-of-life" (EOL), it should break down cleanly into reusable parts and recyclable materials, rather than becoming permanent, unrecyclable waste.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DFD matters in KTU 2024 syllabus:**
> With global regulations like the **EU Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) Directive**, **RoHS**, and **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)** rules under India's *E-Waste Management Rules, 2022*, manufacturers are now legally bound to recover and recycle a minimum percentage of their products. DFD is the engineering response to this regulatory and environmental demand.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Mandatory for KTU Board Exams)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **End-of-Life (EOL)** | The stage where a product is discarded, recycled, remanufactured, or disposed of |
| **Disassembly Index ($D_i$)** | Quantitative measure of how completely a product can be disassembled |
| **Recovery Index ($R_i$)** | Fraction of the product mass that can be recovered through DFD processes |
| **Recyclability Rate** | Percentage of material content that can be recycled after disassembly |
| **Snap-Fit** | A fastening method using flexible plastic features that interlock without tools |
| **Modular Design** | Architecture where the product is built from independent, swappable sub-assemblies |
| **Selective Disassembly** | Targeted removal of specific parts (e.g., hazardous components) for separate treatment |
| **Disassembly Time ($T_D$)** | Total time required to fully dismantle a product into its base components |
| **Disassembly Depth** | The hierarchical level to which a product must be dismantled |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Architecture of DFD

DFD operates on three interconnected levels of design thinking:

### Level 1: Product Architecture
- **Modular Architecture**: Each function is encapsulated in a discrete physical module. This allows one module to be replaced without disturbing the rest of the product.
- **Integral Architecture**: Multiple functions are combined into a single part (e.g., a casing that also acts as a structural frame). This is the **enemy of DFD** and must be avoided.

### Level 2: Connection Design
This is the heart of DFD. Every joint in the product must be classified and evaluated:
- **Preferred Joints**: Threaded fasteners (screws, bolts), snap-fits, clips, quick-release pins, captive screws.
- **Conditionally Acceptable**: Press-fits, rivets (require drilling), hinges with screws.
- **Forbidden in DFD**: Welds, permanent adhesives, heat-bonded joints, rivets that cannot be drilled, ultrasonic welds, chemical bonds.

### Level 3: Material Selection
- **Mono-material design**: Use a single material where possible to avoid separation problems during recycling.
- **Material marking**: ISO 1043 / ISO 11469 codes for plastics (e.g., **>ABS<**, **>PP<**) must be embossed on parts.
- **Avoid hazardous substances**: Lead, mercury, cadmium, hexavalent chromium (RoHS compliance).

## 2.2 The DFD Process Workflow

1. **Identify EOL Options**: Decide what will happen to the product (recycle, reuse, remanufacture, recover energy).
2. **Generate Disassembly Sequence**: Plan the order of operations to reach each target component.
3. **Estimate Disassembly Time and Cost**: Use the equations below to evaluate feasibility.
4. **Evaluate Disassembly Index and Recovery Index**: Quantify the design's DFD performance.
5. **Iterate Design**: Modify the design to improve weak metrics.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **direct extract of KTU-exam-frequent equations** for the DFD module. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Disassembly Index ($D_i$)** | $D_i = \dfrac{N_{rd}}{N_t} \times 100\%$ | $N_{rd}$ = number of recoverable/disassemblable parts; $N_t$ = total number of parts | Percentage of parts that *can* be disassembled. Higher is better. |
| 2 | **Recovery Index ($R_i$)** | $R_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (m_j \cdot r_j)}{M_{total}}$ | $m_j$ = mass of part $j$; $r_j$ = recyclability factor of part $j$ (0 to 1); $M_{total}$ = total product mass | Weighted recyclability of the product |
| 3 | **Recyclability Rate ($RR$)** | $RR = \dfrac{M_{recyclable}}{M_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $M_{recyclable}$ = mass of material that can be recycled; $M_{total}$ = total mass | Overall recyclability percentage |
| 4 | **Disassembly Time ($T_D$)** | $T_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} t_i$ | $t_i$ = time to remove part $i$ (seconds); $N_t$ = total number of parts | Total cumulative time to disassemble the entire product |
| 5 | **Disassembly Cost ($C_D$)** | $C_D = C_{labor} \cdot T_D + C_{tool} + C_{overhead}$ | $C_{labor}$ = labor cost per second; $C_{tool}$ = tooling cost; $C_{overhead}$ = fixed overhead | Total economic cost of disassembly operation |
| 6 | **Disassembly Energy ($E_D$)** | $E_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} (F_i \cdot d_i) \cdot \eta$ | $F_i$ = force to separate joint $i$; $d_i$ = separation distance; $\eta$ = efficiency factor | Total energy consumed during disassembly |
| 7 | **Selective Disassembly Index ($SDI$)** | $SDI = \dfrac{N_{ds}}{N_{d}}$ | $N_{ds}$ = number of parts selectively disassembled; $N_d$ = number of parts requiring selective disassembly (e.g., hazardous) | Effectiveness of targeted disassembly for hazardous or valuable parts |
| 8 | **Reusability Factor ($RF$)** | $RF = \dfrac{N_{reuse}}{N_t} \times 100\%$ | $N_{reuse}$ = number of parts reusable after removal | Percentage of parts that retain functional value post-disassembly |
| 9 | **Disassembly Difficulty Coefficient ($DDC$)** | $DDC = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} \alpha_i \cdot \beta_i \cdot \gamma_i$ | $\alpha_i$ = accessibility factor; $\beta_i$ = tool complexity; $\gamma_i$ = operator skill index | Composite score quantifying ease of disassembly per part |
| 10 | **Net Recovery Value ($NRV$)** | $NRV = V_{recovered} - C_D$ | $V_{recovered}$ = market value of recovered materials/parts | Economic viability check: positive $NRV$ means recovery is profitable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM HINT:**
> Most KTU board questions on DFD will ask you to either **(a) compute $D_i$ or $R_i$** for a given product with a parts list, or **(b) suggest design improvements** to raise these indices. Always state the **units explicitly** and conclude with a one-line engineering interpretation (e.g., "Hence, the product has moderate recoverability and the design needs standardization of fasteners").

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Disassembly Index Calculation (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical numerical question type** that has appeared multiple times in KTU End Semester Examinations. Mastering this guarantees 7 marks.

### Problem Statement
A desktop computer has the following components in its main unit:

| Part No. | Component | Mass (kg) | Disassemblable? | Material Type | Recyclability Factor ($r_j$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Outer Steel Casing | 3.5 | Yes (4 screws) | Steel | 0.95 |
| 2 | Power Supply Unit (PSU) | 1.2 | Yes (2 screws + 1 connector) | Mixed | 0.70 |
| 3 | Motherboard (PCB) | 0.4 | Yes (6 screws, 3 connectors) | PCB with chips | 0.30 |
| 4 | Hard Disk Drive (HDD) | 0.6 | Yes (4 screws, 1 cable) | Mixed | 0.60 |
| 5 | Optical Drive (DVD) | 0.8 | Yes (2 screws) | Mixed | 0.55 |
| 6 | Cooling Fan | 0.2 | Yes (4 snap-fits) | Plastic + Metal | 0.80 |
| 7 | Internal Wiring Harness | 0.3 | Yes (plug-in) | Copper + Plastic | 0.85 |
| 8 | Adhesive-mounted Logo Badge | 0.05 | **No** (epoxy bonded) | Plastic | 0.00 |
| 9 | Side Panel Rivets (×8) | 0.10 | No (riveted permanently) | Aluminum | 0.10 |
| 10 | Soldered Capacitor Bank on Motherboard | 0.08 | No (soldered) | Mixed | 0.20 |

**Total Product Mass** $M_{total} = 7.23$ kg (rounded).

### Step 1: Compute the Disassembly Index ($D_i$)

$$
D_i = \frac{N_{rd}}{N_t} \times 100\%
$$

We classify each part as recoverable ($N_{rd}$) or non-recoverable:

- **Recoverable parts ($N_{rd}$):** 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 → $N_{rd} = 7$
- **Non-recoverable parts:** 8, 9, 10 → 3 parts
- **Total parts ($N_t$):** 10

$$
D_i = \frac{7}{10} \times 100\% = 70\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only **70%** of the parts can be successfully disassembled. The epoxy-bonded badge, rivets, and soldered capacitors are design bottlenecks.

### Step 2: Compute the Recovery Index ($R_i$)

The Recovery Index uses **mass-weighted** recyclability:

$$
R_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (m_j \cdot r_j)}{M_{total}}
$$

We must include **all 10 parts**, including those that are not disassemblable (they still contribute mass, and even partially-recyclable non-disassemblable parts count for their partial recyclability):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{10} (m_j \cdot r_j) &= (3.5)(0.95) + (1.2)(0.70) + (0.4)(0.30) + (0.6)(0.60) \\
&\quad + (0.8)(0.55) + (0.2)(0.80) + (0.3)(0.85) \\
&\quad + (0.05)(0.00) + (0.10)(0.10) + (0.08)(0.20)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now evaluate each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(3.5)(0.95) &= 3.325 \\
(1.2)(0.70) &= 0.840 \\
(0.4)(0.30) &= 0.120 \\
(0.6)(0.60) &= 0.360 \\
(0.8)(0.55) &= 0.440 \\
(0.2)(0.80) &= 0.160 \\
(0.3)(0.85) &= 0.255 \\
(0.05)(0.00) &= 0.000 \\
(0.10)(0.10) &= 0.010 \\
(0.08)(0.20) &= 0.016
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing all terms:

$$
\sum (m_j \cdot r_j) = 3.325 + 0.840 + 0.120 + 0.360 + 0.440 + 0.160 + 0.255 + 0.000 + 0.010 + 0.016
$$

$$
\sum (m_j \cdot r_j) = 5.526 \text{ kg}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_i = \frac{5.526}{7.23} = 0.7643 = 76.43\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Approximately **76.43%** of the product's mass can be recovered through the current disassembly process.

### Step 3: Compute the Selective Disassembly Index (SDI) for Hazardous Parts

Suppose the **Motherboard (Part 3)** and **Capacitor Bank (Part 10)** are classified as **hazardous** (containing lead solder and electrolytic chemicals). Therefore $N_d = 2$ (parts requiring selective disassembly).

Out of these, Part 3 is disassemblable (selectively removable) and Part 10 is not.

$$
N_{ds} = 1
$$

$$
SDI = \frac{N_{ds}}{N_d} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 = 50\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only **50%** of hazardous parts can be selectively removed. This violates WEEE directive best practices (target: 100%).

### Step 4: Disassembly Time Estimation

Assume the operator takes the following times per part (with hand tools):

| Part | Removal Time (s) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 45 |
| 2 | 60 |
| 3 | 120 |
| 4 | 40 |
| 5 | 30 |
| 6 | 15 |
| 7 | 25 |
| 8 | N/A (cannot disassemble) |
| 9 | N/A (cannot disassemble) |
| 10 | N/A (cannot disassemble) |

$$
T_D = \sum_{i=1}^{7} t_i = 45 + 60 + 120 + 40 + 30 + 15 + 25 = 335 \text{ seconds} \approx 5.58 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 5: Disassembly Cost

Assume:
- $C_{labor} = ₹0.50$ per second
- $C_{tool} = ₹20$ (one-time tool wear)
- $C_{overhead} = ₹30$

$$
C_D = (0.50)(335) + 20 + 30 = 167.5 + 20 + 30 = ₹217.50
$$

### Step 6: Net Recovery Value (NRV)

Assume the recovered materials and parts have a market value:

- Recovered steel (Part 1, mass 3.5 kg × 95% × ₹25/kg) = ₹83.13
- Recovered PSU (Part 2, with some reuse value) = ₹150
- Recovered HDD, DVD, Fan, Wiring = ₹200 (combined reuse value)
- PCB (Part 3) recovered as e-waste (low value) = ₹30

$$
V_{recovered} = 83.13 + 150 + 200 + 30 = ₹463.13
$$

$$
NRV = V_{recovered} - C_D = 463.13 - 217.50 = ₹245.63
$$

> **Interpretation:** Since $NRV > 0$, disassembly for recovery is **economically viable** for this desktop computer.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: KTU Exam Pattern Recognition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix to instantly identify the "type" of any DFD question that appears in the KTU 2024 question paper, and structure your answer accordingly.

| Question Trigger Words | Expected Answer Type | Key Formulas to Deploy | Marks Allocation |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Calculate Disassembly Index" | Numerical table + arithmetic | $D_i = N_{rd}/N_t$ | 3-5 |
| "Determine Recovery Index" | Mass-weighted sum | $R_i = \sum(m_j \cdot r_j)/M_{total}$ | 7 |
| "Suggest design improvements" | Qualitative bullet list with justifications | None (design principles) | 5-7 |
| "Compare DFD vs DFA" | Tabular comparison with at least 6 parameters | None (conceptual) | 7 |
| "Discuss DFD guidelines/principles" | Numbered list of 8-10 principles with examples | None (theory) | 7-14 |
| "Compute NRV/Cost" | Step-by-step economic analysis | $C_D, NRV$ | 7 |
| "Sketch disassembly tree/sequence" | Hierarchical diagram + arrow annotations | $T_D$ | 5-7 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DFD (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

This diagram shows the **complete DFD process flow** as it would be applied in an industrial product development cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Concept Stage] --> B[Identify End of Life EOL Strategy]
    B --> C{Reuse or Recycle or Remanufacture or Dispose}
    C --> D[Generate Disassembly Sequence Plan]
    D --> E[Classify All Joints and Connections]
    E --> F{Joint Type Evaluation}
    F -->|Preferred| G[Threaded Fasteners Snap Fits Clips]
    F -->|Conditionally Acceptable| H[Press Fits Rivets Hinges]
    F -->|Forbidden in DFD| I[Welds Adhesives Permanent Bonds]
    G --> J[Select Materials with ISO Marking]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Redesign to Replace Forbidden Joints]
    K --> E
    J --> L[Compute Disassembly Index Di]
    L --> M[Compute Recovery Index Ri]
    M --> N{Di and Ri meet targets}
    N -->|Yes| O[Finalize Design for Manufacture]
    N -->|No| P[Iterate Design Modify Joints]
    P --> E
    O --> Q[Prototype and Validate Disassembly]
    Q --> R[Release to Production]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **feedback loop** from the "Iterate Design" block back to "Classify All Joints." This is the iterative nature of DFD — designs are repeatedly refined until the disassembly metrics meet corporate and regulatory thresholds.

## 4.2 Disassembly Sequence Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This is the type of diagram a KTU examiner expects when the question asks to "sketch the disassembly sequence" of a product like a mobile phone or a printer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Level 0 Complete Product Mobile Phone] --> L1A[Level 1a Back Cover]
    L0 --> L1B[Level 1b Battery]
    L0 --> L1C[Level 1c SIM and Memory Card Tray]
    L0 --> L1D[Level 1d Mid Frame Assembly]
    L1D --> L2A[Level 2a Main PCB with Chips]
    L1D --> L2B[Level 2b Display Module]
    L1D --> L2C[Level 2c Camera Module]
    L1D --> L2D[Level 2d Speaker and Microphone]
    L1D --> L2E[Level 2e Vibrator Motor]
    L1D --> L2F[Level 2f Charging Port Flex Cable]
    L2A --> L3A[Level 3a CPU Chip Soldered]
    L2A --> L3B[Level 3b Battery Connector]
    L2A --> L3C[Level 3c SIM Socket]
    L2A --> L3D[Level 3d Memory Chips Soldered]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** In a well-designed phone (DFD-compliant), Levels 3a and 3d (soldered chips) should ideally be designed as **socketed modules** that can be lifted out, not soldered. The diagram above highlights exactly which nodes are "stuck" — these are the **DFD bottlenecks**.

## 4.3 DFD Principles Mind-Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram organizes the 8 universal DFD design guidelines taught across the KTU manufacturing design curriculum.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[DFD Core Principles] --> P1[1 Minimize Number of Parts]
    P --> P2[2 Use Standard and Identical Fasteners]
    P --> P3[3 Avoid Permanent Joints Welds Adhesives]
    P --> P4[4 Modular Design for Easy Access]
    P --> P5[5 Snap Fits and Quick Release Connections]
    P --> P6[6 Mark All Plastic Parts with ISO 11469 Codes]
    P --> P7[7 Separate Hazardous Materials Early in Sequence]
    P --> P8[8 Ensure Tool Access Clearance for Each Fastener]
    P1 --> P1A[Combine Functions Reduce Part Count]
    P3 --> P3A[Use Screws Bolts Clips Instead]
    P5 --> P5A[Reduces Disassembly Time and Tooling Cost]
    P7 --> P7A[Battery Mercury Switches Removed First]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state units explicitly** (e.g., seconds, kg, ₹). A correct number without units loses 0.5 to 1 mark per sub-part.
> 2. **Do not confuse $D_i$ with $R_i$.** $D_i$ is a *count* of parts; $R_i$ is a *mass-weighted* value. Students frequently swap these formulas — examiners deduct 2 full marks for this.
> 3. **Always end numerical answers with an interpretation sentence.** A bare numerical answer is "engineering-incomplete." Add a one-line statement like *"Hence the design has a moderate disassembly potential and requires fastener standardization."*
> 4. **In design questions**, do not just list principles — *justify* each with one real-world example or consequence.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Design for Disassembly (DFD). List any four DFD guidelines.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** *Design for Disassembly (DFD)* is a product design philosophy that incorporates end-of-life disassembly, repairability, and recyclability considerations into the early design phase of a product, ensuring that the product can be efficiently taken apart into its constituent components and materials for reuse, remanufacturing, or recycling.

**Four DFD Guidelines:**
1. **Minimize the total number of parts** in the product to reduce disassembly operations.
2. **Use standardized fasteners** (same screw size/type) throughout the product so only one tool is needed.
3. **Avoid permanent joints** such as welds, adhesives, and rivets that cannot be undone.
4. **Mark all plastic parts** with ISO 11469 material identification codes for easy sorting during recycling.

*(Any 4 valid guidelines, fully explained, fetch 3 marks. 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for guidelines.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between Disassembly Index ($D_i$) and Recovery Index ($R_i$).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Disassembly Index ($D_i$) | Recovery Index ($R_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Number-based (count of parts) | Mass-weighted (mass of materials) |
| **Formula** | $D_i = N_{rd}/N_t \times 100\%$ | $R_i = \sum(m_j \cdot r_j) / M_{total}$ |
| **Unit** | Percentage (%) | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) or % |
| **Focus** | *Can* the part be removed? | *Is* the material recoverable? |
| **Limitation** | Ignores part mass and material | Requires recyclability factor per part |
| **Example Use** | Measures DFD performance | Measures recycling efficiency |

*(Tabular comparison with 6 rows = 3 marks.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme pattern, Module questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Either **Question A** or **Question B** must be answered.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A washing machine has 12 major components. Out of these, 9 are attached using screws, snap-fits, and plug-in connectors, 2 are riveted permanently, and 1 is welded to the frame. If the total mass of the machine is 60 kg and the mass of the welded component is 8 kg (non-recyclable), compute the **Disassembly Index** and **Recyclability Rate** of the product.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **any seven** design guidelines that should be followed to improve the Disassembly Index of an electronic product like a television or a laptop.

### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

**Given:**
- Total parts $N_t = 12$
- Recoverable (screws/snap-fits/plug-in) $N_{rd} = 9$
- Non-recoverable: 2 riveted + 1 welded = 3 parts
- Total mass $M_{total} = 60$ kg
- Welded part mass = 8 kg (assume recyclability factor = 0)

**Step 1: Disassembly Index**

$$
D_i = \frac{N_{rd}}{N_t} \times 100\% = \frac{9}{12} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Step 2: Recyclable Mass**

Assuming the riveted parts and other components are recyclable (use average recyclability factor 0.80 for the recoverable parts and 0.40 for riveted parts, since they are partially recoverable via shredding):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{recoverable} &= (9 \text{ parts} \times \text{avg mass}) \times 0.80 + (2 \text{ riveted} \times \text{avg mass}) \times 0.40 + (8 \text{ kg welded}) \times 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For simplicity, distribute mass evenly: each part weighs $60/12 = 5$ kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{recyclable} &= (9 \times 5)(0.80) + (2 \times 5)(0.40) + (8)(0) \\
&= 36 + 4 + 0 = 40 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recyclability Rate:**

$$
RR = \frac{M_{recyclable}}{M_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{40}{60} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The washing machine has a *moderate* Disassembly Index of 75%, but only 66.67% of its mass is recyclable. The welded frame is the critical design bottleneck and should be redesigned with bolted joints to improve both metrics.

### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

**Seven DFD Design Guidelines for Electronics:**

1. **Modular Architecture:** Design the TV/laptop as independent sub-assemblies (display, motherboard, power supply). A failing module can be replaced without dismantling the whole product.

2. **Use of Captive Screws and Snap-Fits:** These fasteners cannot be lost during disassembly and are easily removed, reducing the **Disassembly Time ($T_D$)**.

3. **Tool Standardization:** Use one type of screw (e.g., Phillips #2) throughout the product so technicians need only a single tool, reducing **Disassembly Cost ($C_D$)**.

4. **Material Marking (ISO 11469):** All plastic enclosures must carry embossed material codes (e.g., `>ABS<`, `>PC<`) so recyclers can identify and sort them correctly.

5. **Hazardous Component Isolation:** The battery (laptop) or the CCFL backlight inverter (old TVs) must be the *first* part removed in the disassembly sequence, per WEEE directive.

6. **Avoid Adhesives on Enclosures:** Use mechanical clips instead of glue on outer covers. Adhesives contaminate the plastic recycling stream.

7. **Provide Adequate Access Clearance:** Ensure that a screwdriver, plier, or robot gripper can physically reach every fastener. Hidden fasteners under labels or under cables are a major DFD violation.

*(Each point with 1 mark for the principle and an example; 7 points = 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step DFD methodology** adopted during the design phase of a product. List the four key indices used to evaluate a DFD design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A refrigerator has the following major parts:

| Part | Mass (kg) | Disassemblable? | Recyclability Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Outer Cabinet | 40 | Yes (bolted) | 0.90 |
| Compressor | 12 | Yes (bolted + brazed) | 0.75 |
| Copper Tubing | 3 | Yes (brazed, can be cut) | 0.95 |
| Insulation Foam | 8 | No (PU foam bonded) | 0.10 |
| Glass Shelves (×4) | 6 | Yes (sliding) | 0.95 |
| Plastic Drawers (×3) | 4 | Yes (slides) | 0.85 |
| Electronic Control Board | 1 | Yes (plug-in) | 0.40 |
| Door Hinges (welded) | 2 | No (welded) | 0.20 |

Total mass = 76 kg. Compute the **Disassembly Index**, **Recovery Index**, and **Selective Disassembly Index** (assuming only the **Compressor** and **Control Board** are the two hazardous/refrigerant-containing parts).

### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

**DFD Methodology — Step-by-Step Block Diagram Description:**

The DFD methodology proceeds as follows:

1. **Define End-of-Life (EOL) Strategy:** Decide whether the product will be reused, remanufactured, recycled, or disposed of after its useful life. This sets the disassembly depth target.

2. **Generate Disassembly Sequence:** Create a hierarchical disassembly tree (like the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2) showing the order in which parts must be removed.

3. **Analyze Fasteners and Joints:** Classify every joint as Preferred (screws, snap-fits), Conditional (press-fits, rivets), or Forbidden (welds, adhesives).

4. **Compute DFD Metrics:** Calculate **Disassembly Index ($D_i$)**, **Recovery Index ($R_i$)**, **Disassembly Time ($T_D$)**, and **Disassembly Cost ($C_D$)**.

5. **Iterate and Optimize:** If metrics fall below the corporate threshold, redesign the joints and repeat steps 2–4.

**Four Key Indices:**
- **Disassembly Index ($D_i$)** — measures part-count recovery
- **Recovery Index ($R_i$)** — measures mass-weighted material recovery
- **Selective Disassembly Index (SDI)** — measures targeted removal of hazardous parts
- **Reusability Factor ($RF$)** — measures how many parts can be functionally reused

*(Block diagram description: 3 marks; 4 indices with 1 mark each = 4 marks; total 7.)*

### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Given:** Total parts $N_t = 8$; Total mass $M_{total} = 76$ kg.

**Step 1: Disassembly Index**

Recoverable parts: 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7 → $N_{rd} = 6$

Non-recoverable: 4, 8 → 2 parts

$$
D_i = \frac{6}{8} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Step 2: Recovery Index**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (m_j \cdot r_j) &= (40)(0.90) + (12)(0.75) + (3)(0.95) + (8)(0.10) \\
&\quad + (6)(0.95) + (4)(0.85) + (1)(0.40) + (2)(0.20)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 36.00 + 9.00 + 2.85 + 0.80 + 5.70 + 3.40 + 0.40 + 0.40 \\
&= 58.55 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
R_i = \frac{58.55}{76} = 0.7704 = 77.04\%
$$

**Step 3: Selective Disassembly Index (SDI)**

Hazardous parts requiring selective disassembly: Compressor (Part 2) and Control Board (Part 7) → $N_d = 2$

Both are disassemblable → $N_{ds} = 2$

$$
SDI = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0 = 100\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Although the refrigerator has a moderate $D_i$ of 75% (PU foam insulation and welded door hinges are non-recoverable bottlenecks), the $R_i$ of 77.04% is acceptable, and the SDI of 100% confirms that all hazardous/refrigerant parts can be selectively removed for safe treatment — fully WEEE compliant.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU exam recap):**

- **DFD Definition:** Design methodology to make products easily disassemblable for EOL recovery.
- **Core Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $D_i = \dfrac{N_{rd}}{N_t} \times 100\%$
  - $R_i = \dfrac{\sum m_j r_j}{M_{total}}$
  - $T_D = \sum t_i$
  - $C_D = C_{labor} \cdot T_D + C_{tool} + C_{overhead}$
  - $NRV = V_{recovered} - C_D$
- **Three Forbidden Joints:** Welds, Adhesives, Permanent Rivets.
- **Three Preferred Joints:** Screws/Bolts, Snap-Fits, Quick-Release Clips.
- **Three Levels of DFD Thinking:** Product Architecture → Connection Design → Material Selection.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers.
- **Always end with an interpretation** sentence.
- **Use ISO 11469** for plastic marking and **WEEE directive** for EOL recovery targets.
- **Modular > Integral architecture** is the single most powerful DFD strategy.
- **Selective Disassembly** is mandatory for hazardous parts (battery, refrigerant, mercury switches).
- **Examiner's Pet Topics:** Disassembly Index, Recovery Index, design principles list, and disassembly tree diagrams.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If the KTU question offers internal choice in Part B, **always pick the numerical question** (Question A in this bank) over the descriptive one. Numerical answers are easier to score because the marking is binary (formula correct = marks awarded). Descriptive questions require precise keyword matching and risk partial credit loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Design of Manufacture*, **Module 4** introduces the foundational concepts of **Press Tools and Die Design** (also termed *Sheet Metal Forming Tooling*). A **Press Tool** is a precision-engineered tooling system mounted on a mechanical or hydraulic press, used to perform cutting, bending, drawing, or forming operations on sheet metal through a single stroke of the press ram.

A **Die** is defined as a specialized tool consisting of a pair of hardened tool-steel members (the **punch** and the **die block / matrix**) with a carefully shaped cavity, used to produce a desired shape on a sheet-metal blank by plastic deformation or by shearing action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Scope Note:** This module specifically emphasizes the **classification, functional anatomy, and selection logic** of press tools used in high-volume sheet metal production. Subsequent modules in PEMRT522 will extend these concepts into detailed **die-component design calculations, force estimation, strip layout, and die-set selection.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Cookie Cutter & Dough" Model**
>
> Think of manufacturing a million identical paper hearts for a school event. You would never cut each one freehand with scissors. Instead, you would build a **steel shape** (the die) and a **matching pusher** (the punch), mount them on a press, and let the press do the shearing in a single motion. The sheet metal is the "dough," and the press tool is the "cookie cutter on steroids." The die is the female cavity, the punch is the male shape that descends, and the press provides the force. Every cycle, one perfect shape is produced. The "design" of this tool — its clearance, material, strip layout, and guiding — determines the **quality, life, and cost** of every single part produced.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Shear Strength of Mild Steel:** **400 N/mm² (approx.)**
- **Tensile Strength of Mild Steel:** **410–510 N/mm²**
- **Standard Die Clearance (per side):** **5–10 % of stock thickness** for blanking/perforating
- **Press Tool Hardness (Working Parts):** **HRC 58–62** (after heat treatment)
- **Standard Stock Thickness Range for Sheet Metal Press Work:** **0.5 mm to 6 mm**

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The values above are frequently used as the *default assumption* when a numerical problem does not specify a value. Memorize them as your "engineering constants toolkit."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Press Tools? — The Engineering Rationale

Sheet metal components form the backbone of modern manufacturing — from automobile bodies and electrical enclosures to kitchen appliances and aerospace panels. Press tools exist because of the following functional drivers:

- **High Repeatability:** Identical parts at the rate of 20–1000 strokes per minute.
- **Material Economy:** Minimal scrap when properly nested (especially in **progressive dies**).
- **Tolerance Capability:** Achieves IT8–IT11 grades consistently.
- **Cost-Effectiveness at Volume:** The break-even point for press tooling is typically **5,000–10,000 parts**, after which per-part cost plummets.

### 2.2 Classification of Press Operations

Press operations are broadly classified into two super-categories:

| **Category** | **Mechanism** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutting (Shearing) Operations** | Material is separated by shearing fracture | Blanking, Piercing, Trimming, Shaving, Notching, Lancing |
| **Forming (Non-Cutting) Operations** | Material is plastically deformed without removal | Bending, Drawing, Stretching, Embossing, Coining, Flanging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Questions often ask to *"Classify the given press operation and justify."* Always state both the **mechanism** (cutting vs. forming) AND a **one-line functional justification**.

### 2.3 Types of Dies (Core Module 4 Concept)

This is the **heart of Module 4 — Introduction**. The KTU syllabus mandates mastery of the following four die types:

| **Die Type** | **Operations per Stroke** | **Stock Condition** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Die (Single-Operation Die)** | One operation at one station | Strip or coil | Trivial blanking or piercing; low volume |
| **Compound Die** | Two or more operations at the *same station* in a single stroke | Strip (advanced from a single position) | Blanking + Piercing together (e.g., washer with hole) |
| **Progressive Die** | Multiple operations at *successive stations* along the strip | Indexed strip moves station-to-station | High-volume complex parts (e.g., electrical terminals) |
| **Combination Die** | Both cutting AND forming in a single stroke | Strip | Blanking + Drawing together; reduces handling |

### 2.4 Functional Anatomy of a Typical Die (Component Map)

> [!NOTE]
> The following components constitute the **skeletal anatomy** of a standard progressive die. KTU questions frequently ask students to "Label the parts of a press tool diagram" or "Explain the function of [X]."

- **Die Block (Matrix):** Female member containing the cutting/forming cavity.
- **Punch:** Male member that enters the die cavity.
- **Punch Holder / Punch Plate:** Carries the punch and bolts to the press ram.
- **Stripper Plate:** Removes the workpiece / scrap from the punch on the up-stroke.
- **Die Set (Shoe):** Provides precise alignment between upper and lower halves via guide pillars and bushings.
- **Guide Pins & Bushings:** Ensure concentricity of punch and die.
- **Stop Pin / Pilot Pin:** Locates the strip at the correct station.
- **Backing Plate:** Supports the die block against the die-set bolster.
- **Bolster Plate:** Mounted on the press bed; absorbs operating loads.
- **Ejector / Knockout:** Ejects the finished part from the die.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas form the **Module 4 numerical problem foundation.** They will be reused and extended in subsequent modules.

| **Formula** | **Equation** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| **Blanking Force (Shear)** | $F_b = L \times t \times \tau$ | L = perimeter of cut, t = thickness, $\tau$ = shear strength |
| **Piercing Force** | $F_p = L \times t \times \tau$ | Same form as blanking; distinguishes operation |
| **Stripping Force** | $F_s = L \times t \times q$ | q = stripping stress (≈ 5% of shear strength) |
| **Punch Penetration Depth** | $h = (1/3) \cdot t \text{ to } (1/2) \cdot t$ | For clean shearing; t = stock thickness |
| **Die Clearance (Optimum)** | $c = (5\% \text{ to } 10\%) \cdot t$ | Per side, unilateral clearance |
| **Blank Diameter (Circular)** | $D = \sqrt{d_1^{2} + d_2^{2} + d_3^{2} + \ldots}$ | For multiple drawn holes in drawing |
| **Draw Force (Approx.)** | $F_d = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d$ | d = blank dia, $\sigma_d$ = drawing stress |
| **Press Capacity Rule of Thumb** | $F_{press} = 1.2 \times F_{max}$ | 20% safety margin over peak force |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Throughout KTU materials, absolute-value expressions and conditional clauses use the notation $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never the vertical bar `|` character — to maintain LaTeX table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Press tools and dies are the **silent workhorses** of:
- **Automotive Industry:** Body panels (draw dies), brackets (progressive dies).
- **Electronics:** Connector pins, lead frames, IC heat sinks.
- **White Goods:** Washing machine drums, refrigerator shelves.
- **Aerospace:** Lightweight sheet panels for fuselage skins.
- **Packaging:** Bottle caps, cans, food containers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Shear (Cutting) Force Equation

This derivation underpins **every cutting-type press operation** (blanking, piercing, notching, trimming). It is the single most important equation in Module 4.

**Starting Premise:** A punch of perimeter $L$ advances into a sheet of thickness $t$ and shears it across the entire shear surface.

**Step 1 — Identify the Shear Surface Area:**

The shear failure plane runs vertically along the **wall of the cut**, between the punch and the die. Its area is:

$$
A_{shear} = L \times t
$$

where $L$ is the perimeter of the shape being cut and $t$ is the sheet thickness.

**Step 2 — Apply the Shear Strength Definition:**

Shear strength $\tau$ is defined as the **shear force per unit shear area** at which the material fractures:

$$
\tau = \frac{F_{shear}}{A_{shear}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Solve for Force:**

Rearranging for $F_{shear}$:

$$
F_{shear} = \tau \times A_{shear}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Shear Area:**

$$
F_{shear} = \tau \times (L \times t)
$$

Therefore, the **blanking / piercing force** is:

$$
\boxed{F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the Empirical Correction Factor:**

In practice, due to work-hardening, edge condition, and stroke variability, KTU adopts a **multiplier of 1.2–1.3** to determine the **actual press tonnage requirement**:

$$
F_{press} = k \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \tau
$$

where $k \approx 1.2$ is the **overload / safety factor**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A washer of **outer diameter 30 mm** and **inner diameter 15 mm** is to be blanked from a **2 mm thick mild-steel strip**. Given shear strength $\tau = 400$ N/mm², calculate:
1. The **blanking force**.
2. The **stripping force** (assuming $q = 0.05 \times \tau$).
3. The **press tonnage** (using $k = 1.2$).

**Step 1 — Compute the Total Shear Perimeter (Outer + Inner Cut):**

The washer requires blanking the outer circle AND piercing the inner hole. Both contribute to the shear perimeter.

$$
L = \pi \cdot D_o + \pi \cdot D_i
$$

$$
L = \pi \cdot 30 + \pi \cdot 15 = 30\pi + 15\pi = 45\pi
$$

$$
L \approx 141.37 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Blanking Force:**

$$
F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau
$$

$$
F = 141.37 \times 2 \times 400
$$

$$
\boxed{F = 113{,}096 \text{ N} \approx 113.1 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Stripping Force:**

Stripping stress $q = 0.05 \times 400 = 20$ N/mm².

$$
F_s = L \cdot t \cdot q
$$

$$
F_s = 141.37 \times 2 \times 20
$$

$$
\boxed{F_s = 5{,}654.8 \text{ N} \approx 5.65 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Press Tonnage:**

$$
F_{press} = k \cdot F = 1.2 \times 113{,}096
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{press} = 135{,}715 \text{ N} \approx 135.7 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 5 — Analytical Summary Table:**

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Value** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Perimeter | $L$ | 141.37 | mm |
| Blanking Force | $F$ | 113,096 | N (≈ 113.1 kN) |
| Stripping Force | $F_s$ | 5,654.8 | N (≈ 5.65 kN) |
| Press Capacity Required | $F_{press}$ | 135,715 | N (≈ 135.7 kN) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Always carry **two extra decimal places** during intermediate computation and round only at the final step. Examiners penalize premature rounding.

### 3.3 Derivation of Optimum Die Clearance

Clearance $c$ is the **radial gap per side** between punch and die. It is the single most influential parameter in cut quality.

**Step 1 — Shear Mechanism During Cutting:**

As the punch descends, the material first **elastically deflects**, then **plastically rolls**, and finally **fractures**. The fracture begins on the punch side and propagates to meet the die edge.

**Step 2 — Role of Clearance:**

- If $c$ is **too small**: Fracture lines overlap improperly → rough, burred, double-shear edge.
- If $c$ is **too large**: Material is pulled into the gap → large rollover radius, tapering, inaccurate size.

**Step 3 — Empirical Optimum:**

The KTU-adopted empirical rule (derived from extensive industrial data for **mild steel** and **aluminum**):

$$
\boxed{c_{opt} = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \times t}
$$

**Step 4 — Per-Side vs. Total Clearance:**

- **Per-side clearance:** $c = 0.05t$ to $0.10t$
- **Total (diametral) clearance:** $2c = 0.10t$ to $0.20t$

**Step 5 — Worked Example:**

For $t = 2$ mm mild steel:

$$
c = 0.075 \times 2 = 0.15 \text{ mm per side}
$$

$$
\text{Total clearance} = 0.30 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore, the die opening for a 30 mm blank becomes:

$$
D_{die} = D_{punch} + 2c = 30.00 + 0.30 = 30.30 \text{ mm}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Anatomy of a Progressive Press Tool

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Coil / Sheet Stock In] --> B[Uncoiler and Straightener]
    B --> C[Stock Lubrication Unit]
    C --> D[Progressive Die Feed Mechanism]
    D --> E[Station 1 - Pilot and Pierce Holes]
    E --> F[Station 2 - Partial Blank Notch]
    F --> G[Station 3 - Final Blanking]
    G --> H[Stripper Plate Lift Mechanism]
    H --> I[Finished Part Ejection]
    I --> J[Scrap Chute and Collection]
    H --> K[Scrap Strip Recoiler]
    K --> L[Scrap Bailing Unit]

    subgraph UpperHalf["Upper Die Half - Ram Mounted"]
        M[Punch Plate] --> N[Punch 1 - Piercing]
        M --> O[Punch 2 - Notching]
        M --> P[Punch 3 - Blanking]
    end

    subgraph LowerHalf["Lower Die Half - Bolster Mounted"]
        Q[Die Set Shoe] --> R[Die Block - Station 1]
        Q --> S[Die Block - Station 2]
        Q --> T[Die Block - Station 3]
        Q --> U[Stripper Plate]
        Q --> V[Guide Pillars and Bushings]
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Classification Hierarchy of Press Tools

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Press Tools] --> B[Cutting Dies]
    A --> C[Forming Dies]
    A --> D[Compound and Combination Dies]

    B --> B1[Blanking Die]
    B --> B2[Piercing Die]
    B --> B3[Trimming Die]
    B --> B4[Notching Die]
    B --> B5[Shaving Die]
    B --> B6[Lancing Die]

    C --> C1[Bending Die]
    C --> C2[Drawing Die]
    C --> C3[Embossing Die]
    C --> C4[Coining Die]
    C --> C5[Flanging Die]

    D --> D1[Compound Die - Multiple Cuts Same Station]
    D --> D2[Progressive Die - Sequential Stations]
    D --> D3[Combination Die - Cut and Form Together]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Material Flow Through a Press Operation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Press Cycle Start] --> B[Ram Descends]
    B --> C[Punch Contacts Stock]
    C --> D[Material Elastically Deforms]
    D --> E[Plastic Flow Begins]
    E --> F[Fracture Initiates on Punch Side]
    F --> G[Fracture Propagates to Die Edge]
    G --> H[Clean Shear Complete]
    H --> I[Ram Reaches Bottom Dead Center]
    I --> J[Ram Ascends]
    J --> K[Stripper Plate Ejects Part and Scrap]
    K --> L[Stock Advances to Next Station]
    L --> M[Cycle Repeats]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** In all Mermaid diagrams above, the **upper die half** is mounted to the **press ram** and moves vertically. The **lower die half** is bolted to the **press bolster** and is stationary. The **stripper plate** is the only moving member in the lower half — it lifts momentarily to push the part off the punch.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between a "Simple Die" and a "Compound Die" with one suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Simple Die** performs only **one operation per stroke** at a single station. For example, a simple blanking die that produces a flat washer blank without the central hole — the hole is pierced in a separate operation.

A **Compound Die** performs **two or more cutting operations in a single stroke at the same station**. For example, a compound die that blanks the outer contour and pierces the inner hole of a finished washer in **one press stroke**. The stock is advanced and held in the same position while both punches descend together.

| **Feature** | **Simple Die** | **Compound Die** |
|---|---|---|
| Operations per stroke | One | Two or more |
| Tooling cost | Low | High |
| Part accuracy | Moderate | High (concentricity excellent) |
| Setup complexity | Simple | Complex |

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define "Die Clearance" and state its significance in blanking operations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Die clearance** is the **radial gap per side** between the punch and the die block at the cutting edge, measured perpendicular to the cutting direction.

**Significance:**
1. **Too little clearance** → secondary shear, increased punch load, burred edges, rapid tool wear.
2. **Too much clearance** → large rollover radius, excessive taper on the cut edge, inaccurate part dimensions, material pulled into the gap.
3. **Optimum clearance** → clean fracture, vertical cut surface, accurate size, maximum tool life.

For mild steel, the empirical optimum is:

$$
c_{opt} = 5\% \text{ to } 10\% \text{ of stock thickness (per side)}
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q: (a) Classify the different types of press tools with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Press tools are classified as follows:

**1. Simple Die (Single-Operation Die):**
Performs one operation per stroke. Used for low-volume production or preliminary operations.

**2. Compound Die:**
Performs multiple cutting operations at the **same station** in a single stroke. Example: blanking + piercing of a washer. The slug falls through the die; the part is stripped upward. Achieves excellent concentricity.

**3. Progressive Die (Follow Die):**
The strip moves through **sequential stations**, and each station performs one operation. After the final station, a finished part is separated. Used for high-volume complex parts.

**4. Combination Die:**
Performs **both cutting and forming operations** in a single stroke. Example: blanking a flat blank and drawing it into a cup shape in one press cycle.

| **Die Type** | **Operations** | **Stock State** | **Volume** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple | 1 / stroke | Strip | Low |
| Compound | 2+ / stroke (same station) | Strip | Medium |
| Progressive | Multiple / stroke (different stations) | Indexed strip | High |
| Combination | Cut + Form / stroke | Strip | Medium-High |

**(Reference: Refer to the Mermaid concept map in Section 4.2 for the complete hierarchy.)**
