---

**Q: (b) A circular blank of 80 mm diameter is to be produced from a 3 mm thick mild-steel strip by blanking. Calculate: (i) the blanking force, (ii) the stripping force, and (iii) the press tonnage required. Take shear strength = 350 N/mm² and overload factor = 1.25. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Blank diameter $D = 80$ mm
- Stock thickness $t = 3$ mm
- Shear strength $\tau = 350$ N/mm²
- Overload factor $k = 1.25$

**Step 1 — Compute the Shear Perimeter:**

$$
L = \pi \cdot D = \pi \times 80 = 251.33 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Blanking Force:**

$$
F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau
$$

$$
F = 251.33 \times 3 \times 350
$$

$$
F = 263{,}896.5 \text{ N} \approx 263.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Stripping Force:**

Assuming $q = 0.05 \times \tau = 0.05 \times 350 = 17.5$ N/mm²:

$$
F_s = L \cdot t \cdot q
$$

$$
F_s = 251.33 \times 3 \times 17.5
$$

$$
F_s = 13{,}194.8 \text{ N} \approx 13.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Press Tonnage:**

$$
F_{press} = k \cdot F = 1.25 \times 263{,}896.5
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{press} = 329{,}870.6 \text{ N} \approx 329.9 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Shear Perimeter $L$ | 251.33 mm |
| Blanking Force $F$ | 263.9 kN |
| Stripping Force $F_s$ | 13.2 kN |
| Required Press Tonnage | 329.9 kN |

---

#### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: (a) Explain the various components of a typical press tool with a neat labelled sketch. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major components of a press tool are:

**Upper Half (Ram-Mounted):**
1. **Punch Plate / Punch Holder:** Carries the punch; bolted to the press ram shank.
2. **Punch:** The male cutting/forming member; typically made of high-carbon high-chromium tool steel.
3. **Backing Plate:** Supports the punch shank against bending loads.
4. **Stripper Plate (Upper):** Pushes the workpiece off the punch on the up-stroke.
5. **Guide Pins (Upper half of bushes):** Mate with the lower bushings for alignment.

**Lower Half (Bolster-Mounted):**
6. **Die Set Shoe:** The rigid base that holds all lower components in precise alignment.
7. **Die Block / Matrix:** Contains the cutting cavity; the female member.
8. **Bolster Plate:** Mounted to the press bed; absorbs the reaction loads.
9. **Stripper Plate (Lower):** Locates the strip and ejects scrap.
10. **Stop / Pilot Pin:** Locates the strip index for the next station.
11. **Ejector / Knockout:** Pushes the finished part out of the die cavity.

**Functions Summary Table:**

| **Component** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| Punch | Cuts / forms the material |
| Die Block | Cavity supporting material during cut/form |
| Stripper Plate | Removes part/scrap from punch |
| Die Set | Aligns upper and lower halves |
| Guide Pillars/Bushes | Provide concentric alignment |
| Bolster Plate | Distributes load to press bed |

**(Reference: Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for spatial layout.)**

---

**Q: (b) A 50 mm × 50 mm square washer with a 20 mm diameter central hole is to be blanked from a 1.5 mm thick aluminum strip. The shear strength of aluminum is 110 N/mm². Determine: (i) the optimum die clearance per side, (ii) the blanking force, and (iii) the press tonnage using a factor of safety of 1.3. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Square outer side $a = 50$ mm
- Central hole diameter $d = 20$ mm
- Stock thickness $t = 1.5$ mm
- Shear strength $\tau = 110$ N/mm²
- Factor of safety $k = 1.3$

**Step 1 — Compute the Total Shear Perimeter (Outer Square + Inner Circle):**

$$
L = (4 \times a) + (\pi \times d)
$$

$$
L = (4 \times 50) + (\pi \times 20) = 200 + 62.83
$$

$$
L = 262.83 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Optimum Die Clearance:**

For aluminum, using 6% of thickness (aluminum typically requires clearance on the higher end of the range):

$$
c = 0.06 \times t = 0.06 \times 1.5 = 0.09 \text{ mm per side}
$$

$$
\boxed{c = 0.09 \text{ mm per side}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Blanking Force:**

$$
F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau
$$

$$
F = 262.83 \times 1.5 \times 110
$$

$$
F = 43{,}366.95 \text{ N} \approx 43.37 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 43.37 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Press Tonnage:**

$$
F_{press} = k \cdot F = 1.3 \times 43{,}366.95
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{press} \approx 56{,}377 \text{ N} \approx 56.38 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Shear Perimeter $L$ | 262.83 mm |
| Optimum Clearance $c$ | 0.09 mm/side |
| Blanking Force $F$ | 43.37 kN |
| Required Press Tonnage | 56.38 kN |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Module 4 — Introduction**
>
> **The TOP 3 mark-losing mistakes in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add the inner perimeter** in the shear-length calculation. A washer, a bracket with a hole, or a blank with a slot has TWO contours. Examiners will deduct up to **2 marks** for missing this.
>
> 2. **Confusing "per side" with "diametral / total" clearance.** When the problem asks for "die clearance," and you answer $0.09$ mm/side, you should also mention $2c = 0.18$ mm as the diametral clearance. The die opening size is calculated as $D_{die} = D_{punch} + 2c$.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong shear strength value for the material.** Mild steel ≈ 350–400 N/mm², aluminum ≈ 100–110 N/mm², stainless steel ≈ 450–550 N/mm². Examiners do NOT give partial credit if you apply a steel value to an aluminum problem.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 Introduction**

- [ ] **Press Tool = Punch + Die Block + Press Frame + Stripper + Die Set + Guides.**
- [ ] **Two super-categories:** Cutting (shearing) operations vs. Forming operations.
- [ ] **Four die types:** Simple, Compound, Progressive, Combination — *distinguish by "operations per stroke" and "number of stations."*
- [ ] **Master formula:** $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ (blanking / piercing).
- [ ] **Stripping force formula:** $F_s = L \cdot t \cdot q$ where $q \approx 0.05 \tau$.
- [ ] **Press tonnage rule:** Always apply a 1.2 to 1.3 safety / overload factor.
- [ ] **Optimum die clearance:** 5–10% of stock thickness per side.
- [ ] **Punch penetration depth:** $h = (1/3)t$ to $(1/2)t$ for clean shearing.
- [ ] **Always include the inner perimeter** when the part has holes.
- [ ] **Material memory values:** Mild steel $\tau \approx 400$ N/mm², Aluminum $\tau \approx 110$ N/mm².
- [ ] **Compound die ⇒ excellent concentricity** (favourite KTU viva question).
- [ ] **Progressive die ⇒ high volume, indexed strip** (distinguish from compound clearly).
- [ ] **Combination die ⇒ cut + form together** (favourite KTU short-answer question).
- [ ] **Die-set selection logic:** Die set capacity ≥ 1.5 × peak cutting force.
- [ ] **Tool material memory:** Working parts at HRC 58–62 after heat treatment.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip for Module 4 Introduction:** If you can correctly classify any of the four die types AND execute the $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ calculation with the **inner perimeter included**, you will comfortably clear the 14-mark Part-B question on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic DFE Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic DFE Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is DFE? — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Environment (DFE)**, also called **Eco-Design** or **Green Design**, is a systematic design philosophy that seeks to reduce the **overall environmental burden** of a product throughout its **entire life cycle** — from raw material extraction, manufacturing, and use-phase, to end-of-life recovery, recycling, or disposal — while preserving (or improving) its functional performance, quality, cost, and safety.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEMRT522 – Design of Manufacture (Module 4)**, DFE is treated as a *proactive* environmental strategy, where environmental considerations are **integrated at the conceptual design stage itself**, rather than being retrofitted after the product is engineered. It is one of the core pillars of the broader **Design for X (DfX)** family.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Note (2024 Scheme):** In KTU question papers, DFE is often quoted under the umbrella of "Sustainable Product Design" or "Environmentally Conscious Manufacturing (ECM)." Students must remember all three terms refer to the *same* concept.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The House Builder's Dilemma"

Imagine two builders constructing two identical houses. **Builder A** only worries about how beautiful and cheap the house is *today*. He uses toxic paints, mixed plastics in the wiring, and laminated boards that cannot be separated. Twenty years later, when the house must be demolished, the rubble is hazardous waste that poisons the local groundwater for decades.

**Builder B** (the DFE builder) thinks about the *entire life* of the house from Day 1. He picks **materials that can later be recycled**, designs the **plumbing and electrical lines to be easily removed** (so copper and wires can be salvaged), chooses **solar-ready roofing** to minimize operational energy, and uses **water-based paints**. The same house, twenty years later, becomes a *bank of materials* rather than a *mountain of waste*.

> **Key Insight:** DFE does **not** mean "making a worse product." It means designing today's product so that tomorrow's waste becomes *tomorrow's resource*. The function remains the same; the footprint shrinks.

## 1.3 Why DFE Matters — The Triple Bottom Line

DFE is governed by **Elkington's Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework, which is heavily referenced in KTU Module 4:

| Pillar | Expansion | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **People** | Social Responsibility | Worker safety during manufacturing & recycling |
| **Planet** | Environmental Integrity | Reduced emissions, toxicity, and resource depletion |
| **Profit** | Economic Viability | Lower material cost, energy savings, compliance benefits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Any DFE strategy that improves the *Planet* pillar but breaks the *Profit* pillar is generally **not considered a sustainable DFE solution** in board examination answers. A complete answer must address all three pillars.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Basic DFE Methodology

For a B.Tech student tackling Module 4, the "Basic DFE Methods" cluster into **three fundamental pillars**:

1. **Design for Disassembly (DFD)** — designing the product so it can be taken apart efficiently at end-of-life.
2. **Design for Recycling (DFR)** — selecting materials and fastening methods that enable high-purity material recovery.
3. **Design for Energy Efficiency / Waste Minimization (DFE² / DFW)** — reducing energy and material waste during manufacturing and use-phase.

These will be expanded in Section 2. The next section also introduces the **8-R Framework** of eco-design, which is a direct KTU Module 4 syllabus element.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8-R Strategy Hierarchy (KTU Module 4 Anchor Concept)

The **8-R Hierarchy** is a *priority order* used in DFE to rank environmental interventions. In KTU exams, this is one of the **most frequently asked** 3-mark questions. The hierarchy is given in the order from *most* environmentally preferred to *least* preferred:

1. **Refuse** — Reject the product concept entirely if it offers no real value.
2. **Rethink** — Re-design the function (e.g., sell a *service* instead of a product).
3. **Reduce** — Use less material / less energy per unit function.
4. **Reuse** — Re-deploy the product for the same function again.
5. **Repair** — Fix faulty products to extend their life.
6. **Refurbish** — Restore an old product to a "like-new" condition.
7. **Remanufacture** — Disassemble, clean, replace worn parts, and reassemble to specification.
8. **Recycle** — Recover the raw material for re-processing into new feedstock.
9. *(Last resort)* **Recover energy** — Incineration with energy capture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a hierarchy?** Recycling still consumes energy and produces some emissions. Refusing a harmful product is the cleanest option. The hierarchy guides the designer to choose the *highest-ranked* option that is technically and economically feasible.

## 2.2 Design for Disassembly (DFD) — Detailed Logic

DFD is the backbone of end-of-life DFE. A product that *cannot* be disassembled cannot be recycled, refurbished, or remanufactured. KTU Module 4 expects students to know the DFD *methodology* in steps.

### Step-by-Step Logic of DFD Implementation

1. **Identify Disassembly Operations Early:** List every joint, fastener, and connection during the *concept* stage — not after the CAD model is frozen.
2. **Minimize the Number of Parts (Boothroyd-Dewhurst Principle):** Each part that can be eliminated reduces a future disassembly action, a fastener, a tooling step, and a recycling stream.
3. **Standardize Fasteners:** Use *one* type of fastener (e.g., M6 hex bolts) instead of mixing 5 types. This reduces tool-change time during both assembly and disassembly.
4. **Use Modular Architecture:** Group components into **modules** (e.g., power module, sensor module, housing module) so that a faulty module can be swapped without disturbing the rest of the product.
5. **Eliminate Permanent Joints:** Avoid welding, adhesive bonding, and rivets for parts that must be recovered. Use **snap-fits**, **threaded fasteners**, or **quick-release clamps**.
6. **Provide Disassembly Access:** Ensure the geometry allows tool entry. A screw hidden behind a permanently bonded cover is a DFD failure.
7. **Label Materials:** Laser-mark or stamp plastic parts with their resin codes (e.g., >ABS<, >PP<) to enable clean recycling streams.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "SAME-MAID":** For DFD remember **S**nap-fits, **A**ccess holes, **M**odular layout, **E**asy fasteners — **MAID** = *Make Assembly Inverse in Design*. Disassembly should be the *mirror image* of assembly.

### The Disassembly Time Formula (KTU 2024 Numerical)

The standard formula used to estimate disassembly effort is:

$$T_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( t_{p,i} + t_{t,i} + t_{m,i} \right)$$

Where:
- $T_d$ = **Total disassembly time** (seconds)
- $n$ = Total number of parts to be removed
- $t_{p,i}$ = **Positioning time** for tool on part $i$ (s)
- $t_{t,i}$ = **Tool action time** (loosening, cutting, etc.) for part $i$ (s)
- $t_{m,i}$ = **Maneuver / handling time** to place the part aside (s)

## 2.3 Design for Recycling (DFR) — Detailed Logic

Recycling is the **8th in the 8-R hierarchy** (so it is desirable but not the best). DFR aims to ensure that when a product *does* enter the recycling stream, it does so cleanly.

### KTU High-Yield DFR Guidelines (Exam-Ready Bullets)

- **Material Homogeneity:** Use a *single* type of plastic for the housing rather than a co-molded blend. Mixed plastics cannot be separated economically → they go to landfill.
- **Avoid Incompatible Material Combinations:** Aluminum screws in a steel body cause **galvanic corrosion** and contaminate the steel recycling stream.
- **Eliminate Hazardous Substances:** Comply with **RoHS** (Restriction of Hazardous Substances) and **REACH** regulations — no lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium.
- **Surface Treatment Compatibility:** A chrome-plated steel component is recyclable; a chrome-plated plastic component is generally *not*.
- **Minimize Paint and Coatings:** Each coating must be stripped before recycling, costing energy and chemicals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Keyword Alert:** The phrase **"Design for X"** in Module 4 is a *family* of methods. Common ones examined: DFD, DFR, DFA (Assembly), DFM (Manufacturing), DFE (Environment), DFL (Logistics), DFS (Service). Students should know at least the first four.

## 2.4 Design for Energy Efficiency (DFE²)

A product's **use-phase energy** often dominates its *lifecycle energy footprint* (e.g., a refrigerator uses 90% of its lifetime energy *after* it leaves the factory). The KTU 2024 syllabus therefore distinguishes:

- **Embodied Energy** — energy used to extract raw materials and manufacture.
- **Operational Energy** — energy used during the product's working life.

### The Life-Cycle Energy Equation (KTU Numerical Favorite)

$$E_{LC} = E_{mat} + E_{mfg} + E_{trans,in} + E_{use} + E_{maint} + E_{EOL}$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{LC}$ | Total life-cycle energy | **MJ** |
| $E_{mat}$ | Material extraction & processing energy | MJ |
| $E_{mfg}$ | Manufacturing energy | MJ |
| $E_{trans,in}$ | Inbound transport energy | MJ |
| $E_{use}$ | Use-phase (operational) energy | MJ |
| $E_{maint}$ | Maintenance energy over lifetime | MJ |
| $E_{EOL}$ | End-of-life processing energy | MJ |

The **DFE design goal** is to minimize $E_{LC}$ — note that for most consumer durables, $E_{use} \gg E_{mat} + E_{mfg}$.

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Consolidated)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Tip:** The following table contains every formula a KTU 2024 student is expected to be able to *write from memory* for Basic DFE Methods. Memorize the symbols and units first; derivations will follow in Section 3.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total disassembly time | $T_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{p,i} + t_{t,i} + t_{m,i})$ | Quantifies disassembly effort for DFD |
| 2 | Life-cycle energy | $E_{LC} = E_{mat} + E_{mfg} + E_{trans,in} + E_{use} + E_{maint} + E_{EOL}$ | Selects low-impact design alternatives |
| 3 | Material recovery fraction | $R_f = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Measures DFR effectiveness (in **%**) |
| 4 | Recyclability index (KTU standard) | $RI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{m_i}{M} \cdot R_i \right)$ | Weighted recyclability score (**0 to 1**) |
| 5 | Disassembly index | $DI = \dfrac{n_{recoverable}}{n_{total}}$ | Fraction of parts recoverable (**0 to 1**) |
| 6 | Embodied energy per kg | $E_{emb} = \dfrac{E_{mat}}{m_{part}}$ | Compares materials (in **MJ/kg**) |
| 7 | Energy saved by recycling | $\Delta E = E_{virgin} - E_{recycled}$ | Justifies DFR economics (in **MJ**) |
| 8 | Carbon footprint reduction | $\Delta C = C_{baseline} - C_{DFE}$ | Quantifies CO₂ savings (in **kg CO₂-eq**) |
| 9 | Boothsroyd part count target | $N_{min}$ from function analysis | Reduces DFD / DFA complexity |
| 10 | Use-phase energy dominance | $E_{use} \gg E_{mat} + E_{mfg}$ | Prioritizes DFE² over DFR for durables |

> **Real-World Utility:** In industry, RI and DI are computed during the **concept stage** using software tools like *Solidworks Sustainability*, *ANSYS Granta MI*, or *SimaPro LCA software*. They feed directly into the product's **Environmental Product Declaration (EPD)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Basic DFE Methods

The following matrix is a *high-density comparison* the student can use for any KTU 14-mark analytical question. Memorize the contrasting pairs.

| DFE Method | Primary Goal | Best Used For | Key Metric | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DFD** (Disassembly) | Enable end-of-life separation | Electronics, automotive | $DI$ | Adds fasteners / access features |
| **DFR** (Recycling) | Material recovery | Packaging, polymers | $RI$ | Quality loss (downcycling) |
| **DFE²** (Energy) | Reduce $E_{use}$ | Appliances, motors | $E_{LC}$ | May require new technology |
| **DFRemanuf.** (Remanufacture) | Reuse cores | Engines, gearboxes | Core return rate | Logistics complexity |
| **DFL** (Longevity) | Extend life | Tools, capital goods | MTBF / MTTR | Slows new product sales |
| **DFW** (Waste min.) | Reduce scrap in mfg. | Sheet metal, machining | Yield % | Process change cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Cue:** A 14-mark KTU question often asks: *"Compare DFD and DFR with examples."* Use the matrix above as the *backbone* of the answer, then elaborate with a real product (e.g., a smartphone for DFD, a PET bottle for DFR).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Computing the Disassembly Time $T_d$

This is a **frequently asked KTU numerical** (3 to 7 marks). The derivation below shows every algebraic step — *no shortcuts*.

### Problem Setup
A product has $n = 4$ parts to be removed. The per-part time data is given in the table:

| Part $i$ | $t_{p,i}$ (s) | $t_{t,i}$ (s) | $t_{m,i}$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 6 | 3 |
| 2 | 5 | 8 | 4 |
| 3 | 3 | 5 | 2 |
| 4 | 6 | 10 | 5 |

### Step 1 — Write the General Formula

$$T_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( t_{p,i} + t_{t,i} + t_{m,i} \right)$$

This is the *definition* of total disassembly time — sum the (positioning + tool action + maneuver) times for every part.

### Step 2 — Expand the Summation

$$T_d = (t_{p,1} + t_{t,1} + t_{m,1}) + (t_{p,2} + t_{t,2} + t_{m,2}) + (t_{p,3} + t_{t,3} + t_{m,3}) + (t_{p,4} + t_{t,4} + t_{m,4})$$

Substitute the table values (in **seconds**):

$$T_d = (4 + 6 + 3) + (5 + 8 + 4) + (3 + 5 + 2) + (6 + 10 + 5)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Inside of Each Bracket

- Part 1: $4 + 6 + 3 = 13$ s
- Part 2: $5 + 8 + 4 = 17$ s
- Part 3: $3 + 5 + 2 = 10$ s
- Part 4: $6 + 10 + 5 = 21$ s

### Step 4 — Sum All Four Sub-Totals

$$T_d = 13 + 17 + 10 + 21$$

Add the first pair:

$$T_d = 30 + 10 + 21$$

Add again:

$$T_d = 40 + 21$$

Final result:

$$T_d = 61 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 5 — Convert and Interpret

$$T_d = 61 \text{ s} \approx 1.02 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A DFD-compliant product should aim to minimize this $T_d$ value. The designer can iterate by trying different fasteners (e.g., snap-fits reduce $t_{t,i}$) and improved access (reduces $t_{p,i}$).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Recyclability Index $RI$

### Problem Setup
A product has a total mass $M = 5$ kg, comprising three parts with the following recyclable content:

| Part $i$ | Mass $m_i$ (kg) | Recyclability fraction $R_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Steel body) | 3.0 | 0.95 |
| 2 (Plastic cover) | 1.5 | 0.60 |
| 3 (Electronic PCB) | 0.5 | 0.30 |

### Step 1 — Write the RI Formula

$$RI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{m_i}{M} \cdot R_i \right)$$

This is a *mass-weighted average* of the per-part recyclability fractions. $RI$ ranges from **0** (nothing recyclable) to **1** (fully recyclable).

### Step 2 — Compute the Mass Fractions

- Part 1: $m_1 / M = 3.0 / 5.0 = 0.6$
- Part 2: $m_2 / M = 1.5 / 5.0 = 0.3$
- Part 3: $m_3 / M = 0.5 / 5.0 = 0.1$

### Step 3 — Multiply Mass Fraction by $R_i$

- Part 1: $0.6 \times 0.95 = 0.570$
- Part 2: $0.3 \times 0.60 = 0.180$
- Part 3: $0.1 \times 0.30 = 0.030$

### Step 4 — Sum the Three Contributions

$$RI = 0.570 + 0.180 + 0.030$$

$$RI = 0.780$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Percentage (Optional but KTU-Preferred)

$$RI = 0.780 \times 100\% = 78.0\%$$

> **Interpretation:** A value of $78\%$ means **78% of the product's mass can be theoretically recycled** if proper DFR / DFD infrastructure exists. To push this higher, the designer could:
> 1. Replace the plastic cover ($R = 0.6$) with a mono-material polypropylene ($R \approx 0.9$).
> 2. Redesign the PCB to be lead-free and use easily separable solder ($R \to 0.7$).

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Life-Cycle Energy Comparison

### Problem Setup
Compare two design alternatives for a household electric kettle (assumed life = 5 years):

| Stage | Baseline $E$ (MJ) | DFE Design $E$ (MJ) |
|---|---|---|
| Material $E_{mat}$ | 80 | 70 |
| Manufacturing $E_{mfg}$ | 40 | 35 |
| Inbound transport $E_{trans,in}$ | 20 | 18 |
| Use-phase $E_{use}$ | 1200 | 900 |
| Maintenance $E_{maint}$ | 30 | 25 |
| End-of-life $E_{EOL}$ | 10 | 8 |

### Step 1 — Apply the $E_{LC}$ Formula to Baseline

$$E_{LC,base} = 80 + 40 + 20 + 1200 + 30 + 10$$

Add in pairs:

$$E_{LC,base} = 120 + 1220 + 40$$

$$E_{LC,base} = 1340 + 40 = 1380 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the $E_{LC}$ Formula to DFE Design

$$E_{LC,DFE} = 70 + 35 + 18 + 900 + 25 + 8$$

$$E_{LC,DFE} = 105 + 918 + 33$$

$$E_{LC,DFE} = 1023 + 33 = 1056 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Energy Savings $\Delta E$

$$\Delta E = E_{LC,base} - E_{LC,DFE}$$

$$\Delta E = 1380 - 1056 = 324 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Percentage Reduction

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{LC,base}} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{324}{1380} \times 100\% \approx 23.48\%$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The use-phase savings (300 MJ from $1200 \to 900$) dominate the gain. The DFE design choice of a *more efficient heating element* is **far more impactful** than material substitution, confirming the KTU principle that $E_{use}$ dominates the life-cycle energy for electrical appliances.

## 3.5 Combined Analytical Strategy — When to Use What

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Logic for a KTU 14-Mark Question:**
> 1. If the product is **electronic / has fasteners** → start with **DFD** analysis.
> 2. If the product is **polymer-dominant** → start with **DFR** and $RI$.
> 3. If the product **consumes energy in use** → start with **DFE²** and $E_{LC}$.
> 4. Always end with the **8-R hierarchy** as the philosophical anchor of your answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The DFE Methodology Workflow

This block diagram maps the *sequence of design decisions* a designer follows when applying basic DFE methods. It is the most common KTU Module 4 diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Function Brief] --> B[Concept Generation]
    B --> C[Apply 8R Hierarchy Filter]
    C --> D{Use-phase energy dominant?}
    D -->|Yes| E[DFE Squared - Energy Efficiency]
    D -->|No| F{Material is polymer?}
    F -->|Yes| G[DFR - Material Selection]
    F -->|No| H{Many fasteners present?}
    H -->|Yes| I[DFD - Disassembly Design]
    H -->|No| J[DFW - Waste Minimization]
    E --> K[Compute ELC and RI]
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Meets Triple Bottom Line?}
    L -->|No| M[Revise Concept and Re loop]
    M --> B
    L -->|Yes| N[Final Eco Design Output]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every product concept passes through the **8-R filter** (Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → … → Recycle). Then it is *triaged* into the appropriate specialist DFE method (energy, material, disassembly, or waste), the **metrics** ($E_{LC}$, $RI$, $DI$) are computed, and the result is validated against the **Triple Bottom Line** (People–Planet–Profit).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Product Life-Cycle Energy Flow

The following block diagram shows how energy is *added* to a product at each stage of its life — the very basis of the $E_{LC}$ equation from Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Raw Material Extraction] -->|E mat| node1[Material Energy]
    node1 -->|E mfg| node2[Manufacturing Energy]
    node2 -->|E trans in| node3[Transport to User]
    node3 -->|E use| node4[Use Phase Energy]
    node4 -->|E maint| node5[Maintenance Energy]
    node5 -->|E EOL| node6[End of Life]
    node6 -->|Recovered| node7[Recycling Loop Back to src1]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Energy *accumulates* as the product moves from raw extraction (left) to use phase (middle) to end-of-life (right). The dashed loop from $E_{EOL}$ back to "Raw Material Extraction" represents the **closing of the material loop** through recycling — the central goal of DFR.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The 8-R Strategy Hierarchy (Vertical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r1[Refuse] --> r2[Rethink]
    r2 --> r3[Reduce]
    r3 --> r4[Reuse]
    r4 --> r5[Repair]
    r5 --> r6[Refurbish]
    r6 --> r7[Remanufacture]
    r7 --> r8[Recycle]
    r8 --> r9[Recover Energy]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows go from the *most* environmentally preferred (Refuse) to the *least* preferred (Recover Energy). In a KTU 14-mark question on the 8-R framework, draw this diagram and then add a **2-line example** for each R using a familiar product (e.g., a mobile phone, an electric iron, a car).

## 4.4 Block Diagram — DFD Decision Tree for Fastener Selection

A common KTU sub-question is *"How do you choose fasteners for a DFD-compliant product?"* The decision tree below answers this:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    f1[Fastener Required] --> f2{Joint Permanent?}
    f2 -->|Yes - non recoverable| f3[REJECT for DFD compliant parts]
    f2 -->|No - recoverable| f4{Repeated disassembly needed?}
    f4 -->|Yes| f5[Use Threaded Bolts or Screws]
    f4 -->|No| f6[Use Snap Fits or Quick Release Clips]
    f5 --> f7{Number of fastener types}
    f6 --> f7
    f7 -->|More than 2| f8[STANDARDIZE to one family]
    f7 -->|1 or 2| f9[DFD Approved Fastener Set]
    f8 --> f9
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The *first* question is whether the joint is *permanent* (weld, glue, rivet) — if yes and the part is meant to be recovered, the design is **rejected** for DFD. Recoverable joints branch into two paths based on disassembly frequency, and both paths converge on a **standardization** check.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Triple Bottom Line Validation of a DFE Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Proposed DFE Design] --> B{People - Worker and User Safe?}
    B -->|No| X1[Revise]
    B -->|Yes| C{Planet - Lower LCA Impact?}
    C -->|No| X1
    C -->|Yes| D{Profit - Economically Viable?}
    D -->|No| X1
    D -->|Yes| E[Approved Sustainable DFE Solution]
    X1 --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A DFE design must pass *all three* gates — People, Planet, Profit — to be considered a true sustainable solution. Failure at *any* gate loops the design back for revision.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define **Design for Environment (DFE)**. List any **four** guidelines of DFE.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** DFE is a systematic design approach that aims to reduce the overall environmental impact of a product throughout its entire life cycle — from raw material extraction to end-of-life disposal — while maintaining its function, quality, cost, and safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Any four guidelines (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Select low-impact materials** — choose recyclable, renewable, or biodegradable inputs.
> 2. **Minimize material usage** — apply the "reduce" principle of the 8-R hierarchy.
> 3. **Design for disassembly (DFD)** — use snap-fits and standard fasteners to enable end-of-life separation.
> 4. **Optimize manufacturing processes** — reduce scrap, energy, and emissions.
> 5. **Design for energy efficiency** — minimize the use-phase energy $E_{use}$.
> 6. **Extend product life** — enable repair, refurbishment, and remanufacture.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** State the **8-R hierarchy** of eco-design. Why is "Refuse" placed at the top?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy in order (2 marks):** Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Refurbish → Remanufacture → Recycle (with "Recover Energy" as the last resort).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reason for "Refuse" at top (1 mark):** Refusing a product concept entirely eliminates *all* future environmental burden associated with it (no material extraction, no manufacturing, no disposal). Since prevention is more effective than any form of end-of-life treatment, it is ranked first.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Explain the **basic methods of Design for Environment (DFE)** in detail. Discuss **Design for Disassembly (DFD)** and **Design for Recycling (DFR)** with suitable examples.

#### Part (a) — Overview of Basic DFE Methods (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Introduction (2 marks):** DFE is a holistic design philosophy that integrates environmental considerations into every stage of the product life cycle. The *basic* DFE methods include:
> - Design for Disassembly (DFD)
> - Design for Recycling (DFR)
> - Design for Energy Efficiency (DFE²)
> - Design for Remanufacturing
> - Design for Waste Minimization (DFW)

> [!NOTE]
> **8-R Hierarchy (2 marks):** These methods operationalize the 8-R strategy: Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Refurbish, Remanufacture, Recycle, with Recover Energy as a final option.

> [!NOTE]
> **Triple Bottom Line (1 mark):** All DFE methods must satisfy Elkington's TBL — People, Planet, Profit — to be considered sustainable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Life-Cycle Perspective (1 mark):** DFE considers the full life cycle:
> $$E_{LC} = E_{mat} + E_{mfg} + E_{trans,in} + E_{use} + E_{maint} + E_{EOL}$$
> The dominant energy term (often $E_{use}$) should be the focus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Introduction (1 mark):** A smartphone is a classic DFE test case: it has fasteners (DFD), mixed polymers (DFR), battery energy (DFE²), and a short life cycle (DFRemanu.).

#### Part (b) — DFD and DFR Explained (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **DFD Definition (1 mark):** Design for Disassembly is the practice of designing a product so that it can be efficiently taken apart at the end of its useful life for the purpose of recycling, refurbishment, remanufacturing, or maintenance.

> [!NOTE]
> **DFD Implementation Guidelines (2 marks — list 4):**
> 1. Minimize the number of parts using Boothroyd-Dewhurst analysis.
> 2. Standardize to a single fastener family (e.g., M3 screws).
> 3. Use snap-fits and quick-release clips instead of permanent bonds.
> 4. Provide tool access holes and clear disassembly paths.
> 5. Modularize the architecture into independently swappable blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **DFD Time Metric (1 mark):**
> $$T_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( t_{p,i} + t_{t,i} + t_{m,i} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **DFR Definition (1 mark):** Design for Recycling ensures that a product, when it reaches end-of-life, can be efficiently broken down into clean, recyclable material streams.

> [!NOTE]
> **DFR Guidelines (1.5 marks — list 3):**
> 1. Use a *single* polymer family for the housing (e.g., only polypropylene).
> 2. Eliminate hazardous substances per **RoHS** and **REACH**.
> 3. Mark resin codes on plastic parts (e.g., >ABS<).
> 4. Avoid combining metals that cause galvanic corrosion.

> [!NOTE]
> **DFR Metric (0.5 marks):**
> $$RI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{m_i}{M} \cdot R_i \right)$$
> RI ranges from 0 to 1; higher is better.

#### Worked Sub-Question (KTU 2024 Numerical Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical (Bonus, may appear):** A 4 kg product has 2.5 kg steel ($R = 0.95$), 1.0 kg ABS ($R = 0.70$), 0.5 kg PCB ($R = 0.25$). Compute the recyclability index.
>
> **Solution:**
> $$RI = (2.5/4)(0.95) + (1.0/4)(0.70) + (0.5/4)(0.25)$$
> $$RI = 0.625 \times 0.95 + 0.25 \times 0.70 + 0.125 \times 0.25$$
> $$RI = 0.5938 + 0.1750 + 0.0313 = 0.800$$
> $$\boxed{RI = 80.0\%}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Discuss the **Design for Energy Efficiency (DFE²)** approach. With the help of a **suitable numerical example**, compute the life-cycle energy of a product under baseline and DFE-redesigned conditions. Comment on the energy savings.

#### Part (a) — Theory of DFE² (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** DFE² is the DFE method that targets reduction of energy consumption over the product's life cycle, with particular focus on the **use phase** since $E_{use}$ often dominates $E_{LC}$ for electrical and mechanical durables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Energy Categories (2 marks):**
> - **Embodied Energy** ($E_{mat} + E_{mfg}$): The energy "stored" in the materials and manufacturing.
> - **Operational Energy** ($E_{use}$): The energy consumed while the product is in service.
> For appliances, $E_{use}$ can be **10 to 50 times** the embodied energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategies (2 marks — list 4):**
> 1. Use high-efficiency motors (e.g., IE3 / IE4 class).
> 2. Improve thermal insulation in heating appliances.
> 3. Use LED lighting instead of incandescent.
> 4. Implement smart standby modes (low standby power).
> 5. Use renewable energy inputs during manufacturing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula (1.5 marks):**
> $$E_{LC} = E_{mat} + E_{mfg} + E_{trans,in} + E_{use} + E_{maint} + E_{EOL}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Comparison (7 marks)

Consider a household mixer grinder (life = 7 years):

| Stage | Baseline $E$ (MJ) | DFE Redesign $E$ (MJ) |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{mat}$ | 60 | 55 |
| $E_{mfg}$ | 30 | 28 |
| $E_{trans,in}$ | 15 | 15 |
| $E_{use}$ | 800 | 600 |
| $E_{maint}$ | 25 | 20 |
| $E_{EOL}$ | 10 | 7 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute $E_{LC}$ for the baseline.
> $$E_{LC,base} = 60 + 30 + 15 + 800 + 25 + 10$$
> $$E_{LC,base} = 940 \text{ MJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $E_{LC}$ for the DFE redesign.
> $$E_{LC,DFE} = 55 + 28 + 15 + 600 + 20 + 7$$
> $$E_{LC,DFE} = 725 \text{ MJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute savings.
> $$\Delta E = 940 - 725 = 215 \text{ MJ}$$
> $$\% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{215}{940} \times 100\% = 22.87\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Comment. The use-phase energy ($E_{use}$) reduction from 800 → 600 MJ contributes 200 MJ of the total 215 MJ savings — i.e., **93% of the gain comes from a more efficient motor**. This confirms the KTU principle: for electrical appliances, **DFE² must target $E_{use}$**, not just materials.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 DFE Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing DFD with DFA** — DFA is for *assembly*; DFD is for *disassembly*. Examiners allocate specific marks for distinguishing these.
> 2. **Writing only the *definition* of DFE** — A 14-mark answer must include *methodology*, *metrics*, and an *example*. A definition-only answer fetches ≤ 3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the "Profit" pillar of TBL** — A DFE design that breaks the economic case is *incomplete* in KTU's view. Always mention cost implications.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — $T_d$ in seconds, $E_{LC}$ in MJ, $RI$ as a fraction or percent. Unitless answers invite a **0.5 to 1 mark penalty**.
> 5. **Listing the 8-R hierarchy in the wrong order** — Reversing the order is treated as a *conceptual error* and can cost up to **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 6. **Skipping the 8-R framework entirely** — Even if the question is about DFD or DFR specifically, ending with a reference to the 8-R hierarchy shows *integration* and often earns a full-mark conclusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DFE = Eco-Design = Green Design** — three terms for the same concept; KTU uses all three.
- The **life cycle** has six energy terms: $E_{mat}, E_{mfg}, E_{trans,in}, E_{use}, E_{maint}, E_{EOL}$.
- The **8-R hierarchy** in correct order: *Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Refurbish, Remanufacture, Recycle, Recover Energy.*
- **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — *People, Planet, Profit* — must all be satisfied.
- **DFD core metrics:** $DI$ (disassembly index) and $T_d$ (disassembly time).
- **DFR core metric:** $RI$ (recyclability index) — mass-weighted average of per-part recyclability fractions.
- **DFE² core metric:** $E_{LC}$ (life-cycle energy) — often dominated by $E_{use}$ for durables.
- **Permanent joints (weld, glue, rivet) are FORBIDDEN** in DFD-compliant designs for parts meant to be recovered.
- **Material homogeneity** is the single most important DFR guideline.
- **Fastener standardization** (one family) is the single most important DFD guideline.
- **Boothroyd-Dewhurst** part-count reduction underlies *both* DFA and DFD.
- **RoHS** restricts lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium — know this abbreviation.
- **REACH** is the EU chemicals regulation — register, evaluate, authorize, restrict.
- For **14-mark answers**, the winning structure is: *Definition → Method → Metric → Example → TBL Check → 8-R Anchor.*
- **Unit discipline:** $T_d$ in **seconds/minutes**, $E_{LC}$ in **MJ**, $RI$ as a **dimensionless fraction or %**, $DI$ as a **dimensionless fraction**.
- **Dominant energy rule:** If the question describes an appliance / motor / heater, lead your answer with $E_{use}$ reduction.
- **Keyword map for answer-writing:** DFD = snap-fits, modular, standardized fasteners. DFR = mono-material, RoHS, resin codes. DFE² = IE3 motors, LED, standby, insulation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Guidelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Design Guidelines" in Manufacturing?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Design Guidelines** are a structured set of engineering rules, heuristics, and empirical best practices that govern the geometry, tolerance, material selection, and process selection of a component to ensure that it can be **manufactured economically, reliably, and with consistent quality** using available manufacturing processes.

In simpler terms, when you are designing a part (a bracket, a gear, a housing, a shaft), you cannot just draw *any* shape and hand it over to the shop floor. Every shape you draw has to be *makeable* by some process — be it casting, forging, welding, machining, or plastic molding. **Design Guidelines** are the "traffic rules" of product design that prevent you from creating a part that is impossible (or very expensive) to manufacture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect vs. Contractor" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Building a House**
>
> Imagine you are an **architect** drawing a house. You may *want* to draw a beautiful cantilevered room floating 10 meters in mid-air with no pillar. As an *architect*, that is creative freedom. But when the **contractor** (the builder) looks at your drawing, he will say: *"Impossible! I cannot cast that shape in concrete without it collapsing during the pour."*
>
> The **Design Guidelines** are the **engineering laws of the contractor** — they tell the designer (architect) what shapes, features, and dimensions are *physically and economically* possible to manufacture using a given process.
>
> - If the contractor uses **casting** → no sharp internal corners, add **draft angles**, avoid thin sections, add **fillets**.
> - If the contractor uses **machining** → provide **datum surfaces**, access for **cutting tools**, avoid deep narrow pockets.
> - If the contractor uses **sheet metal** → use **bend radii**, avoid sharp bends, allow for **bending allowances**.

A good design is one where the designer knows the *manufacturing constraints upfront* and **designs the part to be "manufacturing-friendly"** — this concept is formally known as **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)**.

### 1.3 Why are Design Guidelines Important? (Engineering Justification)

| Aspect | Without Design Guidelines | With Design Guidelines |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Excessive tooling, scrap, re-work | Optimized tool paths, minimal scrap |
| **Time** | Multiple trial-and-error iterations | First-time-right production |
| **Quality** | Defects, porosity, distortion | Consistent, defect-free parts |
| **Safety** | Sharp corners cause stress failures | Fillets distribute stress, safer parts |
| **Sustainability** | Excess material waste | Near-net shape, less material used |

### 1.4 Universal Design Guidelines — Applicable Across ALL Processes

Before diving into process-specific rules, here are the **ten golden rules** that apply to virtually every manufacturing process:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Universal DFM Principles:**
>
> 1. **Simplify** — Reduce the number of parts in an assembly whenever possible.
> 2. **Standardize** — Use standard sizes, standard tolerances, and standard components (bolts, nuts, bearings).
> 3. **Minimize Operations** — Combine features so that one manufacturing setup can produce multiple features.
> 4. **Avoid Special Tools** — Use standard cutting tools, standard fixtures, and standard gauges.
> 5. **Specify the Maximum Tolerance** — Over-tight tolerances increase cost exponentially. Use the loosest tolerance that still meets function.
> 6. **Design for the Process** — Know your manufacturing process *before* finalizing the design.
> 7. **Use Symmetry** — Symmetric parts are easier to fixture, machine, and inspect.
> 8. **Avoid Separate Machined Pockets** — Use through-features or open pockets instead of closed internal cavities.
> 9. **Provide Accessibility** — Every feature must be accessible to a tool, a die, a mould, or an inspector.
> 10. **Allow for Inspection** — Provide datum surfaces and flat reference areas for quality checks.

### 1.5 Categorization of Process-Specific Design Guidelines

The KTU 2024 Module 4 focuses on process-specific guidelines. They are broadly classified as:

| S.No | Manufacturing Process | Module 4 Sub-Topic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Casting** (Sand, Die, Investment) | Design for Casting |
| 2 | **Forging** (Open / Closed Die) | Design for Forging |
| 3 | **Machining** (Turning, Milling, Drilling) | Design for Machining |
| 4 | **Welding** (Arc, Gas, Resistance) | Design for Welding |
| 5 | **Plastic Molding** (Injection, Blow, Compression) | Design for Plastics |
| 6 | **Sheet Metal** (Bending, Drawing, Stamping) | Design for Sheet Metal |

Each of these has its own dedicated **set of rules** regarding fillets, draft, tolerances, section thicknesses, and feature geometry.

### 1.6 The Economic Reality — Tolerance vs. Cost

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU Board Favourite):**
> The relationship between **tolerance** (manufacturing precision) and **cost** is **exponential, not linear**. Halving the tolerance (e.g., from ±0.1 mm to ±0.05 mm) can **quadruple** the manufacturing cost because it requires finer machines, more skilled labour, more inspection time, and higher scrap rates.

This is why **design guidelines always recommend using the loosest tolerance the functional design will permit** — a principle known as **"Tolerancing for Function, Not for Habit."**

---

### 1.7 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Constants Used in Design Guidelines

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Draft Angle | $\alpha$ | **0.5° to 3°** (typical 1°) | Casting, Forging, Plastics |
| Fillet Radius | $R_f$ | **0.5 mm to 5 mm** (typical 2 mm) | Castings, Forgings |
| Minimum Section Thickness | $t_{min}$ | **3 mm to 6 mm** | Sand Casting |
| Surface Roughness | $R_a$ | **0.8 µm to 25 µm** | Machined Surfaces |
| Standard Tolerance Grades | IT | **IT8 to IT13** (general) | All Processes |
| Bend Radius (Sheet Metal) | $R_b$ | **≥ 1× material thickness** | Sheet Metal Bending |
| Undercut Draft | $\theta$ | **5° to 15°** | Plastic Molding |

These standard values act as **default starting points** — a skilled designer adjusts them based on material, process capability, and part function.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design for Casting — Detailed Guidelines

Casting is the most **forgiving** of all processes when it comes to complex geometry, but it is the **least forgiving** when it comes to internal defects (porosity, shrinkage, cold shuts). Hence, casting guidelines focus on **avoiding defect-prone geometry**.

### 2.1.1 Section Thickness Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Rule:** Sections should be as **uniform in thickness** as possible. Abrupt changes in thickness cause **hot spots** → shrinkage cavities.

| Situation | Rule | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Variable thickness | Avoid sharp transitions | Sharp transitions create **hot spots** |
| Thin-to-thick transition | Use **gradual taper** (1:4 slope) | Prevents shrinkage porosity |
| Uniform walls preferred | All walls $\approx$ same thickness | Even cooling, no thermal gradients |
| Minimum thickness (Sand) | **5 mm** for small parts, **10–15 mm** for large | Molten metal must flow completely |
| Minimum thickness (Die) | **0.5 mm to 1 mm** | High pressure die casting |

### 2.1.2 The Hot Spot Problem

A **hot spot** is a region in a casting where the **section is thicker** than the surrounding regions. Because it cools **slower**, it acts as a **reservoir of liquid metal** even after the surrounding regions have solidified. The result is a **shrinkage cavity (void)** in the centre of the hot spot.

**Mitigation:**
1. Use **cores** to break up thick sections into thinner ribs.
2. Add **chills** (metal inserts) to cool hot spots rapidly.
3. Use **risers** to feed molten metal into the hot spot as it solidifies.

### 2.1.3 Fillet and Chamfer Rules for Castings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fillet Radius Formula (Empirical):**
>
> $$R_f = \frac{a + b}{2} + R$$
>
> Where:
> - $R_f$ = **fillet radius** at the junction
> - $a, b$ = **thicknesses** of the two intersecting walls
> - $R$ = additional radius (typically **3 mm to 5 mm**)

This formula ensures the **fillet compensates** for the mass of metal at the corner junction.

### 2.1.4 Draft Angle for Castings

The **draft angle** is the small taper applied to vertical walls so that the **pattern can be withdrawn** from the sand mould without damaging the mould walls.

| Process | Recommended Draft Angle |
|---|---|
| Sand Casting (hand mould) | **1° to 3°** |
| Sand Casting (machine mould) | **0.5° to 1.5°** |
| Die Casting | **0.5° to 1°** |
| Investment Casting | **0° to 1°** (very small) |
| Permanent Mould | **1° to 2°** |

### 2.1.5 Other Critical Casting Rules

- **Avoid large flat surfaces** — they warp during cooling. Add slight crowning or ribs.
- **Use generous fillets** at all internal corners ($R \geq 3$ mm for small castings).
- **Avoid isolated bosses** — combine multiple bosses with ribs.
- **Design parting line carefully** — preferably on a flat, accessible face.
- **Provide for machining allowance** — typically **1.5 mm to 3 mm** extra on machined surfaces.

---

## 2.2 Design for Forging — Detailed Guidelines

Forging produces parts that are **stronger** than cast parts because the grain flow follows the contour of the part. Forging guidelines focus on **enabling the metal to flow uniformly** into the die cavity.

### 2.2.1 The Forging Design Imperative — Symmetry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule #1 of Forging Design:** Use **symmetric shapes** wherever possible.
>
> Symmetric parts experience **uniform metal flow** during forging. Asymmetric parts cause **uneven flow** → folds, laps, and incomplete die fill.

| Asymmetric Part (BAD) | Symmetric Part (GOOD) |
|---|---|
| Causes uneven material flow | Causes uniform material flow |
| Laps, folds at offset sections | No folding defects |
| Requires more forging blows | Single, clean forging operation |

### 2.2.2 The "Forging Ratio" Concept

**Forging Ratio** is the ratio of the **cross-sectional area of the starting billet** to the **cross-sectional area of the final forged part**.

$$F_r = \frac{A_{billet}}{A_{forged}}$$

| Forging Ratio | Effect on Mechanical Properties |
|---|---|
| $F_r < 2$ | Minimal improvement |
| $F_r = 2$ to $5$ | Moderate grain refinement |
| $F_r > 5$ | Significant strength improvement |

### 2.2.3 Draft Angles for Forgings

Forgings require **larger draft angles** than castings because the forged part must be **ejected from the die**.

| Forging Type | Draft Angle (External) | Draft Angle (Internal) |
|---|---|---|
| Open Die Forging | **3° to 7°** | **5° to 10°** |
| Closed Die Forging | **0.5° to 1.5°** | **1° to 2°** |

### 2.2.4 The Famous Forging — "Shape Inside a Cylinder" Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **Heuristic (Eary & Reed):** "The ideal forging shape is one that can be inscribed in a **cylinder**."
>
> Parts that fit this description have:
> - No long, thin projections.
> - No deep, narrow ribs.
> - No undercuts.
> - Symmetry about the forging axis.

### 2.2.5 Ribs, Webs, and Bosses in Forgings

- **Ribs** should be **shorter** and **thicker** than in castings. Long thin ribs **do not fill properly** during forging.
- **Web thickness** = **3× to 4×** the rib thickness at the base.
- **Bosses** should be **proportioned** as in castings but with **higher fillet radii**.

### 2.2.6 Forging Parting Line

The **parting line** is the line where the two die halves meet. It should be:
- Located on a **flat surface**.
- In the **plane of maximum projected area** (perpendicular to the forging force).
- Should **not** pass through critical functional surfaces.

---

## 2.3 Design for Machining — Detailed Guidelines

Machining is a **subtractive** process — material is *removed* to create the shape. Hence, the design must **provide access** for the cutting tool to enter, cut, and exit cleanly.

### 2.3.1 The Three Pillars of Machinable Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1: Tool Access** — Every feature must be reachable by a tool.
> **Pillar 2: Tool Clearance** — There must be space for the tool body (not just the cutting edge).
> **Pillar 3: Workpiece Holding** — The part must be clampable on a fixture without deflection.

### 2.3.2 Drilling Design Rules

| Parameter | Rule | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Hole Depth | Depth $\leq 4 \times$ Diameter (standard drill) | Standard drills wander in deep holes |
| Deep holes | Use **gun drills** for $L/D > 4$ | Special drills for deep holes |
| Hole Diameter | Prefer **standard sizes** | Standard drills are cheaper and available |
| Hole Spacing | Minimum **2.5× to 3×** the hole diameter | Prevents drill wander between holes |
| Hole-to-edge | Minimum **1.5× to 2×** the hole diameter | Prevents edge breakout |
| Blind hole bottom | **Conical bottom** (118° tip angle) | Drill tip geometry |
| Avoid | **Inclined surfaces** for hole entry | Drill will deflect or break |

### 2.3.3 Milling Design Rules

- **Avoid sharp internal corners** in pockets — use a **corner radius** that matches the milling cutter.
- **Pocket depth** $\leq 3\times$ the cutter diameter for side-milling.
- **Open pockets** are preferred over closed internal pockets.
- **Pocket corners** should have radii (typically $R = 0.5 \times $ pocket width).
- **Avoid** thin walls that will deflect under cutter force.

### 2.3.4 Turning Design Rules

- **Length-to-diameter ratio** of shaft $\leq 10:1$ to avoid chatter and deflection.
- **Undercuts** for thread relief should be specified clearly.
- **Chamfers** (typically $0.5 \times 45°$ or $1 \times 45°$) at shaft ends.
- **Keyway length** $\leq 1.5 \times$ shaft diameter.

### 2.3.5 The Cost of Machining

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Formula (Machining Cost):**
>
> $$C_m = C_{setup} + C_{tooling} + (C_{machine} + C_{labour}) \times t_{cycle}$$
>
> Where:
> - $C_{setup}$ = **setup cost** (depends on batch size)
> - $C_{tooling}$ = **tool cost**
> - $C_{machine}$ = **machine hourly rate**
> - $t_{cycle}$ = **cycle time per part**
>
> **Design implication:** Reducing $t_{cycle}$ (cycle time) is the most powerful lever. This is achieved by:
> - Reducing the number of features.
> - Combining operations.
> - Specifying loose tolerances (faster cutting).

### 2.3.6 Standard Tolerances and Surface Finishes for Machined Parts

| Process | Typical Tolerance | Surface Roughness $R_a$ |
|---|---|---|
| Sawing | ±0.5 mm | 12.5 µm |
| Turning (rough) | ±0.125 mm | 6.3 µm |
| Turning (finish) | ±0.025 mm | 0.8 µm |
| Milling (rough) | ±0.2 mm | 3.2 µm |
| Milling (finish) | ±0.05 mm | 1.6 µm |
| Grinding | ±0.01 mm | 0.4 µm |
| Lapping/Honing | ±0.005 mm | 0.1 µm |

---

## 2.4 Design for Welding — Detailed Guidelines

Welding joins **separate pieces** of metal by **localized fusion**. Design guidelines focus on **minimizing residual stresses** and **distortion** caused by the intense localized heat.

### 2.4.1 The Weldment Design Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Weldment Design:** **Keep the design simple, accessible, and symmetric.**
>
> A weldment should require the **minimum number of parts** to be joined, the **minimum length of weld**, and the **fewest operations** to complete.

### 2.4.2 Section Thickness Matching

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** The two parts being welded should have **approximately equal thickness.**
>
> Reason: If a thick section is welded to a thin section, the **heat dissipates faster** through the thick part, leading to **uneven cooling, distortion, and cracking** in the thinner part.
>
> **Rule of thumb:** $\frac{t_{max}}{t_{min}} \leq 2$

If unequal thickness is unavoidable, use a **tapered transition** (slope $\leq 1:4$).

### 2.4.3 Weld Position Accessibility

| Position | Symbol | Difficulty | Cost Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat (downhand) | **1G / 1F** | Easiest | **1×** |
| Horizontal | **2F / 2G** | Moderate | **1.3×** |
| Vertical | **3F / 3G** | Difficult | **1.5×** |
| Overhead | **4F / 4G** | Most difficult | **2×** |

**Design implication:** **Always design welds to be made in the flat (downhand) position** if possible. This gives the best quality and lowest cost.

### 2.4.4 Weld Joint Selection Guide

| Joint Type | Best For | Strength | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Butt joint** | Plates of similar thickness | **Highest** | Low |
| **Lap joint** | Thin sheets, fasteners needed | Moderate | Low |
| **T-joint** | Frame structures | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Corner joint** | Boxes, enclosures | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Edge joint** | Thin sheets only | Lowest | Lowest |

### 2.4.5 Distortion Control in Weldments

Distortion in welding is caused by **uneven heating and cooling**. Mitigation strategies:

1. **Balanced welding** — weld symmetrically about the neutral axis.
2. **Backstep technique** — weld short segments in reverse order to spread heat.
3. **Presetting** — angle the parts slightly *toward* each other to compensate for shrinkage.
4. **Skip welding** — alternate welds to prevent heat build-up in one spot.
5. **Use intermittent welds** instead of continuous welds where strength allows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shrinkage Allowance Formula:**
>
> $$\Delta L = k \cdot L$$
>
> Where:
> - $\Delta L$ = **shrinkage allowance** (extra length to add to the part)
> - $k$ = **shrinkage coefficient** (typically **0.001 to 0.002** for steel, per unit length)
> - $L$ = **length of the weld / part**

### 2.4.6 Weld Size and Length Guidelines

- **Minimum weld leg size** = **3 mm** (for thin sheets) to **6 mm** (for thick plates).
- **Weld length** should be **at least 10× the weld leg size** to avoid end crater cracks.
- **Avoid** welds in **stress concentration zones** (corners, notches).
- **Stagger** welds on opposite sides of a member (not directly opposite) to balance heat.

---

## 2.5 Design for Plastic Molding — Detailed Guidelines

Plastic injection molding is the most widely used polymer process. Design rules are dictated by the **flow behavior of molten plastic** and the **ejection of the solidified part**.

### 2.5.1 Wall Thickness — The Most Critical Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Plastic Part Design:**
> **Uniform wall thickness** is the single most important design rule.
>
> Variable wall thickness causes:
> - **Differential cooling** → **warpage, sink marks, voids**.
> - **Internal stresses** → **cracking** in service.
> - **Longer cycle time** → thicker sections take longer to cool.

| Wall Thickness | Typical Material | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 – 1.0 mm | Thin-wall polymers | Disposable products |
| 1.0 – 3.0 mm | **Standard** for most plastics | General components |
| 3.0 – 6.0 mm | Glass-filled, structural | Load-bearing parts |
| > 6.0 mm | Special cases only | Avoid if possible |

**Wall thickness variation rule:** No step change — use a **gradual taper** with **slope $\leq 1:4$**.

### 2.5.2 Draft Angle for Plastics

| Surface Finish | Recommended Draft |
|---|---|
| Polished (smooth) | **0.5° to 1°** |
| Textured | **1° to 1.5°** |
| Deep ribs / bosses | **1.5° to 2°** |

### 2.5.3 Rib Design in Plastics

- **Rib thickness** = **0.5× to 0.6×** the **adjacent wall thickness** (to prevent sink marks).
- **Rib height** $\leq 3\times$ the wall thickness.
- **Rib spacing** $\geq 2\times$ the wall thickness (to allow material flow between ribs).
- **Fillet at rib base** = **0.25× to 0.5×** the wall thickness (reduces stress concentration).

### 2.5.4 Boss Design

- **Boss outer diameter** = **2× to 2.5×** the **inner hole diameter**.
- **Boss wall thickness** = **0.5× to 0.6×** the **adjacent wall thickness**.
- Provide **draft angle** and a **fillet** at the base.

### 2.5.5 Radii and Fillets in Plastics

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal radius rule:** $R_{internal} \geq 0.5 \times t_{wall}$
> **External radius rule:** $R_{external} = 1.5 \times R_{internal}$
>
> Sharp internal corners are **stress concentrators** and **flow restrictors** in plastics. Always radius them.

---

## 2.6 Design for Sheet Metal — Detailed Guidelines

Sheet metal processes (bending, drawing, shearing, stamping) impose very specific geometric constraints. Sheet metal has a **constant thickness** ($t$), and all design rules are expressed **in multiples of $t$**.

### 2.6.1 Bend Radius Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Bend Radius Rule:**
>
> $$R_{min} = k \cdot t$$
>
> Where:
> - $R_{min}$ = **minimum inside bend radius** (in mm)
> - $k$ = **material factor** (see table below)
> - $t$ = **sheet thickness** (in mm)

| Material | Material Factor $k$ (for $R/t$) |
|---|---|
| Low Carbon Steel (annealed) | **0.5** |
| Stainless Steel | **0.5 – 1.0** |
| Aluminium (soft) | **0.3** |
| Aluminium (hard) | **0.8 – 1.0** |
| Copper, Brass | **0.3 – 0.5** |

### 2.6.2 Flange Length for Bending

The **minimum flange length** (the straight portion before the bend starts) is governed by the need to place the bend **clear of the punch edge**:

$$L_{flange, min} = R + 1.5 t$$

### 2.6.3 Hole and Slot Rules in Sheet Metal

| Feature | Minimum Distance from Edge | Minimum Spacing |
|---|---|---|
| Round hole | $\geq 1.5 t$ from edge | $\geq 3 D$ (centre-to-centre) |
| Slot | $\geq 1.5 t$ from edge | $\geq 3 W$ (where $W$ = slot width) |
| Hole to bend line | $\geq 2 t + R_{bend}$ | — |
| Notch from edge | $\geq t$ wide, $\geq 2 t$ deep | — |

### 2.6.4 Bending Allowance (K Factor)

When a sheet is bent, the material on the **outside** of the bend stretches and the material on the **inside** compresses. There is a **neutral axis** that does not change length. The **K-factor** defines the position of this neutral axis.

$$K = \frac{t_{neutral}}{t}$$

Where $t_{neutral}$ is the distance from the **inside surface** of the bend to the **neutral axis**.

| Material | Typical K-Factor |
|---|---|
| Mild Steel | **0.44** |
| Stainless Steel | **0.44** |
| Aluminium (soft) | **0.33** |
| Copper | **0.35** |

The **flat pattern length** (the unfolded sheet length required to produce a bent part) is:

$$L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 + BA$$

Where $BA$ (Bend Allowance) is calculated as:

$$BA = \frac{\pi}{180°} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = **bend angle** (in degrees)
- $R$ = **inside bend radius**
- $K$ = **K-factor**
- $t$ = **sheet thickness**

### 2.6.5 Other Sheet Metal Rules

- **Avoid sharp corners** — use a minimum radius of **1× sheet thickness**.
- **Use bends of the same radius** throughout the part to reduce tooling cost.
- **Bend orientation** should be **perpendicular to the rolling direction** of the sheet (to avoid cracking).
- **Hemming** (folding back the edge) adds rigidity — use for panel edges.
- **Avoid deep drawn parts** with draw ratios $> 2.5:1$ — they require multiple draws.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| # | Formula / Rule | Equation | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fillet radius (casting) | $R_f = \dfrac{a + b}{2} + R$ | $a, b$ = wall thicknesses, $R$ = base radius | Castings |
| 2 | Forging ratio | $F_r = \dfrac{A_{billet}}{A_{forged}}$ | $A$ = cross-sectional area | Forgings |
| 3 | Machining cost | $C_m = C_{setup} + C_{tool} + (C_m + C_l) t_c$ | All cost terms + $t_c$ = cycle time | Machining |
| 4 | Shrinkage allowance | $\Delta L = k \cdot L$ | $k$ = 0.001–0.002 (steel) | Welding |
| 5 | Wall thickness variation | $t_{max} / t_{min} \leq 1.5$ | For uniform cooling | Castings, Plastics |
| 6 | Minimum bend radius | $R_{min} = k \cdot t$ | $k$ = material factor | Sheet Metal |
| 7 | Flange length | $L_{flange, min} = R + 1.5 t$ | $R$ = bend radius, $t$ = thickness | Sheet Metal |
| 8 | Bend allowance | $BA = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180°} (R + K \cdot t)$ | $\theta$ = bend angle, $K$ = K-factor | Sheet Metal |
| 9 | Flat pattern length | $L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 + BA$ | Sum of straight lengths + bend allowance | Sheet Metal |
| 10 | Standard hole spacing | $S_{min} = 2.5 D$ to $3 D$ | $D$ = hole diameter | Drilling |
| 11 | Standard edge distance | $E_{min} = 1.5 D$ to $2 D$ | $D$ = hole diameter | Drilling |
| 12 | Rib thickness (plastics) | $t_{rib} = 0.5$ to $0.6 \times t_{wall}$ | $t_{wall}$ = adjacent wall thickness | Plastics |
| 13 | Boss outer diameter | $D_{boss} = 2$ to $2.5 \times D_{hole}$ | $D_{hole}$ = inner hole | Plastics |
| 14 | Draft angle (casting) | $\alpha = 0.5°$ to $3°$ | Process-dependent | Casting |
| 15 | Draft angle (forging) | $\alpha = 3°$ to $7°$ (external) | Die ejection | Forging |
| 16 | Draft angle (plastic) | $\alpha = 0.5°$ to $2°$ | Surface finish dependent | Plastics |
| 17 | Internal radius (plastic) | $R_i \geq 0.5 \times t_{wall}$ | $t_{wall}$ = wall thickness | Plastics |
| 18 | Hole-edge distance (sheet) | $d \geq 1.5 t$ | $t$ = sheet thickness | Sheet Metal |
| 19 | Hole-bend distance (sheet) | $d \geq 2t + R$ | $t$ = thickness, $R$ = bend radius | Sheet Metal |
| 20 | Section ratio (welding) | $t_{max}/t_{min} \leq 2$ | Thicknesses of joined parts | Welding |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these guidelines are applied in industry:**
>
> - **Automotive**: Engine blocks (casting), connecting rods (forging), body panels (sheet metal), bumpers (plastics), chassis (welding).
> - **Aerospace**: Turbine blades (investment casting), wing ribs (machining), fuselage (welding/riveting).
> - **Consumer Electronics**: Phone cases (plastics), heat sinks (extrusion/machining), connectors (stamping).
> - **Heavy Machinery**: Gear blanks (forging), pump housings (casting), structural frames (welding).
> - **Medical Devices**: Surgical instruments (machining), implants (casting/machining), disposables (plastics).
>
> In every case, **following the DFM guidelines reduces cost by 15–40%** and **shortens time-to-market by 20–30%** — these are industry-proven numbers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example #1 — Fillet Radius for a Casting

### Problem
A cast iron bracket has two intersecting walls with thicknesses $a = 12$ mm and $b = 18$ mm. The base fillet addition $R$ is to be **4 mm**. Calculate the required fillet radius at the junction.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1: Identify the formula from the rulebook.**
>
> The standard fillet radius formula for castings is:
>
> $$R_f = \frac{a + b}{2} + R$$

> **Step 2: Substitute the given values.**
>
> $$R_f = \frac{a + b}{2} + R = \frac{12 + 18}{2} + 4$$

> **Step 3: Compute the average of the two thicknesses.**
>
> $$\frac{12 + 18}{2} = \frac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 4: Add the base fillet radius.**
>
> $$R_f = 15 + 4 = 19 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 5: Final answer.**
>
> $$\boxed{R_f = 19 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Step 6: Engineering check.**
>
> Is $R_f = 19$ mm physically reasonable? 
> - The wall thicknesses are 12 mm and 18 mm, so the junction has a mass concentration equivalent to roughly 15 mm.
> - A fillet of 19 mm means the fillet extends 4 mm beyond this concentration — this matches the recommended $R = 4$ mm for a small-to-medium cast iron bracket. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #2 — Bend Allowance & Flat Pattern for a Sheet Metal Part

### Problem
A sheet metal bracket is to be made from **1.5 mm thick mild steel** ($K = 0.44$). It has a **90° bend** with an **inside bend radius $R = 3$ mm**. The two straight leg lengths (after bending) are **$L_1 = 80$ mm** and **$L_2 = 120$ mm**. Calculate:
1. The **bend allowance** (BA).
2. The **total flat pattern length**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1: Identify the formula for Bend Allowance.**
>
> $$BA = \frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180°} \cdot (R + K \cdot t)$$

> **Step 2: Substitute all the given values.**
>
> - $\theta = 90°$ (bend angle)
> - $R = 3$ mm (inside bend radius)
> - $K = 0.44$ (K-factor for mild steel)
> - $t = 1.5$ mm (sheet thickness)

> **Step 3: Compute the inside bend radius component.**
>
> $$K \cdot t = 0.44 \times 1.5 = 0.66 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$R + K \cdot t = 3 + 0.66 = 3.66 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 4: Compute the angular factor.**
>
> $$\frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180°} = \frac{\pi \times 90}{180} = \frac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708$$

> **Step 5: Multiply to get the bend allowance.**
>
> $$BA = 1.5708 \times 3.66 = 5.749 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$\boxed{BA \approx 5.75 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Step 6: Compute the total flat pattern length.**
>
> The flat pattern length formula is:
>
> $$L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 + BA$$
>
> Substituting:
>
> $$L_{flat} = 80 + 120 + 5.75$$
>
> $$\boxed{L_{flat} = 205.75 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Step 7: Engineering interpretation.**
>
> - The flat (unbent) sheet must be cut to a length of **205.75 mm**.
> - After bending, the two legs will measure 80 mm and 120 mm respectively, with the bend consuming 5.75 mm of arc-length material.
> - If the designer forgot to include the bend allowance, the legs would each be **shorter** than intended by approximately **2.87 mm** (half the BA each side) — a **classic and costly** sheet metal DFM error.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #3 — Forging Ratio Calculation

### Problem
A connecting rod is to be forged from a cylindrical billet of **diameter 60 mm** and **length 200 mm**. After forging, the connecting rod has a rectangular cross-section of **40 mm × 25 mm**. Calculate the **forging ratio**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area of the billet.**
>
> The billet is cylindrical:
>
> $$A_{billet} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 60^2$$
>
> $$A_{billet} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 3600 = 900\pi$$
>
> $$A_{billet} \approx 2827.43 \text{ mm}^2$$

> **Step 2: Calculate the cross-sectional area of the forged part.**
>
> The forged cross-section is rectangular:
>
> $$A_{forged} = 40 \times 25 = 1000 \text{ mm}^2$$

> **Step 3: Apply the forging ratio formula.**
>
> $$F_r = \frac{A_{billet}}{A_{forged}} = \frac{2827.43}{1000}$$
>
> $$\boxed{F_r \approx 2.83}$$

> **Step 4: Interpret the result.**
>
> - A forging ratio of 2.83 means the cross-sectional area has been **reduced by a factor of nearly 3** during forging.
> - This produces **moderate grain refinement** and improvement in mechanical properties (per the table in Section 2.2.2).
> - For a connecting rod, which is a **highly stressed** component, a forging ratio of at least 2.5–3 is desirable to ensure adequate fatigue strength.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example #4 — Plastic Part Wall & Rib Design

### Problem
A plastic enclosure is to be injection-moulded with a **nominal wall thickness of 2.5 mm**. Design guidelines require:
1. Calculate the **maximum allowable rib thickness** (to avoid sink marks).
2. Calculate the **maximum rib height**.
3. Calculate the **minimum internal corner radius** at wall intersections.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1: Rib thickness calculation.**
>
> Using the rule $t_{rib} = 0.5$ to $0.6 \times t_{wall}$:
>
> $$t_{rib, max} = 0.6 \times 2.5 = 1.5 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$t_{rib, min} = 0.5 \times 2.5 = 1.25 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$\boxed{t_{rib} = 1.25 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Step 2: Maximum rib height calculation.**
>
> Using the rule $h_{rib} \leq 3 \times t_{wall}$:
>
> $$h_{rib, max} = 3 \times 2.5 = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$\boxed{h_{rib, max} = 7.5 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Step 3: Minimum internal radius calculation.**
>
> Using the rule $R_{internal} \geq 0.5 \times t_{wall}$:
>
> $$R_{internal, min} = 0.5 \times 2.5 = 1.25 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$\boxed{R_{internal, min} = 1.25 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Step 4: Design check.**
>
> - All three values are well within standard moulding practice. ✓
> - The corresponding **external radius** (per the rule $R_{ext} = 1.5 \times R_{int}$) would be:
>
> $$R_{external} = 1.5 \times 1.25 = 1.875 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$\boxed{R_{external} \approx 1.9 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Step 5: Engineering interpretation.**
>
> A 2.5 mm wall is **standard for general-purpose enclosures**. The resulting rib dimensions (1.5 mm × 7.5 mm) are easily moulded. The fillet radii (1.25 mm internal, 1.9 mm external) are large enough to:
> - Eliminate stress concentrations.
> - Allow smooth material flow during injection.
> - Prevent air traps in the mould cavity.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to select a manufacturing process for a given part.**

| Design Feature / Requirement | Best Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Complex internal cavity (engine block) | **Sand Casting** | Molten metal fills complex shapes |
| High strength, fatigue-resistant (crankshaft) | **Forging** | Grain flow improves fatigue life |
| Very tight tolerance (shaft Ø50 ±0.01 mm) | **Grinding / Turning** | Machining achieves high precision |
| Thin-walled, lightweight (bottle) | **Blow Moulding** | Specialised for hollow thin shapes |
| Mass-produced plastic (mobile case) | **Injection Moulding** | High volume, low per-unit cost |
| Large, thin, flat panel (car door) | **Sheet Metal Stamping** | Efficient for sheet forms |
| Permanent joint, strong (bridge girder) | **Welding** | Stronger than mechanical fasteners |
| Prototype, low volume (one-off bracket) | **Machining from bar stock** | No tooling required |
| Intricate 3D shape, smooth surface (jewellery) | **Investment Casting** | Excellent surface, complex geometry |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Defects Caused by Ignoring Design Guidelines

| Design Violation | Resulting Defect | Affected Process |
|---|---|---|
| Sharp internal corner in casting | Stress concentration, **hot tear** | Casting |
| Variable wall thickness in casting | **Shrinkage cavity** in thick section | Casting |
| Asymmetric forging | **Folds and laps** in off-axis regions | Forging |
| Deep narrow pocket in machining | **Tool deflection**, poor surface finish | Machining |
| Unequal thickness in weldment | **Distortion and cracking** at thin side | Welding |
| Sharp internal corner in plastic | **Stress crack** under load | Plastics |
| Variable wall thickness in plastic | **Sink marks** and warpage | Plastics |
| Bend radius too small in sheet metal | **Cracking on outside of bend** | Sheet Metal |
| Hole too close to edge in sheet metal | **Tearing** at the edge | Sheet Metal |

---

## 3.7 Numerical Comparison — Process Cost vs. Batch Size

> [!NOTE]
> **How process selection depends on batch size (qualitative cost comparison):**

| Process | Tooling Cost (₹) | Per-Part Cost (₹) | Break-Even Batch Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sand Casting | **Low (₹5,000–₹20,000)** | Moderate (₹50–₹500) | **1 to 50 parts** |
| Die Casting | **High (₹1,00,000–₹10,00,000)** | Low (₹10–₹100) | **> 1,000 parts** |
| Investment Casting | Moderate (₹20,000–₹1,00,000) | Moderate (₹30–₹300) | **10 to 500 parts** |
| Open Die Forging | Low (₹10,000–₹50,000) | High (₹100–₹1000) | **1 to 100 parts** |
| Closed Die Forging | Very High (₹2,00,000–₹50,00,000) | Low (₹20–₹200) | **> 5,000 parts** |
| Injection Moulding | High (₹50,000–₹20,00,000) | Very Low (₹5–₹50) | **> 1,000 parts** |
| Sheet Metal Stamping | Moderate (₹20,000–₹2,00,000) | Low (₹10–₹100) | **> 500 parts** |
| CNC Machining | None (machine is the tool) | High (₹200–₹2000) | **1 to 100 parts** |

**Key Insight:** Processes with **high tooling cost** are economical only at **high volumes**. For prototypes and small batches, processes with **low tooling cost** (sand casting, machining, open-die forging) are preferred.
