# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"What is a draft angle? Why is it provided in castings and forgings? List the typical draft angle values for sand casting, die casting, and closed-die forging."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A draft angle is a small taper applied to the vertical (or near-vertical) surfaces of a pattern, forging, or moulded plastic part to enable easy **withdrawal of the pattern from the mould** or **ejection of the part from the die**, without damaging the mould walls or the part itself.

> **Why it is provided (1 mark):** 
> 1. **In castings:** It allows the wooden or metal pattern to be pulled out of the sand mould without breaking or eroding the sand walls.
> 2. **In forgings:** It allows the forged part to be ejected from the die cavity after the forging operation is complete.
> 3. **In plastics:** It allows the solidified plastic part to be ejected from the mould cavity by the ejector pins.

> **Typical values (1 mark):**
> | Process | Draft Angle |
> |---|---|
> | Sand Casting | 1° to 3° |
> | Die Casting | 0.5° to 1° |
> | Closed-Die Forging | 0.5° to 1.5° |

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"State the rule for minimum bend radius in sheet metal bending. How is the bend allowance calculated?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Minimum Bend Radius Rule (1.5 marks):**
> The minimum inside bend radius in sheet metal is given by:
>
> $$R_{min} = k \cdot t$$
>
> Where $k$ is the **material factor** (e.g., 0.5 for mild steel, 0.3 for soft aluminium) and $t$ is the **sheet thickness**. Bending below this radius causes **cracking on the outside surface** of the bend.

> **Bend Allowance Formula (1.5 marks):**
> The bend allowance is the **arc length of the neutral axis** through the bend region:
>
> $$BA = \frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180°} \cdot (R + K \cdot t)$$
>
> Where:
> - $\theta$ = bend angle (in degrees)
> - $R$ = inside bend radius
> - $K$ = K-factor (ratio of neutral axis distance to thickness)
> - $t$ = sheet thickness
>
> The **flat pattern length** of the bent part is then: $L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 + BA$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question B.1 (A) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**"Explain the general design guidelines for **castings**. Discuss in detail: (a) the importance of uniform section thickness and the hot-spot problem with mitigation strategies, and (b) the rules for fillet radius, draft angle, and parting line."**

#### (a) Uniform Section Thickness and Hot-Spot Problem — 7 Marks

> **Uniform Section Thickness Rule (2 marks):**
> The single most important rule in casting design is that **all sections of a casting should be as uniform in thickness as possible**. Variable section thickness leads to **non-uniform cooling rates**, which in turn leads to internal defects and distortion. The recommended ratio is $t_{max} / t_{min} \leq 1.5$.

> **The Hot-Spot Problem (3 marks):**
> A **hot spot** is a localized region where the casting section is **thicker** than the surrounding regions. Because of its higher thermal mass, this region **solidifies last** while the surrounding thinner regions have already solidified. As the surrounding metal contracts during solidification, it cannot feed the still-liquid hot-spot region, resulting in a **shrinkage cavity (void)** in the centre of the hot spot. This is one of the most common and serious defects in castings.

> **Mitigation Strategies (2 marks):**
> 1. **Use cores** to hollow out thick sections, converting them into thinner rib-stiffened panels.
> 2. **Use chills** (metal inserts placed in the mould adjacent to the hot spot) to accelerate local cooling.
> 3. **Use risers** (reservoirs of molten metal placed above the hot spot) to feed liquid metal into the shrinking region.
> 4. **Redesign** the geometry to use tapered transitions (slope $\leq 1:4$) between thick and thin sections.

#### (b) Fillet Radius, Draft Angle, and Parting Line — 7 Marks

> **Fillet Radius Rule (2.5 marks):**
> All **internal corners** of a casting must have a generous **fillet radius** to:
> - Eliminate stress concentration.
> - Prevent hot tears (cracks formed during solidification shrinkage).
> - Allow smooth flow of molten metal into the corner.
>
> The empirical formula is:
>
> $$R_f = \frac{a + b}{2} + R$$
>
> Where $a$ and $b$ are the thicknesses of the two intersecting walls, and $R$ is a base radius (typically 3–5 mm).

> **Draft Angle Rule (2.5 marks):**
> All vertical walls of a casting pattern must have a **slight taper (draft angle)** to enable pattern withdrawal from the sand mould. Typical values:
> - Sand casting (hand mould): **1° to 3°**
> - Sand casting (machine mould): **0.5° to 1.5°**
> - Die casting: **0.5° to 1°**
> - Investment casting: **0° to 1°**
> - Permanent mould: **1° to 2°**

> **Parting Line Considerations (2 marks):**
> The **parting line** is the line of separation between the cope (upper) and drag (lower) halves of the mould. It should be:
> 1. Located on a **flat, easily accessible surface** of the casting.
> 2. Placed such that both mould halves have **positive draft** (no undercuts that would prevent mould separation).
> 3. Avoid passing through **critical machined surfaces** (to minimize post-casting machining).
> 4. Kept as **simple and short** as possible.

---

### 📌 Question B.1 (B) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**"Explain the general design guidelines for **plastic injection-moulded parts**. Discuss in detail: (a) the importance of uniform wall thickness, and (b) the rules for ribs, bosses, fillets, and draft angles."**

#### (a) Importance of Uniform Wall Thickness — 7 Marks

> **The Golden Rule (2 marks):**
> Uniform wall thickness is the **single most important design rule** for plastic injection-moulded parts. Variable wall thickness causes a cascade of defects.

> **Why Uniformity Matters (3 marks):**
> 1. **Differential cooling:** Thick sections cool slower than thin sections. As the thin section solidifies and shrinks, it constrains the still-shrinking thick section, creating **internal residual stresses**.
> 2. **Sink marks and voids:** The surface above a thick section "sinks" inward as the underlying material contracts after the surface has already solidified, creating visible depressions.
> 3. **Warping:** Differential shrinkage causes the part to bend or twist out of shape.
> 4. **Cycle time penalty:** The cycle time is determined by the **thickest section** (it must cool completely before ejection). Variable thickness therefore unnecessarily extends cycle time and increases cost.

> **Design Strategy (2 marks):**
> When unequal thickness is functionally required, use a **gradual taper** between thick and thin regions with a **maximum slope of 1:4** (i.e., for every 1 mm of thickness change, allow 4 mm of horizontal distance). This allows material to flow smoothly and cool gradually.

#### (b) Ribs, Bosses, Fillets, and Draft Angles — 7 Marks

> **Rib Design Rules (2 marks):**
> - Rib thickness = **0.5× to 0.6×** the adjacent wall thickness (to avoid sink marks).
> - Rib height $\leq 3 \times$ wall thickness.
> - Rib spacing $\geq 2 \times$ wall thickness.
> - Fillet at rib base = **0.25× to 0.5×** wall thickness.
> - Always alternate rib directions on opposite sides of a panel (zig-zag pattern) to balance stiffness and minimize warpage.

> **Boss Design Rules (1.5 marks):**
> - Boss outer diameter = **2× to 2.5×** the inner hole diameter.
> - Boss wall thickness = **0.5× to 0.6×** the adjacent wall thickness.
> - Provide draft angle on the boss walls (1° to 2°).
> - Add a fillet at the boss base for stress relief.

> **Fillet Rules (1.5 marks):**
> - Internal radius: $R_{int} \geq 0.5 \times t_{wall}$ (eliminates stress concentration, aids material flow).
> - External radius: $R_{ext} = 1.5 \times R_{int}$ (provides uniform wall transition).
> - Sharp corners are **never** acceptable in plastic parts.

> **Draft Angle Rules (2 marks):**
> | Surface Finish | Draft Angle |
> |---|---|
> | Polished (smooth) | 0.5° to 1° |
> | Textured | 1° to 1.5° |
> | Deep ribs/bosses | 1.5° to 2° |
>
> Draft is essential for ejecting the part from the mould without damage.

---

### 📌 Question B.2 (A) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**"Explain the design guidelines for **sheet metal parts**. With neat sketches, describe: (a) the rules for minimum bend radius, flange length, and hole-edge distance, and (b) the concept of K-factor and bend allowance with a worked example."**

#### (a) Bend Radius, Flange Length, and Hole-Edge Distance — 7 Marks

> **Minimum Bend Radius Rule (2.5 marks):**
> $$R_{min} = k \cdot t$$
> Where $k$ is the material factor and $t$ is the sheet thickness.
>
> | Material | k |
> |---|---|
> | Mild Steel | 0.5 |
> | Aluminium (soft) | 0.3 |
> | Stainless Steel | 0.5–1.0 |
>
> Bending below $R_{min}$ causes **outside-surface cracking**.

> **Flange Length Rule (2 marks):**
> The minimum straight length of flange before a bend is:
>
> $$L_{flange, min} = R + 1.5 t$$
>
> This ensures the bend line is **clear of the punch edge** and prevents tearing.

> **Hole-Edge and Hole-Bend Distance (2.5 marks):**
> | Feature | Minimum Distance |
> |---|---|
> | Round hole from edge | $\geq 1.5 t$ |
> | Slot from edge | $\geq 1.5 t$ |
> | Hole to bend line | $\geq 2t + R_{bend}$ |
> | Hole centre-to-centre spacing | $\geq 3D$ (where D = hole dia) |
>
> If holes are too close to an edge or bend, the metal **tears** during the bending operation.

#### (b) K-Factor, Bend Allowance, and Worked Example — 7 Marks

> **K-Factor Concept (2 marks):**
> The **K-factor** is defined as the ratio of the distance from the **inside surface** of the bend to the **neutral axis** (the axis that neither stretches nor compresses) to the sheet thickness:
>
> $$K = \frac{t_{neutral}}{t}$$
>
> Typical values: Mild Steel = 0.44, Aluminium (soft) = 0.33, Copper = 0.35.

> **Bend Allowance Formula (2 marks):**
>
> $$BA = \frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180°} \cdot (R + K \cdot t)$$

> **Worked Example (3 marks):**
> A sheet metal bracket of **2 mm thick mild steel** ($K = 0.44$) is bent through **60°** with an **inside bend radius of 4 mm**. Calculate the bend allowance.
>
> $$BA = \frac{\pi \times 60}{180} \times (4 + 0.44 \times 2)$$
> $$BA = \frac{\pi}{3} \times (4 + 0.88)$$
> $$BA = 1.0472 \times 4.88$$
> $$\boxed{BA \approx 5.11 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### 📌 Question B.2 (B) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**"Explain the design guidelines for **welding**. Discuss in detail: (a) the rules for section thickness matching and weld position accessibility, and (b) the causes and prevention of weldment distortion including the shrinkage allowance formula."**

#### (a) Section Thickness Matching and Weld Position — 7 Marks

> **Section Thickness Matching (3 marks):**
> Parts being welded should have **approximately equal thickness**:
>
> $$\frac{t_{max}}{t_{min}} \leq 2$$
>
> Reason: Unequal thickness causes **uneven heat dissipation** — the thicker part draws heat away from the weld zone faster, leading to **rapid solidification, cracking, and distortion** in the thinner part. When unequal thickness is unavoidable, use a **tapered transition** with slope $\leq 1:4$.

> **Weld Position Accessibility (4 marks):**
> Welding can be performed in four positions, each with very different difficulty and cost:
>
> | Position | Difficulty | Cost Multiplier |
> |---|---|---|
> | Flat (1G/1F) | Easiest | 1× |
> | Horizontal (2F/2G) | Moderate | 1.3× |
> | Vertical (3F/3G) | Difficult | 1.5× |
> | Overhead (4F/4G) | Most difficult | 2× |
>
> **Design imperative:** Always design the weldment so that all welds can be made in the **flat (downhand) position**. This requires careful orientation of the parts and provision of **rotary fixtures** for large weldments. Welds in overhead or vertical positions are slower, more expensive, and produce lower-quality joints.

#### (b) Weldment Distortion and Shrinkage Allowance — 7 Marks

> **Causes of Distortion (2 marks):**
> Weldment distortion arises from the **localized intense heat** of the welding arc. As the weld metal and the surrounding **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** cool and contract, they pull the surrounding (cooler, rigid) metal with them, causing the entire structure to **bend, twist, or shrink**.

> **Prevention Strategies (2.5 marks):**
> 1. **Balanced welding:** Weld symmetrically about the neutral axis.
> 2. **Backstep technique:** Weld short segments in reverse direction to spread heat input.
> 3. **Skip welding:** Alternate weld beads along the joint.
> 4. **Presetting:** Tilt the parts *toward* each other by a calculated angle to compensate for shrinkage.
> 5. **Use intermittent welds** instead of continuous welds when full-strength continuous welds are not required.

> **Shrinkage Allowance Formula (2.5 marks):**
> The amount by which the part must be **oversized** before welding to compensate for shrinkage is:
>
> $$\Delta L = k \cdot L$$
>
> Where $k$ is the **shrinkage coefficient** (typically 0.001 to 0.002 for mild steel, per unit length) and $L$ is the length of the part.
>
> **Example:** For a 1000 mm long steel weldment, $\Delta L = 0.0015 \times 1000 = 1.5$ mm. The part must be cut 1.5 mm longer than the final required length.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing draft angles across processes:** Students often write a single draft angle value (e.g., 1°) and apply it to all processes. **KTU expects process-specific values** — sand casting needs 1–3°, die casting needs 0.5–1°, forging needs 3–7°. Loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units:** KTU examiners *do* deduct marks if units are missing in numerical answers. Always write "mm" or "°" explicitly.
>
> 3. **Incomplete bend allowance formula:** A common error is writing $BA = \pi \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)$ without the $\frac{1}{180°}$ conversion factor. The angle $\theta$ **must** be in degrees, hence the conversion. This is a 1–2 mark penalty.
>
> 4. **Missing the "why":** KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes **understanding the reason** behind a rule, not just stating the rule. If asked "why is uniform wall thickness important in castings?", simply writing "to avoid defects" is insufficient. You must say: "Uniform wall thickness ensures **uniform cooling rates**, which prevents **hot spots**, **shrinkage cavities**, and **thermal stresses**."
>
> 5. **Skipping sketches:** For 14-mark questions, a **labelled diagram or flowchart** is essentially mandatory. A text-only answer, no matter how correct, typically receives only 10–11 out of 14 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up K-factor and material factor k:** The **K-factor** is a *specific sheet-metal-bending* term (0.33 to 0.5). The **k-factor** (lowercase) in the minimum bend radius formula is a *material-specific* value (0.3 to 1.0). Do not interchange them.
>
> 7. **Forgetting process-specific terminology:** Saying "fillets" in the context of welding is incorrect. In welding, the analogous feature is a **"weld toe"** or **"weld reinforcement"**. Use the correct terminology for the correct process.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Design Guidelines**

### 🎯 Casting Design — Key Points
- ✅ **Uniform wall thickness** — single most important rule ($t_{max}/t_{min} \leq 1.5$).
- ✅ **Fillet formula:** $R_f = (a+b)/2 + R$.
- ✅ **Hot spots** → shrinkage cavities → use cores, chills, risers.
- ✅ **Draft angles:** 0.5° to 3° (process-dependent).
- ✅ Avoid large flat surfaces, isolated bosses, sharp internal corners.

### 🎯 Forging Design — Key Points
- ✅ **Symmetry** is the #1 rule for forgings.
- ✅ **Forging ratio:** $F_r = A_{billet}/A_{forged}$; aim for $F_r \geq 2.5$ for stressed parts.
- ✅ **Draft angles:** 3° to 7° (open die), 0.5° to 1.5° (closed die).
- ✅ Use **larger fillets** than castings.
- ✅ Avoid deep, narrow ribs (do not fill properly).
- ✅ "Ideal forging fits inside a cylinder" heuristic.

### 🎯 Machining Design — Key Points
- ✅ **Tool access, tool clearance, workpiece clamping** — the three pillars.
- ✅ **Drilling:** depth $\leq 4D$, edge distance $\geq 1.5D$, spacing $\geq 2.5D$.
- ✅ **Pockets** should be open, with corner radii matching the cutter.
- ✅ **Shaft** length-to-diameter $\leq 10:1$.
- ✅ Cost is dominated by **cycle time** — minimize features.

### 🎯 Welding Design — Key Points
- ✅ **Equal section thickness** ($t_{max}/t_{min} \leq 2$); taper transition if not.
- ✅ **Flat position** is preferred (1× cost vs 2× for overhead).
- ✅ **Weld length** $\geq 10\times$ leg size; minimum leg size = 3 mm.
- ✅ **Stagger** welds on opposite sides of a member.
- ✅ **Shrinkage allowance:** $\Delta L = k \cdot L$ ($k = 0.001$ to $0.002$ for steel).
- ✅ **Minimum number of parts, minimum weld length, simple design.**

### 🎯 Plastic Molding Design — Key Points
- ✅ **Uniform wall thickness** is THE golden rule.
- ✅ **Rib thickness** = 0.5–0.6 × wall thickness.
- ✅ **Boss OD** = 2–2.5 × hole diameter.
- ✅ **Internal radius** $\geq 0.5 \times$ wall thickness.
- ✅ **Draft angle:** 0.5° to 2° (more for textured surfaces).
- ✅ **External radius** = 1.5 × internal radius.

### 🎯 Sheet Metal Design — Key Points
- ✅ **Minimum bend radius:** $R_{min} = k \cdot t$.
- ✅ **K-factor** = position of neutral axis / thickness (0.33 for Al, 0.44 for steel).
- ✅ **Bend allowance:** $BA = (\pi \cdot \theta / 180°) \times (R + K \cdot t)$.
- ✅ **Flat pattern length:** $L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 + BA$.
- ✅ **Hole from edge:** $\geq 1.5t$; **Hole from bend:** $\geq 2t + R$.
- ✅ **Flange length:** $\geq R + 1.5t$.
- ✅ **Bend perpendicular to rolling direction** (avoids cracking).

### 🎯 Universal DFM Principles (Process-Independent)
- ✅ **Simplify** — fewer parts.
- ✅ **Standardize** — standard sizes, standard tolerances.
- ✅ **Minimize operations** — combine features.
- ✅ **Avoid special tools** — use standard tooling.
- ✅ **Tolerancing for function, not for habit** — loosest tolerance that works.
- ✅ **Design for the process** — know the process *before* finalizing the design.
- ✅ **Symmetry** — easier to fixture, machine, inspect.
- ✅ **Accessibility** — every feature must be reachable.
- ✅ **Inspectability** — provide datum surfaces.

### 🎯 Tolerance-Cost Relationship
- ✅ **Exponential**, not linear.
- ✅ Halving tolerance → ~4× cost increase.
- ✅ Always specify the **loosest tolerance the function allows**.

### 🎯 Process Selection (Quick Reference)
- ✅ **High volume + complex geometry + metal** → Die casting / closed-die forging.
- ✅ **High volume + simple geometry** → Sheet metal stamping.
- ✅ **High volume + plastic** → Injection moulding.
- ✅ **Low volume + high strength** → Open-die forging + machining.
- ✅ **Prototype / one-off** → Machining from bar stock / 3D printing.
- ✅ **Complex internal cavity** → Investment casting.
- ✅ **Permanent joint + high strength** → Welding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark KTU question on design guidelines, structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition** (2 marks) — State the rule/guideline clearly.
> 2. **Reason/Justification** (3 marks) — Explain WHY the rule exists.
> 3. **Numerical values** (3 marks) — Provide the specific dimensions/angles/tolerances.
> 4. **Sketch/Diagram** (3 marks) — A neat labelled diagram.
> 5. **Example or Exception** (3 marks) — Show a worked example or note where the rule does not apply.
>
> Following this structure typically guarantees **12+ marks out of 14**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Example Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEMRT522 – Design of Manufacture (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Example Application"** refers to a fully worked, end-to-end **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA) case study** in which the design rules, cost equations, and process-selection logic taught across the earlier modules are applied to a *single, real engineering component* (e.g., a bracket, lever, housing, or shaft). It is the **synthesis problem** of the course — the place where individual rules (draft angles, fillet radii, tolerance stack-up, minimum wall thickness, machining allowances) are integrated into one coherent, optimised design solution that satisfies **functional requirements, manufacturing feasibility, and cost targets simultaneously**.

Mathematically, the goal of such an application is to minimise the *Total Life-Cycle Cost* function:

$$
C_{total} \;=\; C_{material} \;+\; C_{manufacturing} \;+\; C_{assembly} \;+\; C_{rejection}
$$

subject to the design constraints (strength, stiffness, tolerance, weight, ergonomics, and process capability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** A "Design of Manufacture" Example Application is **not** a theory question. It is a numerical/analytical synthesis problem in which the student must (i) identify DFM violations in a given component, (ii) propose a redesigned version, (iii) justify the redesign using at least **three** DFM rules, and (iv) compute a quantitative cost or tolerance improvement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Diagnosis"

Think of an Example Application like a **doctor examining a patient**:

| Step | Doctor's Clinic | DFM Example Application |
|------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Patient arrives with symptoms | A 2-D drawing of a poorly-designed part is given |
| 2 | Doctor runs tests (BP, X-ray) | Engineer audits the drawing against DFM checklists |
| 3 | Doctor prescribes medicine/surgery | Engineer redesigns the part (fillets, draft, uniform walls) |
| 4 | Cost of treatment is estimated | Cost saving is computed ($$\Delta C$$) |
| 5 | Patient is cured | Part is now **castable, machinable, and assemblable** at minimum cost |

Just as a medical student learns more from *one* well-worked case than from a thousand lectures, KTU expects the student to master *one* completely solved DFM example because it integrates **every rule from every module**.

## 1.3 Scope of the Worked Example (Module 4 Context)

The Module-4 Example Application will integrate:

- **Design for Sand Casting** (uniform wall thickness, draft, fillets, parting line, cores).
- **Design for Machining** (machining allowances, tolerance allocation, tool access).
- **Design for Assembly (DFA)** (minimum part count, fastening direction, symmetry).
- **Cost estimation** (material, machining, setup, rejection cost).
- **Tolerance stack-up analysis** (worst-case and statistical/RSS).

> [!NOTE]
> The standard unit assumptions used throughout the KTU valuation key are: **Cost in ₹ (Indian Rupees)**, dimensions in **mm**, tolerances in **± mm**, and process capability in **6σ = 6 × standard deviation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DFM Audit Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

When you are given a component drawing, the audit proceeds in **seven logical stages**. Every KTU valuation key expects this order:

1. **Functional Decomposition** – Identify load path, mating features, datum surfaces.
2. **Process Selection** – Decide primary shaping process (casting / forging / sheet metal / machining from billet) using the material, batch size, and complexity.
3. **DFM Rule Audit** – Tick off the checklist for the *selected* process.
4. **Identify Violations** – Sharp internal corners, uneven walls, deep pockets, wrong parting line, no draft, unmachinable pockets.
5. **Redesign** – Apply the corrective rule to each violation.
6. **Cost & Tolerance Recalculation** – Quantify the improvement.
7. **DFA Check** – Can the part count be reduced? Can assembly be simplified?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind every rule:** Every DFM rule exists to either **(a) eliminate a defect mode** (porosity, shrinkage, warpage, tool breakage) or **(b) reduce cycle time / cost**. The valuation examiner rewards answers that quote the *root cause*, not just the rule.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet) — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $$C_{piece}=C_{material}+C_{setup}+C_{machining}+C_{rej}$$ | Total cost per piece (₹) | Cost comparison between designs |
| 2 | $$C_{setup}=\dfrac{K_{setup}}{N}$$ | Setup cost amortised over batch size N | Identifies breakeven batch |
| 3 | $$C_{machining}=t_{m}\cdot R_{m}+C_{tool}$$ | Machining cost = (cycle time × machine rate) + tool cost | Process optimisation |
| 4 | $$C_{material}=V\cdot \rho \cdot C_u$$ | Material cost = Volume × density × unit cost | Material substitution studies |
| 5 | $$C_{rej}=P_{rej}\cdot C_{piece}$$ | Rejection cost = Probability of rejection × piece cost | Quality economics |
| 6 | $$T_{wc}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}T_{i}$$ | Worst-case tolerance stack-up (additive) | Safety-critical assemblies |
| 7 | $$T_{stat}=\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}T_{i}^{2}}$$ | Statistical (RSS) tolerance stack-up | Normal-distribution assemblies |
| 8 | $$C_{pk}=\min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$ | Process capability index | Tolerance feasibility check |
| 9 | $$\sigma_{process}=\dfrac{T_{process}}{6}$$ | Std. deviation from a known process tolerance | Tolerance vs capability study |
| 10 | $$\Delta C \;=\; \dfrac{C_{original}-C_{redesign}}{C_{original}}\times 100\%$$ | Percentage cost saving | Final answer in KTU design problems |
| 11 | $$\text{Min. wall thickness (cast iron)}\geq 6\text{ mm}$$ | Casting rule (rough) | Wall-thickness audit |
| 12 | $$\text{Draft angle}\geq 1^{\circ}\text{ to }3^{\circ}$$ | Casting / forging rule | Pattern drawing audit |
| 13 | $$\text{Inside fillet radius}\geq \dfrac{1}{3}\text{ to }\dfrac{1}{2}\times \text{adjacent wall}$$ | Casting/fillet rule | Stress + castability |
| 14 | $$\text{Machining allowance (sand cast)}\approx 1.5\text{–}3\text{ mm}$$ | Machining stock | Cost & process selection |
| 15 | $$\text{DFA index}=\dfrac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}}\times 100\%$$ | Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA index | Assembly efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $$K_{setup}$$ = setup cost (₹), $$N$$ = batch size, $$t_m$$ = machining time (min), $$R_m$$ = machine rate (₹/min), $$C_{tool}$$ = tool cost (₹), $$V$$ = volume (m³ or cm³), $$\rho$$ = density (kg/m³ or g/cm³), $$C_u$$ = unit cost (₹/kg), $$P_{rej}$$ = rejection probability, $$USL/LSL$$ = upper/lower spec limits, $$\mu$$ = process mean, $$\sigma$$ = process standard deviation, $$N_{min}$$ = theoretical minimum part count.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DFM Example Application is the *core competency* expected of a **Production / Design Engineer** in Indian manufacturing industries such as Tata Motors, Bharat Forge, Hindalco, Mahindra, and Kalyani Group. Every new product launch (a bracket for an EV, a steering knuckle, an inverter housing) is preceded by exactly such an audit — failure to perform it leads to the well-documented **"Design–Manufacture Mismatch"** that costs Indian SMEs an estimated **3–8 % of revenue per year** in rework and rejection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Reference Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A **Hinge Bracket** for a machine tool is currently produced by *sand casting* followed by limited machining on the bearing bore and the two mounting pads. The existing drawing (call it **Design A**) has the following manufacturing violations observed by the production engineer:
> 1. Non-uniform wall thickness (varies from **8 mm to 25 mm**).
> 2. Sharp internal corners (0 mm radius) at the rib–wall junction.
> 3. A deep pocket (depth **40 mm**, opening **20 mm × 20 mm**).
> 4. No draft angle on vertical faces.
> 5. Tight tolerance (±0.05 mm) specified on the entire rib – unnecessarily strict.
> 6. Parting line passes through a critical machined bore.
>
> Batch size $$N = 1000$$ pieces/year. You are required to **(a)** apply DFM rules to redesign the part (call it **Design B**), **(b)** compute the cost saving per piece, and **(c)** verify the tolerance specification using process capability.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1 — Process & Material Confirmation

- **Material:** Grey Cast Iron (Grade FG 200), $$\rho = 7200 \text{ kg/m}^3$$, $$C_u = ₹120/\text{kg}$$.
- **Primary process:** Sand casting (justified by complex shape and moderate batch size).
- **Secondary process:** Finish machining of two mounting pads + one bearing bore.
- This step is worth **1 mark** in KTU for explicitly stating the process rationale.

#### Step 2 — DFM Rule Audit & Redesign Mapping

| S.No. | Violation in Design A | DFM Rule Applied | Corrective Action in Design B |
|-------|----------------------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Wall thickness varies 8–25 mm | **Uniform wall thickness** (max-min ≤ 2:1 ratio) | Redesign to a uniform **12 mm** wall with **ribbed reinforcements** |
| 2 | Sharp internal corner at rib–wall | **Fillet radius ≥ $$\tfrac{1}{3}$$ × wall thickness** | Provide $$R = 5 \text{ mm}$$ internal fillet |
| 3 | Deep pocket 40 × 20 × 20 mm | **Max pocket depth ≤ 2 × opening width** | Reduce pocket depth to **30 mm** and widen opening to **30 × 30 mm** |
| 4 | No draft on vertical faces | **Draft angle ≥ 1° for sand casting** | Provide **1.5°** draft on all vertical faces |
| 5 | ±0.05 mm on rib (irrelevant feature) | **Allocate tolerance by function (principle of independence)** | Relax rib tolerance to **±0.5 mm** (as-cast) |
| 6 | Parting line through machined bore | **Parting line must avoid machined surfaces** | Shift parting line to the **flange edge** |

Each row above is worth up to **1 mark** in a 7-mark sub-question (rule + justification + corrective value).

#### Step 3 — Cost Calculation (The Numerical Core)

**3.3.1 Design A (Original) — Cost per Piece**

Let:
- Cast weight of Design A: $$W_A = 2.4 \text{ kg}$$ (with thick sections, risers, runners).
- Machining time: $$t_{m,A} = 12 \text{ min}$$ (deep pocket + 3 datum changes).
- Setup cost: $$K_{setup} = ₹1500$$.
- Machine rate: $$R_m = ₹8/\text{min}$$.
- Tool cost per piece: $$C_{tool,A} = ₹30$$.
- Rejection rate: $$P_{rej,A} = 0.08$$ (8 % — high because of shrinkage at thick sections).

$$
C_{material,A} \;=\; W_A \cdot C_u \;=\; 2.4 \times 120 \;=\; ₹288
$$

$$
C_{setup,A} \;=\; \dfrac{K_{setup}}{N} \;=\; \dfrac{1500}{1000} \;=\; ₹1.5
$$

$$
C_{machining,A} \;=\; t_{m,A}\cdot R_m + C_{tool,A} \;=\; 12\times 8 + 30 \;=\; ₹126
$$

$$
C_{rej,A} \;=\; P_{rej,A}\cdot (C_{material,A}+C_{setup,A}+C_{machining,A})
$$

$$
C_{rej,A} \;=\; 0.08 \times (288+1.5+126) \;=\; 0.08 \times 415.5 \;=\; ₹33.24
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{piece,A} \;=\; 288+1.5+126+33.24 \;=\; ₹448.74\,}
$$

**3.3.2 Design B (Redesigned) — Cost per Piece**

After redesign:
- Cast weight drops to $$W_B = 1.7 \text{ kg}$$ (thinner uniform walls, less riser metal).
- Machining time drops to $$t_{m,B} = 6 \text{ min}$$ (shallow pocket, single setup).
- Rejection rate drops to $$P_{rej,B} = 0.02$$ (2 % — uniform wall eliminates shrinkage).
- Tool cost drops to $$C_{tool,B} = ₹15$$ (no deep-pocket tool).

$$
C_{material,B} \;=\; 1.7 \times 120 \;=\; ₹204
$$

$$
C_{setup,B} \;=\; ₹1.5 \text{ (unchanged for same batch)}
$$

$$
C_{machining,B} \;=\; 6 \times 8 + 15 \;=\; ₹63
$$

$$
C_{rej,B} \;=\; 0.02 \times (204+1.5+63) \;=\; 0.02 \times 268.5 \;=\; ₹5.37
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{piece,B} \;=\; 204+1.5+63+5.37 \;=\; ₹273.87\,}
$$

**3.3.3 Cost Saving — Final Numerical Answer**

$$
\Delta C_{piece} \;=\; C_{piece,A}-C_{piece,B} \;=\; 448.74-273.87 \;=\; ₹174.87 \text{ per piece}
$$

$$
\Delta C_{annual} \;=\; 174.87 \times 1000 \;=\; ₹1{,}74{,}870 \text{ per year}
$$

$$
\Delta C_{\%} \;=\; \dfrac{174.87}{448.74}\times 100 \;\approx\; 38.97\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Percentage cost saving} \;\approx\; 39\%\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Always present the cost calculation in a **labelled table** with the five line items (material, setup, machining, tool, rejection). Examiners award part-marks for *each* correctly-computed line — never combine them into a single number.

#### Step 4 — Tolerance Stack-Up Verification (Process Capability)

**Specification of the bearing bore in Design A:** $$\phi 30 \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$$.

**Process selected:** Finish boring on a standard lathe.
- Mean bore diameter $$\mu = 30.00 \text{ mm}$$.
- Process standard deviation $$\sigma = 0.012 \text{ mm}$$ (from historical SPC data).
- Therefore $$6\sigma = 0.072 \text{ mm}$$, so the *natural* process tolerance is **±0.036 mm** (i.e. $$USL - LSL = 0.072$$).

**Specification width:** $$USL - LSL = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$.

**Process Capability Index $$C_p$$:**

$$
C_p \;=\; \dfrac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma} \;=\; \dfrac{0.10}{0.072} \;\approx\; 1.39
$$

**Process Capability Index $$C_{pk}$$ (assuming centred process):**

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; C_p \;\approx\; 1.39
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU standard):** A process is **capable** for an existing part only if $$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$$ (industrially accepted) and **highly capable** if $$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$$. Here $$C_{pk} = 1.39 \ge 1.33$$ → *barely capable*. **Hence the ±0.05 mm specification is feasible but the process margin is low** — a tolerance *relaxation* to ±0.10 mm would be strongly recommended in Design B, *and* this is exactly the DFM tolerance principle applied.

#### Step 5 — DFA (Design for Assembly) Cross-Check

Original assembly uses **6 parts** (bracket + 2 bolts + 2 washers + 1 nut) — an unnecessarily split design.

**Redesign (Design B + DFA):**
- Combine the bracket with the original *separate* washer boss into **one** cast feature.
- Use a **captive** (self-retaining) bolt to eliminate the separate washer.

$$
N_{actual} = 6 \quad\longrightarrow\quad N_{min} = 4
$$

$$
\text{DFA}_{old}=\dfrac{4}{6}\times100\%=66.7\% \quad\longrightarrow\quad \text{DFA}_{new}=\dfrac{4}{4}\times100\%=100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Boothroyd's DFA criterion is met **fully** in Design B.

#### Step 6 — Final Redesign Summary Table (Board-Exam Ready)

| Performance Metric | Design A (Original) | Design B (Redesigned) | Improvement |
|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| Cast weight (kg) | 2.40 | 1.70 | **−29.2 %** |
| Machining time (min) | 12 | 6 | **−50.0 %** |
| Rejection rate | 8 % | 2 % | **−75.0 %** |
| Cost / piece (₹) | 448.74 | 273.87 | **−39.0 %** |
| $$C_{pk}$$ of bore | 1.39 (marginal) | 1.67+ (highly capable) | Process margin ↑ |
| DFA index | 66.7 % | 100 % | +33.3 % |
| DFM rules violated | 6 | 0 | **All corrected** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The DFM Example Application Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Component Drawing Given] --> B0[Step 1: Functional & Material Analysis]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2: Primary Process Selection]
    C0 --> D0{Step 3: DFM Rule Audit}
    D0 --> E0[Wall Thickness Check]
    D0 --> F0[Fillet & Corner Check]
    D0 --> G0[Draft Angle Check]
    D0 --> H0[Parting Line Check]
    D0 --> I0[Tolerance Allocation Check]
    D0 --> J0[DFA Part-Count Check]
    E0 --> K0[Step 4: Compile Violation List]
    F0 --> K0
    G0 --> K0
    H0 --> K0
    I0 --> K0
    J0 --> K0
    K0 --> L0[Step 5: Redesign each Violation]
    L0 --> M0[Step 6: Cost Recalculation for Redesign]
    M0 --> N0[Step 7: Tolerance Cpk Verification]
    N0 --> O0[Step 8: Final Comparison Table]
    O0 --> P0[End: Deliver Cost & Quality Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The audit is *parallel* for the six DFM rules (E0–J0) but the *redesign* and *costing* (L0–O0) are sequential because each redesign iteration affects the next. This is the **order** a KTU examiner expects in the answer script.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Function ↔ Manufacturing Constraint Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FUNC[Functional Requirements]
      F1[Load Bearing 5 kN]
      F2[Mounting on Frame]
      F3[Rotating Shaft Ø30 fits]
    end
    subgraph MFG[Manufacturing Constraints]
      M1[Sand Casting Feasible?]
      M2[Machining of Bore feasible?]
      M3[Batch 1000: Setup Amortised?]
    end
    subgraph DFM[DFM Rules Triggered]
      R1[Uniform Walls 12 mm]
      R2[Draft 1.5 deg]
      R3[Fillet R5 Internal]
      R4[Bore Tol plus minus 0.10 mm]
    end
    F1 --> M1 --> R1
    F2 --> M1 --> R2
    F3 --> M2 --> R3
    F3 --> M2 --> R4
    M3 --> M1
    M3 --> M2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Block — Design A vs Design B

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AROW[Design A Original]
      A1[Sharp Corners 0 mm]
      A2[Wall 8 to 25 mm nonuniform]

      A5[Tol plus minus 0.05 mm over tight]
      A6[Cost 448.74 Rs]
    end
    subgraph BROW[Design B Redesigned]
      B1[Fillet R5 mm]
      B2[Uniform Wall 12 mm]
      B3[Shallow Pocket 30 mm]

      B6[Cost 273.87 Rs]
    end
    A1 -->|Apply Rule| B1
    A2 -->|Apply Rule| B2

    A4 -->|Apply Rule| B4
    A5 -->|Apply Rule| B5

```
