<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Environmentally Responsible Product Assessment (ERPA). Mention any two international standards governing it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Environmentally Responsible Product Assessment (ERPA) is a systematic, quantitative methodology used to evaluate the total environmental impact of a product across its entire life cycle — from raw material extraction to end-of-life disposal — with the objective of guiding design improvements. **(1.5 marks)**

The two governing international standards are:
1. **ISO 14040 / ISO 14044** — Framework and Requirements for Life Cycle Assessment. **(1 mark)**
2. **ISO 14001** — Environmental Management Systems (EMS). **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is a "Functional Unit" in LCA? Why is it critical to define it correctly?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Functional Unit (FU)** is the quantified performance of a product system that serves as the reference basis for all LCA input and output data normalization. Example: "transporting 1 passenger over 100 km" or "lighting a room of 50 m² for 10,000 hours." **(1.5 marks)**

Criticality:
- It ensures **comparability** between alternative designs that deliver the same function. **(0.75 mark)**
- Without a proper FU, LCAs of two products are mathematically incomparable and may lead to **wrong design decisions** (a known failure mode in industry — e.g., the paper-vs-plastic bag debate). **(0.75 mark)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **four mandatory phases of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** as per **ISO 14040**. State the purpose of each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A foundry process emits per ton of cast output: CO₂ = 1850 kg, CH₄ = 9 kg, N₂O = 0.4 kg. Use GWP100 factors (CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265). Calculate the **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** in kg CO₂-equivalent. If the same product is now made by machining from a billet that consumes 1200 kWh of grid electricity (grid factor 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh), compare the two GWP values and identify the more environmentally responsible route. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The four mandatory phases of LCA as per ISO 14040 are:

**Phase 1 — Goal and Scope Definition:** Establishes the purpose, the functional unit, system boundaries, and cut-off criteria. **Output:** Goal definition report. **(1.5 marks)**

**Phase 2 — Life Cycle Inventory (LCI):** Compilation and quantification of all inputs (energy, raw materials) and outputs (emissions, waste) throughout the life cycle. **Output:** LCI table per functional unit. **(1.5 marks)**

**Phase 3 — Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA):** Translates LCI data into impact categories using characterization factors. Steps: classification, characterization, normalization, weighting. **Output:** Impact category scores. **(1.5 marks)**

**Phase 4 — Interpretation:** Identifies significant issues, evaluates completeness/sensitivity/consistency, and provides conclusions and recommendations aligned with the goal. **(1.5 marks)**

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the block schematic — 1 mark for diagram)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Recall the LCIA characterization equation.**

$$ \text{GWP} = \sum_s \text{LCI}_s \times \text{GWP}_{100,s} $$

**Step 2 — Foundry route calculation.**

$$ \text{GWP}_{foundry} = (1850 \times 1) + (9 \times 28) + (0.4 \times 265) $$

$$ \text{GWP}_{foundry} = 1850 + 252 + 106 = 2208 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} $$

**(2.5 marks for full working)**

**Step 3 — Machining route calculation.**

$$ \text{GWP}_{machining} = 1200 \text{ kWh} \times 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kWh} $$

$$ \text{GWP}_{machining} = 984 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} $$

**(2 marks for full working)**

**Step 4 — Comparison and conclusion.**

$$ \Delta \text{GWP} = 2208 - 984 = 1224 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq (saving)} $$

$$ \text{Percentage reduction} = \frac{1224}{2208} \times 100 \approx 55.4\% $$

**Conclusion:** The machining route is more environmentally responsible in terms of GWP, with a **~55.4% reduction** in carbon footprint compared to the foundry route. **(1.5 marks for comparison + conclusion)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the final numbers and skip the intermediate product terms. **Each multiplication step must be shown explicitly.** A common error is forgetting the N₂O factor (which contributes 106 kg CO₂-eq — a non-trivial 4.8% of the total). Missing it loses 0.5 mark.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **Design for Environment (DfE)** concept. List the **9R / 8R waste hierarchy** strategies with one engineering example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two product alternatives have the following LCA profile per functional unit. Calculate the **single-score Eco-indicator value** using the given weights, and recommend the better design. **(7 marks)**

| Impact Category | Weight (Wᵢ) | Design P (Pt/unit) | Design Q (Pt/unit) |
|---|---|---|---|
| GWP100 | 0.30 | 28 | 35 |
| Acidification | 0.20 | 12 | 9 |
| Eutrophication | 0.20 | 18 | 22 |
| Human Toxicity | 0.30 | 22 | 15 |

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Design for Environment (DfE)** is the systematic integration of environmental considerations into product and process design with the objective of reducing the overall life cycle environmental burden. It applies eco-data (LCA, eco-indicators) to drive material, process, and architecture decisions early in the design phase. **(2 marks)**

**The 9R Hierarchy (in descending order of preference):** **(5 marks, 0.5 each for strategy + example)**

1. **Refuse** — Eliminate the function (e.g., removing a disposable coffee stirrer).
2. **Rethink** — Deliver function as service (e.g., Xerox leasing printers instead of selling).
3. **Reduce** — Light-weight automotive body panels.
4. **Reuse** — Refillable glass milk bottles.
5. **Repair** — Modular smartphones with replaceable batteries.
6. **Refurbish** — Remanufactured industrial compressors.
7. **Remanufacture** — Airbus re-engineering of aircraft engines.
8. **Recycle** — Closed-loop aluminium can recycling.
9. **Recover** — Waste-to-energy incineration with heat capture.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Single-score formula.**

$$ S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \times W_i $$

**Step 2 — Compute for Design P.**

$$ S_P = (28 \times 0.30) + (12 \times 0.20) + (18 \times 0.20) + (22 \times 0.30) $$

$$ S_P = 8.4 + 2.4 + 3.6 + 6.6 = 21.0 \text{ Pt} $$

**(2.5 marks for full working)**

**Step 3 — Compute for Design Q.**

$$ S_Q = (35 \times 0.30) + (9 \times 0.20) + (22 \times 0.20) + (15 \times 0.30) $$

$$ S_Q = 10.5 + 1.8 + 4.4 + 4.5 = 21.2 \text{ Pt} $$

**(2.5 marks for full working)**

**Step 4 — Comparison and recommendation.**

$$ \Delta S = S_Q - S_P = 21.2 - 21.0 = 0.2 \text{ Pt} $$

$$ \text{Percentage difference} = \frac{0.2}{21.0} \times 100 \approx 0.95\% $$

**Conclusion:** Design **P is marginally better** with a 0.2 Pt (≈0.95%) lower eco-indicator score. However, since the difference is less than 5%, the design team should re-examine the sensitivity of weights before making a final commitment. **(2 marks for conclusion + caveat)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 2:** Two common mistakes cost marks here:
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply each Eᵢ by its Wᵢ** — students sometimes sum the Eᵢ columns and compare raw sums, which is dimensionally meaningless when weights differ.
> 2. **Not stating the percentage difference** — KTU awards the "engineering interpretation" mark only when the student quantifies the magnitude, not just states "P is better."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ERPA = LCA + DfE + ISO 14001 EMS.** Do not conflate them.
- **ISO 14040 mandates exactly 4 LCA phases:** Goal & Scope → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation. Memorize in order.
- **Functional Unit (FU)** is non-negotiable. Always state it first in any LCA answer.
- **Characterization equation:** $E_i = \sum_s \text{LCI}_s \times CF_{s,i}$. Units are always **kg-eq per FU**.
- **9 impact categories** exist in the CML/ReCiPe baseline; a single substance can score in multiple categories.
- **GWP100 of CH₄ = 28**, **N₂O = 265**, **CO₂ = 1** (memorize these three — they appear every KTU exam).
- **Single-score weighting is normative, not scientific** — flag this caveat in your answers for full marks.
- **The 9R / 8R hierarchy** runs from *Refuse* (strongest) to *Recover* (weakest).
- **Use-phase dominance:** for energy-using products, use phase contributes 60–90% of total GWP.
- **Streamlined LCA (S-LCA)** is acceptable only for internal screening — never for public eco-claims.
- **Cradle-to-grave** = raw material → disposal. **Cradle-to-cradle** = raw material → recycled back as raw material (ideal circular design).
- **DFE principle:** *"Lock in low impact at the design stage; recovery at the disposal stage is too late."*
- **Always show each multiplication step** in numerical LCA problems — KTU examiners reward process discipline, not just final answers.
- **Units must be dimensionally consistent:** kg CO₂-eq ≠ kg SO₂-eq. Never add across impact categories.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weighted Sum Assessment Method
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Weighted Sum Assessment Method (WSAM)**, also widely referenced as the **Weighted Sum Model (WSM)** or **Simple Additive Weighting (SAW) Method**, is the most fundamental and widely deployed **Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM)** technique employed in engineering design evaluation. 

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme course *PEMRT522 – Design of Manufacture*, WSAM is formally defined as:

> A scoring-based decision support methodology in which a finite set of design alternatives is evaluated against a finite set of weighted design criteria, where the overall merit of each alternative is computed as the **linear additive aggregation** of its normalized performance scores multiplied by their corresponding criterion weights. The optimal design alternative is the one yielding the **maximum composite score**.

Mathematically, for an MCDM problem with $m$ alternatives and $n$ criteria, WSAM collapses the multi-dimensional evaluation vector into a **single scalar decision index** through weighted linear combination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Focus**
> 
> In *Design of Manufacture*, WSAM is positioned as a critical tool under the broader umbrella of **Conceptual Design Evaluation and Selection**. It allows a design engineer to rationally down-select from a large concept pool (often generated via TRIZ, Morphological Charts, or Brainstorming) to a single manufacturable, market-ready design proposal. It is also the **precursor / baseline** against which more advanced MCDM methods (e.g., AHP, TOPSIS, ELECTRE, PROMETHEE) are compared.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Shopping" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a supermarket to buy a laptop. You are flooded with 15 models, but you must pick **exactly one**. Each laptop is judged by:
- Performance (Speed)
- Price
- Battery Life
- Build Quality
- After-Sales Service

Now, you personally care more about **Performance** and **Battery Life** than about the *Service Center* being 50 km away. So you mentally assign *weights*:

| Criterion | Your Priority Weight |
| :--- | :---: |
| Performance | 0.35 |
| Battery Life | 0.30 |
| Build Quality | 0.15 |
| Price | 0.15 |
| After-Sales Service | 0.05 |

You rate every laptop on a 1–10 scale for each of these 5 things. You then multiply the rating by the weight and add them up. The laptop with the **highest total weighted score** is the one you buy.

**That is exactly the Weighted Sum Assessment Method** — only the "shopping" is happening in a design engineering office, and the "laptops" are competing design concepts for a gearbox, a robotic arm, a refrigerator compressor, or any other manufactured artifact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Designers Love WSAM**
> 1. It is **computationally trivial** — no eigenvalues, no iterative solvers, no fuzzy logic.
> 2. The output is **one single number per alternative**, which is unambiguous for management approval.
> 3. It forces the design team to **explicitly debate and document** the importance of each criterion — this debate itself is often more valuable than the final score.

## 1.3 Underlying Assumptions (Mandatory KTU Question)

For the weighted sum aggregation to be **mathematically valid** (i.e., for the linear combination to produce a meaningful scalar), the following assumptions **must** hold:

1. **Additivity of Preferences:** The total utility of a design alternative is the sum of the partial utilities contributed by each criterion.
2. **Independence of Criteria (Mutual Preferential Independence):** The preference for performance on one criterion does not depend on the performance level on another criterion.
3. **Common Scale / Normalization:** All criteria scores must be transformed to a **common dimensionless scale** (typically [0, 1]) before aggregation; otherwise, a criterion measured in megapixels could dominate a criterion measured in rupees.
4. **Weight Closure:** $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$, where $w_j \geq 0$ for all $j$.

> [!WARNING]
> If the criteria are **not independent** (e.g., "Strength" and "Weight" of a chassis are often inversely correlated), the simple additive model can produce **rank reversals**. In such cases, KTU examiners expect students to mention that **AHP or TOPSIS** would be more appropriate — this is a frequent 3-mark question.

## 1.4 Role of WSAM in the Engineering Design Process

WSAM sits firmly inside the **Conceptual Design Phase** of Pahl & Beitz's systematic design methodology, which is a prescribed part of the KTU 2024 Manufacturing Design syllabus:

$$\text{Need Identification} \rightarrow \text{Problem Clarification} \rightarrow \text{Functions \& Specifications} \rightarrow \text{Concept Generation} \rightarrow \boxed{\textbf{Concept Evaluation (WSAM)}} \rightarrow \text{Embodiment Design} \rightarrow \text{Detail Design}$$

Without a defensible evaluation method, concept generation is wasted effort. WSAM provides the **defensibility**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Mathematical Framework

Consider an MCDM problem with the following defined sets and matrices:

- **Set of Alternatives:** $A = \{A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots, A_m\}$
- **Set of Evaluation Criteria:** $C = \{C_1, C_2, C_3, \ldots, C_n\}$
- **Weight Vector:** $W = \{w_1, w_2, w_3, \ldots, w_n\}$, where $w_j$ is the relative importance of criterion $C_j$
- **Raw Decision Matrix:** $X = [x_{ij}]_{m \times n}$, where $x_{ij}$ is the raw performance rating of alternative $A_i$ against criterion $C_j$
- **Normalized Decision Matrix:** $R = [r_{ij}]_{m \times n}$, where $r_{ij} \in [0, 1]$

### 2.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Decision Matrix

Construct the $m \times n$ raw score matrix by tabulating the engineering measurement or subjective rating of each alternative against each criterion.

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix}
x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1n} \\
x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
x_{m1} & x_{m2} & \cdots & x_{mn}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.1.2 Step 2 — Classify Criteria by Direction

Before normalization, each criterion $C_j$ must be declared as one of two types:

- **Benefit Criterion (B):** Higher raw value = better design. *Examples:* strength, efficiency, battery life, customer rating.
- **Cost Criterion (C):** Lower raw value = better design. *Examples:* manufacturing cost, weight, emissions, cycle time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Applying the wrong normalization direction (e.g., dividing by max for a cost criterion) is the **single most common mistake** in WSAM exam answers. Always re-check the criterion direction.

### 2.1.3 Step 3 — Normalize the Decision Matrix

**For Benefit Criteria (higher is better):**

$$
r_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij}}{\max_{i}(x_{ij})}
$$

**For Cost Criteria (lower is better):**

$$
r_{ij} = \frac{\min_{i}(x_{ij})}{x_{ij}}
$$

The denominator for benefit criteria is the **column maximum**; for cost criteria, it is the **column minimum**. After normalization, all entries lie in the interval $[0, 1]$, and the "best" alternative for each criterion scores exactly $1.0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Normalization Schemes** (acceptable per KTU syllabus)
> - **Linear Max-Min Scaling:** $r_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij} - \min_i x_{ij}}{\max_i x_{ij} - \min_i x_{ij}}$
> - **Vector Normalization:** $r_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij}}{\sqrt{\sum_i x_{ij}^2}}$
> 
> However, the **Max / Min** scheme above is the **default and most-tested** in KTU 2024 ESE papers.

### 2.1.4 Step 4 — Construct and Validate the Weight Vector

The weights $w_j$ reflect the **relative importance** of each criterion and are determined by one of:

| Weighting Method | When Used |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Direct Rating (Subjective)** | Small design team, single expert, fast turnaround |
| **AHP (Saaty's Pairwise Comparison)** | Conflicting expert opinions, high-stakes decisions |
| **Delphi Method** | Multi-stakeholder consensus, government / safety-critical |
| **Entropy Method (Objective)** | Large data sets, criteria weights derived from data variance |
| **Swing Weighting** | Trade-off-based weight assignment for portfolio decisions |

Regardless of method, the **closure condition** must hold:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1, \quad w_j \geq 0 \ \forall j
$$

### 2.1.5 Step 5 — Compute the Weighted Sum Score

For each alternative $A_i$, the Weighted Sum Assessment score is computed as the **scalar dot product** of its normalized row vector and the weight vector:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

In compact matrix form:

$$
S = R \cdot W^T
$$

where $S$ is an $m \times 1$ column vector of final scores.

### 2.1.6 Step 6 — Rank and Select

Rank the alternatives in **descending order** of $S_i$. The optimal design is:

$$
A^* = \arg\max_{i} (S_i)
$$

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table. Every WSAM numerical in KTU 2024 ESE reduces to these equations.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Meaning | Boundary Conditions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Weight Closure | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$ | $w_j$ = weight of $j$-th criterion | $w_j \geq 0$ |
| 2 | Normalization (Benefit) | $r_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij}}{\max_i x_{ij}}$ | $r_{ij} \in [0, 1]$ | Higher raw = better |
| 3 | Normalization (Cost) | $r_{ij} = \dfrac{\min_i x_{ij}}{x_{ij}}$ | $r_{ij} \in [0, 1]$ | Lower raw = better |
| 4 | WSAM Score | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | $S_i$ = merit index of $A_i$ | $0 \leq S_i \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Matrix Form | $S = R \cdot W^T$ | $S \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ | $R \in [0,1]^{m \times n}$ |
| 6 | Optimal Selection | $A^* = \arg\max_i S_i$ | $A^*$ = best alternative | Unique if scores distinct |
| 7 | Max Possible Score | $S_{\max} = 1$ | Achievable only if one alt. is best in all $n$ criteria | Theoretical upper bound |
| 8 | Linear Max-Min Scale | $r_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij} - \min_i x_{ij}}{\max_i x_{ij} - \min_i x_{ij}}$ | Used when zero is not the natural baseline | $r_{ij} \in [0,1]$ |
| 9 | Geometric Mean Form (Optional) | $S_i^{geom} = \prod_{j=1}^{n} (r_{ij})^{w_j}$ | Used when trade-offs are multiplicative | Compensatory |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Weighted Sum Assessment Method is not a classroom exercise — it is **industry workhorse**. Concrete applications include:

- **Automotive OEM Concept Selection:** Comparing 5–10 drivetrain architectures on criteria like cost, NVH, fuel economy, weight, and manufacturability.
- **Tool Material Selection in Manufacturing:** Evaluating HSS, Carbide, Ceramic, and CBN inserts based on hardness, wear resistance, cost, and thermal stability.
- **Supplier Selection:** Procurement teams scoring vendors on price, quality, lead time, and ESG compliance.
- **Sustainable Manufacturing:** Comparing process routes (machining vs. additive vs. forming) on energy, scrap, lead time, and cost.
- **Product Portfolio Management:** R&D prioritizing new product ideas on market size, technical feasibility, and IP risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compensatory vs. Non-Compensatory Behavior**
> 
> WSAM is a **fully compensatory** method. This means a very poor score on one criterion can be "compensated" by an excellent score on another. For example, a design with low safety (0.2) but exceptional low cost (1.0) and high performance (1.0) under weights (0.5, 0.25, 0.25) would still score $S = 0.5(0.2) + 0.25(1.0) + 0.25(1.0) = 0.6$ — which might look acceptable despite the safety violation. In **safety-critical** applications, this is a serious flaw, and **non-compensatory** methods (e.g., min-max or lexicographic) are mandated instead. KTU examiners love this nuance question.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 KTU-Style Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (Frequently asked in KTU 2024 ESE):**
> 
> A manufacturing company is designing a **new pedal assembly for an electric two-wheeler**. Four alternative design concepts $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4$ have been generated and are to be evaluated on **three criteria**:
> - $C_1$: Manufacturing Cost (in ₹) → **Cost Criterion** (lower is better)
> - $C_2$: Fatigue Life (in $10^4$ cycles) → **Benefit Criterion** (higher is better)
> - $C_3$: Ease of Assembly (rated 1–10 by an expert panel) → **Benefit Criterion** (higher is better)
> 
> The design team has assigned weights: $w_1 = 0.45$, $w_2 = 0.35$, $w_3 = 0.20$. Using the **Weighted Sum Assessment Method**, determine the optimal design concept and rank all alternatives.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Construct the Raw Decision Matrix

From engineering analysis and expert ratings, the raw scores are:

$$
X = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
& C_1 \text{ (Cost ₹)} & C_2 \text{ (Life} \times 10^4) & C_3 \text{ (Assembly)} \\
\hline
A_1 & 240 & 12 & 8 \\
A_2 & 310 & 15 & 6 \\
A_3 & 180 & 9 & 7 \\
A_4 & 270 & 14 & 9 \\
\end{array}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Identify Criterion Directions and Extract Min/Max

From the matrix:

- For $C_1$ (Cost, lower is better): $\min_i x_{i1} = 180$ (achieved by $A_3$)
- For $C_2$ (Life, higher is better): $\max_i x_{i2} = 15$ (achieved by $A_2$)
- For $C_3$ (Assembly, higher is better): $\max_i x_{i3} = 9$ (achieved by $A_4$)

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Apply the Appropriate Normalization

**Criterion $C_1$ (Cost Criterion) — use $\min / x_{ij}$:**

$$
r_{i1} = \frac{180}{x_{i1}}
$$

Computing each:

$$
r_{11} = \frac{180}{240} = 0.7500
$$

$$
r_{21} = \frac{180}{310} = 0.5806
$$

$$
r_{31} = \frac{180}{180} = 1.0000
$$

$$
r_{41} = \frac{180}{270} = 0.6667
$$

**Criterion $C_2$ (Benefit Criterion) — use $x_{ij} / \max$:**

$$
r_{i2} = \frac{x_{i2}}{15}
$$

Computing each:

$$
r_{12} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.8000
$$

$$
r_{22} = \frac{15}{15} = 1.0000
$$

$$
r_{32} = \frac{9}{15} = 0.6000
$$

$$
r_{42} = \frac{14}{15} = 0.9333
$$

**Criterion $C_3$ (Benefit Criterion) — use $x_{ij} / \max = 9$:**

$$
r_{i3} = \frac{x_{i3}}{9}
$$

Computing each:

$$
r_{13} = \frac{8}{9} = 0.8889
$$

$$
r_{23} = \frac{6}{9} = 0.6667
$$

$$
r_{33} = \frac{7}{9} = 0.7778
$$

$$
r_{43} = \frac{9}{9} = 1.0000
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Construct the Normalized Decision Matrix

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
0.7500 & 0.8000 & 0.8889 \\
0.5806 & 1.0000 & 0.6667 \\
1.0000 & 0.6000 & 0.7778 \\
0.6667 & 0.9333 & 1.0000
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Sanity Check:** Every column contains at least one entry equal to $1.0$ (the normalized maximum), confirming our min/max extraction is correct.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Verify Weight Closure

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{3} w_j = 0.45 + 0.35 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Compute Weighted Sum Score for Each Alternative

**For $A_1$:**

$$
S_1 = (0.45 \times 0.7500) + (0.35 \times 0.8000) + (0.20 \times 0.8889)
$$

$$
S_1 = 0.3375 + 0.2800 + 0.1778
$$

$$
\boxed{S_1 = 0.7953}
$$

**For $A_2$:**

$$
S_2 = (0.45 \times 0.5806) + (0.35 \times 1.0000) + (0.20 \times 0.6667)
$$

$$
S_2 = 0.2613 + 0.3500 + 0.1333
$$

$$
\boxed{S_2 = 0.7446}
$$

**For $A_3$:**

$$
S_3 = (0.45 \times 1.0000) + (0.35 \times 0.6000) + (0.20 \times 0.7778)
$$

$$
S_3 = 0.4500 + 0.2100 + 0.1556
$$

$$
\boxed{S_3 = 0.8156}
$$

**For $A_4$:**

$$
S_4 = (0.45 \times 0.6667) + (0.35 \times 0.9333) + (0.20 \times 1.0000)
$$

$$
S_4 = 0.3000 + 0.3267 + 0.2000
$$

$$
\boxed{S_4 = 0.8267}
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Rank and Select

$$
S_4 (0.8267) > S_3 (0.8156) > S_1 (0.7953) > S_2 (0.7446)
$$

**Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Alternative | Composite Score $S_i$ | Engineering Interpretation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **1** | $A_4$ | $0.8267$ | **OPTIMAL** — Best balance of cost, life, and assembly |
| 2 | $A_3$ | $0.8156$ | Cheapest, but poor fatigue life |
| 3 | $A_1$ | $0.7953$ | Mid-range on all three criteria |
| 4 | $A_2$ | $0.7446$ | Longest life, but costliest and hardest to assemble |

### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Sensitivity Analysis (Bonus KTU Point)

> [!TIP]
> A complete KTU 14-mark answer should include a **sensitivity check**. What if the cost weight is reduced from 0.45 to 0.30 and reallocated to fatigue life (now 0.50)? Re-evaluating:
> 
> $S_1 = 0.30(0.75) + 0.50(0.80) + 0.20(0.889) = 0.225 + 0.400 + 0.178 = 0.803$
> 
> $S_2 = 0.30(0.581) + 0.50(1.00) + 0.20(0.667) = 0.174 + 0.500 + 0.133 = 0.807$
> 
> $S_3 = 0.30(1.00) + 0.50(0.60) + 0.20(0.778) = 0.300 + 0.300 + 0.156 = 0.756$
> 
> $S_4 = 0.30(0.667) + 0.50(0.933) + 0.20(1.00) = 0.200 + 0.467 + 0.200 = 0.867$
> 
> **Result:** $A_4$ still wins, and the ranking is **stable** — this gives the design team high confidence to proceed with $A_4$ for embodiment design.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision-Making Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes when WSAM is the **right** tool vs. when it is the **wrong** tool. This is a frequent 7-mark "discussion" question in KTU 2024.

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 3–5 alternatives, 3–6 criteria, all **independent** | **WSAM** (use it) | Simple, transparent, fast |
| Criteria are **correlated** (e.g., weight ↔ cost) | AHP or TOPSIS | WSAM double-counts the correlated effect |
| 10+ alternatives, **objective weight** needed | Entropy-WSAM hybrid | Pure WSAM weights become guess-work at scale |
| **Safety-critical** with hard thresholds (e.g., min strength) | Lexicographic / Goal Programming | WSAM's compensability can hide a safety violation |
| Decision-maker is **inexperienced** | WSAM | Easiest to explain to non-engineers |
| **Group decision** with conflicting expert opinions | Fuzzy WSAM or AHP | Captures linguistic uncertainty ("moderately important") |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of WSAM

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **full end-to-end information flow** of the Weighted Sum Assessment Method as it is executed by a design team.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([Problem Statement and Design Need]) --> B1[Identify Decision Makers and Stakeholders]
    B1 --> B2[Generate m Design Alternatives A1 ... Am]
    B2 --> C1[List n Evaluation Criteria C1 ... Cn]
    C1 --> C2{Classify Each Criterion as Benefit or Cost}
    C2 -->|Benefit| D1[Collect Raw Scores xij]
    C2 -->|Cost| D1
    D1 --> D2[Construct Raw Decision Matrix X]
    D2 --> E1[Extract Column Max for Benefit Criteria]
    D2 --> E2[Extract Column Min for Cost Criteria]
    E1 --> F1[Normalize Benefit: rij = xij divided by max]
    E2 --> F2[Normalize Cost: rij = min divided by xij]
    F1 --> G1[Build Normalized Matrix R in 0 to 1]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Determine Criterion Weights wj]
    H1 --> H2{Validate Weight Closure: sum wj equals 1}
    H2 -->|Not Closed| H3[Re-normalize Weights]
    H3 --> H1
    H2 -->|Closed| I1[Compute Si = sum wj times rij for each i]
    I1 --> J1[Rank Alternatives in Descending Order of Si]
    J1 --> K1[Select A star equals argmax Si]
    K1 --> L1[Perform Sensitivity Analysis on Weights]
    L1 --> M1([Final Design Concept Approved for Embodiment Design])
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping — Where WSAM Sits in Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Need Identification] --> P2[Problem Clarification]
    P2 --> P3[Concept Generation Methods: TRIZ, Morphological, Brainstorming]
    P3 --> P4[Concept Pool: 10 to 30 Alternatives]
    P4 --> P5([WSAM Evaluation and Selection])
    P5 --> P6[Single Chosen Concept]
    P6 --> P7[Embodiment Layout Design]
    P7 --> P8[Detail and Manufacturing Drawings]
    P8 --> P9([Prototype and Production])
```

## 4.3 Data Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Decision Matrix X with mixed units] --> R2[Direction Classification: Benefit or Cost]
    R2 --> R3[Min Max Normalization Layer]
    R3 --> R4[Normalized Matrix R dimensionless in 0 to 1]
    R4 --> R5[Weight Vector W sum equals 1]
    R5 --> R6[Matrix Multiplication R times W transpose]
    R6 --> R7[Composite Score Vector S of size m by 1]
    R7 --> R8[Sorted Rank List: 1 to m]
    R8 --> R9[Optimal Design: A star]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Diagrams**
> 
> - **Rectangular nodes** = data or processing steps.
> - **Diamond nodes** = decision points (branching logic).
> - **Rounded/pill nodes** = entry and exit points of the methodology.
> - **Arrows** = direction of data flow. The pipeline is **strictly feed-forward** — no feedback loops, which is a defining property of WSAM.

## 4.4 Schematic of a Typical Design Evaluation Sheet

A real KTU lab or industry design review would represent WSAM as a **Pugh Matrix–style weighted scorecard**. Although we cannot render a spreadsheet here, the structure is conceptually:

```
                 C1 (Cost)   C2 (Life)   C3 (Assembly)
                 w1=0.45     w2=0.35     w3=0.20
                 ─────────   ─────────   ─────────
A1 (Steel)        0.75        0.80        0.89
A2 (Al 6061)      0.58        1.00        0.67
A3 (Plastic)      1.00        0.60        0.78
A4 (Magnesium)    0.67        0.93        1.00
                 ─────────   ─────────   ─────────
WEIGHTED Σ:     0.45×col    0.35×col    0.20×col
                 ─────────   ─────────   ─────────
A1 TOTAL:         0.3375      0.2800      0.1778    → S1 = 0.7953
A2 TOTAL:         0.2613      0.3500      0.1333    → S2 = 0.7446
A3 TOTAL:         0.4500      0.2100      0.1556    → S3 = 0.8156
A4 TOTAL:         0.3000      0.3267      0.2000    → S4 = 0.8267  ★ BEST
```
