# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the Weighted Sum Assessment Method. State any two assumptions of WSAM.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The Weighted Sum Assessment Method (WSAM) is a Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) technique used in engineering design to evaluate a finite set of design alternatives against a finite set of weighted criteria. The overall merit of each alternative is computed as the linear additive aggregation of its normalized performance scores multiplied by the corresponding criterion weights, and the alternative with the **maximum composite score** is selected.

**Two key assumptions:**
1. **Mutual Preferential Independence:** The preference rating of an alternative on one criterion is independent of its rating on other criteria.
2. **Common Scale / Normalization Validity:** All raw scores can be transformed to a common dimensionless interval (typically [0, 1]) so that weighted linear addition is meaningful.

*(Acceptable alternative: weight closure $\sum w_j = 1$; additivity of partial utilities.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Benefit and Cost criteria in the context of WSAM normalization. Give one engineering example of each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Benefit Criterion | Cost Criterion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction** | Higher raw value = better design | Lower raw value = better design |
| **Normalization Formula** | $r_{ij} = x_{ij} / \max_i(x_{ij})$ | $r_{ij} = \min_i(x_{ij}) / x_{ij}$ |
| **Engineering Example** | Fatigue life of a shaft (cycles to failure) | Manufacturing cost of a bracket (₹) |
| **Another Example** | Tensile strength (MPa) | Energy consumption (kWh) |
| **Score Range** | $r_{ij} \in (0, 1]$, max = 1 | $r_{ij} \in (0, 1]$, max = 1 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Choice Question]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Full WSAM Numerical

A manufacturing company is developing a new **portable hand-held electric screwdriver** and has shortlisted four design concepts $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4$. The design team has identified **three evaluation criteria** with weights as given below:

| Criterion | Description | Type | Weight |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $C_1$ | Manufacturing Cost (₹) | Cost | 0.40 |
| $C_2$ | Battery Runtime (minutes) | Benefit | 0.35 |
| $C_3$ | Tool Weight (grams) | Cost | 0.25 |

The raw performance data is:

| Alternative | $C_1$ (₹) | $C_2$ (min) | $C_3$ (g) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A_1$ | 850 | 90 | 480 |
| $A_2$ | 1100 | 120 | 520 |
| $A_3$ | 700 | 75 | 600 |
| $A_4$ | 950 | 105 | 450 |

**Using the Weighted Sum Assessment Method:**

**(a)** Construct the decision matrix, identify the criterion directions, and normalize the matrix using the appropriate max/min scheme. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compute the weighted sum score for each alternative, rank them, and identify the optimal design. Comment on the validity of the result. (7 Marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Step 1 — Decision Matrix and Directions (3 Marks)**

The decision matrix is already tabulated above. Criterion directions:

- $C_1$ (Cost) → **Cost Criterion** (lower ₹ is better)
- $C_2$ (Battery Runtime) → **Benefit Criterion** (more minutes is better)
- $C_3$ (Tool Weight) → **Cost Criterion** (lower grams is better)

Extract column extrema:

- $\min_i x_{i1} = 700$ (from $A_3$)
- $\max_i x_{i2} = 120$ (from $A_2$)
- $\min_i x_{i3} = 450$ (from $A_4$)

**Step 2 — Normalization (4 Marks)**

For $C_1$ (Cost): $r_{i1} = 700 / x_{i1}$

$$
r_{11} = \frac{700}{850} = 0.8235
$$

$$
r_{21} = \frac{700}{1100} = 0.6364
$$

$$
r_{31} = \frac{700}{700} = 1.0000
$$

$$
r_{41} = \frac{700}{950} = 0.7368
$$

For $C_2$ (Benefit): $r_{i2} = x_{i2} / 120$

$$
r_{12} = \frac{90}{120} = 0.7500
$$

$$
r_{22} = \frac{120}{120} = 1.0000
$$

$$
r_{32} = \frac{75}{120} = 0.6250
$$

$$
r_{42} = \frac{105}{120} = 0.8750
$$

For $C_3$ (Cost): $r_{i3} = 450 / x_{i3}$

$$
r_{13} = \frac{450}{480} = 0.9375
$$

$$
r_{23} = \frac{450}{520} = 0.8654
$$

$$
r_{33} = \frac{450}{600} = 0.7500
$$

$$
r_{43} = \frac{450}{450} = 1.0000
$$

**Normalized Matrix:**

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
0.8235 & 0.7500 & 0.9375 \\
0.6364 & 1.0000 & 0.8654 \\
1.0000 & 0.6250 & 0.7500 \\
0.7368 & 0.8750 & 1.0000
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Weight Closure Check:** $0.40 + 0.35 + 0.25 = 1.00$ ✓

**(b) Step 3 — Weighted Sum Scores (4 Marks)**

**For $A_1$:**

$$
S_1 = 0.40(0.8235) + 0.35(0.7500) + 0.25(0.9375)
$$

$$
S_1 = 0.3294 + 0.2625 + 0.2344 = \boxed{0.8263}
$$

**For $A_2$:**

$$
S_2 = 0.40(0.6364) + 0.35(1.0000) + 0.25(0.8654)
$$

$$
S_2 = 0.2545 + 0.3500 + 0.2163 = \boxed{0.8209}
$$

**For $A_3$:**

$$
S_3 = 0.40(1.0000) + 0.35(0.6250) + 0.25(0.7500)
$$

$$
S_3 = 0.4000 + 0.2188 + 0.1875 = \boxed{0.8063}
$$

**For $A_4$:**

$$
S_4 = 0.40(0.7368) + 0.35(0.8750) + 0.25(1.0000)
$$

$$
S_4 = 0.2947 + 0.3063 + 0.2500 = \boxed{0.8510}
$$

**Step 4 — Ranking (1 Mark)**

$$
S_4 (0.8510) > S_1 (0.8263) > S_2 (0.8209) > S_3 (0.8063)
$$

| Rank | Alternative | Score |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **1** | $A_4$ | **0.8510 ★ OPTIMAL** |
| 2 | $A_1$ | 0.8263 |
| 3 | $A_2$ | 0.8209 |
| 4 | $A_3$ | 0.8063 |

**Step 5 — Validity Comment (2 Marks)**

- **Stability:** The score margin between Rank 1 ($A_4$) and Rank 2 ($A_1$) is $\Delta S = 0.0247$, which is about $3\%$ of the total possible score — moderately tight. A small change in weights could potentially flip the top two, so a **sensitivity analysis** is recommended.
- **Engineering interpretation:** $A_4$ wins because it is the **lightest** (best on $C_3$) with a competitive battery life and acceptable cost. $A_3$ is cheapest but is **rejected** because its poor battery and heavy weight outweigh its cost advantage.
- **Compensability caveat:** WSAM allowed $A_2$'s poor cost score (₹1100) to be partly offset by its best-in-class battery life — a decision the design team should validate in a **physical prototype test** before final commitment.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Conceptual + Light Numerical)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure** of the Weighted Sum Assessment Method as applied to the evaluation of manufacturing process routes (e.g., machining vs. forming vs. additive manufacturing). Include the role of **criterion weighting**, **normalization**, and **score aggregation** in your answer. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A foundry is comparing three casting processes — **Sand Casting ($A_1$)**, **Die Casting ($A_2$)**, and **Investment Casting ($A_3$)** — for producing a **complex aluminum gearbox housing**. The evaluation criteria and weights are:

| Criterion | Type | Weight |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $C_1$: Tooling Cost (₹ Lakhs) | Cost | 0.30 |
| $C_2$: Dimensional Accuracy (IT grade) | Benefit | 0.40 |
| $C_3$: Production Rate (pieces/hour) | Benefit | 0.30 |

The raw data are:

| Alt. | $C_1$ | $C_2$ (IT grade, lower IT number = better) | $C_3$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A_1$ | 0.5 | 14 | 12 |
| $A_2$ | 25.0 | 8 | 60 |
| $A_3$ | 3.0 | 11 | 20 |

**Note:** For $C_2$, the IT grade is a *cost-like* number (lower is better, even though we classified it as "Benefit" on the table — clarify the direction). Using WSAM, compute scores and recommend the optimal process. (7 Marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Procedural Explanation (7 Marks)**

The Weighted Sum Assessment Method for evaluating manufacturing process routes follows six canonical steps:

1. **Define the Decision Context:** Clearly state the part to be manufactured, the production volume, and the design constraints (material, tolerance, surface finish, batch size).

2. **Generate Process Alternatives:** Using process selection methods (e.g., Ashby material-process charts, machine capability databases, or expert brainstorming), list all candidate processes. For example, for a metal bracket: sand casting, die casting, investment casting, forging, extrusion, CNC machining, sheet metal forming, additive manufacturing.

3. **Identify Evaluation Criteria:** Common criteria for process selection include:
   - Manufacturing cost (per part and per batch)
   - Dimensional accuracy achievable
   - Surface finish
   - Production rate / cycle time
   - Material utilization / scrap %
   - Energy consumption
   - Tooling cost and life
   - Flexibility for design changes
   - Environmental impact (emissions, recyclability)

4. **Assign Criterion Weights:** Use a suitable method (AHP, direct rating, Delphi, or entropy) to assign weights $w_j$ such that $\sum w_j = 1$. The weights must reflect the strategic priorities of the firm (e.g., for a low-volume aerospace part, accuracy and flexibility outweigh cost; for a high-volume consumer part, cost and rate dominate).

5. **Normalize Raw Scores:** Convert all raw scores to $[0, 1]$ using $r_{ij} = x_{ij}/\max$ for benefit criteria and $r_{ij} = \min/x_{ij}$ for cost criteria. This step is critical because manufacturing criteria are typically measured in **incommensurate units** (₹, mm, m², kWh, pieces/hr).

6. **Aggregate and Rank:** Compute $S_i = \sum_j w_j r_{ij}$ for each process, rank them, and perform a **sensitivity analysis** to ensure the winning process is not a fragile artifact of weight choice.

**Role of each component:**

- **Weighting** encodes the *strategic priorities* and resolves trade-offs explicitly.
- **Normalization** removes *unit-dependence* and ensures no single criterion mathematically dominates the sum.
- **Aggregation** reduces the multi-dimensional evaluation to a single *scalar decision index* for unambiguous comparison.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Re-classify $C_2$ Direction:**

A lower IT grade number (e.g., IT 8) represents *higher* accuracy. So in terms of "more is better" — accuracy improves as the IT number decreases. Therefore, $C_2$ is a **Cost Criterion** in our normalization scheme (lower IT number = better accuracy).

**Step 2 — Extract Extrema:**

- $\min_i x_{i1} = 0.5$ (Tooling cost, lower is better)
- $\min_i x_{i2} = 8$ (IT grade, lower is better)
- $\max_i x_{i3} = 60$ (Production rate, higher is better)

**Step 3 — Normalize:**

$C_1$ (Cost): $r_{i1} = 0.5 / x_{i1}$:

$$
r_{11} = 1.0000, \quad r_{21} = 0.0200, \quad r_{31} = 0.1667
$$

$C_2$ (Cost, lower IT better): $r_{i2} = 8 / x_{i2}$:

$$
r_{12} = \frac{8}{14} = 0.5714, \quad r_{22} = \frac{8}{8} = 1.0000, \quad r_{32} = \frac{8}{11} = 0.7273
$$

$C_3$ (Benefit): $r_{i3} = x_{i3} / 60$:

$$
r_{13} = \frac{12}{60} = 0.2000, \quad r_{23} = \frac{60}{60} = 1.0000, \quad r_{33} = \frac{20}{60} = 0.3333
$$

**Normalized Matrix:**

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
1.0000 & 0.5714 & 0.2000 \\
0.0200 & 1.0000 & 1.0000 \\
0.1667 & 0.7273 & 0.3333
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Weighted Sum Scores:**

$$
S_1 = 0.30(1.0000) + 0.40(0.5714) + 0.30(0.2000) = 0.3000 + 0.2286 + 0.0600 = \boxed{0.5886}
$$

$$
S_2 = 0.30(0.0200) + 0.40(1.0000) + 0.30(1.0000) = 0.0060 + 0.4000 + 0.3000 = \boxed{0.7060}
$$

$$
S_3 = 0.30(0.1667) + 0.40(0.7273) + 0.30(0.3333) = 0.0500 + 0.2909 + 0.1000 = \boxed{0.4409}
$$

**Step 5 — Ranking and Recommendation:**

| Rank | Process | Score |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **1** | **Die Casting ($A_2$)** | **0.7060 ★ RECOMMENDED** |
| 2 | Sand Casting ($A_1$) | 0.5886 |
| 3 | Investment Casting ($A_3$) | 0.4409 |

**Recommendation:** **Die Casting ($A_2$)** is the optimal process for a **complex aluminum gearbox housing** under the given criteria. It offers the **best dimensional accuracy (IT 8)** and the **highest production rate (60 pcs/hr)**, which collectively dominate its high tooling cost.

**Engineering Justification:** A gearbox housing is a critical-tolerance part produced in medium-to-high volume — exactly the niche where die casting excels. The high tooling cost (₹25 Lakhs) is amortized over the production run, and the per-part cost becomes competitive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Direction Misclassification on $C_2$ (–2 marks):** Treating "IT grade" as a benefit criterion is a trap. A lower IT number means *tighter* (better) tolerance. Many students divide by max instead of using min — **re-read the criterion carefully**.
> 2. **Forgetting Weight Closure Check (–1 mark):** Even if the answer is otherwise correct, omitting the $\sum w_j = 1$ verification loses a mark.
> 3. **Skipping the Engineering Interpretation (–2 marks):** A WSAM answer that ends with a ranking table but no engineering commentary is considered "computational only" and is marked down. Always state *why* the winner wins, in design terms.
> 4. **Unit Errors in Normalization (–1 mark):** Tooling cost is given in "₹ Lakhs" but the normalization uses the raw number — keep the unit consistent throughout.
> 5. **No Sensitivity Discussion (–1 mark):** A complete 14-mark answer mentions, in one line, whether the ranking is robust to weight perturbation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WSAM at a Glance — The 12 Points You Cannot Afford to Miss**

- [ ] **WSAM is a linear additive MCDM method** that converts multi-criterion evaluation into a single scalar score per alternative.
- [ ] **The decision matrix $X$** is $m \times n$, where $m$ = number of alternatives, $n$ = number of criteria.
- [ ] **Two criterion directions exist:** Benefit (higher is better → divide by max) and Cost (lower is better → divide by min). Misclassifying direction is the #1 error.
- [ ] **Normalization is mandatory** because criteria are measured in different units (₹, mm, MPa, kWh, etc.). After normalization, all $r_{ij} \in [0, 1]$.
- [ ] **Weight closure:** $\sum_{j=1}^n w_j = 1$ and $w_j \geq 0$ for all $j$.
- [ ] **The composite score** is $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^n w_j \cdot r_{ij}$, computed independently for each alternative.
- [ ] **The optimal alternative** is $A^* = \arg\max_i S_i$.
- [ ] **WSAM is fully compensatory** — a very poor score on one criterion can be offset by excellent scores on others. This is its main weakness in safety-critical decisions.
- [ ] **Mutual preferential independence** is a foundational assumption; if violated, switch to AHP or TOPSIS.
- [ ] **The max possible score is $S_{\max} = 1$**, achievable only if one alternative dominates all others in every criterion.
- [ ] **Sensitivity analysis on weights** is best practice and frequently earns bonus marks in KTU 2024 ESE.
- [ ] **WSAM is a precursor** to advanced methods: AHP (adds hierarchy + consistency check), TOPSIS (adds geometric distance to ideal), ELECTRE (adds outranking flows), PROMETHEE (adds preference functions).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If the question gives you **different units** (₹, MPa, mm, °C), the first sentence of your answer should be: *"Since the criteria are in incommensurate units, normalization to a common scale is essential before linear aggregation."* This single sentence signals full conceptual command and often secures 1–2 grace marks from KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lifecycle Assessment Method
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lifecycle Assessment Method

# Lifecycle Assessment (LCA) Method

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** is a systematic, standardized, and quantitative methodology used to evaluate the environmental burdens associated with a product, process, or service throughout its entire life span — from **raw material extraction** (cradle) to **final disposal** (grave) — by compiling an inventory of relevant energy and material inputs and environmental releases, and assessing the potential environmental impacts associated with these inputs and releases.

The methodology is internationally standardized under **ISO 14040:2006** (Principles and Framework) and **ISO 14044:2006** (Requirements and Guidelines). For KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, the four-phase iterative structure prescribed by ISO 14040 is treated as the canonical reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The Lifecycle Assessment method is a **Decision-Support Tool** in Design for Manufacture (DFM) and Design for Environment (DFE) frameworks. It quantifies environmental performance *before* the product is manufactured, allowing eco-conscious design choices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biographical Audit" of a Product

Imagine you are writing the complete biography of a human being, from conception (parents' genetic material) to cremation/burial (final disposal). A biographer would not skip school, marriage, career, illness, and old age. Similarly, **LCA audits the "biography" of a product**:

- **Conception** = Raw material extraction (mining, drilling, harvesting)
- **Childhood** = Material processing and refinement
- **Schooling** = Manufacturing of components
- **Career** = Distribution, use, and maintenance
- **Retirement** = End-of-life: reuse, recycling, or landfill

Just as a biography captures *every* stage's impact on society, an LCA captures *every* stage's impact on the **environment** — air, water, soil, and energy resources.

### 1.3 Why is LCA Important in Design of Manufacture?

1. **Avoid Burden Shifting:** Prevents solving one environmental problem (e.g., reducing manufacturing emissions) by creating another (e.g., toxic recycling waste).
2. **Holistic Decision Making:** Enables comparison between material alternatives (e.g., steel vs. aluminum vs. composites for an automobile hood).
3. **Regulatory Compliance:** Aligns with EU CE Marking, RoHS, WEEE, and India's E-Waste Management Rules.
4. **Cost Optimization:** Identifies the *true* cost of energy, water, and waste across the supply chain.
5. **Marketing Claims (EPD):** Provides third-party-verified data for Environmental Product Declarations.

### 1.4 Key Standardized Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Functional Unit (FU)** | The quantified performance of a product system for use as a reference unit (e.g., "1 liter of packaged drinking water delivered to the consumer"). |
| **System Boundary** | The interface between the product system and the environment/nature/other product systems. |
| **Reference Flow** | The amount of product required to fulfil the functional unit. |
| **Cradle-to-Grave** | Full life cycle from raw material extraction to final disposal. |
| **Cradle-to-Gate** | From raw material extraction to factory exit (partial LCA). |
| **Gate-to-Gate** | From factory entry to factory exit (single process). |
| **Cradle-to-Cradle** | Closed-loop system where end-of-life product becomes input for another product. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** A common KTU question framing is *"Distinguish between Cradle-to-Gate and Cradle-to-Grave."* Memorize the **system boundary** differences precisely.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ISO 14040 Four-Phase Framework

The ISO 14040 standard prescribes a **four-phase iterative** structure. *Iteration* is a critical concept — results from later phases often require revisiting earlier phases.

#### Phase 1: Goal and Scope Definition

**Why:** Defines the *purpose*, *boundary*, and *audience* of the study.
**How:** The practitioner must explicitly declare:
- **Intended Application:** Internal R&D, marketing claim, policy support, comparative assertion.
- **Reasons for the Study:** Why this LCA is being conducted.
- **Target Audience:** Internal management, regulators, public, customers.
- **Scope:** Defines system boundary, functions, functional unit, allocation procedures, impact categories, methodology, data quality requirements, assumptions, and limitations.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Functional Unit (FU)** is the most critical element. *All* subsequent input/output data are normalized to the FU. Changing the FU invalidates the entire study.

#### Phase 2: Life Cycle Inventory Analysis (LCI)

**Why:** Quantifies the *flows* — energy, raw materials, emissions to air/water/soil, and co-products.
**How:** Data is collected for every unit process within the system boundary, then aggregated.

**LCI Procedure (Four Steps):**
1. **Data Collection** — For each unit process: energy inputs, raw materials, ancillary inputs, products, co-products, emissions to air, water, and soil.
2. **Data Validation** — Cross-check mass/energy balances.
3. **Allocation Procedures** — When a process yields multiple co-products, the environmental burden must be **partitioned** (allocated) among them. Methods: physical causality, economic value, energy content.
4. **Aggregation & Reporting** — Relate all data to the reference flow (the flow required to fulfil the functional unit).

#### Phase 3: Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)

**Why:** Translates inventory data (kg of CO₂, kg of NOₓ, MJ of energy) into **environmental impact scores**.
**How:** LCIA is a three mandatory + two optional step procedure (per ISO 14044):

| Step | Mandatory? | Purpose |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Classification** | Mandatory | Assign each LCI result to one or more impact categories (e.g., CO₂ → Climate Change; CH₄ → Climate Change + Ozone Depletion). |
| **Characterization** | Mandatory | Multiply each classified substance by its **Characterization Factor (CF)** and aggregate within a category. |
| **Normalization** | Optional | Express category indicator result relative to a reference (e.g., per capita global emissions of that category). |
| **Grouping** | Optional | Sort/prioritize categories (e.g., into human health, ecosystem quality, resources). |
| **Weighting** | Optional | Convert across categories using value-choices (panel-derived weights). |

#### Phase 4: Life Cycle Interpretation

**Why:** Synthesizes LCI and LCIA results into **conclusions and recommendations**.
**How:** Three iterative elements:
1. **Identification of Significant Issues** — Dominant contributions, hotspots.
2. **Evaluation** — Completeness, sensitivity, consistency checks.
3. **Conclusions, Limitations, and Recommendations** — Transparent reporting aligned with the defined goal.

### 2.2 Major Environmental Impact Categories

| Impact Category | Characterization Example (CF Units) | Reference Substance |
|----------------|-------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Climate Change (GWP₁₀₀)** | kg CO₂-eq per kg substance | **CO₂** |
| **Ozone Depletion (ODP)** | kg CFC-11-eq per kg substance | **CFC-11** |
| **Acidification (AP)** | kg SO₂-eq per kg substance | **SO₂** |
| **Eutrophication (EP)** | kg PO₄³⁻-eq per kg substance | **PO₄³⁻** |
| **Photochemical Oxidant Formation (POCP)** | kg C₂H₄-eq per kg substance | **C₂H₄** (ethylene) |
| **Human Toxicity (HTP)** | kg 1,4-DB-eq per kg substance | **1,4-dichlorobenzene** |
| **Abiotic Resource Depletion (ADP)** | kg Sb-eq per kg substance | **Antimony (Sb)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "100" in GWP₁₀₀** denotes the **time horizon of 100 years** over which the Global Warming Potential is averaged. This is the most commonly used horizon by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{Impact Category Indicator} = \sum_{i} (m_i \times CF_i)$ | Aggregated impact per category. $m_i$ = mass of substance $i$; $CF_i$ = characterization factor. | Impact category units (e.g., kg CO₂-eq) |
| 2 | $GWP_{100} = \sum_{i} (m_i \times GWP_{100,i})$ | Total Global Warming Potential. | kg CO₂-eq |
| 3 | $C E D = \sum_{i} E_{i,\text{non-renew}} + \sum_{j} E_{j,\text{renew}}$ | Cumulative Energy Demand: non-renewable + renewable. | MJ (Megajoules) |
| 4 | $\text{Normalized Impact} = \frac{\text{Category Indicator}}{\text{Reference Value}}$ | Normalization against total annual emissions of a region. | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Weighted Score} = \sum_{c} w_c \times \text{Indicator}_c$ | $w_c$ = weighting factor for category $c$ (subjective, panel-derived). | Eco-points |
| 6 | $E I_{99} = \sum_{c} \left( \frac{\text{Impact}_c}{\text{Reference}_c} \times w_c \right)$ | Eco-Indicator 99 single-score methodology. | Pt (eco-points) |
| 7 | $CF_{i,\text{cat}} = \frac{\text{Potential of } i \text{ in cat}}{\text{Potential of reference in cat}}$ | Definition of any characterization factor. | Ratio (dimensionless or category-specific) |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{material}} = \frac{m_{\text{useful output}}}{m_{\text{raw input}}}$ | Material yield efficiency used in LCI. | Dimensionless (0–1) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Volkswagen, BMW, and Tesla use LCA to choose between steel-intensive, aluminum-intensive, and carbon-fiber body structures.
- **Packaging:** Coca-Cola's PlantBottle vs. PET bottle LCA showed 18% reduction in carbon footprint.
- **Electronics:** Apple's Product Environmental Reports use LCA for iPhones and MacBooks.
- **Construction:** Cement and steel industries use LCA to qualify for Green Building certifications (LEED, GRIHA, BREEAM).
- **Manufacturing Process Selection:** LCA-driven choice between conventional machining vs. additive manufacturing (3D printing) for aerospace brackets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Global Warming Potential Calculation

A **steel component** of mass **1 kg** is produced through a manufacturing route. The process emits the following greenhouse gases into the atmosphere (per 1 kg of steel produced):

| Substance | Mass Emitted (kg) | GWP₁₀₀ (kg CO₂-eq / kg substance) |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
| CO₂ | 1.85 | **1** |
| CH₄ (methane) | 0.012 | **28** |
| N₂O (nitrous oxide) | 0.0009 | **265** |

**Task:** Calculate the total Global Warming Potential (GWP₁₀₀) per 1 kg of steel.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**
The total GWP is the mass-weighted sum of all greenhouse gases, each scaled by its characterization factor:

$$
GWP_{100} = \sum_{i} m_i \cdot CF_{GWP_{100}, i}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each gas term.**

$$
GWP_{100} = (m_{CO_2} \cdot CF_{CO_2}) + (m_{CH_4} \cdot CF_{CH_4}) + (m_{N_2O} \cdot CF_{N_2O})
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in the numerical values.**

$$
GWP_{100} = (1.85 \cdot 1) + (0.012 \cdot 28) + (0.0009 \cdot 265)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term individually.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1: } & 1.85 \times 1 = 1.85 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} \\
\text{Term 2: } & 0.012 \times 28 = 0.336 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} \\
\text{Term 3: } & 0.0009 \times 265 = 0.2385 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the contributions.**

$$
GWP_{100} = 1.85 + 0.336 + 0.2385 = 2.4245 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{GWP_{100} = 2.4245 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq per kg of steel}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even though methane and nitrous oxide have small absolute masses, their high GWP₁₀₀ values (28 and 265 respectively) make them **non-negligible contributors** (~24% of the total). This demonstrates the importance of *characterization* in LCIA — a "small emission" can be climatically significant.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Eco-Indicator 99 Single-Score Calculation

A laptop manufacturing process yields the following normalized, characterized impacts (per functional unit = "1 laptop manufactured"):

| Impact Category | Indicator (kg-eq) | Reference Value | Weighting Factor $w_c$ |
|-----------------|-------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Climate Change | 220 kg CO₂-eq | 6860 kg CO₂-eq/person/year | 0.30 |
| Acidification | 0.85 kg SO₂-eq | 51 kg SO₂-eq/person/year | 0.15 |
| Eutrophication | 0.05 kg PO₄³⁻-eq | 1.6 kg PO₄³⁻-eq/person/year | 0.15 |
| Resource Depletion | 12 kg Sb-eq | 0.62 kg Sb-eq/person/year | 0.40 |

**Task:** Compute the Eco-Indicator 99 single score in **eco-points (Pt)**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the Eco-Indicator 99 formula.**

$$
EI_{99} = \sum_{c} \left( \frac{\text{Indicator}_c}{\text{Reference}_c} \right) \times w_c \times 100
$$

The factor 100 is a scaling constant (to express scores in convenient eco-point magnitudes).

**Step 2 — Compute the normalized fraction for each category.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{Climate}} &= \frac{220}{6860} = 0.03207 \\
N_{\text{Acidification}} &= \frac{0.85}{51} = 0.01667 \\
N_{\text{Eutrophication}} &= \frac{0.05}{1.6} = 0.03125 \\
N_{\text{Resource}} &= \frac{12}{0.62} = 19.3548
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply each normalized value by its weighting factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Climate}} &= 0.03207 \times 0.30 = 0.009621 \\
S_{\text{Acidification}} &= 0.01667 \times 0.15 = 0.002501 \\
S_{\text{Eutrophication}} &= 0.03125 \times 0.15 = 0.004688 \\
S_{\text{Resource}} &= 19.3548 \times 0.40 = 7.7419
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the weighted contributions and apply the scaling factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum S_c &= 0.009621 + 0.002501 + 0.004688 + 7.7419 = 7.7587 \\
EI_{99} &= 7.7587 \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{EI_{99} = 775.87 \text{ Pt per laptop}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The **Resource Depletion** category dominates (≈ 99.7% of total score) because the normalized value (19.35) is an order of magnitude larger than the others. Engineers should target this category first — for instance, by selecting recycled rare-earth materials or extending product life to reduce virgin-resource demand.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) for Manufacturing

A pump housing is produced via three sequential processes. Energy inputs are:

| Process | Electricity (kWh) | Coal (MJ) | Natural Gas (MJ) |
|---------|-------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Casting | 5.2 | 12 | 8 |
| Machining | 8.0 | 0 | 0 |
| Surface Finishing | 3.0 | 0 | 5 |

**Energy Conversion Factor:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert electricity to MJ.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cast,elec}} &= 5.2 \times 3.6 = 18.72 \text{ MJ} \\
E_{\text{mach,elec}} &= 8.0 \times 3.6 = 28.80 \text{ MJ} \\
E_{\text{fin,elec}} &= 3.0 \times 3.6 = 10.80 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum non-renewable and renewable separately.**

Non-renewable (coal + natural gas + grid electricity, treated as non-renewable-dominated):

$$
CED_{\text{non-renew}} = (12 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 5) + (18.72 + 28.80 + 10.80)
$$

$$
CED_{\text{non-renew}} = 25 + 58.32 = 83.32 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{CED_{\text{total}} \approx 83.32 \text{ MJ per pump housing}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** The **machining** stage is the most energy-intensive, despite using no fossil fuels directly — because it relies on grid electricity whose generation in India is ~75% coal-based. This insight can drive selection of near-net-shape processes (e.g., precision casting or powder metallurgy) to reduce machining allowance.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: LCA Application in Manufacturing Decision-Making

| Design Choice | Cradle Impact | Gate Impact | Use-Phase Impact | End-of-Life Impact | Best LCA Choice |
|---------------|---------------|-------------|------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Steel vs. Aluminum automotive part | Al = high bauxite mining; Steel = lower | Al = high electrolysis energy | Al = lightweight → less fuel | Both recyclable | **Al** if use-phase > 150,000 km |
| Conventional vs. 3D-printed bracket | 3DP = lower (less raw material) | 3DP = high electricity (laser) | Identical | 3DP = better (no assembly) | **3DP** for low-volume complex parts |
| Glass vs. PET bottle | Glass = higher (sand, soda ash) | Glass = higher (furnace) | Identical | Glass = infinite recycle | **Glass** for returnable systems |
| Paper vs. Plastic grocery bag | Paper = higher (trees, water) | Paper = high (pulping) | Paper = heavier transport | Paper = biodegradable | **Plastic** if reused 4+ times |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ISO 14040 Four-Phase LCA Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PHASE 1: Goal and Scope Definition"] --> B["PHASE 2: Life Cycle Inventory LCI"]
    B --> C["PHASE 3: Life Cycle Impact Assessment LCIA"]
    C --> D["PHASE 4: Life Cycle Interpretation"]
    D --> E["Conclusions, Limitations, Recommendations"]
    D --> A
    D --> B
    D --> C
    A --> F["Functional Unit, System Boundary, Data Quality"]
    B --> G["Energy, Materials, Emissions, Co-products"]
    C --> H["Classification, Characterization, Normalization, Weighting"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The four phases are *iterative* — the bidirectional arrows between Interpretation (Phase 4) and the earlier three phases (1, 2, 3) emphasize that findings may require **revision of scope**, **revised data collection**, or **re-calculation of impacts**. This is the *Iterative Nature* prescribed by ISO 14040 §4.2.3.2.

### 4.2 Cradle-to-Grave vs. Cradle-to-Gate vs. Cradle-to-Cradle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Material Extraction"] --> B["Material Processing"]
    B --> C["Manufacturing"]
    C --> D["Distribution"]
    D --> E["Use Phase"]
    E --> F["End of Life Disposal"]

    A -.-> B -.-> C
    C -.-> C
    F -.-> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows depict the **Cradle-to-Grave** path (A → B → C → D → E → F). Dashed arrows from raw material to factory exit represent **Cradle-to-Gate** (terminates at C). The inner loop C → C is **Gate-to-Gate** (a single facility). The loop from F back to A depicts the **Cradle-to-Cradle** closed-loop paradigm where End-of-Life output becomes raw material for a new product.

### 4.3 LCIA Mandatory and Optional Elements (ISO 14044)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["LCI Results from Phase 2"] --> Q1["Classification: Assign to Impact Categories"]
    Q1 --> Q2["Characterization: Multiply by CF and Sum"]
    Q2 --> R1["Mandatory: Category Indicator Calculated"]
    R1 --> S1["Optional: Normalization vs. Reference"]
    S1 --> S2["Optional: Grouping by Theme"]
    S2 --> S3["Optional: Weighting by Panel Values"]
    S3 --> T1["Single Score Optional Output"]
    R1 --> T1
    T1 --> U1["Phase 4: Interpretation"]
```

### 4.4 Allocation Procedures — Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Unit Process Yields Multiple Co-products"] --> Y{"Can System be Subdivided?"}
    Y -->|Yes| Z["Avoid Allocation: Subdivide the Process"]
    Y -->|No| AA{"Physical Causality Available?"}
    AA -->|Yes| AB["Allocate by Physical Causality (mass, energy, exergy)"]
    AA -->|No| AC{"Economic Value Available?"}
    AC -->|Yes| AD["Allocate by Economic Value (revenue share)"]
    AC -->|No| AE["Document and Seek Expert Judgment"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** ISO 14044 mandates the **hierarchy of allocation**: (1) Subdivide first, (2) Use physical causality if subdivision is impossible, (3) Use economic value only as the last numerical fallback. Allocation is one of the *most examiner-tested* topics in KTU Module 4.

### 4.5 Application of LCA in Design of Manufacture — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["Product Requirement Specification"] --> D2["LCA Study Goal and Functional Unit"]
    D2 --> D3["Inventory: Material, Energy, Process"]
    D3 --> D4["Impact Assessment Across Categories"]
    D4 --> D5["Interpretation: Identify Hotspots"]
    D5 --> D6{"Design Alternatives Compare"}
    D6 -->|Alternative A| D7["Material A, Process A, Route A"]
    D6 -->|Alternative B| D8["Material B, Process B, Route B"]
    D7 --> D9["Single-Score Comparison"]
    D8 --> D9
    D9 --> D10["Final Design Selection Eco-Optimized"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow shows LCA's role as a **decision-support engine** within the Design of Manufacture workflow. Each block represents a discrete analytical step that transforms inputs (raw environmental data) into outputs (design decisions). The final block (D10) feeds the selection of a *lower-impact* design alternative without compromising functional performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) as per ISO 14040. List its four phases.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a systematic, standardized methodology defined by **ISO 14040:2006** for compiling and evaluating the **inputs, outputs, and potential environmental impacts** of a product system throughout its life cycle, from raw material acquisition to final disposal.
>
> **Four Phases (1 mark):**
> 1. Goal and Scope Definition
> 2. Life Cycle Inventory Analysis (LCI)
> 3. Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)
> 4. Life Cycle Interpretation

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a "Functional Unit" in LCA? Why is it critical for comparative studies?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Functional Unit (FU)** is the *quantified performance* of a product system that serves as a reference unit for the LCA study (e.g., "1 kg of packaged milk delivered to the consumer," or "1,000 hours of illumination from a 60 W LED bulb").
>
> **Importance (1.5 marks):** The FU is critical for **comparative LCA studies** because it ensures **equivalence of function**. Two products that differ in size, mass, or lifetime can only be compared meaningfully when their environmental burdens are normalized to the *same functional output*. Without FU, comparing an LED bulb to an incandescent bulb is invalid — one must normalize to "X hours of equivalent light output."

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **four phases of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** as per the **ISO 14040 framework**, with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define and distinguish between **Cradle-to-Grave**, **Cradle-to-Gate**, **Gate-to-Gate**, and **Cradle-to-Cradle** system boundaries. Give **one industrial example** for each. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four phases prescribed by ISO 14040 are:

**Phase 1 — Goal and Scope Definition (≈ 2 marks)**

This phase defines the *purpose* and *scope* of the LCA. Key deliverables are:
- Stating the **intended application** (e.g., comparative assertion, internal R&D).
- Defining the **functional unit (FU)** — the quantified reference performance.
- Setting the **system boundary** — which life-cycle stages to include/exclude.
- Specifying **data quality requirements** (time-related, geographical, technological coverage).
- Declaring **assumptions and limitations** upfront.

**Phase 2 — Life Cycle Inventory Analysis — LCI (≈ 2 marks)**

LCI is the *data-collection* phase. For every unit process within the system boundary, the practitioner quantifies:
- **Inputs:** Raw materials, energy (fuel, electricity), ancillary materials.
- **Outputs:** Products, co-products, emissions to **air** (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ), **water** (BOD, heavy metals), and **soil** (solid waste).
- **Allocation** is applied when a single process yields multiple co-products.
- Result: A complete **inventory table** expressed per **reference flow**.

**Phase 3 — Life Cycle Impact Assessment — LCIA (≈ 2 marks)**

LCIA translates the LCI inventory into **impact scores**. ISO 14044 prescribes:
- *Mandatory:* Classification (assign substances to categories) and Characterization (multiply by CF and sum).
- *Optional:* Normalization, Grouping, Weighting.
- Output: A set of **category indicators** (e.g., kg CO₂-eq, kg SO₂-eq) and optionally a **single score**.

**Phase 4 — Life Cycle Interpretation (≈ 1 mark)**

This phase identifies **significant issues**, performs **sensitivity and uncertainty analyses**, and formulates **conclusions, limitations, and recommendations** consistent with the original goal.

> **Block Diagram (must be drawn in exam):** Same as Section 4.1 above, showing the four blocks A → B → C → D and the iteration loops from D back to A, B, C.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Cradle-to-Grave (≈ 2 marks):**
Covers the *entire* life cycle: from **raw material extraction (cradle)** → manufacturing → distribution → use → **final disposal (grave)**.
*Example:* LCA of a 1.5 L PET soft-drink bottle — from crude oil extraction to landfill/incineration of the empty bottle.

**Cradle-to-Gate (≈ 2 marks):**
Covers raw material extraction up to the **factory exit gate** (before distribution/use/EoL).
*Example:* An EPD (Environmental Product Declaration) for a roll of aluminum sheet as delivered by the rolling mill.

**Gate-to-Gate (≈ 2 marks):**
A *partial* LCA covering **one process or one facility** — from its input gate to its output gate.
*Example:* Environmental burden of the CNC machining cell alone, ignoring material extraction and end-of-life.

**Cradle-to-Cradle (≈ 1 mark):**
A *closed-loop* analysis where the **end-of-life product becomes the raw material for a new product** of equal value.
*Example:* Aluminum cans collected, remelted, and re-rolled into new cans — the same product is reborn, no "grave."

> **Distinguishing Table (must reproduce in exam):**

| Boundary | Start | End | Typical Use |
|----------|-------|-----|-------------|
| Cradle-to-Grave | Raw material | Final disposal | Full corporate sustainability |
| Cradle-to-Gate | Raw material | Factory gate | B2B EPDs, supplier disclosure |
| Gate-to-Gate | Factory input | Factory output | Internal process improvement |
| Cradle-to-Cradle | Raw material | Re-fed as raw material | Circular-economy, closed-loop |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) phase** in detail, describing the **mandatory and optional elements**. Illustrate with a numerical example of computing a **Global Warming Potential (GWP₁₀₀) score** from three emissions: 2.5 kg CO₂, 0.015 kg CH₄, and 0.001 kg N₂O. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **Eco-Indicator 99 methodology** for converting diverse impact categories into a **single score**, with a worked numerical example involving three categories. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**LCIA Mandatory and Optional Elements (≈ 4 marks):**

| Element | Mandatory? | Function |
|---------|------------|----------|
| **Classification** | Yes | Group LCI results into impact categories (e.g., CO₂ → Climate Change; SO₂ → Acidification). |
| **Characterization** | Yes | Multiply LCI mass by **characterization factor (CF)** and aggregate within a category. |
| **Normalization** | No | Divide category indicator by a reference (e.g., global annual per-capita emissions). |
| **Grouping** | No | Sort categories into Human Health / Ecosystem Quality / Resources. |
| **Weighting** | No | Apply value-based weights to produce a single score. |

**Numerical GWP₁₀₀ Calculation (≈ 3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Use the formula:

$$
GWP_{100} = m_{CO_2} \cdot 1 + m_{CH_4} \cdot 28 + m_{N_2O} \cdot 265
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
GWP_{100} = (2.5 \times 1) + (0.015 \times 28) + (0.001 \times 265)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 \text{ contribution} &= 2.5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} \\
\text{CH}_4 \text{ contribution} &= 0.420 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} \\
\text{N}_2\text{O} \text{ contribution} &= 0.265 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} \\
\text{Total } GWP_{100} &= 2.5 + 0.420 + 0.265 = 3.185 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{GWP_{100} = 3.185 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Eco-Indicator 99 Methodology (≈ 3.5 marks):**

Developed by PRé Sustainability (Netherlands) in 1999, Eco-Indicator 99 is a **damage-oriented** impact assessment method. Its structure:

1. **Damage Categories:** Human Health, Ecosystem Quality, Resources.
2. **Normalization:** Each impact category indicator is divided by a reference (e.g., total annual European emissions of that category).
3. **Weighting:** Panel-derived weights reflect societal concern (e.g., Human Health > Resources).
4. **Single Score:** A single eco-point (Pt) value enables comparison across products.

**Worked Example (≈ 3.5 marks):**

Consider a product with three impact categories:

| Category | Indicator | Reference | Weight |
|----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Climate Change | 100 kg CO₂-eq | 10,000 kg CO₂-eq | 0.40 |
| Acidification | 0.5 kg SO₂-eq | 100 kg SO₂-eq | 0.35 |
| Resources | 0.02 kg Sb-eq | 0.05 kg Sb-eq | 0.25 |

**Step 1 — Normalize:**

$$
N_1 = \frac{100}{10000} = 0.01, \quad N_2 = \frac{0.5}{100} = 0.005, \quad N_3 = \frac{0.02}{0.05} = 0.4
$$

**Step 2 — Weight:**

$$
S_1 = 0.01 \times 0.40 = 0.004, \quad S_2 = 0.005 \times 0.35 = 0.00175, \quad S_3 = 0.4 \times 0.25 = 0.1
$$

**Step 3 — Sum and scale by 100:**

$$
EI_{99} = (0.004 + 0.00175 + 0.1) \times 100 = 10.575 \text{ Pt}
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{EI_{99} = 10.575 \text{ eco-points}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the time horizon:** Always state "GWP₁₀₀" — never write just "GWP" without the horizon. KTU examiners *will* deduct one mark.
> 2. **Confusing FU and Reference Flow:** FU is the *function* (e.g., "1 km of illumination"); Reference Flow is the *amount of product* to provide that function (e.g., "1 LED bulb"). Do not interchange.
> 3. **Unit errors:** GWP is expressed as **kg CO₂-eq**, not kg CO₂. The "-eq" is mandatory.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram:** A 7-mark LCA question *requires* a labeled block diagram. Drawing only text loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Mixing ISO 14040 and ISO 14044:** ISO 14040 is the *principles*, ISO 14044 is the *requirements*. Examiners test this distinction.
> 6. **Not mentioning iteration:** Forgetting the *iterative* nature of LCA (Interpretation feeds back to all earlier phases) is a frequent omission costing 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LCA** = cradle-to-grave environmental impact assessment of a product or service.
- **Standard:** ISO 14040 (Principles) + ISO 14044 (Requirements).
- **Four Phases:** Goal & Scope → Inventory (LCI) → Impact Assessment (LCIA) → Interpretation. **All four are iterative, not linear.**
- **Functional Unit (FU)** is the *most critical* element; *all* data are normalized to it.
- **LCI** = data collection (inputs and outputs); **LCIA** = translation into impact scores via **Characterization Factors (CFs)**.
- **Mandatory LCIA steps:** Classification + Characterization. **Optional:** Normalization, Grouping, Weighting.
- **Allocation Hierarchy (ISO 14044):** (1) Subdivide → (2) Physical causality → (3) Economic value.
- **GWP₁₀₀** = 100-year time-horizon Global Warming Potential in **kg CO₂-equivalent**.
- **Eco-Indicator 99** = damage-oriented, three-damage-category, single-score methodology.
- **System Boundaries:** Cradle-to-Grave (full), Cradle-to-Gate (raw material to factory), Gate-to-Gate (single process), Cradle-to-Cradle (closed-loop).
- **LCA Use Cases:** Material selection, process selection, supplier comparison, EPD preparation, circular-economy design.
- **Key Formula:** $GWP = \sum m_i \cdot CF_i$; $CED = E_{\text{non-renew}} + E_{\text{renew}}$; $EI_{99} = 100 \cdot \sum (N_c \cdot w_c)$.
- **Limitation:** LCA is **data-intensive** and **assumption-sensitive**; results are valid only for the declared FU and system boundary.
- **KTU Tip:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** for any 7+ mark question on LCA phases. Examiners reward structured, diagram-supported answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques to Reduce Environmental Impact
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques to Reduce Environmental Impact

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Environmental Impact" in Manufacturing?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Environmental Impact* in the context of manufacturing is any change to the environment—whether adverse or beneficial—wholly or partially resulting from an organization's activities, products, or services. In a manufacturing lifecycle, this includes **resource depletion**, **energy consumption**, **air/water/soil emissions**, **solid waste generation**, and **end-of-life disposal burdens**, typically quantified through standardized metrics such as **kg CO₂-equivalent (kg CO₂e)**, **kg SO₂-equivalent (acidification)**, and **kg Sb-equivalent (abiotic resource depletion)**.

In KTU 2024 terminology, the *Techniques to Reduce Environmental Impact* are the systematic, science-based methodologies, design philosophies, and process strategies employed across the **product life cycle (PLC)** to minimize the ecological footprint of manufactured artefacts without compromising functional performance, cost, or quality.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Factory as a Forest"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a manufacturing plant is a large living tree in a forest. A young sapling takes water, sunlight, and minerals (raw materials + energy), grows, and drops leaves (waste/emissions) which then become food for the soil (recyclable by-products). An *un-sustainable factory* is like a tree that is cut faster than it regrows, poisons the soil, and dumps toxic sap into the river.
>
> *Techniques to Reduce Environmental Impact* are the horticultural practices—**pruning, composting, rainwater harvesting, intercropping**—that mimic the closed-loop cycles of a healthy forest. Just as permaculture closes nutrient loops, *green manufacturing* closes material and energy loops through **Design for Environment (DFE)**, **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)**, and the **3R Hierarchy (Reduce → Reuse → Recycle)**.

## 1.3 Why is This Topic a KTU High-Priority Module?

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Module 4 of *Design of Manufacture* integrates the environmental dimension into the design-for-X philosophy. The **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDG-9 & SDG-12)** and India's **National Mission on Sustainable Habitat** mandate that every B.Tech mechanical/manufacturing graduate must be able to:

- Identify the *eco-hotspots* in a product's lifecycle.
- Apply **DFE guidelines** during the *conceptual* and *embodiment* design stages.
- Quantify environmental burdens using **LCA** (ISO 14040/14044).
- Select **environmentally preferable materials** and **near-net-shape processes**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Lens:** Questions in this module are almost always framed around a *real product* (e.g., an automobile bumper, a refrigerator, a mobile phone chassis). The examiner expects you to (1) identify the life cycle stages, (2) pinpoint the highest-impact stage, and (3) suggest *specific, technical* mitigation techniques.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Environmental Impact Reduction

| Pillar | Core Idea | KTU Key Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar 1: Prevent at Source** | Eliminate waste/emission before it is created | **Pollution Prevention (P2)** |
| **Pillar 2: Close the Loop** | Re-introduce outputs back as inputs | **Circular Economy / Industrial Ecology** |
| **Pillar 3: Minimize Burden** | Reduce quantity/severity of unavoidable impact | **End-of-Pipe Treatment** |

> The KTU 2024 syllabus prioritizes **Pillar 1 and Pillar 2** over Pillar 3, because prevention is **always hierarchically superior** to treatment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Environmental Impact Reduction Techniques

The techniques taught under KTU 2024 Module 4 are organized into **five strategic clusters**. Each cluster addresses a specific life-cycle stage from **Cradle → Grave → Cradle**.

### Cluster A — Design-Stage Techniques (Upstream)

These are decisions made *before* a single chip is cut.

1. **Design for Environment (DFE)** — A systematic design philosophy that integrates environmental considerations into the product development process. KTU 2024 emphasizes *8 DFE Rules* (Brezet's 7 + extended).

2. **Design for Disassembly (DFD)** — Design that enables easy separation of components at end-of-life using *snap-fits, fewer fasteners, single-material assemblies*.

3. **Design for Recycling (DFR)** — Avoids incompatible material joints, uses mono-materials, and tags polymers with **ISO 11469** resin codes.

4. **Design for Energy Efficiency (DFEE)** — Reduces operational energy via *low-friction surfaces, lightweighting, regenerative systems*.

5. **Design for Remanufacturing (DFRem)** — Components designed to be inspected, cleaned, repaired, and reassembled.

6. **Design for Durability / Longevity** — Extending product life is the *single most effective* eco-strategy, because the impact per functional unit drops.

7. **Design for Minimal Material Usage** — Topology optimization, hollow shafts, thin-wall castings.

8. **Design for Modular Upgrade** — Replace only the *defective module* (e.g., smartphone battery) instead of the whole product.

### Cluster B — Material-Stage Techniques

9. **Material Substitution** — Replacing a high-impact material (e.g., virgin aluminium) with a *lower-impact alternative* (e.g., recycled aluminium, bio-composites).

10. **Use of Renewable / Bio-based Materials** — Bamboo, natural fibre composites, bioplastics (PLA, PHA).

11. **Lightweighting** — High strength-to-weight alloys (Al, Mg, Ti, advanced high-strength steels) reduce transportation energy.

12. **Material Selection Indices** — The Ashby method extended with **eco-indicators** (e.g., embodied energy, CO₂ footprint per kg).

### Cluster C — Process-Stage Techniques (Manufacturing)

13. **Near-Net-Shape Manufacturing (NNS)** — Casting, forging, powder metallurgy, additive manufacturing (3D printing) that minimize machining scrap. Machining swarf can be 50–80% of the input bar weight in conventional turning — NNS collapses this.

14. **Dry / Near-Dry Machining** — Eliminating or drastically reducing cutting fluids (MQL — Minimum Quantity Lubrication).

15. **Aggressive Machining Parameters** — Higher cutting speeds/depths reduce *specific energy* (energy per cm³ of material removed).

16. **Cold Working over Hot Working** — Eliminates furnace energy and scale loss.

17. **Group Technology / Cellular Manufacturing** — Reduces material handling, transport, and setup waste.

18. **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** — Material utilization can reach **>95%** versus 5–15% in subtractive machining of complex parts.

### Cluster D — Energy-Stage Techniques

19. **Energy Efficiency Improvement** — IE3/IE4 motors, VFD drives, LED lighting, waste-heat recovery.

20. **Renewable Energy Integration** — Solar PV on factory roofs, biomass boilers, wind turbines.

21. **Cogeneration / Combined Heat & Power (CHP)** — Uses waste heat from electricity generation for process heating, achieving efficiencies of **80–90%** versus 30–40% for separate generation.

22. **Process Integration / Pinch Analysis** — Matches hot/cold streams in a plant to minimize external heating/cooling.

### Cluster E — End-of-Life Techniques

23. **The 3R Hierarchy** — **Reduce → Reuse → Recycle** (in that priority order; KTU 2024 explicitly tests this order).

24. **The 9R Framework** — Extended circular-economy ladder: *Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Refurbish, Remanufacture, Repurpose, Recycle, Recover*.

25. **Closed-Loop Recycling** — Recycled material re-enters the *same* product (e.g., PET bottle → PET bottle).

26. **Open-Loop Recycling (Downcycling)** — Material degrades in quality (e.g., PET → polyester fibre).

27. **Safe Disposal / Biodegradation** — For unavoidable waste.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule (Examiners love this):** The hierarchy is *always* **Reduce > Reuse > Recycle > Recover > Dispose**. Disposal is the *last* resort, never the first.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes every quantitative equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 4. Note the careful use of `\vert` (NOT the pipe character `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units | KTU 2024 Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Material Yield (Efficiency)** | $Y = \dfrac{M_{\text{useful part}}}{M_{\text{raw input}}} \times 100\%$ | $M_{\text{useful}}$ = mass of finished part; $M_{\text{raw}}$ = mass of starting stock | % | Higher = greener. AM can reach $Y > 95\%$. |
| 2 | **Specific Energy (Cutting)** | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{Q \cdot v}$ where $Q = f \cdot d$ | $P$ = cutting power (W); $v$ = cutting speed (m/s); $f$ = feed (mm/rev); $d$ = depth (mm) | J/mm³ or W·s/mm³ | Lower $E_s$ = greener machining. |
| 3 | **Energy Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{useful output}}}{E_{\text{total input}}} \times 100\%$ | $E_{\text{useful}}$ = energy in desired work; $E_{\text{total}}$ = all energy in | % | CHP can exceed 80%. |
| 4 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (Energy)** | $OEE_E = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = availability, $P$ = performance, $Q$ = quality | fraction | Running at full load is *greener* than idling. |
| 5 | **Recycling Rate (Closed Loop)** | $R_{\text{cl}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{recycled into same product}}}{M_{\text{total material input}}} \times 100\%$ | mass fractions | % | True circularity indicator. |
| 6 | **Recycled Content (Virgin Fraction)** | $X_v = 1 - X_r$ where $X_r = \dfrac{M_{\text{recycled}}}{M_{\text{total}}}$ | $X_r$ = recycled fraction; $X_v$ = virgin fraction | fraction | $X_r = 0.35$ means 35% recycled. |
| 7 | **CO₂ Equivalent (Global Warming Potential)** | $GWP = \sum_i (m_i \cdot GWP_i)$ | $m_i$ = mass of pollutant $i$; $GWP_i$ = 100-yr GWP factor | kg CO₂e | CO₂ = 1; CH₄ = 28; N₂O = 265 (IPCC AR5). |
| 8 | **Carbon Footprint per Functional Unit** | $CF = \dfrac{\text{Total GWP over life cycle}}{\text{Functional Unit (e.g., km driven, hours of use)}}$ | LCA output ÷ service delivered | kg CO₂e/FU | The fairest impact measure. |
| 9 | **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED)** | $CED = \sum_j (E_j \cdot f_j)$ | $E_j$ = energy of form $j$ (electrical, thermal, etc.); $f_j$ = primary energy factor | MJ (primary) | Includes feedstock energy. |
| 10 | **Eco-Efficiency (Eco-indicator 99)** | $EI = \dfrac{\text{Environmental Impact Score}}{\text{Product Value Score}}$ | dimensionless | - | Lower EI = more sustainable. |
| 11 | **Embodied Energy per kg** | $EE = \dfrac{E_{\text{total, cradle-to-gate}}}{M_{\text{product}}}$ | Total energy from raw extraction to factory gate | MJ/kg | Al ≈ 170 MJ/kg; Steel ≈ 25 MJ/kg; Wood ≈ 10 MJ/kg. |
| 12 | **Demanufacturing Time (DFD Index)** | $DTD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{di} \cdot n_i)$ | $t_{di}$ = avg disassembly time of joint type $i$; $n_i$ = number of joints of type $i$ | seconds | Lower DTD = better for DFD. |
| 13 | **LCA Impact Category Weighting** | $S_{total} = \sum_k w_k \cdot S_k$ | $w_k$ = weight of category $k$; $S_k$ = normalized score | dimensionless | $\sum w_k = 1$. |
| 14 | **MQL Fluid Flow Rate** | $Q_{MQL} \le 50 \text{ ml/h}$ | MQL = Minimum Quantity Lubrication | ml/h | vs. flood cooling at 10–20 L/min. |
| 15 | **Transport Energy per kg-km** | $E_t = m \cdot d \cdot k_{mode}$ | $m$ = mass; $d$ = distance; $k_{mode}$ = specific energy of mode (kJ/kg·km) | kJ | Truck ≈ 1.8; Rail ≈ 0.3; Ship ≈ 0.15. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Mistake:** Students often write $|x|$ for absolute value inside markdown tables, which **breaks the table rendering**. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX. This protocol is mandated by the KTU 2024 digital answer-script platform.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Automotive Industry** — Lightweighting an EV by 100 kg reduces lifetime emissions by ~2 tonnes CO₂e.
- **Electronics** — DFD enables urban mining; 1 tonne of mobile-phone PCBs contains more gold than 1 tonne of gold ore.
- **Aerospace** — AM (e.g., GE LEAP fuel nozzle) reduces 18 parts to 1, weight by 25%, and fuel burn by 15%.
- **White Goods (Refrigerators)** — Phase-out of HFCs reduced GWP of refrigerant by >1000×.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section converts the qualitative content above into *quantitative* derivations and worked examples. Since Module 4 is partially qualitative, the derivations focus on **LCA phases, material/energy flow mathematics, and worked numerical problems**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Four Phases of LCA (ISO 14040)

> [!NOTE]
> The Life Cycle Assessment methodology prescribed by **ISO 14040:2006** and **ISO 14044:2006** has **four mandatory phases**. This derivation is a *structural* derivation — it shows how each phase mathematically feeds the next.

**Step 1 — Goal and Scope Definition**
Define the **functional unit (FU)**. The FU is the reference to which *all* inputs and outputs are normalized. For example: *"Provide 1000 hours of illumination from an LED bulb."*

$$
FU = \text{service delivered, not product mass}
$$

**Step 2 — Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)**
Construct a flow table of all elementary flows (resources in, emissions out) per FU.

$$
LCI = \{ (m_i^{in}, m_j^{out}) \}_{i=1..n, \; j=1..m} \quad \text{[kg per FU]}
$$

**Step 3 — Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)**
Convert each inventory item into impact category scores using *characterization factors*.

$$
S_k = \sum_{x} CF_{x,k} \cdot m_x
$$

where $S_k$ is the score for impact category $k$ (e.g., kg CO₂e for GWP), $CF_{x,k}$ is the characterization factor of substance $x$ in category $k$, and $m_x$ is the mass of $x$ emitted.

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
Compare scenarios, perform sensitivity analysis, and draw conclusions. A single-score aggregation is *optional*:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot S_k^{norm}
$$

where $w_k$ is the weight, $S_k^{norm}$ is the normalized-and-weighted score, and $\sum_k w_k = 1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Yield Improvement Through Near-Net-Shape (NNS) Processes

**Problem Statement:** A steel shaft of finished mass 2.0 kg is currently machined from a hot-rolled round bar of mass 8.0 kg. The proposed alternative is a forged near-net-shape blank of mass 2.6 kg. Calculate the **material yield improvement** and the **mass of swarf saved per 1000 parts**.

**Step 1 — Conventional (Subtractive) Process Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{conv}} &= \frac{M_{\text{useful}}}{M_{\text{raw}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{2.0 \text{ kg}}{8.0 \text{ kg}} \times 100\% \\
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Near-Net-Shape Process Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{NNS}} &= \frac{M_{\text{useful}}}{M_{\text{raw,NNS}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{2.0 \text{ kg}}{2.6 \text{ kg}} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 76.92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Yield Improvement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Y &= Y_{\text{NNS}} - Y_{\text{conv}} \\
&= 76.92\% - 25\% \\
&= 51.92 \text{ percentage points}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Relative Yield Improvement (R-ratio)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{Y_{\text{NNS}}}{Y_{\text{conv}}} = \frac{76.92}{25} \\
&\approx 3.08 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{a 208% relative gain}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Swarf Saved Per 1000 Parts**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m_{\text{swarf/part}} &= M_{\text{raw,conv}} - M_{\text{raw,NNS}} = 8.0 - 2.6 = 5.4 \text{ kg} \\
M_{\text{swarf, 1000 parts}} &= 5.4 \times 1000 = 5400 \text{ kg} = 5.4 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Embodied Energy Saved (Steel EE ≈ 25 MJ/kg)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= M_{\text{swarf, 1000 parts}} \times EE_{\text{steel}} \\
&= 5400 \text{ kg} \times 25 \text{ MJ/kg} \\
&= 135{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 37{,}500 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — CO₂ Saved (Steel ≈ 1.85 kg CO₂e per kg virgin steel)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta CO_2 &= 5400 \text{ kg} \times 1.85 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg} \\
&\approx 9990 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} \;\;(\approx 10 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Insight:** A 14-mark Part B question may *not* give the embodied energy or CO₂ factor; the student must remember and apply the standard steel value ($\approx 25$ MJ/kg, $\approx 1.85$ kg CO₂e/kg). This is a high-mark-differentiation step.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Comparison: Dry vs. Flood vs. MQL Machining

**Problem Statement:** A CNC turning operation on mild steel uses the following parameters:
- Spindle power (flood cooling): $P_{\text{flood}} = 7.5$ kW
- Spindle power (dry): $P_{\text{dry}} = 6.8$ kW
- Spindle power (MQL): $P_{\text{MQL}} = 6.95$ kW
- Cutting speed $v = 150$ m/min = $2.5$ m/s
- Feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev
- Depth of cut $d = 2.0$ mm
- MRR (flood) = $Q = f \cdot d \cdot v$

**Step 1 — Material Removal Rate (MRR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= f \cdot d \cdot v \\
&= (0.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/rev}) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}) \times 2.5 \text{ m/s} \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 3600 \text{ s/h} \\
&\approx 35.3 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Specific Energy for Each Mode**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{s,\text{flood}} &= \frac{P}{Q_{\text{vol}}} = \frac{7500 \text{ W}}{1.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 6.0 \times 10^9 \text{ J/m}^3 = 6.0 \text{ GJ/m}^3 \\
E_{s,\text{dry}} &= \frac{6800}{1.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.44 \text{ GJ/m}^3 \\
E_{s,\text{MQL}} &= \frac{6950}{1.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.56 \text{ GJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Saved per Hour (Dry vs. Flood)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{\text{hourly}} &= (P_{\text{flood}} - P_{\text{dry}}) \times 3600 \text{ s} \\
&= (7.5 - 6.8) \text{ kW} \times 3600 \text{ s} \\
&= 700 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 2.52 \text{ MJ/h} = 0.7 \text{ kWh/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Coolant Waste Avoided (MQL vs. Flood)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flood flow rate} &\approx 15 \text{ L/min} = 900 \text{ L/h} \\
\text{MQL flow rate} &\approx 50 \text{ ml/h} \\
\text{Water + oil saved per hour} &= 900 - 0.05 \approx 899.95 \text{ L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation illustrates the **dual benefit** of MQL: marginal energy savings (≈7%) but **massive** waste-fluid elimination (>99.9%).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Carbon Footprint of Material Substitution

**Problem Statement:** A car bumper is currently made of **virgin steel** (mass = 12 kg). An alternative is **glass-fibre reinforced polymer (GFRP)** (mass = 6 kg) with the same functional performance over the vehicle's life. The vehicle covers 150,000 km. Calculate the *life-cycle CO₂ saving* per bumper, using:

- Steel: $EE = 25$ MJ/kg, $CF = 1.85$ kg CO₂e/kg
- GFRP: $EE = 90$ MJ/kg, $CF = 5.5$ kg CO₂e/kg
- Vehicle *operational* emission: reducing 1 kg of vehicle mass saves **0.5 g CO₂e/km** (lifetime).

**Step 1 — Material-Phase (Cradle-to-Gate) CO₂**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_2^{\text{steel, mat}} &= 12 \times 1.85 = 22.2 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} \\
CO_2^{\text{GFRP, mat}} &= 6 \times 5.5 = 33.0 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Use-Phase (Operational) CO₂ Saved by Lightweighting**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m &= 12 - 6 = 6 \text{ kg} \\
CO_2^{\text{use, saved}} &= 6 \text{ kg} \times 0.5 \text{ g CO}_2\text{e/km} \times 150{,}000 \text{ km} \\
&= 6 \times 0.5 \times 150{,}000 \text{ g} \\
&= 450{,}000 \text{ g} = 450 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Net Life-Cycle Saving**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta CO_2^{\text{life cycle}} &= CO_2^{\text{use, saved}} - (CO_2^{\text{GFRP, mat}} - CO_2^{\text{steel, mat}}) \\
&= 450 - (33.0 - 22.2) \\
&= 450 - 10.8 \\
&= 439.2 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e per bumper}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** This problem is the *Killer Question* type — it shows that a *worse* material-phase impact (GFRP > Steel) can be *overwhelmingly compensated* by the use-phase savings. The student must **not stop at Step 1** — this is the single most common error in valuation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Techniques to Impact Categories

| Technique ↓ / Impact → | **Resource Depletion** | **Energy Use** | **CO₂ Emissions** | **Toxicity** | **Waste** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lightweighting | M | M | **H** | L | L |
| NNS / AM | **H** | M | M | L | **H** |
| Dry / MQL Machining | L | M | L | **H** | **H** |
| Material Substitution | **H** | M | **H** | **H** | M |
| Remanufacturing | **H** | **H** | **H** | M | **H** |
| Renewable Energy | L | **H** | **H** | L | L |
| DFD / DFR | M | M | M | M | **H** |
| 3R Hierarchy | M | M | M | M | **H** |

*H = High reduction, M = Moderate, L = Low.* This matrix is a KTU-board examiner's favourite "select any 2 techniques and justify" question stem.
