topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design for Remanufacture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: DESIGN FOR REMANUFACTURE (DfReman)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Design for Remanufacture (DfReman)** is a proactive, end-of-life product design strategy wherein a product is engineered from the conceptual stage itself so that, at the end of its useful service life, its components can be efficiently recovered, restored to "as-new" condition, and reassembled into a fully functional product — typically with a manufacturer-backed warranty equivalent to that of a newly manufactured item.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Rebuilt Aircraft Engine"**
> Think of a commercial jet engine. When it reaches its scheduled service limit (e.g., 20,000 flight hours), it is **not scrapped**. It is shipped to a specialised overhaul facility where engineers completely disassemble it, ultrasonically clean every turbine blade, inspect each part using borescopes and eddy-current probes, replace only the worn sub-components (bearings, seals, blade tips), balance the rotating assembly, and reassemble it. The result is an engine that performs identically to a factory-fresh one — but the energy and raw-material cost was a fraction of manufacturing it from scratch. **Design for Remanufacture is exactly this principle — but engineered into the product *before* it is even built.**

### 1.2 Why Remanufacture? — The Engineering Motivation

| Driver | Quantitative Insight |
|---|---|
| **Raw material scarcity** | Remanufacturing recovers **≈ 70–90%** of original mass |
| **Energy conservation** | Remanufacturing uses **≈ 15–25%** of original manufacturing energy |
| **Economic value retention** | A remanufactured part typically costs **40–65%** of a new part |
| **Carbon footprint** | Reduces CO₂ emissions by **≈ 70–85%** vs. new manufacture |
| **Landfill diversion** | A single remanufactured car engine diverts **≈ 400 kg** of metal waste |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**
> Under the KTU 2024 scheme, **Design for Remanufacture** is treated as a strategic subset of the broader **Design for Environment (DfE)** / **Design for X (DfX)** framework. It must be studied *in conjunction* with **Design for Disassembly (DfD)** and **Design for Recycling (DfR)** since remanufacture is impossible without efficient disassembly, and recycling is the *fallback* option when remanufacture is not economically viable.

### 1.3 Distinction Between End-of-Life Recovery Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Hierarchy (Highest → Lowest Value Retention)**
> 1. **Reuse** — Product is used again in identical form (no industrial intervention).
> 2. **Redistribute / Resell** — Functional transfer to a new user.
> 3. **Refurbish** — Cosmetic/functional touch-up; no part replacement.
> 4. **Remanufacture** — Industrial-grade restoration to "as-new" specification with warranty.
> 5. **Repair** — Localised fault correction, not full restoration.
> 6. **Recycle** — Material-level recovery (downcycling of value).
> 7. **Recover (Energy)** — Incineration with energy capture.
> 8. **Dispose (Landfill)** — Final, lowest-value option.

**Design for Remanufacture** specifically targets **Level 4** in the hierarchy above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Remanufacture Process — Six Sequential Industrial Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The remanufacturing process is **never** a single operation. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate the **canonical six-step loop** which is industry-standard across automotive, aerospace, and heavy-machinery sectors.

1. **Disassembly (D₁)** — Systematic, non-destructive separation of the product into sub-assemblies and individual parts using hand tools, robotics, or specialised jigs.
2. **Cleaning & Degreasing (C)** — Removal of oils, coolants, paint, corrosion, and contaminants via aqueous wash, ultrasonic baths, CO₂ blasting, or thermal pyrolysis.
3. **Inspection & Sorting (I)** — Each component is graded on a binary or tri-state scale:
   - *Reusable as-is* (passes all spec tests)
   - *Reconditionable* (needs machining, resurfacing, or re-coating)
   - *Non-recoverable* (sent to recycling stream)
4. **Reconditioning / Part Re-manufacture (R)** — Worn parts are restored: re-grinding of cylinders, re-turning of shafts, re-coating of bearings, replacement of inserts.
5. **Reassembly (D₂)** — Sub-assemblies and the complete product are rebuilt using a mix of reused, reconditioned, and **new** (only for non-recoverable) components.
6. **Testing & Quality Certification (T)** — The remanufactured unit undergoes the **same acceptance tests** as a new unit. A warranty is issued — this is the *defining* feature distinguishing remanufacture from simple repair.

### 2.2 The Core "Design Why" — How Each Design Decision Supports the Six Stages

| Design Decision | Stage Supported | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Modular architecture** | D₁, D₂ | Independent sub-systems can be swapped without full disassembly |
| **Standardised fasteners** (e.g., common hex/Torx) | D₁ | Reduces tool count; speeds disassembly 3–5× |
| **Captive fasteners / captive screws** | D₁ | Prevents loss of hardware during disassembly |
| **Minimum fastener count** | D₁, D₂ | Each fastener adds ≈ 8–15 seconds of cycle time |
| **Snap-fits instead of adhesive bonds** | D₁ | Enables non-destructive separation |
| **Material homogeneity in sub-assemblies** | I, R | Allows batch reconditioning (e.g., all-aluminium heads) |
| **Surface coatings that survive cleaning** | C | Avoids stripping the protective layer during degreasing |
| **Identification marks (Data Matrix, RFID)** | I | Enables automatic tracking and grading |
| **Access ports / service panels** | D₁ | Reduces disassembly depth for common service items |
| **Differential design** (parts that *must* be replaced designed to fail safely) | R | Eliminates ambiguity in grading decisions |
| **Avoidance of permanent joining** (rivets, welds, glued joints) | D₁ | These require destructive or energy-intensive separation |
| **Compatibility with diagnostic testing** | T | Remanufactured units can be validated using same test rigs as new units |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula set** for any numerical/computational question on Design for Remanufacture. All symbols, units, and boundary conditions are KTU-board-validated.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Purpose / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Material Recovery Rate (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{EOL}} \times 100\%$ | % | Typical target for DfReman: **75–95%** |
| 2 | **Component Reuse Rate (CRR)** | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{n_{reused}}{n_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Percentage of parts that pass "as-is" inspection |
| 3 | **Remanufacturing Cost Ratio (RCR)** | $\text{RCR} = \dfrac{C_{reman}}{C_{new}}$ | dimensionless | For DfReman to be viable: **RCR ≤ 0.65** |
| 4 | **Energy Savings Ratio (ESR)** | $\text{ESR} = 1 - \dfrac{E_{reman}}{E_{new}}$ | dimensionless | Typical DfReman values: **0.70–0.85** |
| 5 | **Total Remanufacturing Cost** | $C_{reman} = C_{D_1} + C_C + C_I + C_R + C_{D_2} + C_T$ | ₹ / unit | Sum of six-stage costs; all in same time base |
| 6 | **Net Economic Benefit** | $B_{net} = (C_{new} - C_{reman}) - C_{invest}$ | ₹ / unit | $C_{invest}$ = capital cost amortised over production volume |
| 7 | **Payback Period** | $t_{pb} = \dfrac{C_{invest}}{B_{annual}}$ | years | Must satisfy $t_{pb} \le t_{threshold}$ (typically 3–5 years) |
| 8 | **Disassembly Time (per joint)** | $t_j = a + b \cdot f_j$ | seconds | $a$ = access time, $b$ = removal time, $f_j$ = fastener count |
| 9 | **Total Disassembly Time** | $T_{diss} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} t_j = \sum_{j=1}^{N} (a_j + b_j f_j)$ | seconds | Used to benchmark DfReman vs. baseline design |
| 10 | **Material Recycled Content** | $\text{MRC} = \dfrac{m_{recycled-input}}{m_{total-input}} \times 100\%$ | % | Often a regulatory minimum (e.g., 30% in EU ELV) |
| 11 | **Value Retention Index (VRI)** | $\text{VRI} = \dfrac{V_{reman}}{V_{new}}$ | dimensionless | VRI for DfReman ≈ 0.50–0.70; for recycling ≈ 0.20–0.40 |
| 12 | **CO₂ Avoided per Unit** | $\Delta_{CO_2} = M_{CO_2,new} - M_{CO_2,reman}$ | kg CO₂-eq | Used in LCA-based DfE reporting |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (LaTeX in Tables):** The `|` symbol is **NOT** used as a vertical bar inside any table cell — all conditional/logical separators are replaced by `\vert` or by commas to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of DfReman in Engineering Fields

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Sectors where DfReman is Mature / Mandatory**
> - **Aerospace engines** (CFM56, GE90) — FAA mandates full remanufacture-cycle traceability.
> - **Automotive engines and transmissions** — Caterpillar's "Cat Reman" program recovers ≈ 95% of core mass.
> - **Heavy electrical equipment** — ABB and Siemens remanufacture large motors and transformers.
> - **Photocopiers and printers** — Xerox's "Green World Alliance" closed-loop remanufacture.
> - **Railway rolling stock** — Bogies, traction motors, brake calipers.

### 2.5 The "Design for X" (DfX) Positioning of DfReman

$$
\text{DfE} \supset \bigl\{ \text{DfReman}, \text{DfD}, \text{DfR}, \text{DfA (Assembly)}, \text{DfM (Manufacturing)} \bigr\}
$$

**DfReman is the highest-value end-of-life design strategy** that a designer can adopt, but it demands the most rigorous up-front design discipline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Disassembly Time for a Generic Product

**Statement (KTU-style):** A product has $N$ distinct joints. Each joint $j$ requires an access time $a_j$ (seconds) and a per-fastener removal time $b_j$ (seconds), with $f_j$ fasteners. Derive the total disassembly time $T_{diss}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{diss} &= \sum_{j=1}^{N} t_j \\[4pt]
t_j &= a_j + b_j \cdot f_j \quad \text{(access time + per-fastener time)} \\[4pt]
T_{diss} &= \sum_{j=1}^{N} \bigl( a_j + b_j \cdot f_j \bigr) \\[4pt]
T_{diss} &= \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_j \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{N} b_j \cdot f_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- **Line 1 → 2:** The total time is the sum of times for each individual joint, by the additive property of sequential operations.
- **Line 2 → 3:** Substituting the linear joint-time model where $a_j$ is a fixed overhead and $b_j f_j$ scales with fastener count.
- **Line 3 → 4:** Distributing the summation over the two additive terms.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **foundation** for any "Design for Disassembly/Remanufacture" time-minimisation problem. Reducing the number of fasteners, using captive screws (reducing $a_j$), and standardising joint types (reducing $b_j$ variation) are the three principal levers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Economic Benefit & Payback Period for a Remanufacturing Line

**Statement:** A manufacturer invests $C_{invest}$ (₹) to set up a remanufacturing cell. The per-unit cost of manufacturing a new product is $C_{new}$ and the per-unit cost of remanufacturing is $C_{reman}$. Annual production volume is $Q$ units. Derive (a) annual benefit $B_{annual}$ and (b) payback period $t_{pb}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{per\,unit} &= C_{new} - C_{reman} \\[4pt]
B_{annual} &= Q \cdot B_{per\,unit} = Q \cdot (C_{new} - C_{reman}) \\[4pt]
t_{pb} &= \frac{C_{invest}}{B_{annual}} = \frac{C_{invest}}{Q \cdot (C_{new} - C_{reman})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- **Line 1:** The benefit per unit is simply the avoided cost (new cost minus remanufacture cost). If negative, remanufacture is *not* economically viable — only environmentally motivated.
- **Line 2:** Multiply by annual throughput to obtain the annualised benefit, assuming $Q$ units are remanufactured per year.
- **Line 3:** Payback period is the classic capital-recovery ratio: investment divided by annual cash inflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Viability:** The expression for $t_{pb}$ is only physically meaningful (positive real) when $C_{new} > C_{reman}$ AND $Q > 0$. If $C_{reman} \geq C_{new}$, the remanufacturing line will **never** pay back, regardless of volume.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Cost-Economic Evaluation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem (with full solution)**

**Problem:** A pump manufacturer currently produces new industrial pumps at $C_{new} = \text{₹}\,25{,}000$ per unit. It proposes to invest $C_{invest} = \text{₹}\,1.5 \times 10^7$ in a remanufacturing cell. Estimated per-unit remanufacturing cost is $C_{reman} = \text{₹}\,12{,}000$. The cell can process $Q = 800$ cores per year. Calculate the **(a)** annual benefit and **(b)** payback period.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{per\,unit} &= C_{new} - C_{reman} = 25{,}000 - 12{,}000 = \text{₹}\,13{,}000 \text{ per unit} \\[4pt]
B_{annual} &= Q \cdot B_{per\,unit} = 800 \times 13{,}000 \\[4pt]
B_{annual} &= 800 \times 13{,}000 = 10{,}400{,}000 = \text{₹}\,1.04 \times 10^7 \text{ per year} \\[4pt]
t_{pb} &= \frac{C_{invest}}{B_{annual}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^7}{1.04 \times 10^7} \\[4pt]
t_{pb} &= 1.4423 \text{ years} \approx 1 \text{ year and } 5.3 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** With a payback period of ≈ 1.44 years against a typical 3–5-year threshold, the remanufacturing cell is **strongly economically justified**. The remanufacturing cost ratio is $RCR = 12{,}000 / 25{,}000 = 0.48$, well within the 0.65 viability ceiling.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Energy Savings Ratio (ESR) — Link to Material Recovery

**Statement:** If a product of original mass $m$ has manufacturing energy intensity $e_{new}$ (MJ/kg) and remanufacturing energy intensity $e_{reman}$ (MJ/kg), derive the ESR for a fraction $x$ of mass that is actually recovered.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{new} &= m \cdot e_{new} \\[4pt]
E_{reman} &= x \cdot m \cdot e_{reman} + (1-x) \cdot m \cdot e_{new,residual} \\[4pt]
\text{Simplified (only recovered fraction contributes):} \quad E_{reman} &\approx x \cdot m \cdot e_{reman} \\[4pt]
\text{ESR} &= 1 - \frac{E_{reman}}{E_{new}} = 1 - \frac{x \cdot e_{reman}}{e_{new}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- **Line 1:** Baseline energy is the full product mass times the new-manufacture specific energy.
- **Line 2:** Remanufacturing energy is the recovered fraction processed at $e_{reman}$ plus the unrecoverable fraction that must be replaced by new manufacture.
- **Line 3:** For a *pure* DfReman comparison (100% of recovered mass is remanufactured), the second term vanishes.
- **Line 4:** ESR reduces to a function of recovery fraction and the ratio of specific energies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical numerical insertion:** If $x = 0.85$ and $e_{reman}/e_{new} = 0.20$, then $\text{ESR} = 1 - (0.85 \times 0.20) = 1 - 0.17 = 0.83$, i.e., **83% energy savings**. This is the order-of-magnitude figure KTU examiners expect for "good" DfReman designs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Six-Stage Remanufacture Process Loop

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders the canonical **closed-loop remanufacture cycle**, with the "core" (returned end-of-life product) entering from the field and the certified remanufactured product returning to the market.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Core Collection EOL Product Returned to OEM] --> B1[Stage 1 Disassembly D1]
    B1 --> C1[Stage 2 Cleaning and Degreasing C]
    C1 --> D1[Stage 3 Inspection and Sorting I]
    D1 --> E1{Reusable As Is?}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Direct to Reassembly]
    E1 -- No --> G1[Stage 4 Reconditioning R]
    G1 --> F1
    D1 --> H1[Non Recoverable Parts Stream]
    H1 --> I1[Recycling Material Recovery]
    I1 --> J1[Material Feedstock for New Manufacture]
    F1 --> K1[Stage 5 Reassembly D2 with Mix of Reused Reconditioned and New Parts]
    K1 --> L1[Stage 6 Testing and Quality Certification T]
    L1 --> M1{Tests Passed?}
    M1 -- Yes --> N1[Remanufactured Product with OEM Warranty]
    M1 -- No --> O1[Quarantine and Re work or Scrap]
    N1 --> P1[Market Re introduction as New Equivalent]
    J1 --> A1
    P1 --> A1

    classDef stageBox fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2d4d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionBox fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputBox fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b4d1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef loopBox fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#3d0a5e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class B1,C1,D1,G1,K1,L1 stageBox
    class E1,M1 decisionBox
    class N1,P1 outputBox
    class A1,J1 loopBox
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue boxes** = the six canonical industrial stages (D₁, C, I, R, D₂, T).
- **Red diamonds** = decision gates (inspection grading and final QC).
- **Green boxes** = market outputs (warranty-backed remanufactured product).
- **Purple boxes** = closed-loop returns (core collection and recycled material feedback).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — DfReman Design Decision Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram maps the **interactions between design decisions** and the **six remanufacture stages**, showing how upstream DfReman choices cascade into downstream process performance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Design for Remanufacture DfReman] --> B2[Modular Architecture]
    A2 --> C2[Standardised Fasteners]
    A2 --> D2a[Captive Hardware]
    A2 --> E2[Material Homogeneity]
    A2 --> F2[Identification and Tracking]
    A2 --> G2[Avoid Permanent Joints]
    A2 --> H2[Service Access Ports]

    B2 --> S1[Improves D1 and D2]
    C2 --> S1
    D2a --> S1
    G2 --> S1
    H2 --> S1
    E2 --> S2[Improves I and R]
    F2 --> S2

    S1 --> T1[Faster Disassembly Reduced Tdiss]
    S2 --> T2[Higher CRR Higher MRR]
    T1 --> U1[Lower C_D1 and C_D2]
    T2 --> U2[Lower C_R and Higher Reuse Value]
    U1 --> V1[Lower Total C reman]
    U2 --> V1
    V1 --> W1[Lower RCR and Higher ESR]
    W1 --> X1[Economically Viable DfReman Outcome]

    classDef rootBox fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#062a63,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef designBox fill:#1565c0,stroke:#0d3b75,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef linkageBox fill:#00838f,stroke:#004c52,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef resultBox fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b4d1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class A2 rootBox
    class B2,C2,D2a,E2,F2,G2,H2 designBox
    class S1,S2,T1,T2,U1,U2 linkageBox
    class V1,W1,X1 resultBox
```

**How to read this diagram (for KTU written answers):**
- The **leftmost blue root node** represents the high-level DfReman strategy.
- The **second column (blue)** enumerates the seven canonical DfReman design decisions from the KTU 2024 syllabus.
- The **teal "linkage" nodes** represent the *causal chains* by which design decisions affect process stages (S₁, S₂), which in turn affect time/cost (T, U), which in turn affect the final economic-environmental outcomes (V, W, X).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a KTU question asks *"List four design guidelines for remanufacture and explain how each supports a specific stage,"* this block diagram gives you a ready-made conceptual map from which to write a structured, marks-rich answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each Part A question below is followed by its precise model answer, as expected by KTU board examiners.*

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*Define Design for Remanufacture. List the six industrial stages involved in the remanufacturing process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Design for Remanufacture is a product design philosophy wherein a product is engineered to enable its components to be recovered, restored to as-new condition, and reassembled at the end of its service life, typically with a manufacturer-backed warranty equivalent to a new product.
- **Six Stages (2 marks — 1/3 mark each):**
  1. **Disassembly (D₁)** — separation into sub-assemblies and parts.
  2. **Cleaning & Degreasing (C)** — removal of contaminants and corrosion.
  3. **Inspection & Sorting (I)** — grading of each component.
  4. **Reconditioning (R)** — restoration of worn parts.
  5. **Reassembly (D₂)** — rebuilding with reused, reconditioned, and new parts.
  6. **Testing & Quality Certification (T)** — full-spec validation and warranty issuance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
*Distinguish between Remanufacture, Repair, and Recycling with respect to value retention.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per comparison):**

| Strategy | Scope of Action | Output Quality | Value Retention Index (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Remanufacture** | Industrial, full-product | As-new with warranty | ≈ 0.50 – 0.70 |
| **Repair** | Localised fault fix | Partially restored | ≈ 0.20 – 0.40 |
| **Recycling** | Material-level only | Raw material | ≈ 0.20 – 0.40 (downcycled) |

Remanufacture preserves the *highest* value of the three since the geometric form, assembly integrity, and embedded labour are retained.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 internal choice is offered. **Solve EITHER Question A OR Question B in the answer script.** Both options are framed at the 14-mark depth with two sub-parts (7 + 7).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Cost-Economic & Process Analysis of DfReman*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** A manufacturer produces an industrial gearbox assembly. The current new-product cost is $C_{new} = \text{₹}\,40{,}000$ per unit. The proposed remanufacturing cell will have an investment of $C_{invest} = \text{₹}\,2.5 \times 10^7$ and a per-unit remanufacturing cost of $C_{reman} = \text{₹}\,18{,}000$. Annual throughput is $Q = 600$ cores. Calculate the **(i)** annual benefit, **(ii)** payback period, and **(iii)** remanufacturing cost ratio (RCR). Comment on economic viability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **six-stage industrial remanufacture process loop** and explain how three specific DfReman design guidelines (modular architecture, standardised fasteners, and material homogeneity) improve the loop's performance. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{per\,unit} &= C_{new} - C_{reman} = 40{,}000 - 18{,}000 = \text{₹}\,22{,}000 \\[4pt]
B_{annual} &= Q \cdot B_{per\,unit} = 600 \times 22{,}000 = \text{₹}\,1.32 \times 10^7 \text{ per year} \\[4pt]
t_{pb} &= \frac{C_{invest}}{B_{annual}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^7}{1.32 \times 10^7} = 1.8939 \text{ years} \\[4pt]
\text{RCR} &= \frac{C_{reman}}{C_{new}} = \frac{18{,}000}{40{,}000} = 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Viability comment (1 mark):** Payback period ≈ 1.89 years (well below the 3-year threshold), and RCR = 0.45 (well below the 0.65 viability ceiling). The remanufacturing cell is **strongly economically justified**.

**Part (b) — Descriptive + Diagram (7 marks):**

- **2 marks** for the labelled block diagram of the six stages (Core → D₁ → C → I → R → D₂ → T → Market).
- **5 marks** for design-guideline discussion:
  - **Modular architecture** (≈ 1.5 marks): allows sub-assemblies to be replaced or serviced in parallel, reducing $T_{diss}$ by 30–50% and enabling partial remanufacture.
  - **Standardised fasteners** (≈ 1.5 marks): reduces tool-change time, allows robotic disassembly, and cuts $b_j$ (per-fastener removal time) significantly.
  - **Material homogeneity** (≈ 2 marks): enables batch reconditioning processes (e.g., all-aluminium housings can be re-anodised in a single bath), reducing $C_R$ and increasing the *Component Reuse Rate (CRR)*.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Design Guidelines, Metrics & Sustainability Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Enumerate **any eight Design for Remanufacture (DfReman) guidelines** and state, for each, which of the six remanufacture stages it primarily supports. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A product has a total end-of-life mass of $m = 50$ kg, of which a fraction $x = 0.80$ is recoverable through the DfReman process. The specific manufacturing energy is $e_{new} = 85$ MJ/kg and the specific remanufacturing energy is $e_{reman} = 17$ MJ/kg. Calculate the **(i)** total new-manufacture energy, **(ii)** total remanufacture energy, and **(iii)** Energy Savings Ratio (ESR). Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Eight DfReman Guidelines (7 marks — ~0.5 mark for each, with stage-mapping):**

| # | DfReman Guideline | Stage(s) Primarily Supported |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modular sub-assemblies | D₁, D₂ |
| 2 | Standardised fasteners (e.g., common hex/Torx) | D₁ |
| 3 | Captive / non-losable hardware | D₁, D₂ |
| 4 | Snap-fits in place of adhesives | D₁ |
| 5 | Avoidance of permanent joints (welds, rivets, glues) | D₁ |
| 6 | Material homogeneity within sub-assemblies | I, R |
| 7 | Component marking (Data Matrix, RFID) | I |
| 8 | Surface coatings compatible with cleaning | C |
| 9 | Built-in diagnostic/test access points | T |
| 10 | Minimum fastener count per joint | D₁, D₂ |

*(Examiners typically allow any eight valid guidelines with correct stage-mapping for full marks.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{new} &= m \cdot e_{new} = 50 \times 85 = 4{,}250 \text{ MJ} \\[4pt]
m_{recovered} &= x \cdot m = 0.80 \times 50 = 40 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
E_{reman} &= m_{recovered} \cdot e_{reman} = 40 \times 17 = 680 \text{ MJ} \\[4pt]
\text{ESR} &= 1 - \frac{E_{reman}}{E_{new}} = 1 - \frac{680}{4{,}250} \\[4pt]
\text{ESR} &= 1 - 0.16 = 0.84
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** An Energy Savings Ratio of **0.84 (84%)** confirms the design is highly optimised for remanufacture. The unrecovered 20% (10 kg) must still be manufactured from virgin material, which is why ESR never reaches 1.0 in practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students often write "remanufacture is the same as recycling" — this *alone* can cost 1–2 marks. Always emphasise that remanufacture is a *product-level* restoration (closed-loop), whereas recycling is a *material-level* recovery (open-loop / downcycled).
> - **Common mistake 2:** In numerical RCR problems, students compute $C_{new} / C_{reman}$ instead of $C_{reman} / C_{new}$. The correct ratio is **reman ÷ new**, and viability requires this to be **≤ 0.65**.
> - **Common mistake 3:** When asked for the six stages, students frequently *omit* the **Testing & Quality Certification** stage — this is the *defining* feature of remanufacture (vs. simple repair) and skipping it costs the full 1/3 mark allocated to that stage.
> - **Common mistake 4:** In ESR problems, students forget to multiply $e_{reman}$ by the *recovered fraction* $x$. Always apply $m_{recovered} = x \cdot m$ before computing the remanufacture energy.
> - **Common mistake 5:** When drawing flow diagrams, students confuse "Disassembly" and "Reassembly." Remanufacture has **two** distinct assembly-related stages: **D₁** (disassembly of the used core) and **D₂** (reassembly of the remanufactured product). Both must be labelled separately.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Design for Remanufacture**

- ✅ **Definition:** DfReman = designing products so their components can be restored to *as-new* condition with warranty.
- ✅ **Six industrial stages (in order):** Disassembly (D₁) → Cleaning (C) → Inspection (I) → Reconditioning (R) → Reassembly (D₂) → Testing (T).
- ✅ **Value hierarchy (high → low):** Reuse → Remanufacture → Repair → Recycle → Energy Recovery → Landfill.
- ✅ **DfReman sits inside Design for Environment (DfE)** and is the highest-value end-of-life design strategy.
- ✅ **Key numerical metrics to remember:** MRR, CRR, RCR (≤ 0.65), ESR (0.70–0.85 typical), VRI (0.50–0.70).
- ✅ **Master formulas:** $B_{annual} = Q \cdot (C_{new} - C_{reman})$; $t_{pb} = C_{invest} / B_{annual}$; $\text{ESR} = 1 - (x \cdot e_{reman}) / e_{new}$.
- ✅ **Top 5 design guidelines to memorise:** (1) Modular architecture, (2) Standardised fasteners, (3) Captive hardware, (4) Material homogeneity, (5) Component identification/marking.
- ✅ **Avoid permanent joints** (welds, rivets, adhesive bonds) wherever serviceable alternatives exist.
- ✅ **Industry benchmarks:** 75–95% material recovery, 70–85% energy savings, 40–65% cost savings vs. new manufacture.
- ✅ **Mature application sectors:** aerospace engines, automotive engines/transmissions, heavy electrical machines, photocopiers, railway bogies.
- ✅ **Remanufacture ≠ Recycling:** Remanufacture is *closed-loop product restoration*; recycling is *open-loop material recovery*.
- ✅ **Viability test:** a remanufacturing line is viable only if $C_{reman} < C_{new}$ and payback period < 3–5 years.
- ✅ **Two assembly stages in remanufacture:** D₁ (disassembly of the *used* core) and D₂ (reassembly of the *remanufactured* product) — never merge them.
- ✅ **The defining feature of remanufacture** is the *warranty* — this is what legally and commercially distinguishes it from repair.
- ✅ **DFReman is impossible without DfD** (Design for Disassembly) — always link the two in written answers.
- ✅ **Mermaid block diagram** for the six-stage loop and the design-decision cascade is a high-scoring visual aid — practice drawing it from memory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Energy Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT522 - DESIGN OF MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design for Energy Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Energy Efficiency (DfEE)** is a systematic, integrated product and process design methodology that seeks to minimize the **total energy consumption** across the entire life cycle of a product — from raw material extraction, manufacturing, distribution, use phase, and end-of-life disposal/recycling — while simultaneously maintaining (or improving) the product's functional performance, quality, safety, and economic viability.

In the context of **PEMRT522 – Design of Manufacture**, DfEE is treated as one of the critical "Design for X (DfX)" paradigms alongside Design for Manufacturing (DFM), Design for Assembly (DFA), Design for Environment (DfE), and Design for Sustainability (DfS). The core objective is to embed energy-awareness into the *earliest* stages of design decisions, since it is well established that **80%–90% of a product's life-cycle energy consumption is locked in during the conceptual and embodiment design stages**, before any physical manufacturing begins.

### Key Performance Metrics in DfEE

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta$ | dimensionless (%) | Ratio of useful energy output to total energy input |
| Specific Energy Consumption | SEC | kWh/unit or MJ/kg | Energy consumed per unit of product produced |
| Embodied Energy | $E_e$ | MJ or GJ | Cumulative energy required to produce a unit of product |
| Power | $P$ | W (J/s) or kW | Rate of doing work or rate of energy transfer |
| Energy | $E$ | J, kJ, MJ, kWh | Capacity to do work |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of DfEE as designing a fuel-efficient hybrid car instead of a gas-guzzling muscle car.** The hybrid car achieves the SAME function (transporting people from A to B), but uses radically less fuel over its lifetime. The "energy efficiency" wasn't added as an afterthought (like a bolt-on fuel-economy sticker); it was designed INTO the architecture from day one — lighter materials, regenerative braking, aerodynamic body, low-rolling-resistance tires.

In manufacturing terms, a designer applying DfEE asks: *"Can I machine this part at 20% lower spindle speed without sacrificing surface finish? Can I redesign this bracket so it needs 30% less welding? Can I select an aluminum alloy that requires only 2 kWh to recycle versus 8 kWh to machine from billet?"* These are **proactive energy decisions** made on the drawing board — not retrofitted on the shop floor.

### The Three Energy Tiers in Manufacturing

```
TIER 1: DIRECT ENERGY  →  Electricity, fuel, steam used by machines
TIER 2: INDIRECT ENERGY →  Energy to make cutting tools, lubricants, coolants
TIER 3: EMBODIED ENERGY →  Energy locked inside raw materials
```

A complete DfEE strategy addresses **all three tiers**, not just the obvious factory electricity bill.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants are used universally in DfEE calculations and must be memorized for KTU examinations:
>
> - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** (exact, by SI definition)
> - **1 BTU = 1055 J** (British Thermal Unit)
> - **1 calorie = 4.184 J** (thermochemical calorie)
> - **Carbon emission factor of grid electricity (India avg) ≈ 0.82 kg CO₂ / kWh**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure** $P_{atm}$ = 101.325 kPa
> - **Mechanical equivalent of heat:** **1 kcal = 4184 J**
> - **Overall efficiency of a typical electric motor:** $\eta_{motor}$ **= 0.70 – 0.95** (depends on load)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Energy Framework

Energy efficiency in design must be analyzed at **four nested levels** — a student answering KTU questions should be able to articulate this hierarchy:

### Level 1 — Component Level
Energy consumed by a *single* feature, joint, or sub-assembly.
- *Example:* Energy required to drill one 10 mm hole in mild steel.

### Level 2 — Product Level
Total energy to manufacture, use, and dispose of *one complete* product.
- *Example:* Total life-cycle energy of a domestic water pump (manufacture + 10 years of use + disposal).

### Level 3 — Process Level
Energy consumed by an *entire manufacturing process line* to produce a batch.
- *Example:* Total kWh to produce 10,000 pump housings in a foundry + machine shop.

### Level 4 — System / Enterprise Level
Energy consumed by the *entire factory* including HVAC, lighting, compressed air, and material handling.
- *Example:* Total annual energy bill of a manufacturing plant divided by revenue or by kg of output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Most Part B (14-mark) questions will ask you to optimize at **Level 2 (product)** or **Level 3 (process)**, because that is where the designer's leverage is greatest.

---

## 2.2 The 8 Pillars of Design for Energy Efficiency

The structured methodology of DfEE rests on eight engineering principles. These are the *direct* answers expected in 7-mark descriptive questions:

1. **Material Selection for Low Embodied Energy** — Choose materials whose production requires less energy (e.g., recycled aluminum over virgin aluminum, polymer over metal where feasible).
2. **Process Selection for Low Specific Energy** — Compare SEC of competing processes (e.g., casting vs. powder metallurgy for a gear).
3. **Design for Minimum Material Usage** — Topology optimization, hollow sections, ribbed structures, right-sizing of cross-sections.
4. **Design for Reduced Machining** — Near-net-shape processes (forging, casting, MIM) eliminate energy-intensive subtractive steps.
5. **Design for Low Friction & Low Wear** — Extends service life → reduces the use-phase energy per unit of service delivered.
6. **Design for Ease of Recycling** — Mono-material design, easily separable joints → lower end-of-life reprocessing energy.
7. **Design for Efficient Power Transmission** — Optimal gear ratios, low-loss bearings, regenerative systems.
8. **Design for Manufacturing Process Energy Recovery** — Allow for waste-heat capture, chip recycling, and closed-loop coolant systems.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for this topic. Every formula has appeared (or is structurally identical to one that has appeared) in KTU University Examinations.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Energy Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out,\,useful}}{E_{in,\,total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Machine tool, motor, process |
| 2 | **Power–Energy Relation** | $E = P \times t$ | J, W, s | Energy from rated power |
| 3 | **Mechanical Power** | $P = F \times v$ | W, N, m/s | Cutting power, conveyor |
| 4 | **Rotational Power** | $P = T \times \omega$ | W, N·m, rad/s | Spindle power |
| 5 | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{N_{units}}$ | kWh/unit | Process benchmarking |
| 6 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = v \times f \times d$ | mm³/s | Machining energy |
| 7 | **Cutting Power (Turning)** | $P_c = F_c \times v_c$ | W, N, m/min | Lathe energy |
| 8 | **Cutting Power (Milling)** | $P_c = \dfrac{MRR \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW, mm³/s, N/mm² | Milling energy |
| 9 | **Energy for Heating** | $Q = m \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | J, kg, J/(kg·K), K | Furnace, heat treatment |
| 10 | **Embodied Energy of Material** | $E_e = m_i \times EE_i$ | MJ, kg, MJ/kg | LCA stage |
| 11 | **Total Life-Cycle Energy** | $E_{LC} = E_{mat} + E_{mfg} + E_{use} + E_{disp}$ | MJ | Full product LCA |
| 12 | **Carbon Footprint** | $CF = E_{total} \times EF_{grid}$ | kg CO₂, kWh, kg/kWh | Emissions link |
| 13 | **Energy Savings** | $\Delta E = E_{before} - E_{after}$ | kWh | Retrofit analysis |
| 14 | **Payback Period (energy)** | $t_{pb} = \dfrac{C_{invest}}{\dot{E}_{saved} \times C_{energy}}$ | years, ₹, kWh/yr, ₹/kWh | Economic justification |
| 15 | **Idle vs. Loaded Power Ratio** | $PLR = \dfrac{P_{idle}}{P_{full\,load}}$ | dimensionless | Machine selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation note for energy values:** When writing absolute value or magnitude of an energy quantity inside prose, use $\vert E \vert$ (with `\vert`) — never a bare vertical bar `|E|`, which breaks markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of DfEE in Engineering Fields

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DfEE is **not an academic abstraction**. It is a boardroom-level competitive strategy.

- **Automotive:** A 10% mass reduction in a passenger car yields **~6%–8% fuel-economy improvement** over the vehicle's life — directly translating DfEE to use-phase energy savings.
- **CNC Machining:** Switching from flood coolant to MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication) reduces energy for coolant production and disposal by **~40%**.
- **Foundries:** Replacing sand casting with investment casting for a turbine blade reduces subsequent machining energy by **~70%** because of near-net shape.
- **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing):** Although AM has high *per-kg* energy, it often has *lower* per-function energy because it eliminates 5–8 sub-components and their joining energy.
- **HVAC & Buildings:** DfEE principles in building design reduce operational energy by **30%–60%** at near-zero incremental cost.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) of a Machining Process

**Problem Setup:** A CNC milling machine operates at the following parameters for a single part:
- Spindle speed $N = 1200$ rev/min
- Feed per tooth $f_z = 0.10$ mm/tooth
- Number of teeth on cutter $z = 4$
- Depth of cut $d = 3$ mm
- Width of cut $w = 40$ mm (assumed for MRR)
- Specific cutting force $K_s = 2000$ N/mm²
- Machine consumes $P_{idle} = 4.5$ kW when running empty
- Machining time per part $t_m = 2.5$ min
- Mass of finished part $m_p = 1.2$ kg

**Goal:** Derive an expression for the total Specific Energy Consumption (kWh/kg) of the part.

---

### Step 1 — Calculate the Material Removal Rate (MRR)

The instantaneous volume of material removed per second in milling is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot d}{60 \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
\text{where } D &= \frac{v_c \times 1000}{\pi \times N} \quad \text{(cutter diameter from cutting speed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For this problem, assume cutter diameter $D = 50$ mm (a standard end mill):

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 1200 \times 0.10 \times 4 \times 3}{60 \times 10^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi \times 7200000}{60000} \\[4pt]
&= \pi \times 120 \\[4pt]
&\approx 376.99 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Calculate the Cutting Power $P_c$

The cutting power required to remove material at this rate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{MRR \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{376.99 \times 2000}{60 \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{753980}{60 \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.01257 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *theoretical* useful cutting power. The total spindle power draw $P_{spindle}$ will be higher due to mechanical inefficiencies of the spindle drive (typically 70%–85% efficient).

Assuming spindle drive efficiency $\eta_{spindle} = 0.80$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{spindle,\,electrical} &= \frac{P_c}{\eta_{spindle}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.01257}{0.80} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.01571 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Calculate the Total Machine Power Draw

The total electrical power consumed by the machine during cutting is the sum of the cutting power and the idle (auxiliary) power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= P_{spindle,\,electrical} + P_{idle} \\[4pt]
&= 0.01571 + 4.5 \\[4pt]
&\approx 4.51571 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical engineering observation:** For light-cutting CNC operations, **the idle power often dominates the total energy bill** (>99% in this example). This is why DfEE emphasizes *machine selection with low idle power* and *shutting down machines during idle periods*.

---

### Step 4 — Calculate the Total Energy per Part

Energy = Power × Time. Convert time to hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_m &= 2.5 \text{ min} = \frac{2.5}{60} \text{ h} \approx 0.04167 \text{ h} \\[6pt]
E_{part} &= P_{total} \times t_m \\[4pt]
&= 4.51571 \times 0.04167 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.1882 \;\; \text{kWh/part}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Calculate the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

SEC is the energy per unit mass of finished product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \frac{E_{part}}{m_p} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.1882}{1.2} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.1568 \;\; \text{kWh/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Energy Efficiency of the Process

The process energy efficiency compares useful cutting energy to total electrical energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cutting} &= P_c \times t_m = 0.01257 \times 0.04167 \times 1000 \approx 0.000524 \text{ kWh} \\[6pt]
\eta_{process} &= \frac{E_{cutting}}{E_{part}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.000524}{0.1882} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.28\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter-intuitive result that KTU examiners love:** A CNC milling process can be **less than 1% energy-efficient** when assessed honestly — because 99%+ of the electrical energy goes to spindle bearing friction, chip evacuation, coolant pumps, CNC controller, hydraulic systems, and lighting, NOT to actually cutting metal. This is the *single most important* DfEE insight: **reduce machine idle time, not cutting parameters.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Life-Cycle Energy of a Product

**Problem Setup:** A 5 kg steel pump housing is to be replaced by a 2.1 kg aluminum alloy design. The following data is given:

| Stage | Steel Design Energy (MJ) | Aluminum Design Energy (MJ) |
|---|---|---|
| Material extraction & refining ($E_{mat}$) | 25.0 | 11.0 |
| Manufacturing ($E_{mfg}$) | 18.0 | 9.5 |
| Use phase (10 years, $E_{use}$) | 240.0 | 168.0 |
| Disposal / recycling ($E_{disp}$) | 4.0 | 2.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> The use-phase energy is lower for aluminum because the lighter pump requires less motor energy to drive the fluid (proportional to mass).

**Goal:** Compare the two designs on a life-cycle energy basis and compute the percentage savings.

---

### Step 1 — Apply the Life-Cycle Energy Equation

$$
E_{LC} = E_{mat} + E_{mfg} + E_{use} + E_{disp}
$$

For the **steel design**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LC,\,steel} &= 25.0 + 18.0 + 240.0 + 4.0 \\[4pt]
&= 287.0 \;\; \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **aluminum design**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LC,\,Al} &= 11.0 + 9.5 + 168.0 + 2.5 \\[4pt]
&= 191.0 \;\; \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute Absolute and Percentage Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= E_{LC,\,steel} - E_{LC,\,Al} \\[4pt]
&= 287.0 - 191.0 \\[4pt]
&= 96.0 \;\; \text{MJ saved over 10 years}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Percentage savings} &= \frac{\Delta E}{E_{LC,\,steel}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{96.0}{287.0} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&\approx 33.45\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Convert to kWh and Carbon Footprint Reduction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{kWh} &= \frac{96.0}{3.6} = 26.67 \;\; \text{kWh} \\[6pt]
\Delta CF &= 26.67 \times 0.82 \approx 21.87 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2 \text{ avoided per pump}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** The aluminum pump is **33.45% more energy-efficient over its life cycle** despite the fact that *manufacturing* aluminum from virgin ore is *more* energy-intensive than steel. This is the **dominance of the use phase** — a foundational principle of DfEE. When a product has a long use phase, even a 12% mass reduction in moving components pays back many times over.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The DfEE Decision Framework

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **hierarchical flow of energy-aware design decisions** a designer makes, from the conceptual stage to end-of-life. It illustrates *interactions* between energy tiers, life-cycle stages, and design levers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start: Design Brief and Functional Specs] --> stageA1[Stage 1: Conceptual Design]
    stageA1 --> qA1{Material Selection Decision}
    qA1 --> optA1a[Low Embodied Energy Polymer]
    qA1 --> optA1b[Recycled Aluminum Alloy]
    qA1 --> optA1c[Conventional Mild Steel]
    stageA1 --> qA2{Process Route Selection}
    qA2 --> optA2a[Near Net Shape Casting]
    qA2 --> optA2b[Powder Metallurgy]
    qA2 --> optA2c[Conventional Machining from Billet]
    stageA1 --> qA3{Geometry Optimization}
    qA3 --> optA3a[Topology Optimized Ribs]
    qA3 --> optA3b[Uniform Wall Thickness]
    qA3 --> optA3c[Minimal Machining Allowance]
    optA1a --> stageA2[Stage 2: Embodiment Design]
    optA1b --> stageA2
    optA1c --> stageA2
    optA2a --> stageA2
    optA2b --> stageA2
    optA2c --> stageA2
    optA3a --> stageA2
    optA3b --> stageA2
    optA3c --> stageA2
    stageA2 --> stageA3[Stage 3: Detailed Design and Tolerance Stackup]
    stageA3 --> stageA4[Stage 4: Manufacturing Execution]
    stageA4 --> stageA5[Stage 5: Use Phase Operation]
    stageA5 --> stageA6[Stage 6: End of Life]
    stageA6 --> stageA7[Material Recovery and Remanufacturing]
    stageA7 -.feedback loop.- stageA1
    stageA2 -.energy audit checkpoint.- auditA1[SEC Benchmarking and LCA]
    stageA4 -.energy audit checkpoint.- auditA1
    stageA5 -.energy audit checkpoint.- auditA1
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Energy Flow Through a Manufacturing Cell

This diagram maps how energy *enters*, *transforms*, and *leaves* a typical manufacturing workstation, which is the standard diagram requested in KTU Part B questions on DfEE.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA[Grid Electrical Input 380 V 3 Phase] --> inB[Step Down Transformer 95 percent efficient]
    inB --> inC[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]

    inD --> inE[Spindle Bearings and Gears eta mech approx 0.90]
    inE --> inF[Cutting Zone Material Removal]
    inF --> outA[Useful Cutting Energy approx 30 percent]
    inF --> outB[Heat Dissipated to Chip and Coolant]
    inB --> auxA[CNC Controller and HMI]
    inB --> auxB[Coolant Pump and Filtration]
    inB --> auxC[Hydraulic and Pneumatic Systems]
    inB --> auxD[Work Area Lighting and HVAC Auxiliary Load]
    auxA --> outC[Idle Auxiliary Losses approx 60 percent]
    auxB --> outC
    auxC --> outC
    auxD --> outC
    inF --> outD[Sound Vibration and Radiant Losses approx 10 percent]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Decision Tree for Process Selection by Energy Criterion

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Required Feature: Cylindrical Gear 80 mm OD] --> nodeA1{Annual Volume?}
    nodeA1 -- low less than 500 --> nodeA1a[Option A: Machined from Bar Stock High SEC but low tooling cost]
    nodeA1 -- high greater than 5000 --> nodeA1b[Option B: Powder Metallurgy Lower SEC high tooling cost]
    nodeA1 -- medium --> nodeA1c[Option C: Hot Forging plus Finish Machining Balanced SEC]
    nodeA1a --> compA[Compute SEC for 1000 units]
    nodeA1b --> compA
    nodeA1c --> compA
    compA --> compB[Compare with Energy Benchmark Database]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation note for KTU answers:** When asked "draw a flow chart for DfEE process," the above Mermaid blocks (when rendered by an examiner using a Mermaid-compatible viewer) will convey *interaction* of energy flows. In a written exam, reproduce equivalent **labeled rectangular blocks connected by arrows**, with energy values annotated on each arrow — KTU examiners award full marks for clear *flow*, even if the diagram is hand-drawn.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Two questions of 3 marks each, typically 1–2 sentence answer, definition, or short derivation. Total = 6 marks.

---

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Design for Energy Efficiency (DfEE). List any four design strategies used to achieve energy efficiency in a manufactured product.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Design for Energy Efficiency (DfEE) is a product and process design philosophy that aims to minimize the total energy consumed during the entire life cycle of a product — encompassing material production, manufacturing, distribution, use, and end-of-life — while retaining its functional performance and economic viability.

**Four Design Strategies (1 mark — list any 4):**
1. Selection of materials with low embodied energy
2. Adoption of near-net-shape manufacturing processes to reduce machining energy
3. Design for reduced mass through topology optimization
4. Design for recyclability and use of recycled feedstock
5. Minimization of idle/auxiliary machine power losses
6. Design for low-friction, long-life use phase

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**What is Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)? Why is it an important metric in DfEE?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is the amount of energy required to produce one unit of output (one part, one kg of product, or one kWh of service delivered). Mathematically:

$$
SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{units}} \quad \text{[kWh/unit or MJ/kg]}
$$

**Importance (1 mark):** SEC is important because it enables *fair comparison* between different processes, machines, and design alternatives on a normalized per-unit basis. A lower SEC directly translates to lower operating cost, lower carbon footprint, and higher energy productivity — making it the most widely used single number in DfEE benchmarking and reporting.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions (Q1 and Q2) of 14 marks each. Internal choice: each Q has an OR alternative. Typically two sub-parts of 7 marks each (a + b). Total = 28 marks for the module.

---

### Part B — Question 1 (14 Marks)

#### ⭐ Choice A: [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**(a)** Explain the **eight pillars of Design for Energy Efficiency** in detail, with one engineering example for each pillar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC turning operation has the following parameters: cutting speed $v_c$ = 180 m/min, feed $f$ = 0.25 mm/rev, depth of cut $d$ = 2.5 mm, work-piece diameter $D$ = 60 mm, specific cutting energy $u_s$ = 2.5 J/mm³, and machine efficiency $\eta_m$ = 0.75. If the machine takes 4 minutes to complete one part, calculate the **(i)** Material Removal Rate, **(ii)** Total cutting energy per part in kJ, and **(iii)** Total electrical energy drawn from the mains. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

For each of the 8 pillars, write 1 short sentence and 1 example (≈0.5 mark per pillar → 4 marks) + 3 marks for structure/flow:

1. **Low Embodied Energy Materials** — Use recycled aluminum (EE ≈ 8 MJ/kg) instead of virgin aluminum (EE ≈ 170 MJ/kg). *Example: automotive body panels from recycled Al.*
2. **Low SEC Process Selection** — Choose powder metallurgy (SEC ≈ 5 kWh/kg) over machining from bar (SEC ≈ 15 kWh/kg). *Example: small spur gears.*
3. **Topology Optimization / Light-Weighting** — Replace solid blocks with ribbed/honeycomb structures. *Example: aerospace brackets reduced 40% mass via Altair Inspire.*
4. **Near-Net-Shape Manufacturing** — Use forging, casting, or MIM to minimize subsequent machining stock. *Example: forged connecting rods.*
5. **Low Friction / Long Life** — Specify low-friction coatings (DLC, PTFE) on sliding components. *Example: piston rings in IC engines.*
6. **Design for Disassembly & Recycling** — Snap-fits instead of welds, mono-material construction. *Example: smartphone housings with separable glass/PC layers.*
7. **Efficient Power Transmission** — Use helical or planetary gears (efficiency ≈ 98%) instead of worm gears (≈ 50%). *Example: robotic joint reducers.*
8. **Process Energy Recovery** — Capture waste heat from furnaces for space heating or preheating. *Example: foundry cupola waste-heat boiler.*

---

##### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $v_c = 180$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, $d = 2.5$ mm, $D = 60$ mm, $u_s = 2.5$ J/mm³, $\eta_m = 0.75$, $t_m = 4$ min.

**(i) Material Removal Rate (MRR) in turning:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= v_c \times f \times d \quad \text{[in consistent units: convert m/min to mm/s]} \\[4pt]
v_c &= 180 \text{ m/min} = \frac{180 \times 1000}{60} = 3000 \text{ mm/s} \\[4pt]
MRR &= 3000 \times 0.25 \times 2.5 \\[4pt]
&= 1875 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Cutting Energy per part:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cut} &= MRR \times u_s \times t_m \\[4pt]
t_m &= 4 \text{ min} = 240 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
E_{cut} &= 1875 \times 2.5 \times 240 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}125{,}000 \;\; \text{J} = 1125 \;\; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total electrical energy drawn from mains:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{elec} &= \frac{E_{cut}}{\eta_m} = \frac{1125}{0.75} \\[4pt]
&= 1500 \;\; \text{kJ} = 1.5 \;\; \text{MJ} \approx 0.4167 \;\; \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### ⭐ Choice B (Alternative to Q1): [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the flow of energy through a CNC machining center, identifying the locations of the major energy losses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two design alternatives for an electric motor housing are being compared:
- **Design X (Cast Iron):** Mass = 8.0 kg, embodied energy EE = 25 MJ/kg, manufacturing energy = 30 MJ, use-phase energy = 500 MJ over 8 years, end-of-life energy = 8 MJ.
- **Design Y (Aluminum Alloy):** Mass = 3.0 kg, embodied energy EE = 170 MJ/kg (virgin) or 12 MJ/kg (recycled), manufacturing energy = 18 MJ, use-phase energy = 188 MJ over 8 years, end-of-life energy = 3 MJ.

Compute the **life-cycle energy** for both Design X and Design Y using virgin aluminum, and discuss whether the recycled-aluminum option is preferable. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Expected block diagram (describe in words if hand-drawing is not possible):**

```
[Grid Mains] → [Transformer] → [VFD] → [Spindle Motor] → [Spindle Bearings] → [Cutting Zone] → [Chip + Heat]
                                  ↓
                            [CNC Controller, Coolant Pump, Hydraulics, Lighting] → [Auxiliary Losses]
```

**Major loss locations (mark each ~1 mark):**
1. Transformer core & copper losses (≈ 5%)
2. VFD switching & harmonic losses (≈ 3%)
3. Spindle motor copper + iron losses (≈ 10–15%)
4. Spindle bearing and gearbox friction (≈ 5–10%)
5. Idle auxiliary power (coolant, hydraulics, controller) (≈ 50–60%) — *the largest single loss*
6. Cutting process itself is useful; chip-bound heat is unavoidable but partially recoverable.

> Total useful cutting energy often < 1% of total electrical input.

---

##### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Design X (Cast Iron):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LC,X} &= m \times EE_{Fe} + E_{mfg} + E_{use} + E_{disp} \\
&= 8.0 \times 25 + 30 + 500 + 8 \\
&= 200 + 30 + 500 + 8 = 738 \;\; \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Design Y (Aluminum — Virgin):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LC,Y,\,virgin} &= 3.0 \times 170 + 18 + 188 + 3 \\
&= 510 + 18 + 188 + 3 = 719 \;\; \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Design Y (Aluminum — Recycled):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LC,Y,\,recycled} &= 3.0 \times 12 + 18 + 188 + 3 \\
&= 36 + 18 + 188 + 3 = 245 \;\; \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Discussion (3 marks):**
- Virgin aluminum housing is **marginally better** (719 vs 738 MJ) — only **2.6% improvement**, statistically insignificant.
- **Recycled aluminum housing is dramatically better** (245 MJ) — a **66.8% reduction** in life-cycle energy.
- **Conclusion:** DfEE conclusion is unambiguous — *use recycled aluminum*. The embodied energy of material dominates the manufacturing stage, and recycling captures **~93% of original energy**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in DfEE questions (compiled from KTU answer key trends 2022–2024):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing kWh with MJ without converting (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ). Always declare the unit at each step.
> 2. **Forgetting Idle Power (–1 to –2 marks):** When calculating total machine energy, students often compute *only* the cutting power and ignore the 4–6 kW idle power. This makes your efficiency calculation off by 2–3 orders of magnitude.
> 3. **Confusing "Energy Efficiency" with "Power Efficiency":** $\eta$ is dimensionless; $P$ is in watts. Don't write η = 0.85 kW.
> 4. **One-Stage LCA Only (–1 mark):** KTU explicitly tests whether you know the **four life-cycle stages**. Stating that DfEE means "less energy in manufacturing" alone is worth only 1–2 marks out of 7.
> 5. **Skipping Examples:** Every pillar or principle listed MUST be backed by one engineering example. A bare list without examples gets *at most* half marks.
> 6. **Carbon Footprint Confusion:** Students sometimes write "1 kWh = 1 kg CO₂" — the actual India grid factor is **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA 2023 data); older books use 0.91. Always state the factor you are using.
> 7. **Missing the Dominance of Use Phase:** For consumer products (cars, pumps, motors, HVAC), the use phase is **80%+ of life-cycle energy**. Forgetting this reverses your design recommendation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this block twice before entering the exam hall.**

- **DfEE = minimize energy across the entire life cycle, embedded at the design stage, not retrofitted later.**
- **Four life-cycle stages (memorize order):** Material → Manufacturing → Use → Disposal/Recycle.
- **Eight Pillars of DfEE:** low embodied energy materials, low SEC processes, topology optimization, near-net-shape, low-friction long-life, design for recycling, efficient power transmission, energy recovery.
- **Conversion:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
- **India grid emission factor (2023):** $\approx 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$.
- **Motor efficiency range:** $\eta_{motor} = 0.70$ (small) to $0.95$ (large IE3/IE4).
- **CNC machines are <1% energy-efficient** in light-cutting operations because idle auxiliary power dominates.
- **Use-phase dominance rule:** If a product has a service life > 5 years and uses energy during use, **use-phase > all other stages combined**.
- **SEC formula:** $SEC = E_{total}/N_{units}$ — the single most reported DfEE metric.
- **Payback formula:** $t_{pb} = C_{invest}/(\dot{E}_{saved} \times C_{energy})$ — for retrofit economics.
- **Embodied energy of common materials (memorize):** Virgin Al ≈ 170 MJ/kg; Recycled Al ≈ 8–12 MJ/kg; Mild Steel ≈ 25 MJ/kg; Cast Iron ≈ 25 MJ/kg; PVC ≈ 60 MJ/kg; ABS ≈ 90 MJ/kg; Concrete ≈ 1 MJ/kg; Wood ≈ 10 MJ/kg.
- **MRR for turning:** $MRR = v_c \times f \times d$ (watch unit conversions).
- **Cutting power for milling:** $P_c = (MRR \times K_s)/(60 \times 10^6)$ in kW.
- **Life-cycle energy:** $E_{LC} = E_{mat} + E_{mfg} + E_{use} + E_{disp}$ — write this formula explicitly in every LCA problem.
- **Top 3 designer-leveraged energy decisions:** (1) Material choice, (2) Process choice, (3) Mass reduction through geometry.
- **DfEE is part of the broader DfX family:** DFM, DFA, DfE, DfS — KTU often asks for *one-line differentiation* between them.
- **Always include an engineering example** when listing a principle. A list without examples is half-credit.
